An agency performs some but not all diligence steps; it may be treated as not having used reasonable diligence and therefore be precluded from court-order relief for later-discovered Part 2 records.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Preclusion from seeking court order to use/disclose later-discovered Part 2 records

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_11_008 applies when an agency later discovers it possesses Part 2 records and did not use reasonable diligence under §2.3; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_14_001 defines the safe-harbor diligence actions and the 60-day timing requirement that must be satisfied to avoid preclusion.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If diligence is not performed correctly/timely, the agency is precluded from seeking a court order to use/disclose those Part 2 records later discovered in its possession.

business_rules.rule_name: Intermediary must list disclosures for prior 3 years
business_rules.description: The final rule extends the “look back” period for an intermediary’s required list of disclosures from 2 years to 3 years under § 2.24. The intermediary (not the Part 2 program itself) is responsible for compliance with the required list of disclosures under § 2.24. The stated rationale is alignment with the accounting of disclosures right in § 2.25 for disclosures made with consent, which uses a 3-year look back period.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an organization qualifies as an “intermediary” under Part 2 and receives records under a general designation in a written patient consent and is obligated to produce a list of disclosures upon request.
business_rules.consequences: Intermediary must be able to produce and support a list of disclosures covering the prior 3 years. Failure to maintain the necessary disclosure logs/data can lead to inability to fulfill Part 2 disclosure-list obligations and increased compliance risk.
business_rules.exceptions: Intermediaries are not subject to HIPAA accounting of disclosures by definition in the final rule discussion because covered entities and business associates are excluded from the intermediary definition; this Part 2 list-of-disclosures requirement remains effective and is not tolled pending HIPAA accounting-of-disclosures finalization.

source_reference.section_id: § 2.24 list of disclosures look-back extension and responsibility
source_reference.source_text: the new right to an accounting of disclosures in § 2.25 for disclosures made with consent, that contains a 3-year look back period. As we have stated prior to this final rule, the intermediary, not the part 2 program itself, is responsible for compliance with the required list of disclosures under §

examples: An intermediary receives Part 2 records through a general designation consent; when a patient requests the list of disclosures, the intermediary produces entries covering the last 36 months.
An intermediary has only 18 months of logs due to system changes; it cannot fulfill the 3-year look back requirement and must remediate logging/retention.
A Part 2 program asks an intermediary for disclosure history; the intermediary remains responsible to respond to the patient request under §2.24 rather than shifting the burden to the program.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Part 2 compliance exposure for failure to provide required list of disclosures

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

dependencies.rationale: 11_009 extends intermediary list-of-disclosures look-back to 3 years and explains alignment with §2.25 accounting of disclosures (20_003). Implementing one generally requires shared logging/data-retention controls to generate disclosure histories over a 3-year period.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If disclosure logging cannot support 3-year output, the entity/intermediary may fail patient disclosure transparency obligations and create enforcement/complaint exposure.

dependencies.rationale: 11_009 applies when an organization qualifies as an intermediary and receives records under a general designation in a written patient consent. 21_005 requires that the consent name the intermediary and specify member participants or a properly limited class. Without a valid intermediary consent, the intermediary relationship (and downstream disclosure governance) is defective.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the consent lacks required intermediary/member participant naming/limitations, disclosures to/through the intermediary may be impermissible and the intermediary may be unable to lawfully operate the disclosure-list workflow.

dependencies.rationale: Rule 11_009 requires intermediaries to maintain a 3-year list of disclosures under §2.24. This complements the general prohibition/permission structure in 001_002 by enabling traceability of disclosures and supporting auditing and response to patient requests.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 11_009 is not met, an intermediary may be unable to evidence that disclosures complied with Part 2 restrictions contemplated by 001_002, increasing enforcement and dispute risk.

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_23_006 requires a 3-year accounting timeframe for consent-based disclosures (Part 2 accounting). BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_11_009 extends an intermediary’s disclosure list to 3 years. A unified disclosure logging system supports compliance with both.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the entity designs logging only for one timeframe or actor, it may be unable to satisfy the other requirement without rework and data gaps.

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_11_009 expands intermediary disclosure list lookback from 2 to 3 years. BR_REGULATEDENTITY_REGULATORY_24_001 governs the overall implementation timeline for the final rule modifications, requiring intermediaries to update logging/retention accordingly.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If intermediary systems are not updated by the compliance date, the intermediary may be unable to produce a compliant 3-year list when requested.

dependencies.rationale: The notice must state the 3-year right; intermediaries are responsible for maintaining and producing the 3-year disclosure list under §2.24. These rules jointly support the patient’s right and the system’s ability to fulfill it.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If intermediaries cannot produce 3-year disclosure lists, patient rights disclosed in the notice may be frustrated in practice, increasing compliance risk and complaint exposure.

dependencies.rationale: 11_009 applies to organizations qualifying as 'intermediary' under Part 2; 26_010 states covered entities and business associates are excluded from 'intermediary'. Thus, when the HIE/health IT vendor is a BA, 11_009 intermediary obligations should not attach.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Misclassification can cause either (a) unnecessary and burdensome intermediary tracking/program build (over-compliance), or (b) failure to meet the correct compliance model (e.g., relying on intermediary logic when BA pathways/consent general designation rules should be used).

business_rules.rule_name: Nursing Facility Must Have RN Onsite 24 Hours a Day
business_rules.description: Nursing facilities subject to the CMS Final Rule minimum staffing standards must have a registered nurse (RN) onsite 24 hours per day, 7 days per week who is available to provide direct resident care, subject to specified regulatory exceptions.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to nursing facilities covered by the CMS Final Rule minimum staffing standards under 42 C.F.R. § 483.35(c) when determining compliance with required nurse staffing availability for resident care.
business_rules.consequences: If met: facility demonstrates compliance with the RN coverage standard. If violated: facility is at risk for CMS survey noncompliance findings, quality-of-care deficiencies, and associated enforcement remedies (e.g., deficiencies, plans of correction, potential payment remedies depending on severity and duration).
business_rules.exceptions: The requirement includes 'certain exceptions' referenced by CMS; the specific exception criteria are not included in this document excerpt and must be validated against 42 C.F.R. § 483.35(c) and related CMS guidance.

source_reference.section_id: 42 C.F.R. § 483.35(c) (RN onsite 24/7 requirement) and CMS Final Rule discussion
source_reference.source_text: Also, nursing facilities under the CMS Final Rule are required to have a registered nurse onsite 24 hours a day, 7 days a week who is available to provide direct resident care (with certain exceptions).

examples: A skilled nursing facility schedules an RN in-house for all night shifts (e.g., 11 p.m.–7 a.m.) and maintains continuous RN presence across all days of the week to meet the 24/7 onsite RN requirement.
A facility relies on an on-call RN who is offsite overnight; even if the RN can arrive within 30 minutes, the facility would not meet the 'onsite 24 hours a day' requirement.

A facility claims an exception applies for a limited period; compliance staff must document the applicable exception basis per 42 C.F.R. § 483.35(c) and retain evidence supporting why the exception is met.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey deficiency citation

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

dependencies.rationale: While 12_001 is a minimum RN coverage requirement (with exceptions), 12_002 requires facilities to assess and adjust staffing to meet acuity needs and document the rationale; that assessment is a key compliance artifact used to justify staffing levels and detect gaps, including RN coverage planning.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Without the facility-wide assessment, the facility may be unable to evidence a compliant basis for staffing decisions and may be at higher risk of survey deficiencies if coverage gaps occur.

business_rules.rule_name: Facility-Wide Assessment Must Be Updated at Least Annually
business_rules.description: Each nursing facility must conduct and document a facility-wide assessment to determine resources necessary to competently care for residents on a daily basis and during emergencies. The facility-wide assessment must be reviewed and updated at least annually and when necessary to address changes in the resident population or facility. Under the CMS Final Rule, facilities must continue performing the annual assessment and adjust nurse staffing to meet resident acuity needs.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to nursing facilities required to comply with 42 C.F.R. § 483.71, including ongoing operations and when there are material changes in resident population acuity, services offered, or facility operations that impact staffing and resources for routine and emergency care.
business_rules.consequences: If met: facility can evidence a compliant basis for resource and staffing decisions, including acuity-based adjustments. If violated: facility is at risk of CMS survey deficiencies for failure to conduct/document/update the assessment and may face quality-of-care enforcement exposure if inadequate resources contribute to poor outcomes.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in this excerpt. (Facilities remain required to update 'when necessary' beyond the annual minimum.)

source_reference.section_id: 42 C.F.R. § 483.71 (facility-wide assessment) and CMS Final Rule linkage
source_reference.source_text: each nursing facility to conduct and document a facility -wide assessment to determine the resources that are necessary to competently care for residents on a daily basis and during emergencies. Facility -wide assessments must be reviewed and updated annually and when necessary to address changes in

examples: After opening a new ventilator-capable unit, a facility updates its facility-wide assessment within the year (not waiting for the annual cycle) to reflect increased RN and respiratory therapy resources needed for emergencies and daily care.

A facility completes a facility-wide assessment once at the beginning of the year but does not update it after a major shift toward higher-acuity residents; this violates the 'when necessary' update requirement.
During annual compliance review, the facility produces the prior 12 months' assessment version history and documentation showing annual review plus interim updates tied to resident-acuity changes.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey deficiency citation

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Contractor Redisclosure Requires Part 2 Binding Contract and Safeguards
business_rules.description: A lawful holder (other than covered entities and business associates) that rediscloses patient identifying information for payment or health care operations to its contractors, subcontractors, or voluntary legal representatives must: (1) have a written contract or comparable legal instrument binding the recipient to 42 CFR part 2 upon receipt; (2) furnish the required notice under § 2.32; (3) require safeguards to prevent unauthorized uses/disclosures; (4) require reporting of any unauthorized uses, disclosures, or breaches to the lawful holder; and (5) limit redisclosure to information necessary for duties under the contract. Contracts may not permit redisclosure to third parties except limited contract-agent scenarios, and the agent may only further disclose back to the contractor or lawful holder of origin.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a non-CE/non-BA lawful holder receives part 2 patient identifying information for payment/health care operations and needs to share it with a contractor/subcontractor/voluntary legal representative to perform those functions on the lawful holder’s behalf under 42 C.F.R. § 2.33(b)(3) and (c).
business_rules.consequences: If met: redisclosures to downstream service providers are permitted within defined constraints. If violated: unlawful redisclosure risk under 42 CFR part 2, potential enforcement exposure and contractual noncompliance; increased breach/unauthorized disclosure risk due to missing binding obligations, notice, or safeguards.
business_rules.exceptions: Redisclosure to a third party is prohibited unless the third party is a contract agent of the contractor/subcontractor helping provide services described in the contract, and the agent only further discloses the information back to the contractor or lawful holder from which the information originated.

source_reference.section_id: 42 C.F.R. § 2.33(c) (lawful holder redisclosure contracts, safeguards, and limits)
source_reference.source_text: a part 2 program, covered entity, or business associate may use and disclose those records for treatment, payment, and health care operations as permitted by the HIPAA regulations, until such time as

examples: A claims administrator (not a covered entity) sends part 2 patient identifying info to a billing contractor; it executes a contract binding the contractor to part 2, includes § 2.32 notice distribution, and requires incident reporting back to the administrator.
A lawful holder shares an entire part 2 record dataset with a contractor even though only eligibility dates are needed; this violates the 'only necessary' redisclosure limitation.
A contractor forwards part 2 data to an unrelated analytics vendor (not a contract agent under the allowed terms); this violates the prohibition on third-party redisclosure.
business_rules.enforcement_action: HHS Part 2 enforcement action; potential civil/criminal exposure depending on statute and facts

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

dependencies.rationale: Rule 001_002 is the broad mandate to comply with Part 2 restrictions regardless of external justifications. Rule 12_003 specifies concrete compliance controls (contracts, safeguards, notices, reporting) for lawful holders redisclosing for payment/operations. The rules work together to prevent impermissible downstream disclosure.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 001_002 is not implemented as a baseline control, lawful holders are more likely to redisclose based on improper rationales (e.g., 'they already know'), undermining 12_003’s required downstream constraints and increasing unlawful redisclosure risk.

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATEDENTITY_REGULATORY_24_001 sets the effective/compliance timeline for implementing Part 2 final rule modifications. BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_12_003 requires written contracts/legal instruments, notices, safeguards, and reporting duties for certain redisclosures; these must be implemented by the compliance date.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If policies/contracts are not implemented by the compliance date, redisclosures to contractors/subcontractors/legal reps may become unlawful under Part 2.

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_12_003 governs how a non-CE/non-BA lawful holder can redisclose Part 2 patient identifying information for payment/health care operations to contractors/subcontractors/legal representatives (requiring binding instruments, notice, safeguards, and reporting). Practically, the lawful holder’s right to possess/use the information for TPO is often established via a valid Part 2 TPO consent consistent with BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENTREQUIREMENT_23_008 (and 23_009).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the underlying TPO consent is invalid or absent, the lawful holder may not have a lawful basis to redisclose at all; 12_003 contracting controls cannot cure an upstream authorization defect.

dependencies.rationale: The redisclosure-to-contractors rule requires safeguards to prevent unauthorized uses/disclosures and reporting of unauthorized uses/disclosures. These operational requirements depend on having documented, implemented Part 2 security policies/procedures covering storage, transmission, destruction, and threat mitigation.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the entity lacks required security policies/procedures, it is less able to implement and enforce the safeguard/reporting obligations in downstream contracts, increasing unlawful redisclosure and breach risk.

dependencies.rationale: The redisclosure rule requires furnishing the required notice under §2.32 when redisclosing to contractors/subcontractors/voluntary legal representatives. In EHR exchange contexts, the abbreviated notice is a practical mechanism to satisfy that notice obligation within system limitations.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the organization cannot operationalize the required notice in constrained EHR workflows, disclosures may proceed without the required notice, increasing Part 2 noncompliance risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 no longer applies after de-identification
business_rules.description: If a Part 2 program de-identifies Part 2 records for disclosure to a public health authority under the HIPAA de-identification standard (45 CFR 164.514(b)), Part 2 protections do not extend to the de-identified information because de-identified information is outside the scope of Part 2 applicability. Once de-identified for that disclosure, Part 2 no longer applies to the de-identified records.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a regulated entity functioning as a Part 2 program (or lawful holder of Part 2 records) de-identifies records consistent with the HIPAA de-identification standard for disclosure (e.g., public health purposes).
business_rules.consequences: If de-identification meets 45 CFR 164.514(b), the resulting de-identified dataset is outside Part 2 and is not subject to Part 2 restrictions. If the dataset is not properly de-identified, Part 2 restrictions continue to apply and disclosure without consent may be impermissible.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text. (Note: This does not determine whether HIPAA or other laws apply to the de-identified data.)

source_reference.section_id: Final Rule discussion—effect of de-identification on Part 2 applicability
source_reference.source_text: We reiterate here that the proposed change should not be construed as extending the protections of part 2 to de-identified information, as such information is outside the scope of § 2.12(a). Thus, once part 2 records are de-identified for disclosure to public health authorities, part 2 no longer applies to the de-identified records.

examples: A Part 2 program removes HIPAA safe harbor identifiers (per 45 CFR 164.514(b)) and sends the de-identified dataset to the state health department; Part 2 no longer applies to that de-identified dataset after disclosure.
A program claims data are de-identified but retains elements that leave a reasonable basis to identify a patient; Part 2 still applies and disclosure without consent may violate Part 2.

A program produces two outputs: (1) identifiable Part 2 record for treatment (Part 2 applies), and (2) a de-identified public health extract (Part 2 does not apply to the extract once properly de-identified).
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

dependencies.rationale: 013_001 states that properly de-identified data under 164.514(b) is outside Part 2; 20_004 aligns the research de-identification standard with 164.514. Implementations should use the same de-identification controls, documentation, and expert/statistical methods for both.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If entities use inconsistent or weaker de-identification approaches, they may erroneously treat identifiable Part 2 data as de-identified and disclose impermissibly in either research or public health contexts.

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 legal proceeding use limits apply to any holder
business_rules.description: Restrictions on using or disclosing Part 2 records to initiate or substantiate criminal charges against a patient, to conduct a criminal investigation of a patient, or to use in any civil, criminal, administrative, or legislative proceeding against a patient apply to any person who obtains the record from a Part 2 program, covered entity, business associate, intermediary, or lawful holder—regardless of the status of the person obtaining the record and regardless of whether the record was obtained in accordance with Part 2.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when any person (including downstream recipients) obtains a Part 2 record from a Part 2 program, covered entity, business associate, intermediary, or lawful holder, including situations where the record may have been obtained outside Part 2’s rules.
business_rules.consequences: If the record is used or disclosed for prohibited purposes in proceedings against the patient, the user/discloser is in violation of the Part 2 restriction described. If properly constrained, use/disclosure for these prohibited purposes is prevented unless another authorization basis (e.g., patient consent or court order where allowed) exists.
business_rules.exceptions: The cited text indicates the restriction applies regardless of whether obtained in accordance with Part 2; no further exceptions are stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: § 2.12(d) Applicability—legal proceeding protections
source_reference.source_text: Paragraph (d) of § 2.12, as finalized, provides that restrictions on the use and disclosure of any record to initiate or substantiate criminal charges against a patient or to conduct any criminal investigation of a patient, or to use in any civil, criminal, administrative, or legislative proceeding against a patient, applies to any person who obtains the record from a part 2 program, covered entity, business associate, intermediary, or lawful holder regardless of the status of the person obtaining the record or whether the record was obtained in accordance with part 2.

examples: A hospital (covered entity) receives Part 2 records and later a litigant seeks to use them in an administrative proceeding against the patient; the restriction applies to the litigant if they obtained the record from a lawful holder.
A business associate receives Part 2 data and cannot use it to help initiate criminal charges against the patient, even if the BA believes the data was shared improperly upstream.
An intermediary routing Part 2 records cannot provide them to law enforcement for a criminal investigation of the patient absent an applicable authorization basis.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

dependencies.rationale: 013_002 applies to any person who obtains Part 2 records from covered pathways; 21_004 clarifies that even when information is obtained through patient access, it still may not be used/disclosed for criminal charge/investigation purposes against the patient—strengthening enforcement against an end-run around Part 2.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Without this linkage, parties might attempt to treat patient-accessed copies as usable for prosecution; 21_004 closes that route consistent with 013_002’s policy.

business_rules.rule_name: Audit or evaluation data may be used only for that purpose
business_rules.description: Patient identifying information obtained during an audit or evaluation under Part 2 may not be used or disclosed for any purposes other than those necessary to complete the audit or evaluation as specified in the audit/evaluation provision.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a Part 2 program, government agency, or any other person obtains patient identifying information during an audit or evaluation under the referenced paragraph (e).
business_rules.consequences: If patient identifying information obtained for audit/evaluation is used or disclosed for another purpose, it is not authorized by the cited paragraph and constitutes noncompliance with Part 2 limitations described. If used strictly to complete the audit/evaluation, the use/disclosure remains within the permitted purpose.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: § 2.53 audit/evaluation limitation (paragraph (e)(6))
source_reference.source_text: (6) The provisions of this paragraph (e) do not authorize the part 2 program, the Federal, state, or local government agency, or any other person to use or disclose patient identifying information obtained during the audit or evaluation for any purposes other than those necessary to complete the audit or evaluation as specified in this paragraph (e).

examples: A state auditor receives identifiable Part 2 records to evaluate program compliance; the auditor may not reuse the list to initiate a separate enforcement investigation against patients.
A contractor performing an audit cannot upload patient identifying information into a general analytics tool for unrelated business development.
An evaluation team may share records with its legal representatives only to complete the evaluation, not to support unrelated litigation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 court order authorizes but does not compel disclosure
business_rules.description: A Part 2 court order entered under Subpart E authorizes a use or disclosure that would otherwise be prohibited, but does not itself compel disclosure. To compel disclosure, a subpoena or similar compulsory legal process is required in addition to an authorizing court order; without a valid subpoena/compulsory process (or if it has expired/been quashed), the record holder may refuse disclosure even if an authorizing order exists.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a regulated entity or other person holds records subject to Part 2 and receives legal process (subpoena/mandate) and/or an authorizing court order under Part 2 Subpart E.
business_rules.consequences: Without an authorizing Part 2 court order, the holder may not disclose in response to a subpoena. With an authorizing order but without a subpoena or other compulsory process, the holder may refuse disclosure. Upon a valid subpoena/compulsory process, the holder must disclose unless another valid legal defense applies (other than Part 2 confidentiality restrictions).
business_rules.exceptions: If there is a valid legal defense to the subpoena/compulsory process other than Part 2 confidentiality restrictions, the holder may refuse even with compulsory process (as stated in the excerpt).

source_reference.section_id: § 2.61 Legal effect of order
source_reference.source_text: An order of a court of competent jurisdiction entered under this subpart is a unique kind of court order. Its only purpose is to authorize a use or disclosure of patient information which would otherwise be prohibited by 42 U.S.C. 290dd–2 and the regulations in this part. Such an order does not compel use or disclosure. A subpoena or a similar legal mandate must be issued to compel use or disclosure.
Section 2.61 includes the requirement that in addition to a court order that authorizes disclosure, a subpoena is required to compel disclosure of part 2 records.

examples: Requester obtains a Part 2 authorizing order but no subpoena; the holder may decline production absent compulsion.
A subpoena alone without an authorizing Part 2 order cannot compel disclosure of Part 2 records.
business_rules.enforcement_action: court sanction for noncompliance with subpoena

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules state the same Part 2 legal-effect principle: a Part 2 court order authorizes disclosure but does not compel; compulsion requires subpoena/other compulsory process in addition to an authorizing order.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_013_004 selected: most complete legal-effect explanation (authorizes vs compels; holder may refuse without compulsory process).

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_013_004 distinguishes between an authorizing order vs compulsion; the authorizing order must meet Part 2 requirements (essential parts only, limited persons, protective measures). BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_013_006 provides those requirements.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If an order lacks required limitations, reliance on it as an authorizing Part 2 order is risky and may lead to unauthorized disclosures or evidentiary challenges.

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 order application must use fictitious patient name
business_rules.description: For a noncriminal-purpose court order application under Part 2, the application must use a fictitious name (e.g., John Doe) and may not contain or otherwise disclose any patient identifying information unless (1) the patient is the applicant, (2) the patient has given written consent meeting Part 2 requirements, or (3) the court has ordered the record of the proceeding sealed from public scrutiny.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any person applying for an order authorizing use/disclosure of Part 2 patient records or testimony for purposes other than criminal investigation/prosecution in a civil, administrative, or legislative proceeding.
business_rules.consequences: If the application includes patient identifying information without meeting one of the permitted bases, it violates the application requirements and risks improper disclosure during the court process. If filed properly (fictitious name, no identifiers unless permitted), it complies with the confidentiality protections during application.
business_rules.exceptions: Patient is the applicant; or patient gives written consent meeting Part 2 requirements; or the court seals the proceeding record from public scrutiny.

source_reference.section_id: § 2.64(a) Application—noncriminal purposes
source_reference.source_text: An application must use a fictitious name, such as John Doe, to refer to any patient and may not contain or otherwise disclose any patient identifying information unless the patient is the applicant or has given written consent (meeting the requirements of the regulations in this part) to disclosure or the court has ordered the record of the proceeding sealed from public scrutiny.

examples: An employer files a civil action and seeks Part 2 records; the motion refers to the patient as John Doe and omits identifiers because there is no patient consent and no sealed record order.
The patient files the application themself; the filing may include identifying information because the patient is the applicant.
The patient signs a Part 2-compliant consent allowing identification in the filing; the applicant may include identifying information consistent with that consent.
business_rules.enforcement_action: court rejection of application or required sealing/redaction

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 court order must be limited to essential records and persons
business_rules.description: A Part 2 court order authorizing a use or disclosure must: (1) limit use/disclosure to only those parts of the patient’s record or testimony relaying those parts that are essential to fulfill the objective of the order; (2) limit use/disclosure to those persons whose need for information is the basis for the order; and (3) include other measures necessary to protect the patient, the physician-patient relationship, and treatment services (e.g., sealing the proceeding record from public scrutiny).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a court issues an authorizing order for noncriminal purposes under Part 2 procedures/criteria (as reflected in the cited section).
business_rules.consequences: Orders that do not include these limitations fail to meet the Part 2 order content requirements described. Properly limited orders constrain downstream redisclosure and reduce risk of unnecessary disclosure.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: § 2.64(e) Content of order—noncriminal purposes
source_reference.source_text: An order authorizing a use or disclosure must: (1) Limit use or disclosure to only those parts of the patient’s record, or testimony relaying those parts of the patient’s record, which are essential to fulfill the objective of the order; (2) Limit use or disclosure to those persons whose need for information is the basis for the order; and (3) Include such other measures as are necessary to limit use or disclosure for the protection of the patient... for example, sealing from public scrutiny the record of any proceeding...

examples: A court order for a benefits appeal authorizes disclosure only of the SUD treatment dates relevant to the appeal, not the full record, and only to the administrative law judge and the parties’ counsel.
An order authorizes testimony but limits it to essential elements and seals the hearing record to protect confidentiality.
A proposed order seeks disclosure of the entire patient record to a broad set of recipients; it should be narrowed to essential parts and persons to comply.
business_rules.enforcement_action: court modification required or disclosure scope limited by order terms

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

dependencies.rationale: In noncriminal-purpose applications, the applicant must use a fictitious name and avoid identifiers unless a permitted basis exists. This protects confidentiality during the process that leads to an authorizing order that must contain §2.64 limitations.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the application improperly includes identifiers, confidentiality may be breached during the court process and the order pathway can be procedurally defective.

business_rules.rule_name: Reasonable diligence steps required within 60 days
business_rules.description: To qualify for the Part 2 investigative-agency safe harbor (limitation on civil/criminal liability when Part 2 records are unknowingly received), the investigative agency must complete ALL required reasonable-diligence steps to determine whether Part 2 applies, and must perform the steps no more than 60 days before requesting records or placing an undercover agent/informant. Required steps include checking SAMHSA’s treatment locator, relevant state databases, and publicly available provider notice/website/location indicators, and other specified diligence actions.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a regulated_entity is acting as (or on behalf of) an investigative agency seeking SUD treatment records or placing an undercover agent/informant and intends to rely on the Part 2 safe harbor for unknowingly receiving part 2 records.
business_rules.consequences: If all steps are completed within the required timeframe, the investigative agency may qualify for the safe harbor limitation on civil/criminal liability. If any step is missing or performed outside the 60-day window, the agency may not qualify for safe harbor protection and may be subject to CARES Act enforcement penalties for failure to meet the prerequisites.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt; if a step is not available (for example, no website exists), the agency must still complete the other required steps; state database search applies only where such a database is available.

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR 2.3 reasonable diligence standard (final rule response)
source_reference.source_text: we are revising the proposed ‘‘reasonable diligence’’ standard to mean taking all of the following actions: searching for the practice or provider among the SUD treatment facilities in SAMHSA’s online treatment locator; searching in a similar state database of treatment facilities where available; checking a practice or program’s website, where available, or its physical location; viewing the entity’s Patient Notice or HIPAA NPP if it is available; and taking all these steps within no more than 60 days before requesting records or placing an undercover agent or informant.

source_reference.section_id: § 2.3 reasonable diligence definition (final rule response)
source_reference.source_text: this rule in the regulatory text of § 2.3 provides that “reasonable diligence” means taking all of the following actions: searching for the practice or provider among the SUD treatment facilities in SAMHSA’s online treatment locator; searching in a similar state database of treatment facilities where available; checking a practice or program’s website, where available, or physical location; viewing the entity’s Patient Notice or HIPAA NPP if it is available; and taking all these steps within no more than 60 days before requesting records or placing an undercover agent or informant.

source_reference.section_id: FR Vol. 89 No. 33 (Feb. 16, 2024), Summary of Major Provisions, Section 2.3—Civil and Criminal Penalties for Violations (reasonable diligence)
source_reference.source_text: Further modifies the 'reasonable diligence' steps to mean taking all of the following actions: searching for the practice or provider among the SUD treatment facilities in SAMHSA’s online treatment locator; searching in a similar state database of treatment facilities where available; checking a practice or program’s website, where available, or physical location; viewing the entity’s Patient Notice or HIPAA NPP if it is available; and taking all these steps within no more than 60 days before requesting records or placing an undercover agent or informant.

examples: Before issuing a subpoena to a clinic, investigators run SAMHSA and state-locator checks and document results within the prior 60 days.
Before deploying an informant, the agency verifies Part 2 applicability indicators and retains screenshots/records of the checks.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil and criminal liability exposure; CARES Act enforcement penalties for failing safe harbor prerequisites

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

deduplication_info.rationale: All rules describe the same Part 2 investigative-agency safe harbor prerequisite: perform ALL specified reasonable-diligence actions within ≤60 days before requesting records or placing an undercover agent/informant. One duplicate is higher-level/summary but still the same requirement.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_14_001 selected: most operationally specific (lists the diligence actions and the 60-day timing requirement).

dependencies.rationale: To meet the safe-harbor prerequisite (“take ALL required reasonable diligence actions”), the entity must know what actions constitute reasonable diligence; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_11_007 enumerates and defines the required diligence steps and timing window used to evidence compliance.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_11_007 diligence steps are not performed/documented, the entity cannot reliably claim compliance with the BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_14_001 safe-harbor prerequisites.

business_rules.rule_name: Safe harbor lost for knowing improper record access
business_rules.description: The Part 2 safe harbor is a limitation on civil and criminal liability and does not provide absolute immunity under Federal or state law when an agency/person knowingly obtains records improperly or under false pretenses. Knowingly obtaining records without following required procedures for the type of request, or obtaining records under false pretenses, is improper and can expose the actor to liability notwithstanding the safe harbor concept.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a regulated_entity (including an agency/person acting in an investigative capacity) obtains or attempts to obtain Part 2 records and claims good-faith protection under the safe harbor.
business_rules.consequences: If records are knowingly obtained improperly or under false pretenses, safe harbor protection does not apply; the actor remains exposed to Federal/state liability and enforcement (civil/criminal).
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Safe harbor limitation and improper/false pretenses
source_reference.source_text: while the safe harbor operates as a limitation on civil and criminal liability under 42 U.S.C. 290dd–2(f), it does not provide absolute immunity under Federal or state law should an agency or person knowingly obtain records improperly or under false pretenses. For example, it would be improper to knowingly obtain records without following the required procedures for the type of request, or under false pretenses.

examples: An investigator falsely represents that a patient has consented to release SUD records; because the records were obtained under false pretenses, safe harbor protection does not apply.
An agency uses the wrong legal process (skipping required court-order steps for the request type) and knowingly proceeds anyway; safe harbor protection is unavailable.

A regulator inadvertently receives part 2 records after completing required diligence steps; absent knowing misconduct, the safe harbor analysis may still be available (subject to meeting prerequisites).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil and criminal liability; potential Federal/state enforcement actions

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

dependencies.rationale: Even if the agency attempts to satisfy BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_14_001, BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_14_002 clarifies that safe harbor does not protect knowing improper acquisition or acquisition under false pretenses—constraining when the 14_001 pathway is usable.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If actors knowingly obtain records improperly, they cannot rely on safe harbor; compliance logic must branch away from safe-harbor protection even if diligence steps were attempted.

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 record segregation is not required on receipt
business_rules.description: Upon receipt of Part 2 records, segregation of records (data segmentation) is expressly not required. The rule neither requires nor prohibits segmentation; it states segregation is not required upon receipt.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a regulated_entity (including a HIPAA covered entity, part 2 program, or other lawful holder) receives Part 2 records into its systems and is determining whether it must segregate those records from other records.
business_rules.consequences: If the entity does not segregate Part 2 records upon receipt, it is not noncompliant solely due to lack of segmentation. If the entity chooses to segment, the rule does not prohibit it.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; other applicable laws/policies may still require special handling in specific contexts.

source_reference.section_id: Section 2.12 Applicability (segregation not required)
source_reference.source_text: The final rule removes data segmentation requirements and instead expressly states that segregation of records is not required upon receipt. This results in the final rule neither requiring nor prohibiting data segmentation

examples: A hospital imports Part 2 records into its EHR without segregating them into a separate module; segmentation is not required upon receipt under this provision.
A health plan’s claims system stores Part 2-related data in the same database as other claims; lack of segmentation alone does not violate this requirement.
A provider elects to segregate Part 2 records for operational control; the rule does not prohibit this approach.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory compliance risk if misinterpreted as mandatory segmentation; potential compliance program findings

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Patient Notice must offer discussion with a contact person
business_rules.description: Programs must establish a patient right to discuss the Patient Notice with a person designated by the program as the contact person, and include information about this right in the header of the Patient Notice.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a regulated_entity (for Part 2 Patient Notice purposes) issues or updates its Patient Notice and defines patient-facing privacy communications.
business_rules.consequences: If the Patient Notice header omits the discussion right or no contact person is designated to support discussions, the entity risks noncompliance with the finalized Patient Notice requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Section 2.22 Patient Notice (right to discuss; header inclusion)
source_reference.source_text: The Department establishes for patients the right to discuss the Patient Notice with a person designated by the program as the contact person and to include information about this right in the header of the Patient Notice

examples: An SUD program updates its Patient Notice header to say: "Questions? You may discuss this notice with our Privacy Contact" and lists a role-based contact point; this satisfies the header inclusion requirement.
A program publishes a Patient Notice but provides no method or person for discussion; it fails to establish the patient’s right to discuss with a designated contact.
A program designates a contact person but does not mention the discussion right in the header; it does not meet the stated header inclusion requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory compliance finding; corrective action plan risk

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

dependencies.rationale: The program must implement the finalized §2.22 notice requirements within 24 months; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_14_004 is one of the concrete Patient Notice elements that must be updated by that deadline.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the deadline passes without updating the Patient Notice header/contact process, the entity is out of compliance with finalized Patient Notice requirements.

business_rules.rule_name: Privacy notices may be joint if all elements are included
business_rules.description: Notices for different purposes (HIPAA, state law, and Part 2) may be separate or joint/combined, but a combined notice is acceptable only if it includes all required elements for each applicable notice regime.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a regulated_entity drafts or updates privacy notices that could include HIPAA NPP content and Part 2 Patient Notice content in one document.
business_rules.consequences: If the entity combines notices but omits any required element, the combined notice is not acceptable and creates compliance risk. If all required elements are present, separate or combined formats are acceptable.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; acceptability is contingent on inclusion of all required elements.

source_reference.section_id: Notice format (separate vs joint/combined) شرط all required elements
source_reference.source_text: notices for different purposes may be separate or joint/ combined so long as the required elements are included. Thus, either using separate HIPAA, state law, or part 2 notices or combining these notices into one form would be acceptable so long as all required elements are included.

examples: A health system issues one combined notice that includes all HIPAA NPP required elements plus all Part 2 Patient Notice required elements; it is acceptable.
A provider combines notices but leaves out the Part 2 complaint process element; the combined notice is not acceptable because required elements are missing.
A small clinic uses separate HIPAA NPP and Part 2 Patient Notice forms; separate notices remain acceptable if each includes required elements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory compliance finding; potential enforcement exposure for defective notice

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

dependencies.rationale: Entities deciding to issue a combined notice must ensure all required elements for each regime are present, and must complete those updates by the 24-month compliance deadline.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: A combined notice missing required Part 2 elements after the deadline can create immediate noncompliance, even if HIPAA elements are present.

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_14_005 allows a combined notice only if it contains every required element for each regime; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_14_004 is one of those required Part 2 elements.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the combined notice omits the discussion right/contact-person header element, the combined notice fails Part 2 notice requirements.

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 Patient Notice changes require compliance within 24 months
business_rules.description: The final rule provides 24 months from the date of publication for compliance with the finalized provisions (including Patient Notice requirements under § 2.22). Regulated entities must update processes, forms, and operational controls to meet the updated § 2.22 requirements by the end of the 24-month compliance period.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to regulated entities subject to the finalized Part 2 rule provisions that require Patient Notice updates under § 2.22.
business_rules.consequences: If the entity does not implement required § 2.22 changes by the end of the 24-month period, it is at risk of being out of compliance and subject to enforcement, corrective action, and reputational harm.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Compliance timeframe statement (24 months from publication)
source_reference.source_text: We also note that this final rule provides 24 months from the date of publication for compliance with its provisions.

examples: A Part 2 program publishes a revised Patient Notice that includes all required new elements within the 24-month window; it meets the compliance deadline.
A covered entity delays updates until after the 24-month period ends; it risks noncompliance for continued use of outdated notices.
A multi-site provider updates the notice content but fails to deploy it at several rural clinics before the 24-month deadline; those sites remain noncompliant.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory noncompliance finding; corrective action plan and potential penalties depending on violation type

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Civil proceedings use limits include administrative and legislative forums
business_rules.description: Restrictions on use and disclosure of Part 2 records in civil proceedings against patients apply in civil proceedings and are expanded to expressly include administrative and legislative proceedings. Testimony conveying information in a record in civil proceedings against patients is also restricted unless there is patient consent or a court order.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a regulated_entity (as a lawful holder, program, or party in proceedings) seeks to use/disclose Part 2 records or testimony conveying record information in a civil, administrative, or legislative proceeding against a patient.
business_rules.consequences: Without patient consent or a valid court order meeting Part 2 requirements, using/disclosing Part 2 records or conveying record content via testimony in such proceedings is restricted and can create regulatory violation risk.
business_rules.exceptions: Consent or a court order are explicitly identified as the gateways; otherwise none stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Section 2.64 Orders for noncriminal purposes (civil proceedings; testimony restriction)
source_reference.source_text: Finalizes proposed changes that expand the types of forums where restrictions on use and disclosure of records in civil proceedings against patients apply to expressly include administrative and legislative proceedings and also restricts the use of testimony conveying information in a record in civil proceedings against patients, absent consent or a court order.

examples: In a state licensing board hearing (administrative proceeding) against a patient, a party attempts to introduce Part 2 records without consent or court order; the use/disclosure is restricted.
In a civil lawsuit against a patient, a clinician is asked to testify about Part 2 record contents; absent consent or a court order, testimony conveying the record information is restricted.
If the patient provides valid consent for the disclosure in a civil proceeding, the restriction gateway is satisfied (subject to Part 2 consent requirements).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Part 2 violation enforcement exposure; potential civil/criminal penalties depending on facts

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_14_007 prohibits proceeding use/disclosure/testimony absent consent or court order. BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_013_004 governs the mechanics of using the court-order pathway (authorizes only; subpoena compels; without both, holder may refuse).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If entities attempt disclosure in proceedings without a valid authorizing order (and compulsory process when compulsion is asserted), they risk Part 2 violations and/or improper refusal/production decisions.

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_14_007 allows proceeding use/disclosure only with patient consent or court order. BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_20_002 dictates the consent form-factor: it cannot be combined with any other purpose consent.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If consent is bundled (e.g., with TPO), the disclosure remains restricted and the entity risks unauthorized disclosure in a proceeding.

business_rules.rule_name: Criminal proceedings use limits include administrative and legislative forums
business_rules.description: Restrictions on uses and disclosures of Part 2 records in criminal proceedings against patients are expanded to expressly include administrative and legislative proceedings. Testimony conveying information in a Part 2 record in criminal proceedings against patients is restricted absent patient consent or a court order.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a regulated_entity seeks to use/disclose Part 2 records or present testimony conveying Part 2 record information in a criminal, administrative, or legislative proceeding against a patient.
business_rules.consequences: Without patient consent or a court order, use/disclosure and testimony conveying Part 2 record content are restricted, creating compliance and enforcement risk if violated.
business_rules.exceptions: Consent or a court order are explicitly identified as gateways; otherwise none stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Section 2.65 Orders for criminal investigation/prosecution of patients (forum expansion; testimony restriction)
source_reference.source_text: Expands the types of forums where restrictions on uses and disclosure of records in criminal proceedings against patients apply to expressly include administrative and legislative proceedings and also restricts the use of testimony conveying information in a part 2 record in criminal proceedings against patients, absent consent or a court order.

examples: A prosecutor attempts to introduce Part 2 treatment records in a criminal proceeding against a patient without a court order or consent; the use is restricted.

A legislative hearing (legislative proceeding) targets a specific patient and requests testimony about Part 2 record content; absent consent or court order, testimony conveying the content is restricted.
A valid Part 2 court order authorizing use/disclosure for the specific proceeding satisfies the gateway (subject to Part 2 court order criteria).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Part 2 violation enforcement exposure; potential civil/criminal penalties depending on facts

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

dependencies.rationale: For proceedings, the order pathway must be correctly implemented: a subpoena alone is insufficient; an authorizing order alone does not compel; both may be needed depending on posture. This is foundational to complying with the restriction rule.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Mismanaging the order/subpoena interaction leads to unauthorized disclosures (Part 2 violations) or failure to comply with valid compulsory process when permitted.

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_14_008 restricts use/disclosure/testimony in criminal/admin/leg proceedings against the patient. A consent exception is only satisfied if the consent is standalone and specific to the proceeding purpose.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Using a combined consent invalidates the consent pathway and can cause a Part 2 violation if records are used/disclosed in proceedings.

dependencies.rationale: When evaluating whether a proposed use/disclosure in a criminal/proceeding context is permitted, entities should apply the foundational restriction in 013_002 and the expanded proceeding framing in 14_008 to ensure consistent enforcement of the limitation.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the 013_002 restriction is not recognized, entities may unlawfully use Part 2 records to support proceedings against the patient, even if obtained downstream.

business_rules.rule_name: Safe harbor liability limit requires initiating all diligence actions
business_rules.description: For the Part 2 safe harbor in § 2.3(b) (as described), the limitation on liability applies only if all specified reasonable diligence actions are initiated. If any initiated action results in knowledge that a program or person holding records is subject to Part 2, no further steps are required to further confirm Part 2 applicability.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an entity seeks to rely on the Part 2 safe harbor limitation on liability and is conducting reasonable diligence to determine whether a program/person holding records is subject to Part 2.
business_rules.consequences: If conditions are met: entity can qualify for safe harbor only after initiating all specified actions; once knowledge is obtained, additional confirmation steps are not required. If violated (e.g., not all actions initiated): entity may not qualify for liability limitation and may face enforcement exposure for improper handling of Part 2 records.
business_rules.exceptions: If any action yields knowledge of Part 2 status, the entity may stop further confirmation steps (but only after initiating required actions as described).

source_reference.section_id: Final Rule discussion of § 2.3(b) safe harbor
source_reference.source_text: for the limitation on liability to apply. We clarify here that if any of the actions taken results in knowledge that a program or person holding records is subject to part 2, no further steps are required to further confirm that

examples: A payer attempts to obtain SUD-related records and wants safe harbor: it documents initiating each required diligence action before relying on liability limitation.
An organization performs only one of the specified actions and proceeds; because not all actions were initiated, it cannot claim the safe harbor limitation if records are mishandled.
During diligence, one action confirms the provider is Part 2; the organization stops further confirmation steps and shifts to Part 2-compliant handling.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loss of safe harbor protection; potential OCR/Part 2 enforcement

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_15_005 states that liability limitation applies only if all specified diligence actions are initiated, and that once any initiated action yields knowledge of Part 2 applicability, no further confirmation steps are required. This governs execution of BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_14_001.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If entities misunderstand initiation/stop rules, they may either (a) fail safe-harbor prerequisites (missing initiated steps) or (b) waste effort and still fail to evidence correct compliance logic.

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 programs must accept complaints and prohibit retaliation
business_rules.description: A Part 2 program must have a process to receive complaints concerning the program’s compliance with Part 2 regulations. The program may not intimidate, threaten, coerce, discriminate against, or take other retaliatory action against any patient for exercising Part 2 rights or for participating in Part 2 processes (including filing a complaint). The program may not require patients to waive the right to file a complaint as a condition of treatment, payment, enrollment, or eligibility.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Part 2 programs’ patient-facing operations and intake/administrative processes whenever a patient seeks to file (or has filed) a complaint, or when the program sets conditions for treatment, payment, enrollment, or eligibility.
business_rules.consequences: If conditions are met: program must provide complaint intake process and protect patients from retaliation; program must not condition services or payment on waiver of complaint rights. If violated: complaint may be filed and investigated; program may face enforcement actions, corrective action requirements, and penalties.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Section 2.4—Complaints of Noncompliance (Proposed; requirements described)
source_reference.source_text: The Department proposed to add § 2.4(a) to require a part 2 program to have a process to receive complaints concerning a program’s compliance with the part 2 regulations. Proposed § 2.4(b) provided that a part 2 program may not intimidate, threaten, coerce, discriminate against, or take other retaliatory action against any patient for the exercise of any right established, or for participation in any process provided for in part 2, including the filing of a complaint. The Department also proposed to add § 2.4(c) to prohibit a part 2 program from requiring patients to waive their right to file a complaint as a condition of the provision of treatment, payment, enrollment, or eligibility

examples: A patient emails a privacy complaint; the program logs it, routes it to compliance staff, and provides a written acknowledgment per its complaint process.
After a patient files a complaint, front-desk staff cancel appointments as punishment; this is retaliatory and prohibited.
A clinic’s intake packet includes a clause stating 'patient waives right to complain'; the clause is removed because it is an impermissible condition of treatment/enrollment.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR/Part 2 investigation; corrective action plan and potential civil monetary penalty

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Conditioning Treatment on TPO Consent Requires Restriction Process
business_rules.description: A Part 2 program should not condition treatment on a TPO consent unless it has taken reasonable steps to establish a workable process to address patients’ requests for restrictions on uses and disclosures for TPO. This aligns with the finalized patient rights to request restrictions on TPO disclosures referenced in § 2.22 (rule of construction) and § 2.26 (right to request restrictions), and reinforces the expectation that disclosures include only information necessary for the disclosure’s purpose (minimum necessary concept as described for Part 2).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the program considers requiring a patient to sign a broad consent covering future TPO uses/disclosures as a condition of receiving treatment (e.g., ongoing treatment).
business_rules.consequences: If the program conditions treatment without first having a workable restriction-request process, it creates heightened risk of noncompliance with the described guardrails and increased confidentiality/ethical practice concerns. If the program has the process, it may condition treatment subject to its consent policies and handling of restriction requests.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt. The text notes Congress did not directly address conditioning treatment, but emphasized guardrails in the CARES Act; implementation may vary based on feasible processes and program policies.

source_reference.section_id: Response on conditioning treatment on TPO consent (references § 2.22 and § 2.26)
source_reference.source_text: We believe that a program should not condition treatment on a TPO consent unless it has taken reasonable steps to establish a workable process to address patients’ requests for restrictions on uses and disclosures for TPO. We are finalizing as proposed in § 2.22 the rule of construction that a patient has the right to request restrictions on disclosures for TPO and in § 2.26 a patient’s right to request restrictions. Additionally, the existing rule provides that all disclosures of part 2 records should include only the information necessary for the purpose of the disclosure.

examples: An OTP requires new patients to sign a blanket TPO consent to start counseling; before doing so, it must implement a process to receive and address restriction requests (e.g., restricting disclosure to a specific health plan contact).
A program conditions ongoing treatment on signing a TPO consent; it must have an operational workflow for documenting, reviewing, and accommodating restriction requests to the extent feasible.
A program allows treatment without TPO consent but offers it as an option; the restriction-request process still applies when patients submit restriction requests for TPO disclosures.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: No Right of Access to Psychotherapy or SUD Counseling Notes
business_rules.description: Neither HIPAA nor 42 CFR Part 2 provides a right of patient access to psychotherapy notes or SUD counseling notes. Under HIPAA, psychotherapy notes are excluded from the right of access in 45 CFR 164.524. Under Part 2, there is no general right of access for Part 2 records; therefore there is no right of access to SUD counseling notes (a narrow subset of Part 2 records). Clinicians may, at their discretion, voluntarily provide patients access to psychotherapy notes and/or SUD counseling notes (or portions).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a patient requests access to psychotherapy notes (HIPAA context) or SUD counseling notes (Part 2 context), including when such notes are part of a designated record set but excluded from access rights.
business_rules.consequences: If the request is for psychotherapy notes/SUD counseling notes, the regulated entity is not required to provide access as a matter of right under these rules. If the entity mistakenly treats these notes as subject to mandatory access, it may disclose highly sensitive content beyond what the regulations require. If the clinician elects to provide voluntary access, it is permitted.
business_rules.exceptions: Clinician discretion: clinicians may voluntarily provide patients with access to psychotherapy notes and/or SUD counseling notes or portions of such notes.

source_reference.section_id: Patient access discussion (45 CFR 164.524 exclusion and Part 2 no general access right)
source_reference.source_text: Neither HIPAA nor part 2 provides a right of access to psychotherapy notes or SUD counseling notes, but for different reasons. Under HIPAA, although psychotherapy notes are part of the designated record set (because the clinician may use them to make decisions about the individual), they are specifically excluded from the right of access in 45 CFR 164.524. Under part 2, there is no general right of access for part 2 records, and thus there is no right of access for SUD counseling notes...

examples: A patient requests a copy of psychotherapy session notes; the provider may deny the request under 45 CFR 164.524’s exclusion for psychotherapy notes, while still providing other accessible records.

A Part 2 program receives a patient request for SUD counseling notes; it is not required to provide access as a matter of right because Part 2 has no general right of access and SUD counseling notes are a narrow subset.
A clinician chooses to share a portion of SUD counseling notes with a patient to support therapy goals; this is allowed as voluntary access.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty
