business_rules.rule_name: Legal proceeding consent must be separate from TPO
business_rules.description: When a patient provides consent for use or disclosure of 42 CFR Part 2 records in civil, criminal, administrative, or legislative investigations and proceedings, that consent must be separate from a consent used to permit uses and disclosures of Part 2 records for other purposes (e.g., treatment, payment, and health care operations (TPO)). This creates a required separation/segmentation of authorization intent so that a general TPO consent cannot be repurposed to authorize legal-proceeding uses/disclosures.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a regulated entity (including a Part 2 program/holder) seeks to obtain or rely upon patient consent to use or disclose Part 2 records for use in an investigation or proceeding (civil, criminal, administrative, or legislative).
business_rules.consequences: If the entity uses a combined or non-separate consent to support a legal-proceeding disclosure/use, the consent is noncompliant for that purpose and the resulting use/disclosure may constitute an unauthorized Part 2 disclosure, creating exposure to federal penalties and evidentiary challenges. If the rule is met, the entity can demonstrate the required guardrail against coerced or bundled consent for legal proceedings.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the provided excerpt (separate consent is an express requirement for legal-proceeding consents).

source_reference.section_id: Section 2.12 discussion; reference to § 2.31(d)
source_reference.source_text: We have added at § 2.31(d) an express requirement that consent for use and disclosure of records in civil, criminal, administrative, and legislative investigations and proceedings be separate from consent to use and disclose part 2 records for other purposes.

examples: A Part 2 program uses one combined consent form for both TPO sharing and a prosecutor’s request; this violates the requirement because legal-proceeding consent must be separate.
A clinic uses two distinct forms: (1) a single future TPO consent and (2) a separate consent titled “Consent for Use/Disclosure in Legal Proceedings”; this satisfies the separation requirement.
A patient revokes the TPO consent but not the separate legal-proceeding consent; the entity must treat them independently because they are separate consents.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal Part 2 enforcement penalties; potential evidentiary exclusion or suppression challenges

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 referrals generally require prior written consent
business_rules.description: A regulated entity subject to 42 CFR Part 2 may not rely on verbal consent as an express permission to disclose Part 2 records for intake and referral; prior written consent is a statutory requirement (42 U.S.C. 290dd–2(b)(1)(A)). When written consent cannot be obtained, the entity should use alternative approaches such as de-identification or having the patient directly provide identifying information to the receiving provider (e.g., via a three-way call), or rely on applicable emergency/disaster disclosure provisions.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a Part 2 program/holder contemplates disclosing patient-identifying Part 2 records to another provider or entity for intake or referral before obtaining written consent.
business_rules.consequences: If the entity discloses patient-identifying Part 2 records for referral/intake based solely on verbal consent, the disclosure is noncompliant and may trigger Part 2 violation exposure. If the entity instead uses de-identified data or patient-direct disclosure workflows until written consent is executed, the entity reduces risk and aligns with statutory requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: Medical emergency disclosures may be permissible; separately, Part 2 permits certain disaster-related disclosures when programs are closed or unable to obtain consent (as noted in the excerpt).

source_reference.section_id: Consent discussion; verbal consent proposal declined
source_reference.source_text: We decline to adopt an express permission to accept a verbal consent to disclose part 2 records for purposes of intake and referral because prior written consent is a statutory requirement in 42 U.S.C. 290dd–2(b)(1)(A); however, some options for handling referrals verbally may be available depending on the circumstances. One approach would be to provide de-identified information about the patient to a potential treatment provider...

examples: A county call center requests that a Part 2 program fax a named patient’s SUD diagnosis based on a phone call; without written consent this is noncompliant unless an emergency exception applies.

A Part 2 program calls a potential receiving provider and shares only de-identified clinical needs (no patient identifiers) to confirm bed availability; then the patient contacts the provider directly to share identifiers—compliant approach.
During an attempted overdose, a program discloses necessary Part 2 information to a hotline call center to provide treatment; this fits the emergency scenario described.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal Part 2 enforcement penalties; complaint investigation risk

Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans

business_rules.rule_name: Training exception allows use of SUD counseling notes
business_rules.description: A Part 2 program may use or disclose SUD counseling notes without obtaining patient consent when the use/disclosure is made by the Part 2 program for its own training programs in which students, trainees, or practitioners learn under supervision to practice or improve skills in group, joint, family, or individual SUD counseling. Audio or video recordings are not included in the definition of SUD counseling notes for this purpose.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the regulated entity is a Part 2 program using/disclosing SUD counseling notes for internal supervised training activities.
business_rules.consequences: If the entity discloses SUD counseling notes for training outside the described supervised internal training exception, the entity must obtain the required consent or the disclosure may violate Part 2. If the entity stays within the internal supervised training exception, it may proceed without consent for that specific training use/disclosure.
business_rules.exceptions: This exception applies only to the program’s own training programs and supervised learning in SUD treatment or mental health contexts; audio/video recordings are excluded from the definition of SUD counseling notes and therefore are not covered by this exception as “notes.”

source_reference.section_id: SUD counseling notes; training exception
source_reference.source_text: The final rule expressly provides an exception from requirements for consent to disclose SUD counseling notes when such use or disclosure is made "by the part 2 program for its own training programs in which students, trainees, or practitioners in SUD treatment or mental health learn under supervision to practice or improve their skills in group, joint, family, or individual SUD counseling." ... the definition of "SUD counseling notes," like the definition of "psychotherapy notes" under HIPAA, does not include such recordings.

examples: A SUD clinic uses counseling notes in a weekly supervised case conference for interns; no patient consent is required under the training exception.
A program sends counseling notes to an external training vendor not operating under the program’s supervised training program; the exception does not apply and consent is required.
A program uses recorded therapy session videos for training; recordings are not “SUD counseling notes,” so the program must evaluate separate consent/authorization requirements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal Part 2 enforcement penalties for disclosures outside exception

business_rules.rule_name: EMTALA screening and stabilization cannot be delayed for payment
business_rules.description: A participating hospital must not delay providing the required medical screening examination (MSE) or further examination/treatment needed to stabilize an emergency medical condition in order to inquire about method of payment, insurance status, or to verify insurance information; financial inquiries must not delay EMTALA clinical obligations.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an individual presents to a participating hospital and requests an MSE (or a request is made on their behalf) for a medical condition, and the hospital is required to provide EMTALA screening and (if EMC exists) stabilizing treatment under 42 CFR 489.24.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: MSE and necessary stabilizing treatment proceed without payment/insurance-related delays. If violated: EMTALA noncompliance may be cited (Tag A-2408/C-2408), exposing the hospital and involved practitioners to CMS enforcement actions, including EMTALA deficiencies and potential civil monetary penalties and/or Medicare provider agreement termination.
business_rules.exceptions: Hospitals may perform reasonable registration processes (including asking whether the person is insured and what insurance) only if the process does not delay screening or treatment and does not unduly discourage the person from remaining for evaluation.

source_reference.section_id: §489.24(d)(4)(i) and Interpretive Guidelines
source_reference.source_text: A participating hospital may not delay providing an appropriate medical screening examination required under paragraph (a) of this section or further medical examination and treatment required under paragraph (d)(1) of this section in order to inquire about the individual’s method of payment or insurance status... a hospital may not delay screening or treatment to any individual while it verifies the information provided.

source_reference.section_id: I. General Information — Prohibition on delaying for payment inquiry
source_reference.source_text: Medical screening examination and/or stabilizing treatment is not to be delayed in order to inquire about payment status; Accept appropriate transfer of individuals with an emergency medical condition if the hospital has specialized capabilities or facilities and has the capacity to treat those individuals;

source_reference.section_id: Tag A-2400/C-2400, §489.20(l) Interpretive Guidelines
source_reference.source_text: Under the provisions of §489.24, hospitals with an emergency department that participate in Medicare are required under EMTALA to do the following: • Not delay examination and/or treatment in order to inquire about the individual’s insurance or payment status;

examples: Registration can collect insurance only after MSE and initiation of stabilizing treatment without delaying care.
Telling a patient to wait for insurance verification before triage/MSE is an EMTALA delay violation risk.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA deficiency citation and potential civil monetary penalty/Medicare termination

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

deduplication_info.rationale: All rules prohibit delaying EMTALA-required screening and/or stabilizing treatment to inquire about payment/insurance status. Same EMTALA requirement and enforcement consequence profile.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_19_001 selected: most complete (explicitly includes insurance verification and tags; same EMTALA duty).

business_rules.rule_name: No prior authorization requests before MSE and stabilizing care begins
business_rules.description: A participating hospital may not seek, or direct an individual to seek, authorization from the individual’s insurance company for screening or stabilization services until after the hospital has provided the required MSE and initiated any further medical examination and treatment needed to stabilize the EMC.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to EMTALA-required services when an individual presents to a hospital that offers emergency services and requires an MSE and, if an EMC is identified, stabilizing treatment.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: payer prior authorization is deferred until after MSE and initiation of stabilizing care. If violated: EMTALA noncompliance may be cited; delays by a receiving hospital in accepting an unstabilized transfer pending financial information may also constitute a violation.
business_rules.exceptions: An emergency physician or non-physician practitioner may contact the patient’s physician for clinical advice at any time as long as it does not inappropriately delay required screening or stabilizing services.

source_reference.section_id: §489.24(d)(4)(ii) and Interpretive Guidelines
source_reference.source_text: A participating hospital may not seek, or direct an individual to seek, authorization from the individual’s insurance company for screening or stabilization services... until after the hospital has provided the appropriate medical screening examination... and initiated any further medical examination and treatment that may be required to stabilize the emergency medical condition...

examples: ED staff call the insurer for prior authorization for CT imaging before initiating the MSE; this is a prohibited delay trigger under EMTALA.
A receiving hospital refuses to accept an unstabilized transfer until insurance coverage is verified; this may be an EMTALA violation.
After the MSE is completed and IV antibiotics started for sepsis, the hospital then contacts the insurer for authorization for admission services.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA deficiency citation and potential civil monetary penalty/Medicare termination

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

dependencies.rationale: 19_001 prohibits delays for payment/insurance inquiry; 19_002 specifically prohibits seeking payer authorization before MSE and initiation of stabilizing treatment. Implementing 19_001 typically requires also implementing the specific 19_002 control to prevent prior-authorization delays.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the broader no-delay principle is not implemented into specific PA workflows, MSE/stabilization may be delayed and EMTALA noncompliance may result.

business_rules.rule_name: EMTALA refusal must be documented with informed written refusal steps
business_rules.description: If an individual (or a person acting on the individual’s behalf) refuses further medical examination and/or stabilizing treatment after the hospital offers it and informs them of risks and benefits, the medical record must contain a description of what was refused. The hospital must take all reasonable steps to secure the individual’s written informed refusal; the written document should indicate the person was informed of risks and benefits.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the hospital has offered further examination and/or stabilizing treatment under EMTALA and the individual (or authorized representative) refuses to consent to examination or treatment.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: refusal is properly documented, supporting EMTALA compliance. If violated: surveyors may cite EMTALA noncompliance for inadequate documentation and failure to take reasonable steps to secure written informed refusal; coercive statements about costs are prohibited and may contribute to EMTALA violations.
business_rules.exceptions: A person may refuse on behalf of a patient only if the patient is incapable of making an informed choice. If written refusal cannot be secured, the record must document the steps taken to try to obtain it.

source_reference.section_id: §489.24(d)(3) and Interpretive Guidelines
source_reference.source_text: The medical record must contain a description of the examination, treatment, or both if applicable, that was refused... The hospital must take all reasonable steps to secure the individual's written informed refusal... The written document should indicate that the person has been informed of the risks and benefits of the examination or treatment...

examples: A patient refuses CT imaging and IV medications after risks/benefits are explained; the chart documents the refused items and includes a signed informed refusal form.
A confused patient lacks capacity; the legally authorized representative refuses admission after risks/benefits are explained, and the refusal is documented with the representative’s signature.
A patient refuses to sign; the clinician documents risks/benefits explained, the refusal reasons, and the steps taken to obtain written refusal.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA deficiency citation and potential civil monetary penalty/Medicare termination

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

dependencies.rationale: 19_003 applies when the individual refuses further exam/stabilizing treatment after it is offered and risks/benefits are explained. 19_002 ensures that the hospital reaches the point of offering/initiating stabilizing care without PA delays; that creates the conditions where refusal documentation is necessary if refusal occurs.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If PA delays prevent timely offering of care, refusal documentation may be incomplete or misleading, increasing EMTALA investigation risk and weakening the record of informed refusal.

business_rules.rule_name: Inpatient EMC must be treated under CoPs and no improper discharge/transfer
business_rules.description: If an inpatient develops an emergency medical condition (EMC), the hospital must meet the patient’s emergency needs in accordance with acceptable standards of practice and may not inappropriately discharge or transfer the patient; hospital Conditions of Participation (CoPs) protections apply to admitted patients.
business_rules.conditions: Applies after an individual has been admitted as an inpatient and later develops an EMC, triggering emergency needs that must be addressed under hospital CoPs.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: inpatient EMC is addressed per acceptable standards and applicable CoPs. If violated: noncompliance with relevant CoPs may subject the hospital to enforcement action.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text; clinical decision-making must remain consistent with acceptable standards of practice and CoPs requirements.

source_reference.section_id: Interpretive Guidelines: §489.24(d)(2)(iii)
source_reference.source_text: If an inpatient develops an EMC, the hospital is required to meet the patient’s emergency needs in accordance with acceptable standards of practice. The hospital CoPs protects patients who are admitted, and the hospital may not discharge or transfer any patient to another facility inappropriately... If a hospital is noncompliant with any of the above COPs, the hospital will be subject to enforcement action.

examples: A newly admitted inpatient develops respiratory failure; the hospital provides emergent stabilization rather than arranging a non-medically-indicated transfer.
An inpatient with an EMC is discharged early due to bed capacity without meeting emergency needs; this may violate CoPs and trigger enforcement.
A medically appropriate transfer is arranged with stabilization steps and proper discharge planning processes consistent with CoPs.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey deficiency and enforcement action for CoP noncompliance

business_rules.rule_name: SUD counseling notes need separate written consent
business_rules.description: When a regulated entity (including a Part 2 program) uses or discloses SUD counseling notes, it must obtain a separate patient consent specifically for SUD counseling notes. This consent must be distinct from other consents used for treatment, payment, and health care operations (TPO) uses/disclosures.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the record content qualifies as SUD counseling notes and the regulated entity intends to use or disclose those notes.
business_rules.consequences: If separate consent is obtained, the entity may use/disclose SUD counseling notes within the scope of that consent. If separate consent is not obtained, the use/disclosure of SUD counseling notes is impermissible under the finalized § 2.31(b) requirement.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the provided excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Section 2.31 Consent Requirements (§ 2.31(b))
source_reference.source_text: Section 2.31 Consent Requirements The final rule adds a new consent requirement at § 2.31(b), requiring separate consent for the use and disclosure of SUD counseling notes. The final rule limits use and disclosure of SUD counseling notes without patient consent in a manner that aligns with the HIPAA Privacy Rule authorization requirements for psychotherapy notes.

examples: A clinic discloses a patient’s Part 2 record to a care coordinator under a TPO consent but does not include SUD counseling notes because no separate SUD counseling notes consent is on file.
A patient signs a dedicated SUD counseling notes consent allowing disclosure of those notes to a named therapist; the clinic may disclose only those notes as authorized.
A provider attempts to share SUD counseling notes with a health plan using only a general TPO consent; the disclosure must be blocked because a separate SUD counseling notes consent is required.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal confidentiality rule violation (42 CFR Part 2) and compliance corrective action risk
data_points_required: record_type_indicator (SUD counseling notes vs other Part 2 record)

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

dependencies.rationale: 20_001 requires a separate consent for uses/disclosures of SUD counseling notes; 17_003 permits use/disclosure without consent for the program’s own supervised training programs (and clarifies that A/V recordings are excluded). This is an exception pathway that overrides the default consent requirement when conditions are met.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If entities do not recognize the override, they may unnecessarily require consents for internal training; if they overapply the exception, they may disclose notes without consent outside permitted training and violate Part 2.

business_rules.rule_name: Proceedings consent must be a standalone document
business_rules.description: Patient consent for the use or disclosure of records (or testimony relaying information contained in a record) in a civil, criminal, administrative, or legislative investigation or proceeding against the patient cannot be combined with a consent to use/disclose a record for any other purpose. The consent for proceedings must be separate/standalone.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when seeking patient permission to use/disclose Part 2 records (or testimony based on those records) in an investigation or proceeding, including proceedings brought against the patient.
business_rules.consequences: If the consent is bundled with other purposes (e.g., treatment consent, TPO consent), it is noncompliant and should not be relied upon for disclosures in investigations/proceedings against the patient. A separate document specific to that purpose must be obtained before using/disclosing records for such proceedings based on consent.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the provided excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Discussion of § 2.31(d) separate consent for proceedings
source_reference.source_text: we created the new requirement for separate consent as § 2.31(d) ... This paragraph provides that patient consent for use and disclosure of records (or testimony relaying information contained in a record) in a civil, criminal, administrative, or legislative investigation or proceeding cannot be combined with a consent to use and disclose a record for any other purpose.

examples: At intake, a program presents one combined form for treatment, billing, and potential court disclosures; the court-disclosure permission must be separated into its own standalone consent to be valid for proceedings.
A patient signs a separate consent that authorizes release of records only for a specified criminal proceeding; the program may disclose only within that standalone scope.
A payer requests a consent clause permitting use in administrative proceedings; the program cannot embed that clause inside the TPO consent form and must use a separate consent document.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal confidentiality rule violation (42 CFR Part 2) and compliance corrective action risk

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_17_001 establishes separation for investigation/proceeding consents vs other purposes (e.g., TPO); BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_20_002 further specifies that proceeding consent cannot be combined with any other purpose consent. Together they define the guardrail against “bundled” consents for legal uses.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If entities implement only general consent workflows, they may inadvertently use invalid consents for proceeding disclosures, triggering Part 2 violations.

business_rules.rule_name: Accounting of consent disclosures covers 3 years
business_rules.description: A regulated entity must be able to provide an accounting of all disclosures made with consent for up to three years prior to the date the accounting is requested. However, a separate provision applies to disclosures for TPO purposes made through an EHR, and the compliance date for § 2.25 is tolled until the HIPAA Accounting of Disclosures provision at 45 CFR 164.528 is revised to address accounting for TPO disclosures made through an EHR.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a patient requests an accounting of disclosures that were made with consent. Includes a special compliance timing condition for accounting of TPO disclosures made through an EHR.
business_rules.consequences: If requested, the entity must be able to generate an accounting reaching back up to 3 years for disclosures made with consent. For TPO disclosures through an EHR, the entity follows the separate provision, and the § 2.25 compliance date is delayed (tolled) until 45 CFR 164.528 is revised as described.
business_rules.exceptions: TPO disclosures made through an EHR are handled under a separate provision, and the § 2.25 compliance date for that aspect is tolled until 45 CFR 164.528 is revised.

source_reference.section_id: Section 2.25 (accounting) and tolling statement
source_reference.source_text: accounting of all disclosures made with consent for up to three years prior to the date the accounting is requested. A separate provision applies to disclosures for TPO purposes made through an EHR. The compliance date for § 2.25 is tolled until the HIPAA Accounting of Disclosures provision at 45 CFR 164.528 is revised to address accounting for TPO disclosures made through an EHR.

examples: A patient requests an accounting on June 1, 2026; the program must be able to account for consent-based disclosures back to June 1, 2023.

A hospital made many TPO disclosures through its certified EHR; it applies the separate EHR-related accounting provision and recognizes the § 2.25 compliance date is tolled until 45 CFR 164.528 is revised as described.
A clinic can only produce 12 months of consent-based disclosure logs; that is insufficient because the accounting must cover up to 3 years prior to the request date.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal confidentiality rule violation (42 CFR Part 2) and compliance corrective action risk

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 research must use HIPAA de-identification
business_rules.description: When Part 2 records are disclosed/used for scientific research, the applicable de-identification standard is aligned with the HIPAA Privacy Rule de-identification standard at 45 CFR 164.514 (replacing prior requirements to render Part 2 data in research reports non-identifiable).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to scientific research uses/disclosures of Part 2 data and to de-identification determinations made for research outputs and datasets under § 2.52 (and throughout Part 2 as described).
business_rules.consequences: Research datasets or outputs derived from Part 2 records must be de-identified according to 45 CFR 164.514. If not de-identified to that standard, the disclosure/creation of the dataset as de-identified is noncompliant.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the provided excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Section 2.52 Scientific Research (alignment to 45 CFR 164.514)
source_reference.source_text: Section 2.52—Scientific Research ... The final rule further aligns with the HIPAA Privacy Rule by replacing the requirements to render part 2 data in research reports non- identifiable with the HIPAA Privacy Rule’s de-identification standard in 45 CFR 164.514.

examples: A university researcher requests Part 2 data; the program provides a dataset only after de-identifying it consistent with 45 CFR 164.514.
A program plans to publish outcomes based on Part 2 records; the dataset must meet HIPAA de-identification at 45 CFR 164.514 before being treated as de-identified.
A contractor removes only names but leaves sufficient identifiers such that 45 CFR 164.514 is not met; the dataset cannot be treated as properly de-identified under this alignment.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal confidentiality rule violation (42 CFR Part 2) and compliance corrective action risk

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Specialty hospital must accept EMTALA transfer if able
business_rules.description: A Medicare-participating hospital with specialized capabilities or facilities may not refuse to accept an appropriate transfer of an individual with an unstabilized emergency medical condition from a referring hospital within the boundaries of the United States if the receiving hospital has capacity to treat the individual. This obligation applies regardless of whether the receiving hospital has a dedicated emergency department. EMTALA recipient hospital obligations do not extend to an individual who has been admitted as an inpatient at the referring hospital (observation status does not count as inpatient).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when: (a) the receiving facility is a participating hospital with specialized capabilities/facilities; (b) a referring hospital seeks an appropriate transfer for an unstabilized emergency medical condition requiring such capabilities; (c) the receiving hospital has capacity; and (d) the referring hospital is within U.S. boundaries (as defined in the interpretive guidance).
business_rules.consequences: If conditions are met, the specialized-capability hospital must accept the appropriate transfer and may not refuse based on non-permitted factors. Refusal when obligated risks an EMTALA deficiency finding under the cited tag and associated enforcement.
business_rules.exceptions: Does not apply to an individual admitted to the referring hospital as an inpatient (defined as expectation to remain at least overnight). No EMTALA obligation to accept transfers from hospitals outside the boundaries of the United States. Observation status is not inpatient.

source_reference.section_id: §489.24(f) Recipient Hospital Responsibilities (Tag A-2411/C-2411)
source_reference.source_text: A participating hospital that has specialized capabilities or facilities ... may not refuse to accept ... an appropriate transfer ... if the receiving hospital has the capacity to treat the individual. (1) ... apply ... regardless of whether the hospital has a dedicated emergency department. (2) ... do not apply to an individual who has been admitted to a referring hospital ... Individuals who are placed in observation status are not inpatients, even if they occupy a bed overnight.

examples: A burn center with open staffed beds is asked to accept an unstabilized burn patient from a U.S. hospital lacking burn capability; it must accept the appropriate transfer.

A psychiatric hospital participating in Medicare has capacity and is requested to accept an appropriate transfer for an unstabilized psychiatric emergency; it must accept even without a dedicated emergency department.

A referring hospital requests transfer of a patient already admitted as an inpatient (expected to remain at least overnight); the recipient hospital’s EMTALA transfer-acceptance obligation does not apply.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA deficiency citation (Tag A-2411/C-2411)

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

dependencies.rationale: 010_004 defines conditions under which an unstabilized transfer can proceed (patient request after risk disclosure or physician/QMP certification). 20_005 defines the recipient-side obligation not to refuse an appropriate transfer when conditions are met. The workflow is sequential across institutions: appropriate transfer request/certification precedes acceptance.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the sending hospital does not meet appropriate transfer conditions, the receiving hospital may dispute the transfer as not “appropriate,” and the overall transfer may fail or create enforcement exposure on either side depending on facts.

dependencies.rationale: 20_005 establishes the duty to accept appropriate transfers when specialized capability and capacity exist. 19_002 prohibits PA/financial authorization delays and notes that receiving-hospital delay pending financial info may itself be a violation—tightening the acceptance workflow controls.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If financial clearance is inserted before acceptance, the receiving hospital may violate EMTALA even when it has capacity and specialized capability.

business_rules.rule_name: Hospital must not retaliate for EMTALA safety reports
business_rules.description: A participating hospital may not penalize or take adverse action against a physician or qualified medical person for refusing to authorize transfer of an individual with an emergency medical condition that has not been stabilized, and may not penalize any hospital employee because the employee reports a violation of EMTALA requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to participating hospitals and to adverse actions tied to: (a) refusal to authorize transfer of an unstabilized emergency medical condition; or (b) employee reporting of an alleged EMTALA violation.
business_rules.consequences: Retaliation is prohibited; adverse employment or professional actions taken for these protected activities risk EMTALA noncompliance findings and associated enforcement under the cited tag.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the provided excerpt.

source_reference.source_text: A participating hospital may not penalize or take adverse action against a physician or a qualified medical person ... because the physician or qualified medical person refuses to authorize the transfer of an individual with an emergency medical condition that has not been stabilized, or against any hospital employee because the employee reports a violation of a requirement of this section.

examples: An ED physician refuses an unsafe transfer for an unstabilized patient; the hospital cannot suspend privileges or impose discipline because of that refusal.
A nurse reports a suspected EMTALA dumping incident to compliance; the hospital cannot terminate or demote the nurse for making the report.
A qualified medical person declines to sign a transfer certification due to lack of stabilization; the hospital cannot reduce shifts as retaliation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA deficiency citation (Tag A-2410/C-2410)

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

dependencies.rationale: 20_006 prohibits penalizing physicians/QMPs for refusing to authorize transfer of an unstabilized EMC and employees for reporting EMTALA violations. Those protected activities occur within the transfer-authorization/certification context described in 010_004.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the organization does not treat transfer-authorization decisions as protected activity, it may retaliate and incur EMTALA noncompliance findings even if transfer paperwork is otherwise compliant.

business_rules.rule_name: Non-covered Part 2 program may allow inspect/copy
business_rules.description: For Part 2 programs that are not HIPAA covered entities, 42 CFR 2.23 does not prohibit the Part 2 program from providing a patient access to their own records, including allowing the opportunity to inspect and copy any records the Part 2 program maintains about the patient.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an entity is a Part 2 program and is not a HIPAA covered entity, and the patient requests access to their own Part 2 records maintained by that program.
business_rules.consequences: If the program chooses to provide access: it may allow inspection and copying of records maintained about the patient. If access is denied based on a mistaken belief that Part 2 prohibits it: increased risk of patient complaints and potential noncompliance with applicable program policies or other legal requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt (this is a permissive statement clarifying Part 2 does not prohibit patient access for non-covered Part 2 programs).

source_reference.section_id: Clarification regarding § 2.23 and patient access for Part 2 programs that are not covered entities
source_reference.source_text: For part 2 programs that are not covered entities, § 2.23 does not prohibit a part 2 program from giving a patient access to their own records, including the opportunity to inspect and copy any records that the part 2 program maintains about the patient.

examples: A standalone SUD counseling center that is not a HIPAA covered entity allows a patient to inspect and copy their visit summaries and lab results maintained by the program.
A non-covered Part 2 program creates a process for patients to request copies of their records without treating the request as a prohibited disclosure under Part 2.
A Part 2 program remains free to provide access but still applies internal identity verification steps before allowing inspection or copying.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Complaint to HHS and required corrective action depending on investigation outcome

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Patient-accessed Part 2 info barred from criminal use
business_rules.description: Information obtained through a patient’s access to their Part 2 record remains subject to restrictions prohibiting use or disclosure to initiate or substantiate criminal charges against the patient or to conduct a criminal investigation of the patient; entities holding such information must not use/disclose it for those criminal purposes absent a permitted Part 2 basis.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when information is obtained by a patient accessing their Part 2 record (e.g., the patient inspects/copies their record) and a party seeks to use or disclose that information for purposes of criminally charging or criminally investigating the patient.
business_rules.consequences: If conditions are met: use/disclosure for the specified criminal purposes is prohibited. If violated: may trigger Part 2 enforcement actions, corrective action requirements, and potential civil/criminal penalties depending on facts and circumstances.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt (the text references a clarified prohibition; other Part 2 exceptions such as court orders may exist in Part 2 generally but are not specified in this excerpt).

source_reference.section_id: Response re: concerns about access/disclosures leading to criminal charges; § 2.23 clarification
source_reference.source_text: One commenter expressed concerns about any access or disclosures that could subject part 2 patients to criminal charges. ... The revisions to § 2.23 clarify the existing prohibition on use and disclosure of information obtained by patient access to their record for purposes of a criminal charge or criminal investigation of the patient.

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR 2.23(b) Patient access and restrictions on use and disclosure
source_reference.source_text: Restriction on use and disclosure of information. Information obtained by patient access to their record is subject to the restriction on use and disclosure of records to initiate or substantiate any criminal charges against the patient or to conduct any criminal investigation of the patient as provided for under § 2.12(d)(1).

examples: A prosecutor cannot rely on patient-accessed Part 2 record content to initiate criminal charges against the patient absent Part 2 authorization.
A law enforcement request for patient-accessed content for investigation triggers the same Part 2 restriction.
business_rules.enforcement_action: HHS enforcement action under Part 2/HITECH framework; potential civil monetary penalty depending on facts

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules express the same restriction: information obtained by a patient accessing their Part 2 record cannot be used or disclosed to initiate/substantiate criminal charges or to conduct a criminal investigation of the patient, consistent with Part 2’s criminal-use restrictions.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_21_004 selected: more explicit that the prohibition applies to information obtained via patient access to the Part 2 record.

business_rules.rule_name: Intermediary consent must name intermediary or participants
business_rules.description: When making a disclosure under Part 2 to an entity qualifying as an “intermediary” (a person who has received records under a general designation in a written patient consent to be disclosed to one or more member participants with a treating provider relationship with the patient), the consent must include the name of the intermediary and either: (A) the name(s) of the intermediary’s member participant(s), or (B) a general designation of participant(s) or class of participants that is limited to participant(s) who have a treating provider relationship with the patient.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Part 2 records are disclosed to an intermediary pursuant to a written patient consent using a general designation, and the intermediary will disclose to one or more member participants.
business_rules.consequences: If consent contains required elements: disclosure to intermediary may proceed consistent with Part 2 consent requirements. If consent lacks required intermediary naming and participant specification/limitation: the disclosure is not authorized by a valid Part 2 consent and may constitute an impermissible disclosure.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt (requirements described as special instructions within Part 2 consent requirements).

source_reference.section_id: Section 2.24—Requirements for Intermediaries (Proposed Rule explanation of consent content requirements)
source_reference.source_text: Intermediary means a person who has received records under a general designation in a written patient consent to be disclosed to one or more of its member participant(s) who has a treating provider relationship with the patient. ... the consent must include the name of the intermediary and one of the following: (A) the name(s) of member participant(s) of the intermediary; or (B) a general designation of a participant(s) or class of participants, which must be limited to a participant(s) who has a treating provider relationship with the patient whose information is being disclosed.

examples: A patient signs a Part 2 consent naming a regional HIE as the intermediary and listing three named clinic participants; the HIE may disclose to those listed participants.

A consent names the intermediary and uses a general designation “treating providers involved in my care” limited to providers with a treating relationship; the intermediary may disclose only to treating providers.
A consent fails to name the intermediary but lists a class of recipients; disclosure to an intermediary under general designation would not meet the described consent content requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: HHS Part 2 enforcement action; corrective action and potential penalty based on facts

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 research disclosure needs HIPAA authorization or waiver
business_rules.description: A regulated entity (as a Part 2 program or other lawful holder) may use or disclose patient identifying information for scientific research only if an authorized program leader (director/managing director/CEO or designee) determines that the recipient is a HIPAA covered entity or business associate that has obtained and documented either (a) the patient’s HIPAA authorization, or (b) a waiver or alteration of authorization, consistent with 45 CFR 164.508 or 45 CFR 164.512(i), as applicable.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when patient identifying information is used or disclosed for the purpose of the recipient conducting scientific research under 42 CFR 2.52(a)(1)(i).
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: the research disclosure is permitted under Part 2 subject to remaining Part 2 requirements. If violated: the disclosure is not permitted under Part 2 and may constitute an improper disclosure, exposing the entity and responsible parties to Part 2 enforcement and HIPAA-aligned penalty frameworks where applicable.
business_rules.exceptions: Other Part 2 provisions may apply, but this rule specifically governs the research pathway described in 42 CFR 2.52(a)(1)(i).

source_reference.source_text: Patient identifying information may be used or disclosed for the purposes of the recipient conducting scientific research if: (1) The person designated as director or managing director, or person otherwise vested with authority to act as chief executive officer or their designee, ... makes a determination that the recipient ... is: (i) A HIPAA covered entity or business associate that has obtained and documented authorization from the patient, or a waiver or alteration of authorization, consistent with 45 CFR 164.508 or 164.512(i), as applicable;

examples: A university medical center requests Part 2 patient identifying information for outcomes research; the Part 2 program CEO confirms the recipient is a HIPAA covered entity and has documented patient authorizations under 45 CFR 164.508.
A covered entity’s IRB grants a waiver of HIPAA authorization under 45 CFR 164.512(i); the Part 2 program director documents that waiver before disclosing identifiers.
A research recipient cannot show HIPAA authorization or a waiver/alteration; the Part 2 program must not disclose patient identifying information under this research provision.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil and/or criminal enforcement under Part 2 as aligned with HIPAA enforcement processes (as applicable)

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_22_002 is a specific permission standard for research disclosures that must be evaluated within Part 2’s restrictions framework (BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_001_002).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the Part 2 gate is skipped, the program may disclose for research without the required authorized-leader determination and HIPAA authorization/waiver documentation, resulting in an impermissible disclosure.

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_22_002 relies on proper authorization/permission prerequisites; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_001_004 controls who may give valid written consent under Part 2 when the patient is a minor with legal capacity.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If parent/guardian consent is used instead of the minor’s consent in a minor-capacity scenario, the disclosure is not authorized under Part 2 and may be unlawful.

business_rules.rule_name: On-prem audit access needs written redisclosure agreement
business_rules.description: If patient records are not downloaded, copied, removed from premises, or forwarded electronically to another system/device, a regulated entity may disclose patient identifying information for an on-premises records review only to a person who agrees in writing to comply with the limitations on use and redisclosure in 42 CFR 2.53(f). This includes certain payers/health plans/QIOs and their contractors, subcontractors, or legal representatives as specified.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to management audits, financial audits, and program evaluation disclosures under 42 CFR 2.53(a) where records remain on premises and are not copied/removed/downloaded/forwarded.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: the entity may allow on-premises review with written assurances limiting use/redisclosure. If violated: disclosure without the written agreement (or outside permitted recipient categories) is not authorized under this provision and may be treated as an improper disclosure under Part 2, creating enforcement and liability risk.
business_rules.exceptions: This rule only covers the 'records not copied or removed' scenario; separate conditions apply when records are copied/removed/downloaded/forwarded under 42 CFR 2.53(b).

source_reference.source_text: If patient records are not downloaded, copied or removed from the premises ... patient identifying information ... may be disclosed ... on the premises ... to any person who agrees in writing to comply with the limitations on use and redisclosure in paragraph (f) ... and who: ... Any person which provides financial assistance ... which is a third-party payer or health plan ... or which is a quality improvement organization (QIO) ... or the contractors, subcontractors, or legal representatives of such person or quality improvement organization;

examples: A health plan sends an auditor onsite to review Part 2 records for a payment integrity audit; before review begins, the auditor signs a written agreement to comply with § 2.53(f) use/redisclosure limits.
A QIO conducts an onsite quality review; the QIO’s contractor signs the required written agreement and reviews records without downloading them.
A consulting firm reviews records onsite but no written agreement is executed; allowing access fails the § 2.53(a) condition.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil and/or criminal enforcement under Part 2 as aligned with HIPAA enforcement processes (as applicable)

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_22_007 describes conditions for on-premises audit/evaluation review with written assurances. It is only evaluated once Part 2 applicability and restrictions are recognized (BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_001_002).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If Part 2 gating is missed, entities may allow on-premises review without required written assurances or to non-permitted recipients, creating an improper disclosure.

business_rules.rule_name: Copied or downloaded audit records require redisclosure limits
business_rules.description: When records containing patient identifying information are copied, removed, downloaded, or forwarded to another electronic system/device for audit/evaluation purposes, the regulated entity may only allow such copying/removal/downloading/forwarding by a person who complies with the limitations on use and disclosure in 42 CFR 2.53(f), and who fits within the recipient categories identified in 42 CFR 2.53(b) (including certain payers/health plans/QIOs and their contractors/subcontractors/legal representatives as specified).
business_rules.conditions: Applies under 42 CFR 2.53(b) when audit/evaluation requires copying/removing/downloading/forwarding records from the Part 2 program or lawful holder’s premises or electronic records environment.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: off-premises or electronic transfer for audit/evaluation is permitted subject to § 2.53(f) controls. If violated: the copying/removal/download/forwarding is not permitted under this section and may constitute an improper disclosure/redisclosure under Part 2, increasing enforcement risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited revision text; separate provisions may apply for other disclosure pathways not addressed in § 2.53(b).

source_reference.source_text: Copying, removing, downloading, or forwarding patient records. Records containing patient identifying information ... may be copied or removed ... or downloaded or forwarded ... by any person who: ... (iii) Comply with the limitations on use and disclosure in paragraph (f) of this section; and ... (ii) Any person which provides financial assistance ... which is a third-party payer or health plan ... or which is a quality improvement organization performing a QIO review, or the contractors, subcontractors, or legal representatives of such person ...

examples: A Medicaid managed care plan requests an encrypted export of Part 2 records for a payment audit; the plan signs an agreement and operationally enforces § 2.53(f) use/redisclosure limits.
A QIO downloads a limited dataset onto an approved device after committing in writing to § 2.53(f) limitations; the download is authorized under § 2.53(b) if other conditions are met.
A payer’s contractor receives a full copy of records without any executed limitation-on-redisclosure agreement; the transfer fails § 2.53(b)(1)(iii).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil and/or criminal enforcement under Part 2 as aligned with HIPAA enforcement processes (as applicable)

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_22_008 is a specific allowed method for audit/evaluation when copying/removal/download/forwarding occurs; it is evaluated within the Part 2 restriction framework of BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_001_002.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If Part 2 restrictions are not applied first, records may be exported to non-permitted recipients or without required use/redisclosure limitations, creating improper disclosure/redisclosure risk.

business_rules.rule_name: CMS program audits need written Part 2 disclosure limits
business_rules.description: Patient identifying information may be disclosed for a Medicare, Medicaid, or CHIP audit or evaluation (including audits/evaluations necessary for CMS-regulated ACOs or similar CMS-regulated organizations, including CMS-regulated Qualified Entities) only if the recipient agrees in writing to comply with applicable requirements, including complying with the limitations on use and disclosure in 42 CFR 2.53(f).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to disclosures under 42 CFR 2.53(e)(1) for Medicare/Medicaid/CHIP audits or evaluations, including those needed for CMS-regulated organizations (e.g., ACOs, QEs).
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: the regulated entity may disclose Part 2 patient identifying information for the audit/evaluation with a written compliance commitment. If violated: disclosure without the required written agreement is not authorized by § 2.53(e) and may be treated as an improper disclosure under Part 2, creating enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited excerpt; other parts of § 2.53(e) may include additional conditions not shown here.

source_reference.source_text: Patient identifying information ... may be disclosed under paragraph (e) ... for the purpose of conducting a Medicare, Medicaid, or CHIP audit or evaluation, including ... a CMS- regulated accountable care organization ... or similar CMS- regulated organization (including a CMS-regulated Qualified Entity (QE)), if the person agrees in writing to comply with the following: * * * (iii) Comply with the limitations on use and disclosure in paragraph (f) of this section.

examples: A CMS-regulated ACO performs a compliance evaluation that needs Part 2 identifiers; the ACO signs a written agreement to comply with § 2.53(f) before receiving data.
A state Medicaid agency’s audit vendor receives Part 2 data after executing a written commitment to the § 2.53(f) use/redisclosure limits.
A CHIP audit requests Part 2 patient identifiers but refuses to sign written limitations; the regulated entity cannot disclose under § 2.53(e) based on this condition.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil and/or criminal enforcement under Part 2 as aligned with HIPAA enforcement processes (as applicable)

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_22_009 imposes a written-agreement condition for CMS program audits/evaluations. This requirement is only invoked after recognizing Part 2 restrictions apply to the requested information (BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_001_002).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If Part 2 applicability is missed, disclosures may be made for CMS-related audits without the required written agreement, creating an unauthorized disclosure under Part 2.

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 programs must follow breach notification; lawful holders exempt
business_rules.description: Breach notification requirements are finalized for Part 2 programs, and lawful holders (as referenced in the excerpt) are not subject to Part 2 breach notification requirements. This aligns Part 2 breach notification for Part 2 programs with the breach notification framework discussed in the rulemaking (HITECH/HIPAA Breach Notification Rule referenced).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when there is a breach of Part 2 records by a Part 2 program. Does not apply to lawful holders (e.g., informal caregivers) as they are not subject to breach notification requirements under the excerpted statement.
business_rules.consequences: If a Part 2 program experiences a breach, it must fulfill breach notification obligations applicable to Part 2 programs. If an entity is only a lawful holder (per the excerpt), it is exempt from Part 2 breach notification requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: Lawful holders are not subject to breach notification requirements (per excerpt).

source_reference.section_id: Final Rule for Lawful Holders and Security of Records (breach notification applicability)
source_reference.source_text: We are finalizing breach notification requirements for part 2 programs; lawful holders are not subject to breach notification requirements.

examples: A Part 2 program discovers unauthorized access to its SUD patient database; it must execute the Part 2 breach notification process applicable to Part 2 programs.

A patient’s family member (lawful holder) mistakenly texts a photo of a Part 2 document to the wrong person; under the excerpt, the family member is not subject to Part 2 breach notification requirements.
A Part 2 program’s employee emails Part 2 records to an unauthorized recipient; the program must treat this as a breach event requiring the program’s breach notification actions.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal enforcement action under 42 CFR Part 2 (including statutory civil penalties referenced in the rulemaking discussion)

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAAPRIVACY_23_003 applies to breaches of Part 2 records by Part 2 programs (and excludes certain lawful holders). Determining Part 2 protection/scope (BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_001_002) is necessary to route to the correct breach framework.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If Part 2 scope is misclassified, the program may either fail to notify when required (treating it as non-Part-2) or over-apply Part 2 breach notification to entities exempted as lawful holders.

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REPORTING_010_007 states that HIPAA breach notification provisions apply to Part 2 program breaches of unsecured records in the same manner as covered entities for PHI. BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAAPRIVACY_23_003 finalizes breach notification requirements for Part 2 programs and clarifies lawful holders are not subject. Together, they define when and how to execute breach notification.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If an entity fails to apply HIPAA breach notification mechanics when a Part 2 program breach occurs, notifications may be incomplete/late and noncompliant with incorporated requirements.

dependencies.rationale: BR_23_003 triggers breach notification duties for Part 2 programs upon a breach. BR_26_004 defines breach for Part 2 records using 45 CFR 164.402 and the low-probability-of-compromise risk assessment standard, which determines whether notification is required.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_26_004 is not applied, a Part 2 program may either fail to notify when required (enforcement risk) or over-notify when not required (unnecessary burden, patient confusion, reputational harm).

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 programs must contract for timely breach notification by vendors
business_rules.description: A Part 2 program is not responsible for breaches caused by its QSOs or business associates; however, the program is responsible for having contractual requirements in place to ensure it is timely notified of a breach by such entities so it can meet its obligations to notify affected individuals.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Part 2 records are held or handled on behalf of a Part 2 program by a Qualified Service Organization (QSO) or a business associate, and a breach occurs at that QSO/business associate.
business_rules.consequences: If appropriate contractual requirements exist and timely notice is received, the Part 2 program can meet its obligation to notify affected individuals. If contractual requirements for timely notice are not in place (or are ineffective), the program risks delayed awareness and noncompliance with its notification obligations to affected individuals.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Discussion of breaches held on behalf of programs by QSOs or business associates
source_reference.source_text: Under HIPAA, a business associate is required to notify a covered entity of breaches and we believe part 2 programs that are not covered entities could obligate their QSOs to notify the programs of breaches through contractual provisions. A part 2 program would not be responsible for breaches by QSOs or business associates. However, the part 2 program is responsible under this rule for having in place contractual requirements to ensure that it is timely notified of a breach by such entities so that it can meet its obligations to notify affected individuals.

examples: A cloud-hosted EHR vendor experiences a breach affecting Part 2 records; the Part 2 program’s contract requires prompt breach notification to the program so it can notify affected individuals.
A billing QSO discovers it mistakenly posted Part 2 records to an unsecured portal; the program must have contractual terms requiring the QSO to notify the program timely.
A Part 2 program has no written breach-notification clause in its QSO agreement; the QSO delays informing the program, increasing the risk the program misses required individual notifications.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal enforcement action under 42 CFR Part 2 for failure to implement required safeguards/contractual controls enabling timely notifications

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAAPRIVACY_23_004 is designed to ensure timely awareness of breaches by QSOs/BAs so the Part 2 program can fulfill the breach notification duties that apply to Part 2 programs under BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAAPRIVACY_23_003.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Without the underlying notification obligation framework, the contractual timing requirement loses purpose; without the contract, the program may miss deadlines for notifying affected individuals.

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_12_003 requires contracts/legal instruments, safeguards, and reporting of unauthorized uses/disclosures when lawful holders redisclose for payment/operations. BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAAPRIVACY_23_004 requires contractual provisions for timely breach notification by QSOs/BAs. Harmonizing contract clauses and reporting pathways supports both obligations.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If contracting and reporting controls are fragmented, downstream parties may not report incidents/breaches promptly or consistently, increasing notification noncompliance risk.

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAAPRIVACY_23_004 requires contractual provisions ensuring timely breach notice from QSOs/BAs; BR_BUSINESS_ASSOCIATE_HIPAA_PRIVACY_19_010 clarifies that in QSO contexts a QSOA is distinct and required, shaping how the program must structure those notice provisions.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the program relies solely on a HIPAA BAA and omits the QSOA when required, it may lack enforceable Part 2-aligned notice terms, leading to delayed breach awareness and missed notification deadlines.

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 accounting must cover consent disclosures for prior 3 years
business_rules.description: The final rule requires that the accounting of disclosures timeframe include disclosures made with consent in the 3 years prior to the date of the request (correcting the timeframe in the regulatory text described).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an individual requests an accounting of disclosures under the finalized Part 2 accounting requirement described in the excerpt, specifically regarding disclosures made with consent.
business_rules.consequences: If the accounting includes consent-based disclosures from the 3-year lookback window, the response meets the stated timeframe requirement. If the accounting omits consent-based disclosures within the prior 3 years, the response is incomplete and may be noncompliant with the Part 2 accounting requirement as described.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Final Rule—§ 2.25 accounting of disclosures correction
source_reference.source_text: Final Rule The final rule adopts all proposed modifications to § 2.25, with a correction to the timeframe in paragraph (a) to require an accounting of disclosures made with consent in the 3 years prior to the date of the request.

examples: A patient requests an accounting on 2026-07-01; the program includes consent-based disclosures back to 2023-07-01 to satisfy the 3-year lookback requirement.
A program provides an accounting covering only the past 12 months; it fails to include consent-based disclosures from the prior 3 years and is noncompliant with the described requirement.
A patient requests an accounting on 2025-01-15; the program includes consent disclosures since 2022-01-15 and excludes older consent disclosures outside the 3-year window.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal enforcement action under 42 CFR Part 2 for failure to provide a compliant accounting of disclosures (when effective/compliance dates apply)

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_23_007 tolls the effective/compliance dates for Part 2 accounting-of-disclosures requirements until the HIPAA/HITECH accounting rule is finalized. BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_23_006 specifies the 3-year lookback timeframe content for those accountings; it only operationally applies when the obligation is in force.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If an entity treats 23_006 as immediately required despite tolling, it may implement misaligned processes; if it ignores 23_006 after tolling ends, it will produce noncompliant accountings.

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_26_002 specifies that §2.25 effective/compliance dates are tolled until HIPAA 45 CFR 164.528 is revised. BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_23_006 defines what the accounting must include (3-year consent-based lookback) once the accounting requirement is active.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Misinterpreting tolling can cause either premature implementation burden or late compliance once the tolling ends.

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 accounting compliance date is tolled until HIPAA rule is final
business_rules.description: The effective and compliance dates for Part 2 programs’ accounting of disclosures requirements are tolled until the effective and compliance dates of a final HIPAA/HITECH accounting of disclosures rule (HITECH Act section 13405(c)). Part 2 programs are also not required to add a statement about the right to an accounting of disclosures in the Part 2 Patient Notice until that future compliance date.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Part 2 programs impacted by the new/modified Part 2 accounting of disclosures requirement described in the excerpt, prior to the finalization of the HIPAA/HITECH accounting of disclosures rulemaking referenced.
business_rules.consequences: Before the tolled compliance date, Part 2 programs do not incur the new accounting compliance obligations described (and do not need to update the Patient Notice to include the accounting-of-disclosures statement). Once the HIPAA/HITECH accounting rule becomes final and its compliance date is set, Part 2 accounting obligations become effective/compliant in alignment.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Tolling of Compliance Date
source_reference.source_text: We are tolling the effective and compliance dates for part 2 programs until the effective and compliance dates of a final rule on the HIPAA/HITECH accounting of disclosures standard (section 13405(c) of the HITECH Act) to ensure part 2 programs do not incur new compliance obligations before covered entities and business associates under the HIPAA Privacy Rule are obligated to comply. We also note the part 2 programs are not required to include the statement of a patient’s right to an accounting of disclosures in the Patient Notice under § 2.22 until the future compliance date of the accounting of disclosures.

examples: A Part 2 program preparing compliance work determines that the accounting-of-disclosures requirement is tolled; it defers implementing the new accounting workflow until the aligned HIPAA compliance date is issued.
A Part 2 program updates its Patient Notice for other changes but does not add the accounting-of-disclosures statement because the future compliance date has not yet occurred.

A Part 2 program that is also a covered entity plans a single implementation project timed to the HIPAA/HITECH accounting compliance date to satisfy both Part 2 and HIPAA requirements when they become effective.
business_rules.enforcement_action: No enforcement exposure for non-implementation of the tolled requirement prior to the aligned compliance date

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

business_rules.rule_name: Single Part 2 TPO consent may use class recipient and no expiration
business_rules.description: For a single consent covering all future uses and disclosures for treatment, payment, and health care operations (TPO), Part 2 consent may describe recipients as a class of persons (including a Part 2 program, covered entity, or business associate) using language such as “my treating providers, health plans, third-party payers, and those helping operate this business.” The purpose statement “for treatment, payment, and health care operations” is sufficient, and the required expiration date/event may be “none.”
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a patient provides consent for all future uses and disclosures for TPO under Part 2 using the aligned consent elements described in the excerpt.
business_rules.consequences: If the consent uses an allowed class-recipient description, contains a sufficient TPO purpose statement, and uses an allowed expiration designation (“none”), the consent meets these described elements for an all-future TPO consent. If the consent lacks these elements or uses an impermissible expiration structure for this consent type, the consent may be invalid for intended TPO uses/disclosures under Part 2.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Single consent for all future uses and disclosures for TPO—required elements
source_reference.source_text: The recipient may be a class of persons including a part 2 program, covered entity, or business associate and the consent may describe the recipient as “my treating providers, health plans, third-party payers, and those helping operate this business” or use similar language... The statement, “for treatment, payment, and health care operations” is a sufficient description of the purpose... The required expiration date or event may be “none” for a consent for all future uses and disclosures for TPO.

examples: A patient signs a Part 2 consent listing recipients as “my treating providers, health plans, third-party payers, and those helping operate this business,” purpose “for treatment, payment, and health care operations,” expiration “none”; the consent meets these described elements.
A consent for all future TPO lists only a facility nickname and no class description; the organization updates the consent to clearly identify an allowed class of recipients.
A consent for all future TPO is drafted with a fixed expiration date of 30 days; the organization revises it to use “none” when the intent is all future TPO uses/disclosures.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal enforcement action under 42 CFR Part 2 for uses/disclosures made without a valid consent meeting required elements

examples: Regional hospital system acting as a HIPAA covered entity operating an EHR
Claims processing vendor acting as a HIPAA business associate for multiple health plans
Community health clinic subject to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENTREQUIREMENT_23_008 defines acceptable elements for an all-future TPO consent. BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_010_010 defines who can provide required Part 2 consent for adults lacking capacity (personal representative if adjudicated; limited program-director consent pathway for payment-only in non-adjudicated scenarios).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the wrong party signs (or program-director consent is used beyond payment-only), the TPO consent pathway fails and disclosures may be unauthorized.

dependencies.rationale: 23_008 defines elements for an all-future TPO consent. 032_003 clarifies that a Part 2 program must not require written consent/authorization as a precondition for patient access to their own records. Therefore, in the patient-access context, consent content requirements are not gating criteria; patient access proceeds without requiring a 23_008-style consent.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 032_003 is not followed, the program may unlawfully delay/deny patient access by demanding a consent containing 23_008 elements, creating Part 2 access noncompliance and complaint risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 TPO consent must include redisclosure warnings and refusal consequences
