business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA enforcement action (hospital/physician sanctions and potential termination of Medicare provider agreement)

examples: Billing specialist with access to claims system containing ePHI
IT administrator with privileged access to EHR infrastructure
Nurse accessing EHR for treatment documentation

dependencies.rationale: A defined contingency plan is essential to operationally achieve timely in-person specialty response when the scheduled on-call physician is unavailable due to competing facility obligations.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Without backup procedures, the hospital is more likely to experience delayed specialist response and potential EMTALA violations for failure to provide appropriate stabilizing treatment.

business_rules.rule_name: Responsible person must store decryption tools separate from data
business_rules.description: For sealed portable devices or original/backup electronic media holding part 2 patient records, the responsible person must (1) be included on the access control list, (2) be provided a means for decrypting the data, and (3) store decryption tools on a device or at a location separate from the data they are used to encrypt or decrypt.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when part 2 patient records are transferred to a portable electronic device or to original and backup electronic media for retention and a responsible person is designated to hold the container under part 2 restrictions.
business_rules.consequences: If the responsible person is on the ACL and maintains separate decryption tools storage, access to encrypted patient identifying information can be controlled and recovered while reducing compromise risk. If the decryption tools are stored with the encrypted data or the custodian is not on the ACL, security controls are deficient and may constitute noncompliance and elevate breach risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Electronic records requirements (b)(2)(iv)
source_reference.source_text: The responsible person must be included on the access control list and be provided a means for decrypting the data. The responsible person must store the decryption tools on a device or at a location separate from the data they are used to encrypt or decrypt.

examples: A records custodian holds the sealed encrypted drive in a secure cabinet; the decryption token is stored in a separate locked safe in a different room.
A custodian keeps the recovery key text file on the same encrypted external drive; this violates the 'separate from the data' requirement.
An IT admin who is not on the access control list is the only person with the decryption method; this violates the ACL inclusion requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal confidentiality enforcement under 42 CFR part 2; corrective action plan requirement

examples: Billing specialist with access to claims system containing ePHI
IT administrator with privileged access to EHR infrastructure
Nurse accessing EHR for treatment documentation

business_rules.rule_name: SUD Counseling Notes May Be Used Without Consent in 3 Cases
business_rules.description: Consent requirements for use/disclosure of SUD counseling notes do not apply in specific situations: (1) use by the originator of the SUD counseling notes for treatment; (2) use or disclosure by the program for its own training programs; or (3) use or disclosure by the program to defend itself in a legal action or other proceeding brought by the patient.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the information qualifies as SUD counseling notes and the proposed use/disclosure is for (1) originator treatment, (2) the program’s own training programs, or (3) defense in a legal action/proceeding brought by the patient.
business_rules.consequences: If the program incorrectly requires consent in these cases: unnecessary administrative burden and treatment/training/legal defense delays. If the program discloses outside these cases without consent: potential impermissible disclosure risk.
business_rules.exceptions: Outside of the listed situations, consent requirements may apply (not detailed in the cited excerpt).

source_reference.section_id: Final Rule (SUD counseling notes; consent requirement exceptions)
source_reference.source_text: With respect to consent for use and disclosure of SUD counseling notes we are finalizing the provision as § 2.31(b). The consent requirement does not apply to SUD counseling notes in certain specific situations such as the: (1) use by the originator of the SUD counseling notes for treatment; (2) use or disclosure by the program for its own training programs; or (3) use or disclosure by the program to defend itself in a legal action or other proceeding brought by the patient.

examples: A counselor (the originator) reviews their own counseling notes to inform the patient’s ongoing treatment; no separate consent is required for this use.
A Part 2 program uses de-identified excerpts of counseling notes internally for staff training as part of its own training programs; the consent requirement does not apply per the exception.
A patient sues the program alleging improper care; the program uses relevant counseling notes to defend itself in the proceeding without obtaining additional patient consent.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Potential Part 2 compliance issue if disclosures occur outside exceptions without consent

examples: EHR patient chart data stored in a database cluster
Claims history file transmitted via secure API to a clearinghouse
Imaging study metadata stored in PACS integrated with EHR

dependencies.rationale: Consent-exception pathways for counseling notes (originator treatment, training, legal defense) require that the material truly meets the counseling-notes definition; otherwise, the entity may inappropriately apply the exception to general medical record content.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Misclassification could result in disclosures without consent of information that is not eligible for the exception, creating Part 2 compliance risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Electronic media must be sanitized within one year of discontinuation
business_rules.description: Within one year of a program’s discontinuation or acquisition, all electronic media on which patient records or patient identifying information resided prior to being transferred (including email and other electronic communications) must be sanitized to render patient identifying information non-retrievable, consistent with policies and procedures established under § 2.16. Additionally, as soon as practicable after the end of the required retention period, the portable device and/or original and backup electronic media must be sanitized to render patient identifying information non-retrievable consistent with § 2.16 policies.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a Part 2 program is discontinued or acquired and electronic records are transferred to specified encrypted storage media; also applies when the retention period ends for stored electronic media containing patient identifying information.
business_rules.consequences: If compliant: residual electronic media does not retain retrievable patient identifying information after transition/retention period. If violated: patient identifying information may remain recoverable on legacy systems/media, constituting a security failure and potential Part 2 violation with aligned HIPAA enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: Sanitization timing after retention period is 'as soon as practicable' rather than a fixed number of days; the one-year requirement applies specifically to media that held the information prior to transfer after discontinuation/acquisition.

source_reference.section_id: Electronic records sanitization requirements (b)(2)(ii) and (v)
source_reference.source_text: Within one year of the discontinuation or acquisition of the program, all electronic media on which the patient records or patient identifying information resided prior to being transferred... including email and other electronic communications, must be sanitized to render the patient identifying information non-retrievable... consistent with the discontinued program’s or acquiring program’s policies and procedures established under § 2.16... As soon as practicable after the end of the required retention period specified on the label, the portable electronic device or the original and backup electronic media must be sanitized to render the patient identifying information non-retrievable

examples: A Part 2 program is acquired and migrates records to encrypted archival media; within one year it must sanitize old servers and mailboxes that previously stored patient identifying information.
A clinic stops operations and exports records to an encrypted portable device; it must sanitize prior laptops and email accounts where patient identifying information resided within one year.
After the labeled retention period expires, the archived encrypted device must be sanitized as soon as practicable; keeping it indefinitely without sanitization violates the rule.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: EHR patient chart data stored in a database cluster
Claims history file transmitted via secure API to a clearinghouse
Imaging study metadata stored in PACS integrated with EHR

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 de-identification must follow HIPAA 164.514(b) standard
business_rules.description: When Part 2 data are included in research reports, they must be in aggregate form where patient identifying information has been de-identified in accordance with the HIPAA Privacy Rule at 45 CFR 164.514(b) such that there is no reasonable basis to believe the information can be used to identify a patient as having or having had a substance use disorder. Separately, the statute prohibits identification of any individual patient directly or indirectly in any research report.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Part 2 records are used for research and results are included in a research report, and/or when the entity is applying a de-identification approach aligned to HIPAA 45 CFR 164.514(b) as described.
business_rules.consequences: If conditions are met: research reporting must ensure de-identification per HIPAA 164.514(b) and reporting must not identify individuals directly or indirectly. If violated: disclosure of identifiable Part 2 information in reports creates noncompliance risk and may trigger investigation and penalties.
business_rules.exceptions: The excerpt notes the Department declined to incorporate HIPAA 'limited data sets' into the Part 2 regulation in this rulemaking.

source_reference.section_id: Research and de-identification alignment discussion; statutory reporting limitation
source_reference.source_text: include part 2 data in research reports only in aggregate form in which patient identifying information has been de- identified in accordance with the requirements of the HIPAA Privacy Rule at 45 CFR 164.514(b) such that there is no reasonable basis to believe that the information can be used to identify a patient as having or having had a substance use disorder. ... Section 290dd–2(b)(2)(B) ... provides that records may be disclosed without consent “[t]o qualified personnel for the purpose of conducting scientific research . . . but such personnel may not identify, directly or indirectly, any individual patient in any report ...”

examples: A research team publishes county-level overdose treatment outcomes; the dataset is de-identified per 164.514(b) and results are aggregated so no individual is identifiable.

A report includes small-cell counts for a rare SUD condition in a rural area, allowing likely re-identification; the report must be adjusted (e.g., further aggregation/suppression) to avoid direct or indirect identification.
A researcher includes de-identified but still linkable narrative excerpts that can identify a patient; this is prohibited because it indirectly identifies an individual in a research report.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR/Part 2 enforcement action; potential civil monetary penalty

examples: EHR patient chart data stored in a database cluster
Claims history file transmitted via secure API to a clearinghouse
Imaging study metadata stored in PACS integrated with EHR

dependencies.rationale: Research reporting compliance depends on using a de-identification method that meets the HIPAA standard; 23_001 is the Part 2 rule that ties de-identification to that standard for disclosure/reporting.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If de-identification fails, research reports may impermissibly identify individuals directly or indirectly, creating significant Part 2 enforcement risk.

business_rules.rule_name: SUD Counseling Notes Must Exclude Specified Clinical Data Elements
business_rules.description: If a program uses the “SUD counseling notes” category, those notes must exclude certain items consistent with HIPAA psychotherapy notes exclusions. Specifically excluded items include: medication prescription and monitoring; counseling session start and stop times; modalities and frequencies of treatment furnished; results of clinical tests; and any summary of the following items: diagnosis, functional status, the treatment plan, symptoms, prognosis, and progress to date. Information critical to diagnosis and treatment (e.g., prognosis and test results) should be included within the patient’s Part 2 record or medical record for use in treatment consultation, medication management, care coordination, and billing.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when data is classified/managed as “SUD counseling notes” for heightened confidentiality protections and separate handling from the general Part 2/medical record.
business_rules.consequences: If excluded elements are placed into SUD counseling notes, the notes may be miscategorized, potentially impairing care coordination and undermining intended segregation/protection logic. If the program correctly excludes the listed elements, critical clinical data remains available in the broader Part 2/medical record to support care coordination and billing.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Final definition discussion—express exclusions from SUD counseling notes
source_reference.source_text: our final definition of “SUD counseling notes” expressly excludes “medication prescription and monitoring, counseling session start and stop times, modalities and frequencies of treatment furnished, results of clinical tests, and any summary of the following items: diagnosis, functional status, the treatment plan, symptoms, prognosis, and progress to date.” ... Information critical to the patients’ diagnosis and treatment such as prognosis and test results, should be within the patient’s part 2 record or medical record...

examples: A counselor’s note includes only personal process notes to recall the session; the patient’s diagnosis and treatment plan summary is documented in the main EHR progress note, not in SUD counseling notes.
A program stores lab toxicology results in the main Part 2/medical record because results of clinical tests are excluded from SUD counseling notes.
A clinician records prognosis in the treatment plan section of the Part 2 record (not in SUD counseling notes) to support medication management and billing documentation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: EHR patient chart data stored in a database cluster
Claims history file transmitted via secure API to a clearinghouse
Imaging study metadata stored in PACS integrated with EHR

business_rules.rule_name: SUD counseling notes must be maintained separately from the medical record
business_rules.description: SUD counseling notes (treated similarly to psychotherapy notes) are expected to be maintained separately from the rest of the individual’s medical record. This implies a data segregation requirement in systems that store or process these notes, to prevent routine inclusion/sharing with the broader record set.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when SUD counseling notes exist as part of a patient’s SUD-related documentation and are stored/managed electronically (e.g., EHR modules, behavioral health platforms, document repositories).
business_rules.consequences: If SUD counseling notes are not maintained separately, the entity increases the likelihood of inappropriate access/disclosure and may be unable to apply heightened protections. If maintained separately, the entity can apply more stringent disclosure controls consistent with the intended protections.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt. The excerpt also notes that recordings are not included in the definition of SUD counseling notes (separate classification may be needed for recordings).

source_reference.section_id: SUD counseling notes; alignment with psychotherapy notes
source_reference.source_text: Under HIPAA, psychotherapy notes must be maintained separately from the rest of the individual’s medical record. We establish a similar expectation with respect to SUD counseling notes in this final rule.

examples: An EHR stores SUD counseling notes in a segregated section requiring elevated access permissions; this aligns with the expectation of separate maintenance.
A clinic documents counseling notes as ordinary progress notes that automatically flow to all treating providers; this fails the separate-maintenance expectation and increases disclosure risk.
A program uses a behavioral health module that flags counseling notes as ‘restricted’ and excludes them from TPO data exports by default; supports separateness and minimization.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal Part 2 enforcement penalties; heightened risk of impermissible disclosure findings

examples: EHR patient chart data stored in a database cluster
Claims history file transmitted via secure API to a clearinghouse
Imaging study metadata stored in PACS integrated with EHR

dependencies.rationale: If excluded elements are incorrectly placed into the counseling-notes bucket, the segregation boundary becomes clinically and operationally invalid, undermining the separate-maintenance control.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Miscalssification can cause inappropriate withholding of clinically necessary information from the main medical record or inappropriate disclosure of sensitive notes due to mixing content types.

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 programs are not required to provide patients SUD counseling notes
business_rules.description: SUD counseling notes fall within the scope of Part 2 records but are separated from the rest of the patient’s SUD and medical record. Under § 2.23, the final rule does not require SUD counseling notes to be disclosed to the patient; however, a clinician may choose to disclose them voluntarily. Additionally, a Part 2 program is not required to obtain a patient’s written consent or other authorization to provide access to the patient.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a patient requests access to SUD counseling notes from a Part 2 program/holder and the requested material meets the definition of SUD counseling notes maintained separately.
business_rules.consequences: If the program treats counseling notes as automatically disclosable upon patient request, it may inadvertently release sensitive counselor notes contrary to the optional/discretionary nature described. If the program withholds counseling notes, it remains compliant under the described rule; if it chooses to disclose voluntarily, it may do so without needing written consent solely for disclosure to the patient.
business_rules.exceptions: State law may impose separate access requirements; the excerpt indicates programs should consider state requirements when deciding whether to use the SUD counseling notes provision.

source_reference.section_id: Patient access; § 2.23 and SUD counseling notes
source_reference.source_text: that SUD counseling notes fall within the scope of part 2 records although they are separated from the rest of the patient’s SUD and medical record under § 2.11 (SUD counseling notes). The final rule therefore does not require under § 2.23 that SUD counseling notes be disclosed to the patient, but a clinician may choose to do so voluntarily. We

examples: A patient requests their entire SUD record; the program provides standard treatment notes but withholds separately maintained SUD counseling notes—permitted under the described rule.

A clinician decides disclosure would support patient engagement and voluntarily shares counseling notes directly with the patient; no separate written consent is required for disclosure to the patient.
A state law grants broader access rights than Part 2; the program must evaluate and comply with applicable state access requirements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal Part 2 enforcement penalties if mishandled; state enforcement risk where state access laws differ

examples: EHR patient chart data stored in a database cluster
Claims history file transmitted via secure API to a clearinghouse
Imaging study metadata stored in PACS integrated with EHR

dependencies.rationale: The discretionary disclosure concept for counseling notes is operationally feasible only if the notes are segregated; otherwise, access workflows may inadvertently include them in standard record releases.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If separation is not implemented, the program may accidentally disclose counseling notes or be unable to honor discretionary withholding without blocking access to the rest of the record.

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 consent must explain written revocation and reliance
business_rules.description: When a Part 2 program uses a written consent for use/disclosure, the consent must inform the patient of the patient’s right to revoke the consent in writing, except to the extent that the Part 2 program (or other lawful holder permitted to disclose patient identifying information) has already acted in reliance on it, and must explain how the patient may revoke consent.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Part 2 consent forms used under 42 CFR Part 2, including consents supporting uses/disclosures for treatment, payment, and health care operations where consent is required.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: patient is informed of revocation rights and process; consent supports compliant uses/disclosures. If violated: consent may be noncompliant; uses/disclosures based on a defective consent increase risk of Part 2 violation and enforcement.
business_rules.exceptions: Revocation right does not apply to the extent the program or lawful holder has already acted in reliance on the consent.

source_reference.section_id: § 2.31(a)(6) consent content requirement
source_reference.source_text: Section 2.31(a)(6) of this final rule requires that the consent must inform the patient of “[t]he patient’s right to revoke the consent in writing, except to the extent that the part 2 program, or other lawful holder of patient identifying information that is permitted to make the disclosure, has already acted in reliance on it, and how the patient may revoke consent.”

examples: A consent form includes: 'You may revoke this consent in writing by submitting the revocation form to the Privacy Officer; revocation will not affect disclosures already made in reliance on your consent.'
A program’s consent form lacks any revocation language; the form must be updated before further reliance for disclosures.
A patient revokes consent after the program already disclosed records to a payer; the prior disclosure remains covered by reliance, but future disclosures stop.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty and corrective action plan under HIPAA-aligned enforcement for Part 2 noncompliance

examples: Access control policy defining role-based access and approval workflow
Configuration management procedure for system hardening and patching
Incident response and contingency plan activation procedure

business_rules.rule_name: Simultaneous call requires written backup response procedures
business_rules.description: If a hospital permits physicians to be on-call simultaneously at two or more facilities, the hospital must have written policies and procedures to follow when the on-call physician is not available to respond because the physician has been called to another hospital. All hospitals where the physician is on-call must be aware of the simultaneous call details and have back-up plans established.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when hospital policy allows simultaneous on-call coverage by a physician for multiple facilities, creating a foreseeable scenario where the physician cannot respond to an on-call request.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: ED staff can execute a defined back-up plan to ensure timely specialty response for emergency medical conditions. If violated: increased risk of EMTALA failures to provide appropriate examination/stabilizing treatment due to unavailable on-call resources; potential CMS survey deficiencies and EMTALA enforcement actions.
business_rules.exceptions: Hospitals may adopt a policy that does not allow simultaneous call; in that case, this specific written backup requirement for simultaneous call is not triggered.

source_reference.section_id: Simultaneous call policy and backup requirement
source_reference.source_text: Hospitals are permitted to allow physicians to be on-call simultaneously at two or more facilities... If a hospital permits simultaneous call, then it must have written policies and procedures to follow when the on-call physician is not available to respond because he/she has been called to another hospital. All hospitals where the physician is on-call need to be aware of the details of the simultaneous call arrangements for the physician and have back-up plans established.

examples: An orthopedist is on-call at Hospital A and Hospital B; Hospital A’s policy requires contacting a designated backup orthopedist when the primary is engaged at Hospital B.
A hospital allows simultaneous call but has no documented backup procedure; the ED cannot reliably obtain specialty coverage, creating EMTALA risk.
A hospital prohibits simultaneous call by policy; the written backup process for simultaneous call is not required.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey deficiency citation and potential EMTALA enforcement action

examples: Access control policy defining role-based access and approval workflow
Configuration management procedure for system hardening and patching
Incident response and contingency plan activation procedure

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 notice must include required plain-language elements
business_rules.description: A Part 2 program must provide a written notice in plain language describing the program’s legal duties and privacy practices. The notice must include: (1) a prominently displayed header statement describing the notice’s contents (uses/disclosures, rights, complaint process) and the right to a copy in paper or electronic form and to discuss the notice with a named or titled contact at a phone number and email; (2) a description of each purpose for which the program is permitted or required to use or disclose records without written consent; (3) if another applicable law is more stringent, the description must reflect the more stringent law; (4) sufficient detail to place the patient on notice; and (5) at least one example of the types of uses and disclosures that require written consent.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when drafting or updating the Part 2 program’s patient-facing notice required under 42 CFR 2.22(b)(1), including the uses and disclosures section.
business_rules.consequences: If compliant: patients receive clear, comprehensive information about privacy practices and rights. If violated: deficient notice content can constitute noncompliance with Part 2 notice requirements and may support enforcement actions and mandated revisions/re-distribution.
business_rules.exceptions: If a use or disclosure is prohibited or materially limited by other applicable law, the notice must reflect the more stringent law (i.e., Part 2 does not override stricter applicable law for notice descriptions).

source_reference.source_text: a part 2 program shall provide notice, written in plain language, of the program’s legal duties and privacy practices... The notice must contain... Notice of Privacy Practices of [Name of Part 2 Program]... YOU HAVE A RIGHT TO A COPY OF THIS NOTICE (IN PAPER OR ELECTRONIC FORM) AND TO DISCUSS IT WITH [ENTER NAME OR TITLE] AT [PHONE AND EMAIL]... The notice must contain: (A) A description of each of the purposes for which the part 2 program is permitted or required by this part to use or disclose records without the patient’s written consent. (B) If... prohibited or materially limited by other applicable law... reflect the more stringent law... (D) A description, including at least one example, of the types of uses and disclosures that require written consent

examples: A notice describes ‘we may disclose for payment’ but omits how patients can file a complaint and omits contact phone/email; the notice is missing required elements.
A state law prohibits a specific disclosure that Part 2 would otherwise permit; the notice must describe the disclosure as prohibited/limited to match the more stringent state law.
A notice lists purposes for disclosures without consent but provides no example of a disclosure requiring written consent; it must add at least one example (e.g., disclosure to an employer).
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: Access control policy defining role-based access and approval workflow
Configuration management procedure for system hardening and patching
Incident response and contingency plan activation procedure

dependencies.rationale: When disclosures require a notice-to-accompany-disclosure and systems limit message length, 19_008 provides a compliant mechanism consistent with the broader notice communication expectations reflected in 009_005.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the abbreviated notice option is not used where needed, disclosures may occur without required accompanying notice, increasing Part 2 compliance risk.

dependencies.rationale: 009_005 establishes the comprehensive structure and required categories for the notice; 001_005 adds specific mandatory statements for Part 2 programs about use/disclosure in proceedings and court-order conditions. Both are needed for a complete compliant notice.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 001_005 content is omitted, the notice built under 009_005 is incomplete and increases risk of prohibited reliance on Part 2 records in proceedings contexts.

dependencies.rationale: Notice drafting/updating, publishing, and workforce/patient-facing rollout are implementation activities that must be completed by the compliance date for any newly required notice content or revised practices stemming from the final rule.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If notice updates are not completed by the compliance date, patient notice content may be outdated or missing required elements, creating Part 2 noncompliance exposure.

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 Programs Must Maintain Security Policies for Paper and Electronic Records
business_rules.description: A Part 2 program or other lawful holder of patient identifying information must have formal policies and procedures in place to reasonably protect against unauthorized uses and disclosures and reasonably anticipated threats/hazards to the security of patient identifying information. Policies and procedures must address (at minimum): (i) paper records—transferring/removing, destroying (including sanitizing hard copy media associated with printouts to render information non-retrievable), secure storage (secure room/locked file cabinet/safe or similar when not in use), using/accessing storage containers/workstations/secure rooms, and de-identification per 45 CFR 164.514(b) such that there is no reasonable basis to believe the information can identify a patient; and (ii) electronic records—creating/receiving/maintaining/transmitting, destroying (including sanitizing electronic media to render information non-retrievable), using/accessing electronic records/media, and de-identification per 45 CFR 164.514(b) to remove reasonable basis for identification. Informal caregivers (family, friends) who are lawful holders are not required to comply with these policy/procedure requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Part 2 programs and other lawful holders of patient identifying information, for both paper and electronic record formats and associated media handling.
business_rules.consequences: If policies/procedures are absent or do not address required elements (paper and electronic), the entity is noncompliant with 42 CFR 2.16(a). If the entity qualifies as an informal caregiver lawful holder, the specific policy requirement does not apply.
business_rules.exceptions: Family, friends, and other informal caregivers who are lawful holders are not required to comply with 42 CFR 2.16(a).

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR 2.16(a)(1)-(2) Security for records
source_reference.source_text: The part 2 program or other lawful holder of patient identifying information must have in place formal policies and procedures to reasonably protect against unauthorized uses and disclosures... These policies and procedures must address... Paper records... Destroying such records, including sanitizing the hard copy media... to render... non-retrievable... Electronic records... Destroying such records, including sanitizing the electronic media... to render... non-retrievable... Exception... Family, friends, and other informal caregivers... are not required to comply with paragraph (a) of this section.

examples: A Part 2 clinic maintains written procedures for shredding paper files and sanitizing copier hard drives so patient identifying information is non-retrievable.
A lawful holder operating an EHR documents procedures for secure access and media sanitization before disposing of drives that stored patient identifying information.
A friend who informally holds printed discharge instructions as a lawful holder is not required to implement the formal policy/procedure set under the informal caregiver exception.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal enforcement for Part 2 noncompliance (insufficient safeguards for patient identifying information)

examples: Access control policy defining role-based access and approval workflow
Configuration management procedure for system hardening and patching
Incident response and contingency plan activation procedure

dependencies.rationale: The obligation in 42 CFR 2.16(a) to maintain formal policies/procedures applies only if the lawful holder is not an informal caregiver excluded by the exception.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Misapplication causes over-enforcement of formal security documentation requirements on excluded holders, distorting compliance posture and training expectations.

business_rules.rule_name: TPO Consent Must Explain Consequences of Refusing to Sign
business_rules.description: When a Part 2 program uses consent for TPO uses/disclosures and conditions treatment on signing that consent, § 2.31 requires the consent to include a statement of the consequences to the patient of refusing to sign the consent. While no specific language is mandated, the consent should state that patient consent is needed (or required) for the program to use/disclose records for TPO (or to help operate its health care business) and include consequence statements reflecting the program’s policies (e.g., limits on ongoing treatment, payment before services, or offering narrower consent options).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the program presents a TPO consent (for future TPO uses/disclosures) and/or conditions treatment on the patient signing the consent.
business_rules.consequences: If the consent includes required consequence statements, the program meets the stated § 2.31 requirement for transparency about refusal impacts. If omitted, the consent is noncompliant with the described § 2.31 content requirement, increasing regulatory risk and undermining informed decision-making.
business_rules.exceptions: The regulation does not require specific language. The program is not required to offer narrower consent options or to reference the right to request restrictions, though it may be helpful.

source_reference.section_id: § 2.31 conditioning treatment on consent; required consequence statement
source_reference.source_text: Thus, with respect to conditioning treatment on consent, § 2.31 requires a statement of “the consequences to the patient of a refusal to sign the consent.” ... consent for TPO use and disclosure should include a statement that patient consent is needed (or required) to allow the program to use and disclose the patient’s records for TPO... The final rule also requires a statement or statements explaining the consequences of failing to sign, based on the program’s consent policies.

examples: A program’s TPO consent form states: “If you do not sign, we will still complete your initial evaluation, but we cannot provide ongoing treatment because we cannot coordinate payment and referrals.”
A program’s TPO consent form states: “If you refuse to sign, you may be required to pay before services are provided.”
A program chooses to include: “You have the right to request restrictions on TPO disclosures and we will make reasonable efforts to accommodate,” although the text indicates this is optional.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: Access control policy defining role-based access and approval workflow
Configuration management procedure for system hardening and patching
Incident response and contingency plan activation procedure

dependencies.rationale: To operationalize a single future TPO consent, the consent form must satisfy §2.31 content requirements, including transparency about refusal consequences when conditioning treatment.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If consequence statements are omitted in conditioning contexts, the consent may be noncompliant, undermining reliance on the single-consent mechanism for ongoing TPO uses/disclosures.

dependencies.rationale: 016_001 governs required content (including consequences of refusing to sign) when using/conditioning treatment on TPO consent. If the signer is not legally authorized (parent instead of capable minor), the consent is invalid regardless of content completeness.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 001_004 is not satisfied, the program may treat a consent as valid when it is not, making downstream TPO uses/disclosures unauthorized and exposing the program to enforcement.

dependencies.rationale: Once a program is operating within Part 2 restrictions (001_002) and chooses to use a TPO consent (and possibly condition treatment on it), it must meet the specific consent content transparency requirement in 016_001.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 001_002 is not applied, the program may skip Part 2-specific consent content requirements or wrongly assume HIPAA consent/authorization norms apply.

dependencies.rationale: The adult-incapacity consent rule determines who may give Part 2 consent (personal representative vs. program director pathway) and limits the program-director pathway to the sole purpose of obtaining payment absent adjudication. The TPO consent content requirements (including refusal consequences) depend on first selecting an authorized consent pathway and aligning the consent scope accordingly.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the program uses the wrong signer or exceeds the permitted purpose (e.g., beyond payment under the program-director pathway), the consent is not authorized for those disclosures even if the form otherwise includes the required refusal-consequences statement.

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENTREQUIREMENT_23_009 establishes that refusal consequences must be disclosed; BR_SECURITY_POLICY_AND_PROCEDURE_CONSENT_016_001 operationalizes/validates that requirement in the specific scenario where treatment is conditioned on signing the TPO consent, guiding what must be stated to remain compliant with § 2.31 transparency requirements.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the consequences statement is not properly included/implemented when conditioning treatment, the consent may be noncompliant and the program risks enforcement and challenges to the validity of disclosures made under that consent.

business_rules.rule_name: Abbreviated Part 2 notice option must fit 80 characters in EHR free-text fields
business_rules.description: Organizations exchanging Part 2 records via EHR/health IT may use the abbreviated notice to accompany disclosure option designed to fit within standard free-text space of electronic systems. The Department previously adopted an abbreviated notice that is 80 characters long for compatibility with health care electronic systems; this abbreviated notice remains a valid option.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an organization must provide a Notice to Accompany Disclosure for Part 2 records and is using EHR/health IT formats where character limits or standard message structures constrain inclusion of longer notice language.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: the organization can operationalize notice requirements in constrained EHR exchange contexts using the abbreviated notice option. If violated (for example, omitting required notice entirely): the disclosure may fail to meet Part 2 notice requirements, increasing Part 2 compliance risk.
business_rules.exceptions: An organization may instead choose one of the longer notices permitted under § 2.32(a)(1) if its systems and workflows support it.

source_reference.section_id: Response re: §2.32(a)(2) abbreviated notice and EHR support
source_reference.source_text: The abbreviated notice under paragraph (a)(2) was primarily intended to support EHR systems. As the Department explained in 2018, “SAMHSA has adopted an abbreviated notice that is 80 characters long to fit in standard free-text space within health care electronic systems.”

examples: A hospital’s ADT feed cannot transmit the full notice language; the hospital uses the 80-character abbreviated notice in the available free-text field.
An HIE supports both long-form and abbreviated notices; it selects the abbreviated notice for HL7 v2 messages and long-form notice for PDF disclosures.
A clinic’s EHR has no field for any notice and sends Part 2 data without any notice; this creates Part 2 compliance risk and requires workflow remediation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Part 2 compliance deficiency risk for disclosures lacking required notice

examples: Access control policy defining role-based access and approval workflow
Configuration management procedure for system hardening and patching
Incident response and contingency plan activation procedure

dependencies.rationale: An entity must first determine it is making a Part 2 disclosure that requires accompanying notice; only then does the EHR character-limit adaptation in 19_008 matter.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 001_002 applicability is missed, disclosures may be made via EHR exchange with no accompanying notice at all, raising Part 2 noncompliance risk.

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_22_008 covers scenarios where records are downloaded/forwarded to another electronic system/device for audit/evaluation. In health IT contexts, BR_SECURITY_POLICY_AND_PROCEDURE_HIPAA_PRIVACY_19_008 provides an operationally compatible method (abbreviated notice) to satisfy notice-to-accompany-disclosure needs within EHR message constraints.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the notice mechanism is not implemented, electronic transfers may occur without the accompanying notice, increasing Part 2 notice noncompliance risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Informal caregivers need not create formal security policies
business_rules.description: An exception is created to the requirement that Part 2 programs and lawful holders create formal policies and procedures to secure Part 2 records: certain lawful holders—specifically family, friends, and other informal caregivers—are expressly excluded from the requirement to develop formal policies and procedures.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a lawful holder of Part 2 records is a non-professional informal caregiver (family member, friend, or similar) holding records outside a professional capacity.
business_rules.consequences: If the lawful holder is an informal caregiver, they are not required under the cited Part 2 provision to develop formal security policies and procedures. If the lawful holder is acting in a professional capacity (or is a Part 2 program/covered entity/business associate as applicable), they remain subject to applicable security policy and procedure requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: The exception is limited to “family, friends, and other informal caregivers” as described; other lawful holders are not covered by this exception.

source_reference.section_id: Final Rule for Lawful Holders and Security of Records (§ 2.16(a))
source_reference.source_text: Specifically, we are creating a new exception for certain lawful holders in new paragraph (a)(2) that expressly excludes “family, friends, and other informal caregivers” from the requirements to develop formal policies and procedures. We expect that informal caregivers and other similar lawful holders who would be subject to this exception still recognize some responsibility to safeguard these sensitive records and exercise caution when handling such records.

examples: A parent receives discharge paperwork containing Part 2 information for a minor and keeps it at home; the parent is not required to develop formal written security policies under § 2.16(a) as described in the excerpt.

A community-based care manager working for an organization (professional capacity) holds Part 2 records; the organization cannot claim the informal caregiver exception and should maintain formal policies and procedures.
A friend temporarily holds printed Part 2 records for a patient; no formal policies are required by the exception, but reasonable safeguarding is encouraged.
business_rules.enforcement_action: No enforcement exposure for lack of formal policies when the lawful holder qualifies as an informal caregiver under the exception

examples: Access control policy defining role-based access and approval workflow
Configuration management procedure for system hardening and patching
Incident response and contingency plan activation procedure

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 programs must implement breach notification policies
business_rules.description: Part 2 programs must establish and implement policies and procedures for notification of breaches of unsecured part 2 records consistent with the requirements of 45 CFR parts 160 and 164, subpart D. The policy objectives include written policies and procedures to address breaches and document actions taken in response, notification to the Secretary and affected patients (and sometimes media), mitigation of harm, and measures to prevent future breaches.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the organization is a part 2 program (or otherwise subject to the part 2 breach notification provisions) and experiences or prepares for a breach of unsecured part 2 records.
business_rules.consequences: If policies and procedures are implemented: the entity supports timely, consistent breach response aligned with HIPAA breach notification constructs. If not implemented: the entity risks noncompliance with part 2 breach notification requirements and heightened enforcement exposure if a breach occurs without proper notification/mitigation/documentation.
business_rules.exceptions: The cited text does not create an exemption for entities also subject to HIPAA; instead, the Department indicates it expects an enforcement process that avoids duplicative reporting by part 2 programs.

source_reference.section_id: Final Rule (adding § 2.16(b) breach notification policies and procedures)
source_reference.source_text: Final Rule The Department adopts the proposal to add paragraph (b) to § 2.16 to require part 2 programs to establish and implement policies and procedures for notification of breaches of unsecured part 2 records consistent with the requirements of 45 CFR parts 160 and 164, subpart D. ... objectives ... maintain written policies and procedures to address breaches and document actions taken ... notification of the Secretary, affected patients, and in some cases the media ... obligations to mitigate harm ... improved measures to prevent future breaches.

examples: A part 2 program discovers an employee emailed unsecured SUD records externally; it follows its written policy aligned to 45 CFR 160/164 subpart D to document actions, mitigate harm, and initiate required notifications.
A non-HIPAA part 2 provider implements breach notification procedures because it cannot rely on existing HIPAA breach workflows.
A part 2 program updates its incident response runbook to explicitly address ‘unsecured records’ and ‘breach’ definitions used for notifications.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty (HITECH-aligned) and/or corrective action plan under part 2 enforcement

examples: Access control policy defining role-based access and approval workflow
Configuration management procedure for system hardening and patching
Incident response and contingency plan activation procedure

dependencies.rationale: Breach notification procedures are most reliably executed when security policies define how records are protected, handled, and evaluated as “unsecured” vs protected, and how incidents are managed.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Without aligned security P&P, breach response may be inconsistent, poorly documented, or delayed, increasing enforcement exposure after an incident.

dependencies.rationale: Part 2 breach notification procedures are aligned to HIPAA breach constructs; the organization must first classify the incident using the breach definition/exceptions before proceeding to notification workflows.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If breach definition logic is not applied, the entity may notify when not required or fail to notify when required, increasing OCR/Part 2 enforcement risk.

dependencies.rationale: If the four-factor assessment cannot establish low probability of compromise, the incident is treated as a breach and Part 2 breach notification procedures must be executed.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the assessment is missing/deficient, the entity cannot defensibly avoid notification, increasing enforcement exposure and audit failure risk.

dependencies.rationale: The reporting rule incorporates HIPAA breach notification provisions for Part 2 programs. The policy/procedure rule requires implementing breach notification policies consistent with those incorporated HIPAA requirements; therefore the incorporation rule sets the controlling framework the policies must follow.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the organization does not align policies to HIPAA breach-notification constructs as incorporated, it risks Part 2 breach-notification noncompliance (wrong recipients, timing, documentation, or mitigation steps).

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAAPRIVACY_23_003 establishes the applicability boundary (Part 2 programs must follow breach notification; lawful holders are not subject under the excerpt). BR_SECURITYPOLICY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_24_003 requires breach notification policies/procedures for entities subject to Part 2 breach notification—so applicability must be determined first.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If applicability is misclassified, an entity may either fail to implement required breach notification policies (programs) or wastefully implement policies not required under Part 2 (certain lawful holders).

dependencies.rationale: BR_SECURITYPOLICY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_24_003 requires breach notification actions (to affected individuals/Secretary/media as applicable) and documentation. If a breach occurs at a QSO/BA, BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAAPRIVACY_23_004 requires the program to have contractual provisions ensuring timely notice from the QSO/BA; without that, the program may not learn of the breach in time to comply.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If contracts do not require timely breach reporting, the program risks late notification and noncompliance with breach notification timelines and documentation expectations.

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATEDENTITY_REGULATORY_24_001 sets the implementation window (effective date + 22-month compliance period). Policy buildout and operational readiness for breach notification procedures must be completed by the compliance date to avoid post-date enforcement exposure.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the organization fails to align implementation to the compliance date, it risks being operationally unprepared when breach notification obligations are enforceable.

dependencies.rationale: Rule 24_003 requires breach notification policies consistent with HIPAA breach notification constructs. Rule 26_004 defines what constitutes a breach for Part 2 by adopting HIPAA’s definition and presumption/risk-assessment approach; without this definition, the notification policy cannot be correctly triggered or scoped.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the breach definition/risk assessment standard is not incorporated, the organization may under-notify (regulatory exposure) or over-notify (unnecessary patient harm, reputational damage, operational cost).

business_rules.rule_name: Complaint Materials Should Address LEP and Disabilities
business_rules.description: Part 2 programs should develop § 2.4 complaint-related materials that are clear and reflect the needs of their patient population. Programs should be mindful that federal civil rights laws may require certain entities (including recipients of Federal financial assistance and public entities) to take appropriate steps to ensure effective communication for individuals with disabilities (including auxiliary aids/services where necessary) and meaningful access for individuals with limited English proficiency (including language assistance services when necessary).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a part 2 program develops or updates § 2.4 complaint process materials and the entity is subject to applicable federal civil rights obligations (e.g., recipients of federal financial assistance/public entities).
business_rules.consequences: If followed: complaint process materials are more accessible and understandable, reducing barriers to reporting and reducing civil rights noncompliance risk. If not followed: increased risk of civil rights complaints/investigations and reduced accessibility of the complaint process.
business_rules.exceptions: This excerpt uses “should/strongly encourages” language and references that legal requirements depend on entity type (e.g., recipients of federal financial assistance, public entities).

source_reference.section_id: § 2.4 complaint process—accessibility/LEP discussion
source_reference.source_text: “Part 2 programs should be mindful that Federal civil rights laws require certain entities… to take appropriate steps… ensure that communications with individuals with disabilities are as effective… including by providing appropriate auxiliary aids and services… recipients of Federal financial assistance must take reasonable steps to ensure meaningful access… for individuals with limited English proficiency… The Department strongly encourages development of § 2.4 materials that are clear and reflect the needs of a program’s patient population.”

examples: A county-operated part 2 clinic provides complaint forms in large print and offers ASL interpreter services upon request as auxiliary aids where necessary.
A federally funded clinic translates complaint instructions into Spanish and uses a phone language line for LEP patients when submitting complaints.
A private program not receiving federal financial assistance still adopts plain-language complaint materials to reflect its patient population, even if specific civil-rights obligations differ.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil rights investigation and corrective action plan

examples: Access control policy defining role-based access and approval workflow
Configuration management procedure for system hardening and patching
Incident response and contingency plan activation procedure

dependencies.rationale: 009_005 requires notice to describe complaint processes; 025_009 provides civil-rights-informed guidance to ensure those materials are understandable and accessible for LEP and disability accommodations.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If accessibility is not addressed, complaint materials may be unusable for portions of the population, increasing civil rights and Part 2 complaint-handling risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Maintain contemporaneous investigation records with six required elements
business_rules.description: For any internal investigation (regardless of size or severity), the entity should maintain a contemporaneous record so a record can be compiled. The investigation record should include: (1) documentation of the alleged violation; (2) a description of the investigative process; (3) copies of interview notes and key document(s); (4) a log of witnesses interviewed and documents reviewed; (5) results of the investigation; and (6) any disciplinary action taken or corrective action implemented.
business_rules.conditions: Applies whenever the regulated entity conducts an internal investigation into alleged violations of its compliance program, applicable Federal/State law, or other misconduct.
business_rules.consequences: If met: supports defensibility of findings, corrective actions, and any self-reporting; enables consistent case management. If violated: inability to demonstrate reasonable inquiry, weakened position in audits/investigations, and reduced credibility with government authorities.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: 1. Investigations of Violations
source_reference.source_text: Regardless of the size or severity of the violation being investigated, a contemporaneous record of the investigation should be maintained, so that a record of the investigation can be compiled. The record should include: documentation of the alleged violation; a description of the investigative process; copies of interview notes and key document; a log of the witnesses interviewed and the documents reviewed; the results of the investigation; and any disciplinary action taken or corrective action implemented.

examples: An entity investigates suspected duplicate billing and keeps a file with the allegation, audit queries, witness interview notes, list of claims reviewed, final conclusions, and repayment/corrective action evidence.
A privacy-related misconduct investigation includes a witness log, email search results, and documented disciplinary action for policy violations.
A small clinic investigates a single incident; even though severity is low, it still keeps a contemporaneous record with the six required elements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Adverse audit/investigation outcome due to insufficient documentation; increased enforcement scrutiny

examples: Access control policy defining role-based access and approval workflow
Configuration management procedure for system hardening and patching
Incident response and contingency plan activation procedure

dependencies.rationale: 24_003 calls for documenting actions taken in response to breaches; 027_007 specifies what an internal investigation record should contain to make the response defensible.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Without investigation records, the entity may be unable to prove timely response, mitigation, and rationale for notification decisions, increasing penalties and corrective action likelihood.

dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_31_008 creates channels for reporting compliance concerns (often generating allegations). BR_SECURITY_POLICY_AND_PROCEDURE_DOCUMENTATION_027_007 requires contemporaneous documentation for any internal investigation. In practice, receiving a report triggers the investigative workflow, and documentation requirements must be executed in sequence after intake.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If reporting exists but investigation documentation is not maintained, the entity cannot evidence reasonable inquiry/corrective action, increasing enforcement and audit vulnerability.

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 Notice Must Include 3 Year Accounting and an Effective Date
business_rules.description: A 42 CFR Part 2 program’s notice must include specific patient rights and required administrative statements. The notice must state the patient’s right to an accounting of disclosures of electronic records under Part 2 for the past 3 years (and related disclosure-list rights as applicable), and the notice must contain the date on which the notice is first in effect, which may not be earlier than the date the notice is printed or otherwise published.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a Part 2 program creates, publishes, or revises its notice of privacy practices/notice regarding legal duties and privacy practices for Part 2 records.
business_rules.consequences: If the notice includes the required 3-year accounting right and compliant effective date, the program meets the cited notice-content obligations. If missing or noncompliant (e.g., effective date earlier than publication/print date), the program risks Part 2 compliance deficiencies and potential enforcement or corrective action requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: Patient rights; Effective date (notice content requirements)
source_reference.source_text: Right to an accounting of disclosures of electronic records under this part for the past 3 years, as provided in § 2.25... (viii) Effective date. The notice must contain the date on which the notice is first in effect, which may not be earlier than the date on which the notice is printed or otherwise published.

examples: A Part 2 program updates its notice and lists the right to an accounting of electronic disclosures for the past 3 years; the effective date is the same day the notice is posted on the website.
A program prints a notice on March 1 but lists an effective date of February 15; this violates the requirement that the effective date may not be earlier than the print/publish date.
A patient requests an accounting; staff use the policy-defined process to retrieve and provide disclosure information covering the prior 3 years for electronic records.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory compliance finding and required corrective action plan

examples: Access control policy defining role-based access and approval workflow
Configuration management procedure for system hardening and patching
Incident response and contingency plan activation procedure

dependencies.rationale: 009_005 addresses plain-language notice structure and core sections (uses/disclosures, rights, complaints, contact), while 31_009 adds mandatory rights statements (3-year accounting) and effective date rules.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If either component set is missing, the notice may be deficient under Part 2, triggering mandated revisions and re-distribution and increasing enforcement exposure.

dependencies.rationale: To draft a compliant notice statement about the 3-year accounting of disclosures, the program must align notice language with the actual accounting obligations (including EHR/TPO limitations).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the underlying accounting rule is misunderstood, the notice may misstate rights (over/under inclusive), leading to patient complaints and Part 2 noncompliance findings.

dependencies.rationale: Even if the notice generally must exist, the specific notice content about the accounting right (and related statements) is deferred; this supersedes any immediate obligation to include that element.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the tolling rule is not applied, programs may update/reissue notices prematurely or be incorrectly cited for missing a notice element that is not yet required.

dependencies.rationale: If the accounting requirement is defined as a 3-year lookback for consent-based disclosures, the notice content describing that right should align to the same timeframe to avoid misleading patients and to meet the specified notice-content obligations.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the 3-year timeframe is not understood/implemented, the notice may be inaccurate (or incomplete), increasing Part 2 notice noncompliance risk once the obligation is in force.

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 security policies must cover paper and electronic safeguards
business_rules.description: A part 2 program or other lawful holder of patient identifying information must have formal policies and procedures to reasonably protect against unauthorized uses/disclosures and reasonably anticipated threats/hazards. Policies/procedures must address specific required topics for paper records (e.g., secure storage, transfer/removal, destruction including sanitizing associated hard copy media) and for electronic records (e.g., create/receive/maintain/transmit, destruction including sanitizing electronic media), and must address de-identification consistent with 45 CFR 164.514(b).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the organization is a 'part 2 program or other lawful holder' maintaining patient identifying information subject to 42 CFR part 2.
business_rules.consequences: If implemented as specified, the organization meets the formal policy/procedure component of part 2 security requirements. If not implemented or missing required topic areas, the organization is out of compliance with 42 CFR 2.16 and may be subject to federal confidentiality enforcement and corrective action obligations.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.source_text: The part 2 program or other lawful holder of patient identifying information must have in place formal policies and procedures to reasonably protect against unauthorized uses and disclosures of patient identifying information and to protect against reasonably anticipated threats or hazards to the security of patient identifying information. These policies and procedures must address all of the following: Paper records, including: Transferring and removing such records; Destroying such records, including sanitizing the hard copy media associated with the paper printouts, to render the patient identifying information non-retrievable; Maintaining such records in a secure room, locked file cabinet, safe, or other similar container... Electronic records, including: Creating, receiving, maintaining, and transmitting such records; Destroying such records, including sanitizing the electronic media on which such records are stored, to render the patient identifying information non-retrievable.

examples: A residential SUD treatment program updates its policies to include locked file cabinet storage when paper charts are not in use and a documented process to sanitize copier/printer drums when decommissioned.
A lawful holder hospital unit adds procedures for sanitizing retired laptops and for encrypting portable media used for record retention transfers.
An organization that has general security policies but no procedures for electronic media sanitization fails the required topic coverage and is noncompliant.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal confidentiality enforcement under 42 CFR part 2; corrective action plan requirement

examples: Access control policy defining role-based access and approval workflow
Configuration management procedure for system hardening and patching
Incident response and contingency plan activation procedure

dependencies.rationale: Before applying the Part 2 formal security P&P requirement to a lawful holder, the entity must be classified; if the holder is an informal caregiver, the P&P requirement is inapplicable.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the exception is not checked, an informal caregiver may be incorrectly required to implement formal policies/procedures, creating false noncompliance findings and unnecessary controls.

dependencies.rationale: Organizations typically operationalize the requirement by combining the minimum-elements view (010_008) with the detailed topic coverage (037_001) to ensure no required areas are missed.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If only one is implemented, required paper/electronic handling elements may be incomplete, leading to partial compliance and increased breach/unauthorized disclosure risk.

dependencies.rationale: Entities that are both HIPAA covered entities and Part 2 programs generally rely on HIPAA safeguard frameworks to implement Part 2-required security policies and procedures comprehensively.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If HIPAA safeguards are weak, Part 2 security P&P are less likely to be effective, increasing unauthorized use/disclosure and audit failure likelihood.

business_rules.rule_name: HIPAA redisclosure for TPO needs no added authorization
business_rules.description: When a covered entity receives part 2 records, it may have a reduced need to obtain additional written patient consent or HIPAA authorization for redisclosure because redisclosure under the HIPAA Privacy Rule does not require patient consent or authorization for TPO and many other purposes.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (1) the covered entity is a recipient of part 2 records and (2) the covered entity rediscloses those records under HIPAA Privacy Rule permissions for TPO or other permitted purposes described as not requiring additional consent/authorization in the excerpt.
business_rules.consequences: If followed: the covered entity avoids unnecessary consent collection for HIPAA-permitted TPO redisclosures, reducing administrative burden. If violated by imposing unnecessary authorization requirements: may delay care coordination and operations; if redisclosures occur outside HIPAA permissions: increased risk of noncompliance and enforcement.
business_rules.exceptions: The excerpt is a cost-savings discussion and does not list specific redisclosure purposes that still require authorization under HIPAA (e.g., marketing), which must be evaluated separately under applicable HIPAA rules.

source_reference.section_id: FR Vol. 89 No. 33 (Feb. 16, 2024), Capital expense savings—recipients of part 2 records
source_reference.source_text: Covered entities and business associates that receive part 2 records will also experience a reduced need to obtain written patient consent or a HIPAA authorization because redisclosure under the HIPAA Privacy Rule does not require patient consent or authorization for TPO and many other purposes.

examples: A health plan receives part 2 records with appropriate permission and rediscloses internally for payment and operations permitted under HIPAA without requesting a new patient authorization.

A covered entity receives part 2 records and shares them with its business associate for claims processing as a HIPAA TPO activity, without collecting a second authorization solely for that redisclosure.
A covered entity attempts to redisclose part 2 records for a non-TPO purpose that may require authorization; it must validate HIPAA requirements instead of relying on the general TPO statement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR enforcement action for improper uses/disclosures under HIPAA Privacy Rule

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

dependencies.rationale: Covered entities receiving Part 2 records may incorrectly treat them as freely redisclosable under HIPAA for TPO without honoring Part 2-specific limitations/notice requirements, creating a tension between HIPAA-permitted redisclosure and Part 2 confidentiality constraints.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If misinterpreted, 005_004 can drive redisclosures that violate 001_002’s Part 2 restriction principle, leading to unlawful disclosure risk and enforcement exposure.

dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_005_004 presumes the covered entity lawfully possesses Part 2 records and can then use HIPAA permissions for redisclosure. For records disclosed under an all-future TPO Part 2 consent, BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENTREQUIREMENT_23_009 requires specific statements that inform the patient and structure expectations about HIPAA redisclosure and Part 2 protections; invalid consent undermines the lawful acquisition premise.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the Part 2 consent lacks required statements, the upstream disclosure may be impermissible, potentially tainting downstream redisclosures and increasing compliance/enforcement exposure.

dependencies.rationale: A covered entity might infer from Rule 005_004 that additional consent/authorization is not needed for many redisclosures. Rule 19_005 explicitly requires Part 2 court order or separate Part 2 consent for proceeding-related uses/disclosures against the patient, overriding the general HIPAA redisclosure pathway when that specific condition is met.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the override is not recognized, a covered entity may wrongly disclose Part 2 records for proceedings against the patient based only on HIPAA permissions, creating high-risk Part 2 violations.

business_rules.rule_name: Privacy Rule Limits PHI Use Without Authorization
business_rules.description: Covered entities must apply appropriate safeguards to protect the privacy of PHI and must follow limits and conditions on uses and disclosures that may be made without an individual’s authorization. Individuals also have rights over PHI, including rights to examine and obtain a copy of health records, direct transmission of an electronic copy of PHI in an EHR to a third party, and request corrections.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the organization is a HIPAA covered entity using or disclosing PHI, especially in contexts where an authorization is not obtained (routine uses/disclosures).
business_rules.consequences: If violated: impermissible use/disclosure risk and potential OCR enforcement action. If met: privacy protections and individual rights are supported.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text (the excerpt notes uses/disclosures may occur without authorization only subject to limits/conditions).

source_reference.section_id: G. HIPAA Privacy and Security Rules (Privacy Rule description)
source_reference.source_text: The Privacy Rule requires appropriate safeguards to protect the privacy of PHI and sets limits and conditions on the uses and disclosures that may be made of such information without an individual’s authorization. The Privacy Rule also gives individuals rights over their protected health information, including rights to examine and obtain a copy of their health records, to direct a covered entity to transmit to a third party an electronic copy of their protected health information in an electronic health record, and to request corrections.

examples: A hospital discloses PHI to a vendor without meeting a permitted disclosure condition or obtaining authorization; this may violate Privacy Rule limits and conditions.
A patient requests an electronic copy of PHI maintained in an EHR to be transmitted to a third party; the covered entity must support this individual right as described.
A health plan implements safeguards (role-based access, policies) to protect PHI privacy consistent with Privacy Rule safeguard expectations.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR investigation and enforcement action (e.g., corrective action plan, civil monetary penalty)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Patients May Restrict TPO Disclosures and Self-Pay Plan Billing
business_rules.description: Patients have a right to request restrictions on uses and disclosures of their records for treatment, payment, and health care operations (TPO). Patients also have a specific right to restrict disclosures to their health plan when the patient has self-paid in full for services.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a patient requests a restriction on TPO uses/disclosures; the health plan restriction scenario applies when the patient has self-paid in full for services and requests restriction of disclosures to the health plan.
business_rules.consequences: If honored as required: increases patient privacy control and may prevent disclosures to a health plan in self-pay-in-full scenarios. If not implemented: risk of impermissible disclosure and patient harm (e.g., employer learning of diagnosis via plan activity as described in the preamble).
business_rules.exceptions: The excerpt notes a limitation: the health-plan restriction right is only available to patients with the means to pay privately in full for SUD treatment/services.

source_reference.section_id: Section 2.26 Right To Request Privacy Protection for Records
source_reference.source_text: Adding a new right for patients to request restrictions on uses and disclosures of their records for TPO... With respect to the right for patients to restrict disclosures to their health plan when patients have self-paid in full for services, patients will benefit by being shielded from potential harmful effects...

examples: A patient asks the Part 2 program to restrict sharing their SUD treatment records for health care operations with certain downstream entities; the program must support the restriction request process as described.

A patient pays in full out-of-pocket and requests that the program not disclose the service to the patient’s health plan; the program must implement the restriction for plan disclosures in this scenario.
A patient cannot pay in full and requests the plan restriction; the program may treat the self-pay-in-full restriction as unavailable per the limitation described.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Potential Part 2/HIPAA compliance finding and complaint exposure if restriction processes are not implemented

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

dependencies.rationale: Before honoring a restriction request made by someone other than the patient, the entity must determine if that person qualifies as the patient’s personal representative for relevant PHI.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If PR status is not assessed, the entity may improperly deny a valid representative or improperly accept restrictions from an unauthorized person.

business_rules.rule_name: Breach definition includes three explicit exceptions
