business_rules.description: An impermissible acquisition, access, use, or disclosure of PHI that compromises the security or privacy of unsecured PHI is a breach, subject to three exceptions: (1) unintentional acquisition/access/use by a workforce member or person acting under authority, in good faith and within scope of authority; (2) inadvertent disclosure by an authorized person to another authorized person at the same covered entity/business associate or organized health care arrangement; and (3) disclosure where the covered entity or business associate has a good faith belief the unauthorized person could not reasonably have retained the information.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a covered entity evaluates an incident involving acquisition/access/use/disclosure of unsecured PHI that is not permitted by the HIPAA Privacy Rule.
business_rules.consequences: If the incident fits one of the three exceptions: it is not treated as a breach under the cited definition. If it does not fit an exception: it is presumed to be a breach unless rebutted via the required low-probability risk assessment.
business_rules.exceptions: The three exceptions listed in the rule text are the exceptions.

source_reference.section_id: HIPAA Breach Notification Rule, 45 CFR 164.402(1)-(2) (breach definition and exceptions)
source_reference.source_text: A breach is the acquisition, access, use, or disclosure of PHI in a manner not permitted by the HIPAA Privacy Rule that compromises the security or privacy of “unsecured” PHI, subject to three exceptions: (1) the unintentional acquisition, access, or use of PHI by a workforce member... in good faith and within the scope of authority; (2) the inadvertent disclosure of PHI by a person authorized to access PHI... to another person authorized to access PHI...; and (3) the covered entity or business associate making the disclosure has a good faith belief that the unauthorized person... would not reasonably have been able to retain the information.

examples: A nurse mistakenly opens the wrong patient chart but immediately closes it; if in good faith and within scope, this can fall under the unintentional access exception.
A billing staff member sends PHI to the wrong coworker who is also authorized to access PHI; this may qualify as an inadvertent disclosure between authorized persons.

A fax containing PHI is sent to a recipient who immediately calls and confirms the fax was unreadable and destroyed; if the entity has a good faith belief the information could not reasonably be retained, the third exception may apply.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Breach presumption rebuttal requires four-factor assessment
business_rules.description: A covered entity may rebut the presumption that an impermissible use or disclosure of unsecured PHI is a breach by demonstrating there is a low probability that PHI has been compromised based on a risk assessment of at least four required factors: (1) nature and extent of PHI involved (including identifiers and likelihood of re-identification); (2) unauthorized person who used PHI or received the disclosure; (3) whether PHI was actually acquired or viewed; and (4) the extent to which risk to the PHI has been mitigated.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an impermissible use or disclosure of unsecured PHI has occurred and the covered entity seeks to determine whether it constitutes a reportable breach.
business_rules.consequences: If the four-factor risk assessment supports a low probability of compromise: the covered entity can rebut the breach presumption. If the covered entity cannot demonstrate low probability based on the required factors: the incident is treated as a breach and triggers applicable breach notification duties.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text beyond the ability to rebut presumption through the specified assessment.

source_reference.section_id: HIPAA Breach Notification Rule risk assessment (four factors) (45 CFR 164.402)
source_reference.source_text: The HIPAA Breach Notification Rule provides that a covered entity may rebut the presumption that such impermissible use or disclosure constituted a breach by demonstrating that there is a low probability that PHI has been compromised based on a risk assessment of at least four required factors: (1) the nature and extent of the PHI involved... (2) the unauthorized person...; (3) whether the PHI was actually acquired or viewed; and (4) the extent to which the risk to the PHI has been mitigated.

examples: A laptop with encrypted PHI is lost; the assessment finds PHI not actually acquired/viewed and strong mitigation (remote wipe), supporting low probability of compromise.
A spreadsheet with full identifiers is emailed to an external, unauthorized recipient; the assessment finds likely acquisition and limited mitigation, so it is treated as a breach.

A staff member accesses the wrong record but logs show no viewing of sensitive fields; mitigation includes immediate access revocation and auditing, supporting a low probability argument if justified under all four factors.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

dependencies.rationale: Rule 007_006 establishes breach presumption and exceptions; only if the incident is not excluded does 007_007 apply to rebut the presumption via required risk assessment factors.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 007_006 is not applied first, entities may incorrectly skip exceptions or apply risk assessments to non-breaches, causing under- or over-notification and enforcement risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Accounting must cover 3 years and EHR-based TPO only
business_rules.description: A Part 2 program must provide a patient, upon request, an accounting of all disclosures made with consent under § 2.31 in the 3 years prior to the date of the request (or a shorter period chosen by the patient). The accounting must meet the requirements of 45 CFR 164.528(a)(2) and (b) through (d). For treatment, payment, and health care operations (TPO), the program must provide an accounting only where such disclosures are made through an electronic health record, and the patient’s right is limited to only the 3 years prior to the request date.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the covered entity is acting as a Part 2 program and receives a patient request for an accounting of disclosures made with consent under 42 CFR 2.31, including TPO disclosures through an EHR.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: patients receive an accounting consistent with the 3-year lookback and applicable HIPAA accounting content requirements. If not complied with: the program risks Part 2 noncompliance (failure to provide accounting; providing incomplete accounting; providing accounting beyond required scope is not prohibited but must meet requirements if provided).
business_rules.exceptions: For TPO, accounting is required only for disclosures made through an electronic health record (EHR). Patient may choose a shorter accounting period than 3 years.

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR 2.25 Accounting of disclosures
source_reference.source_text: A part 2 program must provide to a patient, upon request, an accounting of all disclosures made with consent under § 2.31 in the 3 years prior to the date of the request (or a shorter time period chosen by the patient)... A part 2 program must provide a patient with an accounting of disclosures of records for treatment, payment, and health care operations only where such disclosures are made through an electronic health record.

examples: A patient requests an accounting covering the past 12 months; the Part 2 program provides disclosures made with consent during that period and formats it to meet 45 CFR 164.528 requirements.
A Part 2 program discloses records for payment through an EHR interface; upon request, those EHR-based disclosures must be included in the accounting for up to 3 years.
A Part 2 program makes TPO disclosures via paper/fax outside the EHR; under this rule, it must provide an accounting for TPO only where disclosures are made through an EHR.
business_rules.enforcement_action: federal investigation and corrective action plan

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

dependencies.rationale: Entities must treat a valid PR as the individual for relevant PHI, including fulfilling accounting-of-disclosures rights where applicable.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Failure to verify PR status can cause unauthorized disclosure of accounting data or improper denial, triggering complaints and enforcement risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Paid-in-full patients can restrict disclosure to health plans
business_rules.description: A Part 2 program must permit a patient to request restrictions on uses or disclosures for treatment, payment, or health care operations (TPO). The program is not required to agree to a requested restriction, except it must agree to restrict disclosures of records to a health plan if: (i) the disclosure is for payment or health care operations and is not otherwise required by law; and (ii) the record pertains solely to a health care item or service for which the patient (or someone other than the health plan on the patient’s behalf) has paid the program in full. If the program agrees to a restriction, it may not use/disclose in violation of that restriction, except it may use/disclose for emergency treatment when needed, and must request the emergency provider not further use/disclose the information.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the covered entity is acting as a Part 2 program and receives a patient request to restrict TPO disclosures, particularly restrictions on disclosure to a health plan for items/services paid in full.
business_rules.consequences: If the 'paid-in-full' conditions are met: the program must agree to restrict disclosure to the health plan for payment/operations. If the program agrees to any restriction: it must not violate that restriction except for emergency treatment needs. Noncompliance can create Part 2 violations and enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: Emergency treatment exception allows use/disclosure of restricted record (or information derived from it) when needed for emergency treatment. Restrictions do not prevent uses/disclosures required by law or permitted by Part 2 for purposes other than TPO.

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR 2.26 Right to request privacy protection for records
source_reference.source_text: A part 2 program must permit a patient to request that the part 2 program restrict uses or disclosures... for treatment, payment, or health care operations... a part 2 program is not required to agree to a restriction... A part 2 program must agree to the request of a patient to restrict disclosure of records about the patient to a health plan if: (i) The disclosure is for the purpose of carrying out payment or health care operations and is not otherwise required by law; and (ii) The record pertains solely to a health care item or service for which the patient... has paid the part 2 program in full.

examples: A patient pays cash in full for a counseling visit and requests the program not disclose that visit to their health plan for payment/operations; the program must agree if the disclosure is not otherwise required by law.

The program agrees to a general restriction on TPO disclosures; later the patient arrives unconscious and the restricted information is needed for emergency treatment—disclosure to the ED provider is permitted, and the program must request no further use/disclosure.
A patient requests a restriction but has not paid in full; the program may decline the restriction under the general rule (unless it voluntarily agrees).
business_rules.enforcement_action: federal investigation and corrective action plan

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

dependencies.rationale: HIPAA establishes restriction request rights (including self-pay-to-plan restriction); Part 2 program rule 007_009 mirrors and adds Part 2-specific conditions and enforcement expectations for agreed restrictions.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If only one standard is operationalized, the program may mishandle restriction requests (especially paid-in-full health plan restriction), leading to impermissible disclosures.

dependencies.rationale: A program must confirm legal authority before accepting and implementing restrictions requested by someone acting on the patient’s behalf.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If PR workflows are absent, restrictions may be improperly accepted/denied, leading to violations (e.g., disclosures to plans contrary to a valid restriction).

business_rules.rule_name: Patient Requested Transfer Must Be Written With Reasons
business_rules.description: When an unstabilized EMC transfer is requested by the individual (or legally responsible person), the request must be in writing, must indicate the reasons for the request, and must indicate awareness of the risks and benefits of the transfer after the individual is informed of the hospital’s obligations and transfer risk.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an unstabilized EMC transfer is based on the individual’s request under 42 CFR 489.24(e)(1)(ii)(A).
business_rules.consequences: If the written request includes reasons and acknowledges risks/benefits, the consent basis for transfer is satisfied (subject to appropriate transfer requirements). If missing/insufficient, transfer on this basis is noncompliant and increases EMTALA enforcement risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in this excerpt.

source_reference.source_text: (ii) (A) The individual (or a legally responsible person acting on the individual's behalf) requests the transfer, after being informed of the hospital's obligations under this section and of the risk of transfer. The request must be in writing and indicate the reasons for the request as well as indicate that he or she is aware of the risks and benefits of the transfer;

examples: Patient signs a transfer request form stating: 'I want transfer to Hospital B for neurosurgery not available here' and acknowledges explained risks/benefits; transfer proceeds if also appropriate.
Parent/guardian signs for a minor, documenting the reason for transfer and acknowledgment of transfer risks/benefits.
Patient verbally requests transfer but refuses written request; hospital must rely on physician/QMP certification pathway or continue stabilization rather than treating verbal request as sufficient.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA deficiency citation (Tag A-2409/C-2409)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Physician Must Certify Transfer Benefits Outweigh Risks
business_rules.description: For transfer of an individual with an unstabilized EMC, a physician must sign a certification that, based on information available at the time of transfer, the medical benefits reasonably expected from treatment at another facility outweigh the increased risks of being transferred. The certification must contain a summary of the risks and benefits upon which it is based.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the hospital transfers an individual with an unstabilized EMC based on clinician certification under 42 CFR 489.24(e)(1)(ii)(B), including for women in labor considering risk to woman or unborn child.
business_rules.consequences: If certification is completed with risks/benefits summary, the certification basis for transfer is satisfied (subject to appropriate transfer requirements). If missing/unsigned/without summary, transfer is noncompliant and exposes the hospital to EMTALA enforcement.
business_rules.exceptions: If a physician is not physically present at time of transfer, a qualified medical person may sign with later physician countersign under (e)(1)(ii)(C).

source_reference.source_text: (B) A physician... has signed a certification that, based upon the information available at the time of transfer, the medical benefits reasonably expected... outweigh the increased risks... from being transferred. The certification must contain a summary of the risks and benefits upon which it is based;

examples: Physician documents: benefit—immediate cath lab intervention; risk—arrhythmia during transport; certifies benefits outweigh risks and signs prior to transfer.
Laboring patient requires NICU; physician certification addresses risk to woman/unborn child and expected benefits at receiving facility.
Transfer occurs with a checkbox-only form lacking risk/benefit summary; this fails the certification content requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA deficiency citation (Tag A-2409/C-2409)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: QMP Transfer Certification Requires Physician Countersign
business_rules.description: If a physician is not physically present in the emergency department at the time of transfer, a qualified medical person (QMP) may sign the transfer certification only after a physician, in consultation with the QMP, agrees with the certification and subsequently countersigns it. The certification must contain a summary of risks and benefits.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an unstabilized EMC transfer occurs and the physician is not physically present in the ED at transfer time, and the hospital uses the QMP certification pathway under 42 CFR 489.24(e)(1)(ii)(C).
business_rules.consequences: If QMP certification and later physician countersign (with consultation) are completed, certification pathway is satisfied. If physician does not subsequently countersign or no evidence of consultation/agreement exists, transfer is noncompliant and exposes hospital to EMTALA enforcement risk.
business_rules.exceptions: Not applicable when a physician is physically present and signs certification under (e)(1)(ii)(B).

source_reference.source_text: (C) If a physician is not physically present in the emergency department at the time an individual is transferred, a qualified medical person... has signed a certification... after a physician... in consultation with the qualified medical person, agrees with the certification and subsequently countersigns the certification. The certification must contain a summary of the risks and benefits upon which it is based.

examples: Overnight transfer: PA (QMP per bylaws) signs certification after phone consult with ED physician; physician countersigns later per policy; record includes risk/benefit summary.
NP signs certification but physician never countersigns; this fails EMTALA certification requirements for the QMP pathway.
Physician is physically present and signs the certification directly; QMP pathway is not used.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA deficiency citation (Tag A-2409/C-2409)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

dependencies.rationale: 08_004 allows QMP signature with later physician countersign and consultation; 11_004 underpins the required consult process and governance via bylaws/board-approved documents.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If consult is not performed/documented, QMP certification pathway may fail, invalidating transfer justification and increasing EMTALA liability.

business_rules.rule_name: Receiving Facility Must Agree and Have Space and Staff
business_rules.description: A transfer to another medical facility is appropriate only if: (1) the transferring hospital provides medical treatment within its capacity that minimizes risks; and (2) the receiving facility has available space and qualified personnel and has agreed to accept the transfer and provide appropriate medical treatment.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any transfer of an individual with an unstabilized EMC when the hospital must ensure the transfer is an “appropriate transfer” under 42 CFR 489.24(e)(2).
business_rules.consequences: If receiving facility capacity and acceptance are confirmed and sending hospital provides treatment within capacity to minimize risks, this portion of the appropriate transfer standard is met. If the receiving facility has not agreed or lacks capacity, transfer is not appropriate and increases EMTALA enforcement risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in this excerpt.

source_reference.source_text: (2) A transfer to another medical facility will be appropriate only in those cases in which - (i) The transferring hospital provides medical treatment within its capacity that minimizes the risks...; (ii) The receiving facility -- (A) Has available space and qualified personnel...; and (B) Has agreed to accept transfer of the individual and to provide appropriate medical treatment;

examples: Community hospital administers thrombolytics within capacity before transfer; receiving stroke center confirms bed and accepts transfer.
Receiving hospital declines due to no ICU bed; sending hospital must not transfer under EMTALA until an accepting facility is secured (or stabilize).
Receiving facility agrees but sending hospital provides no pre-transfer treatment within its capacity (e.g., no oxygen/IV fluids when available); transfer may fail appropriate transfer requirements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA deficiency citation (Tag A-2409/C-2409)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

dependencies.rationale: 08_006 specifies the medical records that must accompany an appropriate transfer; it operationalizes 08_005’s requirement to ensure appropriate treatment continuity and risk minimization.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If records are not sent, transfer may be deemed inappropriate and patient safety is compromised due to missing clinical information.

dependencies.rationale: An 'appropriate transfer' includes safe execution; 08_007 defines transport staffing/equipment and life support measures needed to meet the standard implied by 08_005.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If transport is unsafe or lacks required life support, the transfer may be EMTALA-noncompliant and creates significant patient harm risk.

business_rules.rule_name: EMC Records Available Must Be Sent with Transfer
business_rules.description: For an appropriate transfer, the transferring hospital must send to the receiving facility all medical records (or copies) related to the emergency condition that are available at the time of transfer, including history, EMC-related records, observations of signs/symptoms, preliminary diagnosis, diagnostic study results (or telephone reports), treatment provided, test results, informed written consent or required certification (or copy), and the name/address of any on-call physician who refused or failed to appear within a reasonable time. Other records not yet available must be sent as soon as practicable after transfer.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any EMTALA appropriate transfer to another medical facility under 42 CFR 489.24(e)(2)(iii).
business_rules.consequences: If the required records are sent at time of transfer and remaining records are forwarded as soon as practicable, documentation transfer requirement is met. If records are not sent, transfer may be deemed inappropriate and creates EMTALA enforcement exposure and patient safety risk due to incomplete clinical information.
business_rules.exceptions: Other records not readily available or test results not yet available may be sent after transfer, but must be sent as soon as practicable.

source_reference.source_text: (iii) The transferring hospital sends to the receiving facility all medical records... related to the emergency condition... available at the time of the transfer, including available history... observations... preliminary diagnosis, results of diagnostic studies or telephone reports... treatment provided, results of any tests and the informed written consent or certification... and the name and address of any on-call physician... who has refused or failed to appear... Other records... must be sent as soon as practicable after transfer;

examples: At transfer, ED sends face sheet, triage note, labs, CT report (or phone report), meds administered, and physician certification; pending culture results are faxed the next day when resulted.
Sending hospital fails to include the physician certification and diagnostic imaging report; receiving facility lacks key information—transfer documentation requirement not met.
Imaging is performed but final radiology read is pending; a telephone report is sent at transfer, then final report is sent as soon as practicable.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA deficiency citation (Tag A-2409/C-2409)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Transfer Must Use Qualified Transport and Life Support
business_rules.description: For an appropriate transfer, the transfer must be effected through qualified personnel and transportation equipment, as required, including the use of necessary and medically appropriate life support measures during the transfer.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the hospital transfers an individual with an unstabilized EMC to another medical facility under EMTALA and must meet the appropriate transfer requirements.
business_rules.consequences: If qualified personnel/equipment and needed life support measures are used, transfer safety requirement is satisfied. If transfer occurs without appropriate medical transport capability (e.g., no required life support), the hospital may violate EMTALA appropriate transfer requirements and place the patient at significant risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in this excerpt.

source_reference.source_text: (iv) The transfer is effected through qualified personnel and transportation equipment, as required, including the use of necessary and medically appropriate life support measures during the transfer.

examples: Ventilated patient is transferred via ALS ambulance with respiratory support and continuous monitoring ordered by physician.
Cardiac patient at risk of arrhythmia is transferred without cardiac monitoring despite being required; transfer fails the life support measures requirement.
Neonatal transfer uses a specialized neonatal transport team and equipment appropriate to the clinical condition.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA deficiency citation (Tag A-2409/C-2409)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Transfer Refusal Must Be Documented With Risks and Reasons
business_rules.description: When an individual (or person acting on their behalf) does not consent to an offered transfer, the hospital must take all reasonable steps to secure the individual’s written informed refusal. The written document must indicate the person was informed of risks and benefits of the transfer and state the reasons for refusal. The medical record must contain a description of the proposed transfer that was refused. If the individual/representative refuses transfer and refuses to sign a statement, hospital may document such refusals as they see fit.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a hospital offers transfer in accordance with EMTALA transfer requirements and the individual (or representative) refuses to consent to transfer.
business_rules.consequences: If written informed refusal (or alternative documentation when signature refused) is placed in the medical record with risks/benefits and refusal reasons, documentation obligations are met. If not documented, the hospital risks EMTALA documentation deficiency and inability to demonstrate compliance during CMS review (Tag A-2409/C-2409).
business_rules.exceptions: If the individual/representative refuses to sign a refusal statement, the hospital may document refusals as it sees fit (signature not required when impossible).

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR 489.24(d)(5) and Interpretive Guidelines
source_reference.source_text: The hospital must take all reasonable steps to secure the individual's written informed refusal... The written document must indicate the person has been informed of the risks and benefits of the transfer and state the reasons for the individual's refusal. The medical record must contain a description of the proposed transfer... If an individual... refuses to be transferred and also refuses to sign a statement... the hospital may document such refusals as they see fit.

examples: ED offers transfer to trauma center; patient declines; nurse obtains signed refusal form listing risks (bleeding/death) and patient’s reason (does not want to travel).
Patient’s legally responsible representative refuses transfer and signs refusal; record includes the proposed receiving facility and reason for refusal.
Patient refuses transfer and refuses to sign; clinician documents in the medical record that risks/benefits were explained and patient refused both transfer and signature.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA deficiency citation (Tag A-2409/C-2409)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

dependencies.rationale: When refusal involves transfer, 08_008 operationalizes 18_006 with required elements (risks/benefits, reasons for refusal, description of proposed transfer, and alternative documentation if signature refused).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If refusal documentation is incomplete, the hospital may be unable to demonstrate EMTALA compliance and may receive survey deficiencies and enforcement actions.

business_rules.rule_name: EMC patients must be stabilized or transferred
business_rules.description: When an individual has an emergency medical condition, the hospital must provide examination and treatment to stabilize the individual within its capacity and capability and continue providing care until the condition ceases to be an emergency or the individual is properly transferred under EMTALA; inappropriate discharge or transfer of an EMC patient is an EMTALA violation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a hospital (covered entity) has an individual present to the hospital and the individual has an EMC and the hospital has capacity/capability to stabilize or must arrange an appropriate transfer.
business_rules.consequences: If the hospital stabilizes the EMC or properly transfers the patient, EMTALA stabilization obligations are met for this encounter. If the hospital discharges or transfers without stabilization and without an appropriate transfer, the hospital may be found in violation of EMTALA during a CMS investigation/survey.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt. (Other EMTALA exceptions/definitions may apply outside this excerpt.)

source_reference.section_id: §489.24(d) Necessary Stabilizing Treatment
source_reference.source_text: Hospitals are responsible for treating and stabilizing, within their capacity and capability, any individual who presents him/herself to a hospital with an EMC. The hospital must provide care until the condition ceases to be an emergency or until the individual is properly transferred to another facility.

source_reference.section_id: §489.24(d) Necessary Stabilizing Treatment
source_reference.source_text: The hospital must provide care until the condition ceases to be an emergency or until the individual is properly transferred to another facility. An inappropriate transfer or discharge of an individual with an EMC would be a violation of EMTALA.

examples: Hospital provides stabilizing treatment and admits or appropriately transfers if stabilization is beyond capability.
Hospital discharges an EMC patient without stabilization or an appropriate transfer, creating EMTALA violation risk.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA investigation; potential EMTALA violation determination

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules state the same EMTALA stabilization duty framing: hospitals must treat/stabilize within capability and continue care until the EMC is no longer an emergency or the patient is properly transferred; inappropriate discharge/transfer violates EMTALA.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_11_001 selected: slightly more operational detail (within capacity/capability; continue until emergency ceases or appropriate transfer).

business_rules.rule_name: Transferring hospital bears proof if claims stability
business_rules.description: If a hospital is alleged to have violated EMTALA by transferring an unstable individual without an appropriate transfer, and the hospital asserts the patient was stable (EMC resolved), the burden of proof rests with the transferring hospital. Surveyors should assess whether it was reasonable to believe the transferring hospital should have known potential complications during transport, and may request QIO physician review to determine stability and appropriateness of treatment.
business_rules.conditions: Applies during an EMTALA investigation where there is an allegation of improper transfer of an unstable individual and the transferring hospital disputes instability by asserting EMC was resolved/stable.
business_rules.consequences: Hospital must produce evidence supporting stability determination; lack of persuasive evidence increases risk of EMTALA violation finding. QIO physician review may be requested to evaluate stability and appropriateness.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: §489.24(d) Interpretive Guidelines (burden of proof/QIO review)
source_reference.source_text: If a hospital is alleged to have violated EMTALA by transferring an unstable individual without implementing an appropriate transfer according to §489.24(e), and the hospital believes that the individual was stable (EMC resolved) the burden of proof is the responsibility of the transferring hospital... surveyors will request that the QIO physician review the case.

examples: A facility transfers a patient with ongoing respiratory distress and later claims stability; it must provide documentation supporting why stability was reasonably determined at transfer time.
During survey, the hospital cannot show stability assessments or transport risk evaluation; the case is more likely to be cited as a violation.
A hospital documents repeat vitals, symptom resolution, and transport risk considerations; QIO review supports stability, reducing violation risk.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA investigation; evidence burden on transferring hospital

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

dependencies.rationale: When the hospital asserts the patient was stable, surveyors assess reasonableness; transfer certification and its risk/benefit summary are key evidence for stability/appropriateness determinations.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If certification documentation is weak/missing, the hospital’s ability to meet its burden of proof is reduced, increasing likelihood of an EMTALA violation finding.

dependencies.rationale: Surveyors may request records supporting clinical status and transfer appropriateness; missing transfer packets and EMC-related records weaken the hospital’s evidentiary showing.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If documentation is incomplete, the hospital may be unable to demonstrate that it was reasonable to believe no material deterioration would occur during transfer.

business_rules.rule_name: QMP must consult physician when physician absent
business_rules.description: If a physician is not physically present at the time of transfer, qualified medical personnel (as defined by hospital bylaws or other board-approved documents) must consult with a physician to determine whether an individual with an EMC is to be transferred for further stabilizing treatment.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an EMC patient is being transferred and the physician is not physically present at the time transfer decision is made/executed; qualified medical personnel are acting under bylaws/board-approved documents.
business_rules.consequences: Failure to consult with a physician before transferring an EMC patient when required may contribute to an EMTALA violation finding related to inappropriate transfer decision-making.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: §489.24(d) Interpretive Guidelines (physician consultation)
source_reference.source_text: If a physician is not physically present at the time of transfer, then the qualified medical personnel (as determined by hospital bylaws or other board-approved documents) must consult with a physician to determine if an individual with an EMC is to be transferred to another facility for further stabilizing treatment.

examples: An ED PA initiates transfer for a stroke patient; the PA must consult with the on-call physician before transfer if the physician is not physically present.
A nurse coordinator arranges transfer for an unstable patient without physician consultation despite bylaws requiring it; this is a compliance risk.
A QMP consults the ED physician by phone, documents the consult, and proceeds with transfer for further stabilization.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA investigation; potential transfer-process deficiency

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Good faith inpatient admission ends EMTALA duty
business_rules.description: If a hospital screens an individual, finds an EMC, and admits the individual as an inpatient in good faith to stabilize the EMC, the hospital satisfies its special EMTALA responsibilities for that individual. A patient is considered admitted when the decision is made to admit for inpatient services with the expectation the patient will remain at least overnight, typically documented when a physician signs and dates the admission order. If there is a question whether admission was used to avoid EMTALA, surveyors consult RO personnel and the case may be expanded to hospital CoPs and forwarded for violation determination; admitting solely to avoid EMTALA can result in EMTALA liability and enforcement action.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (1) the patient was screened under EMTALA, (2) an EMC is identified, and (3) the hospital admits the patient as an inpatient in good faith for stabilizing treatment; includes compliance scrutiny where admission motive is questioned.
business_rules.consequences: When good faith inpatient admission occurs, EMTALA special responsibilities end (while CoPs still apply). If admission is determined to be solely to avoid EMTALA obligations, hospital remains liable under EMTALA and may be subject to further enforcement action; survey may be expanded to CoPs and referred to RO.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt. (CoPs obligations continue regardless.)

source_reference.section_id: §489.24(d)(2)(i) Exception: Application to Inpatients
source_reference.source_text: If a hospital has screened an individual... found the individual to have an emergency medical condition, and admits that individual as an inpatient in good faith in order to stabilize the emergency medical condition, the hospital has satisfied its special responsibilities... An individual is considered to be “admitted” when the decision is made... with the expectation that the patient will remain in the hospital at least overnight... documented... when a physician signed and dated the admission order.

examples: A patient with an EMC is admitted with an inpatient order signed/dated by the physician, and the plan is overnight care for stabilization; EMTALA special responsibilities end, though CoPs still govern ongoing care and discharge.

A hospital places an inpatient admission order with no intent to provide stabilizing treatment and quickly transfers to avoid EMTALA transfer requirements; this may be treated as an EMTALA avoidance admission with enforcement risk.
During investigation, surveyors suspect avoidance admission and consult RO personnel to expand to CoP survey and forward the case for violation determination.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA violation determination; potential CoP survey expansion and enforcement referral

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Covered entities are excluded from intermediary status
business_rules.description: For 42 CFR part 2 purposes, covered entities are excluded from the definition of “intermediary.” As a result, an entity that is a covered entity is not subject to intermediary-specific obligations (e.g., part 2 intermediary requirements such as list-of-disclosures obligations described in the rulemaking discussion).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when evaluating whether a covered entity (e.g., a hospital/health system) is subject to part 2 “intermediary” requirements for exchange of part 2 records received under a general designation in a written patient consent.
business_rules.consequences: If the organization is a covered entity, it should not be treated as an intermediary for purposes of applying intermediary-specific obligations; misclassification may lead to incorrect compliance program controls and operational burdens.
business_rules.exceptions: Exclusion described also applies to business associates and part 2 programs per the same discussion; intermediary obligations may apply only to organizations that meet the intermediary definition (other than a program, covered entity, or business associate).

source_reference.section_id: Discussion of “intermediary” definition and exclusions
source_reference.source_text: As noted, in response to public comments we are excluding covered entities, business associates, and part 2 programs from the definition of “intermediary.”... Additionally... we have carved out business associates from the definition of “intermediary.” Thus, while a QSO may be a business associate, it cannot at the same time also be considered an intermediary.

examples: A hospital that is a covered entity participates in an HIE and receives part 2 records as PHI; it should not be classified as a part 2 “intermediary” based solely on its exchange role.

A health plan (covered entity) aggregates part 2-related claims data; it is excluded from intermediary status under the final rule discussion and should not implement intermediary-specific list-of-disclosures workflows.

An organization that is not a covered entity, not a business associate, and not a part 2 program may still be an intermediary if it meets the definition; this rule does not exempt such an organization.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Compliance misclassification risk; potential part 2 noncompliance or unnecessary operational burden

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: EMTALA Medical Screening Exam Required for Anyone Who Comes to the ED
business_rules.description: Hospitals with a dedicated emergency department must provide an appropriate medical screening examination (MSE), within the capability of the emergency department (including routinely available ancillary services), to any individual who comes to the ED and requests examination/treatment (or a request is made on the individual’s behalf), or when a prudent layperson would conclude the individual needs examination or treatment for a medical condition; EMTALA applies regardless of Medicare eligibility or ability to pay.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when: (1) the hospital has a dedicated emergency department; and (2) an individual comes to the ED and requests examination/treatment, or a request is made on the individual’s behalf, or a prudent layperson observer would conclude from appearance/behavior that the individual needs examination or treatment for a medical condition.
business_rules.consequences: If met: EMTALA screening obligation satisfied (subject to stabilizing treatment/appropriate transfer obligations not fully quoted here). If violated: EMTALA noncompliance risk, CMS EMTALA findings, and potential enforcement (including civil monetary penalties and potential termination from Medicare provider agreement depending on severity).
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in this excerpt for the ED presentation scenario; additional EMTALA nuances may exist in full § 489.24 and interpretive guidance.

source_reference.section_id: 42 C.F.R. § 489.24(a)(1)(i) and Interpretive Guidelines
source_reference.source_text: if an individual... 'comes to the emergency department'... the hospital must — (i) Provide an appropriate medical screening examination within the capability of the hospital’s emergency department, including ancillary services routinely available to the emergency department, to determine whether or not an emergency medical condition exists. The examination must be conducted by an individual(s) who is determined qualified by hospital bylaws...

source_reference.section_id: I. General Information — EMTALA screening obligation
source_reference.source_text: EMTALA requires hospitals with emergency departments to provide a medical screening examination to any individual who comes to the emergency department and requests such an examination, and prohibits hospitals with emergency departments from refusing to examine or treat individuals with an emergency medical condition (EMC). The provisions of EMTALA apply to all individuals (not just Medicare beneficiaries) who attempt to gain access to a hospital for emergency care.

source_reference.section_id: Tag A-2400/C-2400, §489.20(l) Interpretive Guidelines
source_reference.source_text: Under the provisions of §489.24, hospitals with an emergency department that participate in Medicare are required under EMTALA to do the following: • Provide an appropriate MSE to any individual who comes to the emergency department;

examples: A bystander requests help for a collapsed person at the ED entrance; the hospital must provide an MSE.
A patient arrives with chest pain; the hospital must screen even if uninsured.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA enforcement; potential civil monetary penalty and Medicare participation risk

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

deduplication_info.rationale: All rules impose EMTALA’s screening duty: provide an appropriate MSE to individuals who come to the ED seeking emergency evaluation, regardless of Medicare status or ability to pay. Differences are only additional clarifying triggers in the primary.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_12_004 selected: most specific trigger logic (request made on behalf, prudent layperson standard, ancillary services routinely available).

dependencies.rationale: When an individual presents on hospital property (not ED) with request/signs meeting prudent layperson standard, EMTALA screening obligations apply, leading to MSE requirements.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the trigger is not recognized, the hospital may fail to screen/stabilize individuals outside the ED, creating EMTALA violations.

business_rules.rule_name: EMTALA Duty May Trigger Anywhere on Hospital Property Within 250 Yards
business_rules.description: If an individual who is not a hospital patient comes elsewhere on hospital property (i.e., not to the dedicated emergency department), the hospital’s EMTALA obligation may be triggered if the individual requests examination or treatment for an emergency medical condition or if a prudent layperson observer would believe the individual is suffering from an emergency medical condition. 'Hospital property' includes the entire main hospital campus as defined in § 413.65(a), including parking lot, sidewalk, driveway, or hospital departments, including any building owned by the hospital within 250 yards of the hospital.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to hospitals with dedicated EDs when individuals present on-campus but outside the ED (e.g., parking lot, hospital-owned building within 250 yards) and either request emergency evaluation or have signs/symptoms that would lead a prudent layperson to believe an emergency medical condition may exist.
business_rules.consequences: If met: hospital must respond consistent with EMTALA obligations for screening/stabilization/transfer as applicable. If violated: risk of EMTALA noncompliance findings and related CMS enforcement actions.
business_rules.exceptions: This excerpt later notes distinct treatment for certain registered outpatients receiving a scheduled course of outpatient care; that outpatient nuance is not included as a rule in this batch and should be evaluated in full EMTALA guidance when applicable.

source_reference.section_id: Interpretive Guidelines § 489.24(a)(1)(i) (hospital property definition and trigger)
source_reference.source_text: exits. If an individual who is not a hospital patient comes elsewhere on hospital property (that is, the individual comes to the hospital but not to the dedicated emergency department), an EMTALA obligation on the part of the hospital may be triggered if either the individual

examples: A visitor collapses in the hospital parking lot; because the parking lot is hospital property, the hospital’s EMTALA response obligations may be triggered.

A person appears severely short of breath in a hospital-owned clinic building located 200 yards from the main hospital; this location is within the 250-yard definition and may trigger EMTALA obligations based on prudent layperson standard.

A person with minor symptoms asks for directions in a building 500 yards away; the 250-yard definition in this excerpt would not include that building as 'hospital property' for this purpose (other legal duties may still apply).
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA enforcement; potential civil monetary penalty and Medicare participation risk

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: On-Campus Movement Is Not a Transfer Only If 3 Conditions Are Met
business_rules.description: When an individual is initially screened on-campus outside the ED (or presents to the ED) and is moved to another hospital department or facility on-campus for further screening or stabilizing treatment, the movement is not regarded as an EMTALA transfer only if all three conditions are met: (1) all persons with the same medical condition are moved in such circumstances regardless of ability to pay; (2) there is a bona fide medical reason to move the individual; and (3) appropriate medical personnel accompany the individual.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to movement of individuals between departments/facilities of the same hospital on the same campus for additional screening or stabilizing treatment under EMTALA interpretive guidelines.
business_rules.consequences: If met: movement is treated as intra-hospital movement (not an EMTALA transfer), reducing transfer paperwork/requirements, while maintaining EMTALA duties. If violated: the movement may be treated as an inappropriate transfer/disparate treatment based on ability to pay, increasing EMTALA enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated beyond the requirement that all three conditions be satisfied to avoid the movement being regarded as a transfer for EMTALA purposes.

source_reference.section_id: Interpretive Guidelines § 489.24(a)(1)(i) (movement on campus not a transfer)
source_reference.source_text: If an individual is initially screened... the individual could be moved to another hospital department or facility on-campus... without such movement being regarded as a transfer, as long as: (1) all persons with the same medical condition are moved... regardless of their ability to pay...; (2) there is bona fide medical reason to move the individual; and (3) appropriate medical personnel accompany the individual.

examples: All eye-injury patients needing stationary equipment are moved from the ED to the on-campus eye clinic with an RN escort; this meets the 3 conditions and is not an EMTALA transfer.
Only uninsured patients are moved from the ED to another unit for screening while insured patients remain in the ED; this violates condition (1) and creates EMTALA risk.
A medically unstable patient is moved on-campus without appropriate medical personnel escort; this violates condition (3) even if the move had a bona fide medical reason.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA enforcement; potential civil monetary penalty and Medicare participation risk

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

dependencies.rationale: 12_006 requires the patient be initially screened (or present to ED) and then moved on-campus for further screening/stabilization; thus 12_004’s MSE context is antecedent.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the initial screening context is missing, on-campus movement may be treated as an EMTALA transfer or discriminatory movement based on ability to pay, increasing enforcement risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 Single TPO Consent Allows Future Uses Until Written Revocation
business_rules.description: When a patient provides a single consent for all future uses and disclosures of 42 CFR part 2 records for treatment, payment, and health care operations (TPO), a part 2 program, covered entity, or business associate may use and disclose those records for TPO as permitted by HIPAA regulations until the patient revokes the consent in writing.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when: (1) the patient consent is consistent with § 2.31; and (2) the consent is structured as a 'single consent for all future uses and disclosures' for TPO; and (3) the use/disclosure is for TPO as permitted by HIPAA regulations.
business_rules.consequences: If met: entity may rely on the single TPO consent for ongoing TPO uses/disclosures without collecting a new consent for each routine TPO disclosure. If violated: TPO uses/disclosures without valid consent may constitute unauthorized use/disclosure under 42 CFR part 2.
business_rules.exceptions: Use/disclosure authority ends when the patient revokes the consent in writing. Additional part 2 limits may apply in specific contexts (e.g., criminal, civil, administrative, and legislative proceedings) not fully detailed for all scenarios in this excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: 42 C.F.R. § 2.33(a)(2) (single consent for future TPO)
source_reference.source_text: When the consent provided is a single consent for all future uses and disclosures for treatment, payment, and health care operations, a part 2 program, covered entity, or business associate may use and disclose those records for treatment, payment, and health care operations as permitted by the HIPAA regulations, until such time as the patient revokes such consent in writing.

examples: A patient signs a one-time TPO consent at intake; the program may later disclose part 2 records to a covered entity for payment activities without obtaining a new consent each time, until written revocation.
A patient emails a written revocation; after receipt, the program must stop relying on the single TPO consent for future disclosures.
A clinic attempts to treat a broad HIPAA authorization as the required single TPO consent; this is not compliant unless the consent meets § 2.31 requirements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: HHS Part 2 enforcement action; potential civil/criminal exposure depending on statute and facts

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Hospital must stabilize an emergency medical condition within capability
business_rules.description: If an individual comes to a hospital and the hospital determines the individual has an emergency medical condition, the hospital must provide, within the capabilities of the staff and facilities available, further medical examination and treatment as required to stabilize the medical condition. Stabilized means no material deterioration is likely, within reasonable medical probability, to result from or occur during transfer (and for labor, that delivery of the child and placenta has occurred).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to hospitals when an individual presents and, after medical screening, an emergency medical condition is determined to exist. Applies regardless of Medicare eligibility or payment considerations.
business_rules.consequences: If the hospital fails to provide stabilizing treatment within its capability and capacity after determining an emergency medical condition exists, it is out of compliance with EMTALA stabilization requirements. If the hospital cannot stabilize within its capability, it should implement an appropriate transfer as described in the guidance.
business_rules.exceptions: Subject to provisions of paragraph (d)(2) of the cited regulation (not included in the excerpt). Patient-requested transfer may occur before exhausting all resources if the individual requests transfer and refuses treatment at the sending hospital (as described in the excerpt).

source_reference.section_id: § 489.24(d)(1) General and stabilization definition excerpt
source_reference.source_text: if any individual (whether or not eligible for Medicare benefits) comes to a hospital and the hospital determines that the individual has an emergency medical condition, the hospital must provide either-- (i) Within the capabilities of the staff and facilities available at the hospital, for further medical examination and treatment as required to stabilize the medical condition... defines stabilized to mean: “… that no material deterioration of the condition is likely, within reasonable medical probability, to result from or occur during the transfer...”

examples: An uninsured patient presents with acute asthma exacerbation; after an EMC is determined, the ED provides oxygen and bronchodilators to stabilize within the hospital’s capability regardless of payment.

A patient presents with chest pain and is determined to have an EMC; the hospital initiates stabilizing treatment and only transfers if it cannot stabilize within capability and an appropriate transfer is arranged.

A patient requests transfer to another hospital and refuses treatment at the sending hospital; transfer may occur before exhausting all resources consistent with the excerpt’s patient-requested transfer discussion.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS EMTALA survey deficiency citation

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

dependencies.rationale: The MSE determines whether an EMC exists; once identified, 013_007 defines stabilization and requires stabilizing treatment within capability.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If MSE-to-stabilization sequencing is broken, the hospital may fail to treat EMCs appropriately or misclassify stability, increasing risk of improper transfer/discharge.

business_rules.rule_name: Legal personal representatives must be treated as patient
business_rules.description: A covered entity must treat a person with legal authority to act on behalf of an adult or emancipated minor in making decisions related to health care as the individual’s personal representative, but only with respect to PHI that is relevant to that personal representation (HIPAA Privacy Rule 45 CFR 164.502(g)(2), referenced as mirrored by the Part 2 final rule discussion).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a person presents legal authority to act on behalf of an adult or emancipated minor for health care decision-making and requests access to, or otherwise acts regarding, PHI relevant to that representation.
business_rules.consequences: If conditions are met: covered entity must treat the person as the individual for relevant PHI (e.g., provide access, permit disclosures). If violated: improper denial of access or improper handling of representative rights may result in OCR complaint, investigation, corrective action, and potential civil money penalties.
business_rules.exceptions: Does not extend beyond PHI relevant to the personal representation; the excerpt does not enumerate additional exceptions.

source_reference.section_id: Section 2.15—Patients Who Lack Capacity and Deceased Patients (Response; HIPAA personal representative)
source_reference.source_text: Section 164.502(g)(2) requires a covered entity to treat a person with legal authority to act on behalf of an adult or emancipated minor in making decisions related to health care as the individual’s personal representative with respect to PHI relevant to such personal representation. The definition in this rule mirrors language in the HIPAA Privacy Rule at 45 CFR 164.502(g).

examples: A hospital receives guardianship papers for an incapacitated adult; the guardian requests discharge instructions and medication list. The hospital treats the guardian as the patient for that PHI.
A parent claims authority for a 19-year-old patient but cannot show legal authority; the covered entity does not treat the parent as the personal representative until documentation is provided.
A court-appointed health care proxy requests psychotherapy notes unrelated to the proxy’s authorized decisions; the covered entity limits access to PHI relevant to the representation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR investigation and potential civil monetary penalty

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Deceased SUD disclosures require personal representative consent
business_rules.description: Disclosures of patient identifying information relating to the cause of death of a patient are not restricted when made under laws requiring collection of death or other vital statistics or permitting inquiry into cause of death. Any other use or disclosure identifying a deceased patient as having a substance use disorder (SUD) remains subject to Part 2 requirements; where written consent is required, the personal representative may provide that consent.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when information identifies a deceased patient as having an SUD and the entity is considering use/disclosure. Determine whether disclosure is limited to cause-of-death reporting under vital statistics laws, versus other disclosures (e.g., to family, insurers, attorneys).
business_rules.consequences: If conditions are met: for vital statistics/cause-of-death disclosures, Part 2 does not restrict. For other disclosures: must comply with Part 2; if written consent required, obtain from personal representative before disclosure. If violated: unauthorized disclosure may trigger enforcement under applicable Part 2/HIPAA-aligned enforcement processes and potential penalties.
business_rules.exceptions: Cause-of-death disclosures for vital statistics collection or permitted inquiry into cause of death are not restricted by Part 2, per the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Section 2.15—Patients Who Lack Capacity and Deceased Patients (Response; deceased patients)
source_reference.source_text: With respect to deceased patients, part 2 regulations as finalized “do not restrict the disclosure of patient identifying information relating to the cause of death of a patient under laws requiring the collection of death or other vital statistics or permitting inquiry into the cause of death.” Additionally, the regulations state that “[a]ny other use or disclosure of information identifying a deceased patient as having a substance use disorder is subject to the regulations in this part. If a written consent to the use or disclosure is required, that consent may be given by the personal representative.”

examples: A state vital records office requests cause-of-death details including SUD-related information; the program discloses as permitted for vital statistics without Part 2 restriction.
A deceased patient’s adult child requests the full SUD treatment record; the program requires written consent from the personal representative before any Part 2-regulated disclosure.

An attorney seeks SUD treatment records for a probate dispute; absent an applicable exception, the program treats this as an 'other disclosure' and requires Part 2-compliant authorization/consent (e.g., via personal representative if required).
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR/Part 2 enforcement action; potential civil monetary penalty

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

dependencies.rationale: To obtain valid consent for disclosures identifying a deceased patient as having SUD (outside vital statistics/cause-of-death reporting), the entity must confirm the requester is the legally authorized personal representative.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If PR status is not established, disclosures may proceed without valid consent or be improperly blocked, creating compliance and patient/family harm.

business_rules.rule_name: Nursing Facility Discharge Requires CMS Criteria and 30-Day Notice
business_rules.description: A nursing facility may only transfer or discharge a resident if at least one permissible justification can be demonstrated by the facility: (1) necessary for the resident’s welfare and needs cannot be met at the facility; (2) resident’s health has improved sufficiently so resident no longer needs facility services; (3) safety of individuals in the facility is endangered due to the resident’s clinical/behavioral status; (4) health of individuals in the facility would otherwise be endangered; (5) resident fails, after reasonable and appropriate notice, to pay (or to have paid under Medicare/Medicaid) for the stay (subject to caveats); or (6) facility ceases to operate. CMS regulations also require the facility to provide and document sufficient preparation and orientation to ensure a safe and orderly transfer/discharge, and to send all facility-initiated discharge notices to the resident and a representative of the State ombudsman at least 30 days in advance of discharge.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to facility-initiated transfers/discharges of residents from a nursing facility subject to Requirements of Participation (ROPs), including when behavioral/mental health issues are involved and the facility is initiating discharge.
business_rules.consequences: If the facility cannot demonstrate one of the permissible justifications, the transfer/discharge is inappropriate and risks violating federal regulations. Failure to provide/document preparation and orientation, or failure to provide at least 30 days advance notice to the resident and State ombudsman representative, risks noncompliance findings during surveys and undermines resident safety and due process.
business_rules.exceptions: The excerpt does not specify exceptions to the 30-day notice requirement; it references that the nonpayment justification is subject to certain caveats and that State requirements may also apply.

source_reference.section_id: Facility-Initiated Discharges (42 C.F.R. § 483.15(c)) and 30-day notice requirement
source_reference.source_text: Under the ROPs, nursing facilities may only transfer or discharge a resident if one of the following justifications can be demonstrated by the facility: ... The resident has failed, after reasonable and appropriate notice, to pay for ... a stay at the facility ... The facility ceases to operate. ... CMS regulations require nursing facilities to provide and document sufficient preparation and orientation to residents to ensure a safe and orderly transfer or discharge from the facility. CMS regulations also require nursing facilities to send all facility-initiated discharge notices to a resident and a representative of the State ombudsman at least 30 days in advance of a discharge.

examples: A facility discharges a resident due to aggressive behavior; it must document that the safety of individuals is endangered due to the resident’s clinical/behavioral status and provide documented preparation/orientation for a safe transfer.

A facility initiates discharge because it cannot meet a resident’s complex needs; it must demonstrate that the transfer is necessary for the resident’s welfare and needs cannot be met, and must send notices to the resident and State ombudsman representative at least 30 days before discharge.

A facility initiates discharge for nonpayment; it must show reasonable and appropriate notice was provided and still must meet notice and documentation obligations (subject to any applicable caveats).
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey deficiency citation

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: HIPAA authorization must specifically describe Part 2 records
business_rules.description: A covered entity that receives Part 2 records but is not operating a Part 2 program does not need to create or use a Part 2 consent to disclose those received Part 2 records. Instead, it may use a HIPAA authorization to disclose Part 2 records it receives, provided the authorization is not a general release of medical or other information. The authorization must be specific to Part 2 records or records of SUD treatment and must identify the information in a specific and meaningful fashion per § 2.31.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a covered entity that is not a Part 2 program seeks to redisclose Part 2 records it received (e.g., onward disclosure to another provider, plan operations, or other permitted purpose requiring authorization).
business_rules.consequences: If the covered entity uses a broad ‘all my medical records’ authorization, it fails the specificity requirement for Part 2 records and the disclosure may be noncompliant (Part 2/HIPAA misalignment risk). If it uses an authorization explicitly referencing SUD/Part 2 records and meaningfully describing the information, it can support the disclosure under the described pathway.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Covered entities receiving Part 2 records; authorization specificity
source_reference.source_text: Covered entities that are recipients of part 2 records but are not operating a part 2 program do not need to create or use a part 2 consent. Instead, covered entities that are not part 2 programs may use a HIPAA authorization to disclose part 2 records they receive provided that the authorization is not for the release of medical or other information generally. The authorization form must be specific to part 2 records or records of SUD treatment rather than "my medical records," so that it identifies the information in a specific and meaningful fashion according to § 2.31.

examples: A hospital (not a Part 2 program) receives Part 2 records and uses a patient-signed ‘release all records’ form to send them to an employer; this fails the Part 2 specificity requirement described.
A payer uses an authorization stating ‘SUD treatment records subject to 42 CFR Part 2 from [program name] for dates [x–y]’; this meets the specificity requirement.
A provider attempts to rely on an old authorization that does not mention SUD/Part 2; the covered entity should obtain a new, specific authorization before redisclosure.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal Part 2 enforcement penalties; privacy complaint investigation risk

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

dependencies.rationale: 17_004 is used when an authorization is needed and must be Part 2-specific; 005_004 clarifies that many HIPAA redisclosures (e.g., TPO) do not require additional authorization, reducing unnecessary authorization collection.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the override is missed, organizations may impose unnecessary authorizations, delaying care coordination and operations; if misapplied the other way, disclosures may occur without required authorization.

dependencies.rationale: Even when using a HIPAA authorization to disclose Part 2 records (17_004), the covered entity must apply appropriate safeguards and comply with HIPAA use/disclosure conditions.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 006_003 controls are absent, authorized disclosures may still be executed insecurely or beyond permissible scope, increasing OCR risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Stabilizing Treatment Required for EMC or Labor
business_rules.description: A Medicare-participating hospital with an emergency department must provide necessary stabilizing treatment to an individual with an emergency medical condition (EMC) or an individual in labor, consistent with EMTALA requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies after the MSE identifies an EMC or active labor, and the individual is present at the hospital’s emergency department.
business_rules.consequences: If provided: the hospital fulfills EMTALA stabilization duty. If not provided when required: CMS may initiate procedures for termination from Medicare; OIG may impose civil monetary penalties.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited interpretive guideline excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Tag A-2400/C-2400, §489.20(l) Interpretive Guidelines
source_reference.source_text: Under the provisions of §489.24, hospitals with an emergency department that participate in Medicare are required under EMTALA to do the following: • Provide necessary stabilizing treatment to an individual with an EMC or an individual in labor;

examples: A patient with suspected stroke is found to have an EMC; the ED must provide stabilizing treatment within its capabilities (e.g., airway, blood pressure, imaging as needed for stabilization).
A pregnant patient arrives in active labor; the hospital must provide stabilizing treatment (including delivery if necessary).
A patient with septic shock is identified; the ED must stabilize (e.g., fluids, vasopressors if available) before considering transfer.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey deficiency and potential Medicare provider agreement termination; OIG civil monetary penalty

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

dependencies.rationale: The hospital must first perform an MSE to determine whether an EMC exists; if yes, it must provide necessary stabilizing treatment.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If MSE is not performed or is inadequate, EMCs may be missed and stabilization delayed/omitted, creating high-risk EMTALA violations.

business_rules.rule_name: Appropriate Transfer Required When Stabilization Not Possible
business_rules.description: A Medicare-participating hospital must provide for an appropriate transfer of an individual if the individual requests the transfer or if the hospital does not have the capability or capacity to provide the treatment necessary to stabilize the EMC (or the capability or capacity to admit the individual).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (a) the individual requests transfer, OR (b) the hospital lacks capability/capacity to stabilize the EMC or to admit the individual, after presentation to the emergency department.
business_rules.consequences: If met: transfer is initiated as appropriate under EMTALA processes. If violated (e.g., transfer without meeting EMTALA appropriateness standards or failure to transfer when required): CMS may initiate Medicare termination; OIG may impose civil monetary penalties.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited interpretive guideline excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Tag A-2400/C-2400, §489.20(l) Interpretive Guidelines
source_reference.source_text: Under the provisions of §489.24, hospitals with an emergency department that participate in Medicare are required under EMTALA to do the following: • Provide for an appropriate transfer of the individual if either the individual requests the transfer or the hospital does not have the capability or capacity to provide the treatment necessary to stabilize the EMC (or the capability or capacity to admit the individual);

examples: A patient with severe trauma needs services not available (e.g., neurosurgery) and the hospital lacks capability; the hospital must arrange an appropriate transfer.
A patient requests transfer to another hospital after an MSE; the hospital must follow appropriate transfer procedures rather than simply instructing the patient to drive elsewhere.
The ED is at capacity and cannot safely provide needed stabilization due to lack of qualified personnel; the hospital must arrange an appropriate transfer consistent with EMTALA.
