business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey deficiency and potential Medicare provider agreement termination; OIG civil monetary penalty

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

dependencies.rationale: 18_003 is triggered when the patient requests transfer or the hospital lacks capability/capacity to stabilize/admit; that determination arises after the stabilization duty context is reached.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If stabilization workflows are not executed/assessed first, the hospital may transfer prematurely or fail to arrange a necessary transfer, increasing EMTALA enforcement risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Hospitals Must Accept Transfers When Specialized and Able
business_rules.description: A participating Medicare hospital is required to accept appropriate transfers of individuals with emergency medical conditions when the hospital has specialized capabilities not available at the transferring hospital and has the capacity to treat those individuals.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to recipient hospitals when (1) the transferring hospital lacks specialized capabilities, (2) the receiving hospital has those specialized capabilities, and (3) the receiving hospital has capacity to treat the individual with an EMC.
business_rules.consequences: If met: receiving hospital accepts transfer consistent with EMTALA. If violated: may result in EMTALA noncompliance findings and CMS actions including potential termination from Medicare; may also lead to OIG civil monetary penalties.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited interpretive guideline excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Tag A-2400/C-2400, §489.20(l) Interpretive Guidelines
source_reference.source_text: (m) A hospital (recipient) that suspects it may have received an improperly transferred (transfer of an unstable individual with an emergency medical condition who was not provided an appropriate

examples: A community hospital requests transfer for an unstable patient needing burn unit care; the receiving hospital with a burn unit and capacity must accept the transfer.
A critical access hospital requests transfer for a STEMI patient requiring cath lab services; the receiving hospital with a cath lab and staffed team must accept if it has capacity.
A tertiary hospital with specialized pediatric ICU services must accept a transfer request when it has available beds and staff and the referring hospital lacks that level of care.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey deficiency and potential Medicare provider agreement termination; OIG civil monetary penalty

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

dependencies.rationale: 18_004 is evaluated in the context of a transfer that should be appropriate; 08_005 defines acceptance/capacity elements that align with the receiving hospital’s duty to accept when specialized capability exists.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the transfer context is not evaluated as appropriate/capacity-based, receiving hospitals may improperly refuse or accept transfers, increasing EMTALA noncompliance risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Written Informed Refusal Must Be Obtained or Attempted
business_rules.description: If an individual refuses examination, treatment, or an appropriate transfer under EMTALA, the hospital must obtain or attempt to obtain written and informed refusal of the examination, treatment, or transfer.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an ED individual refuses (a) examination/MSE, (b) stabilizing treatment, or (c) an appropriate transfer.
business_rules.consequences: If met: documentation supports informed refusal and EMTALA compliance record. If violated: EMTALA noncompliance risk and CMS enforcement actions; may also create medicolegal exposure due to lack of refusal documentation.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited interpretive guideline excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Tag A-2400/C-2400, §489.20(l) Interpretive Guidelines
source_reference.source_text: Under the provisions of §489.24, hospitals with an emergency department that participate in Medicare are required under EMTALA to do the following: • Obtain or attempt to obtain written and informed refusal of examination, treatment or an appropriate transfer in the case of an individual who refuses examination, treatment or transfer;

examples: A patient with possible GI bleed refuses blood transfusion and leaves; staff must attempt to obtain written informed refusal documenting risks explained.
A patient refuses transfer to a higher level of care after being diagnosed with an EMC; hospital must attempt written informed refusal of transfer.
A patient refuses MSE and leaves immediately; staff should attempt to obtain refusal documentation and record the attempt when the patient will not sign.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey deficiency and potential Medicare provider agreement termination; OIG civil monetary penalty

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Suspected Improper Unstable Transfer Must Be Reported in 72 Hours
business_rules.description: A recipient hospital that suspects it may have received an individual transferred in an unstable emergency medical condition in violation of EMTALA transfer requirements must promptly report the incident to CMS or the State survey agency within 72 hours of the occurrence.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the receiving hospital has reason to believe it received an improperly transferred unstable EMC individual (i.e., transfer not effected as an appropriate transfer under §489.24(e)).
business_rules.consequences: If reported within 72 hours: fulfills reporting obligation under §489.20(m). If not reported: hospital may be subject to termination of its provider agreement (42 CFR 489.53(a)), per interpretive guidance.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited interpretive guideline excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Tag A-2401/C-2401, §489.20(m) Interpretive Guidelines
source_reference.source_text: A hospital (recipient) that suspects it may have received an improperly transferred (transfer of an unstable individual with an emergency medical condition who was not provided an appropriate transfer according to §489.24(e)(2) ), individual is required to promptly report the incident to CMS or the State Agency (SA) within 72 hours of the occurrence. If a recipient hospital fails to report an improper transfer, the hospital may be subject to termination of it’s provider agreement

examples: A receiving ED discovers the sending hospital transferred an intubated patient without required records or appropriate transport; the receiving hospital reports to CMS/SA within 72 hours.
A patient arrives unstable by private vehicle after being told to leave another ED; if circumstances suggest an improper unstable transfer, the receiving hospital reports within 72 hours.
A receiving hospital learns from EMS that the sending facility did not obtain acceptance prior to transfer of an unstable EMC; the receiving facility reports the suspected violation within 72 hours.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Medicare provider agreement termination (42 CFR 489.53(a)) and potential EMTALA enforcement actions

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

dependencies.rationale: To 'suspect' an unstable improper transfer, the receiving hospital must compare the circumstances against appropriate transfer requirements (e.g., acceptance agreement, stabilization efforts, documentation/transport adequacy).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the receiving hospital cannot validate against the appropriate transfer standard, it may fail to report within 72 hours, risking termination of its provider agreement.

business_rules.rule_name: EMTALA Rights and Medicaid Signs Must Be Conspicuously Posted
business_rules.description: Hospitals defined in §489.24(b) must conspicuously post (1) signs specifying individuals’ rights under EMTALA regarding examination and treatment of emergency medical conditions and women in labor, and (2) signs indicating whether the hospital participates in Medicaid. Signs must be placed in locations likely to be noticed by individuals entering the ED and those waiting in non-traditional ED areas, and the wording must be clear and in simple terms and language(s) understandable by the population served.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any emergency department and areas likely to be noticed by individuals entering/waiting for examination and treatment (e.g., entrance, admitting area, waiting room, treatment area), including areas other than traditional ED spaces when individuals wait there.
business_rules.consequences: If met: signage compliance supports EMTALA requirements. If violated: CMS may cite a deficiency under §489.20(q), with associated EMTALA enforcement risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited interpretive guideline excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Tag A-2402/C-2402, §489.20(q)(1)-(2) Interpretive Guidelines
source_reference.source_text: To comply with the requirements hospital signage must at a minimum: • Specify the rights of individuals with EMCs and women in labor who come to the emergency department for health care services; • Indicate whether the facility participates in the Medicaid program; • The wording of the sign(s) must be clear and in simple terms and language(s) that are understandable by the population served by the hospital; and • The sign(s) must be posted in a place or places likely to be noticed

examples: A hospital posts EMTALA rights signage at ED entrance and inside the waiting room in English and Spanish consistent with the community served.
During a remodel, patient waiting occurs in an overflow hallway; the hospital must ensure EMTALA rights signage is also posted where those patients wait.
A critical access hospital participates in Medicaid; it must post the Medicaid participation information conspicuously alongside EMTALA rights signage.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS survey deficiency citation

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 redisclosure for proceedings against patient needs court order or separate consent
business_rules.description: When a covered entity or business associate receives Part 2 records pursuant to a treatment, payment, and health care operations (TPO) consent, further use/disclosure is generally permitted under HIPAA; however, for uses and disclosures for civil, criminal, administrative, and legislative proceedings against the patient, the disclosing party must comply with Part 2 procedures by obtaining a Part 2 court order under § 2.64 or § 2.65 or obtaining a separate patient consent meeting § 2.31(d).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Part 2 records were originally disclosed based on patient consent consistent with § 2.31 for TPO, and a later request would use/disclose those records or testimony from those records in a proceeding against the patient.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: redisclosure proceeds only with the required Part 2 court order or separate § 2.31(d) consent. If violated: unauthorized Part 2 disclosure/use may trigger Part 2 enforcement exposure, including potential civil and criminal liability under the Part 2 statute and implementing regulations.
business_rules.exceptions: If the proceeding is not against the patient (that is, the patient is a witness or unrelated third party), HIPAA permission at 45 CFR 164.512(e) may govern rather than Part 2 § 2.64/§ 2.65 (see related rule).

source_reference.section_id: Response re: revised §2.33(b) and proceedings against the patient
source_reference.source_text: When disclosed for treatment, payment, and health care operations activities... the recipient may further use or disclose those records in accordance with the HIPAA regulations, except for uses and disclosures for civil, criminal, administrative, and legislative proceedings against the patient... any person seeking to redisclose such records or information in a proceeding against the patient is required to comply with the procedures in § 2.64 or § 2.65 to obtain the part 2 court order or a separate consent of the patient that meets the requirements of new § 2.31(d).

examples: A health plan received Part 2 records for payment under a TPO consent; later, the plan receives a subpoena for a civil proceeding against the patient and must obtain a Part 2 court order or separate § 2.31(d) consent before producing the records.
A provider wants to introduce Part 2 treatment notes as testimony in a criminal proceeding against the patient; the provider must follow § 2.65 or obtain separate § 2.31(d) consent.

A covered entity uses Part 2 records for internal care coordination (TPO) under HIPAA; this is permitted, but using them in a proceeding against the patient is restricted without the Part 2 order/consent.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Part 2 enforcement exposure including potential civil and criminal liability for impermissible use/disclosure

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_040_006 permits further disclosures under HIPAA except for proceedings against the patient. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_19_005 specifies the required Part 2 procedures (court order under §2.64/§2.65 or separate §2.31(d) consent) for that carve-out scenario.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the carve-out is ignored, recipients may disclose Part 2 records into proceedings against the patient without required court order/consent—high enforcement risk.

dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_19_005 requires Part 2 procedures for proceedings against the patient. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_19_006 specifies that when the proceeding is not against the patient, HIPAA 164.512(e) governs instead, overriding the need to obtain Part 2 court orders/consent for that scenario.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Misclassification (treating an 'against the patient' proceeding as 'not against') can cause impermissible disclosure; the reverse can cause unnecessary burden/delay but is less likely to be unlawful.

dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_19_005 applies to Part 2 records received under TPO consent and later requested for proceedings against the patient. If the recipient fails to retain Part 2 status after integration (contrary to BR_COVERED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_039_003), the recipient may not apply required Part 2 court order/consent safeguards.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Failure to retain Part 2 status leads to misrouting under ordinary HIPAA subpoenas and impermissible disclosures.

dependencies.rationale: BR_19_005 addresses a common misstep: treating Part 2 records received under TPO consent as freely usable in proceedings against the patient. BR_001_002 requires compliance with Part 2 restrictions regardless of subpoena/law enforcement status; BR_19_005 specifies the required mechanism (Part 2 court order or separate consent) for this scenario.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Failing to apply Part 2 baseline restrictions can result in disclosures for proceedings against the patient based on HIPAA-only logic or subpoena alone, creating Part 2 violations.

dependencies.rationale: If the records were not lawfully disclosed under a valid TPO consent (including allowed recipient class/purpose/expiration), the later redisclosure logic in BR_19_005 is not the correct compliance pathway; instead the organization is dealing with an upstream impermissible disclosure scenario.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_23_008 is not met, downstream handling under BR_19_005 may be built on an invalid premise (lawful receipt via TPO consent), increasing enforcement risk and complicating remediation.

dependencies.rationale: Missing BR_23_009 statements can render the consent incomplete/invalid, undermining the condition that the recipient received records pursuant to a compliant TPO consent (a key premise of BR_19_005).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_23_009 is not met, BR_19_005 may be incorrectly treated as the governing rule while the more fundamental issue is improper initial disclosure; liability and corrective action scope can expand.

dependencies.rationale: BR_19_005 permits further use/disclosure in proceedings against the patient only if the disclosing party follows Part 2 procedures (court order or separate consent). Where the pathway is a §2.65 order, BR_032_005 specifies mandatory order limitations (essential parts, authorized officials, limited purpose).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the order is overbroad or lacks required limitations, disclosure under BR_19_005 becomes noncompliant and increases Part 2 enforcement exposure and evidentiary risk.

dependencies.rationale: Rule 19_005 requires Part 2 procedures for proceedings against the patient and explicitly allows a separate patient consent meeting Part 2 requirements. Rule 016_001 specifies mandatory content for certain §2.31 consents, making it a legal-content prerequisite for any consent used to authorize such a proceeding-related disclosure in those circumstances.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the separate consent is defective, the organization may proceed without a valid authorization/court order, creating high-risk Part 2 unauthorized disclosure exposure (civil/criminal liability risk depending on facts).

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 redisclosure for proceedings not against patient may follow HIPAA 164.512(e)
business_rules.description: If the underlying judicial or administrative proceeding is not against the patient who is the subject of the Part 2 records, a covered entity is permitted to redisclose the records in accordance with the HIPAA Privacy Rule permission for judicial and administrative proceedings at 45 CFR 164.512(e), consistent with revised § 2.33(b).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Part 2 records were received pursuant to a TPO consent and a later disclosure is requested for a judicial/administrative proceeding where the patient is not the target (for example, the patient is a witness, alternate suspect, or third-party individual).
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: the covered entity may respond under HIPAA 164.512(e) rather than Part 2 § 2.64/§ 2.65 procedures. If violated (for example, treating a proceeding against the patient as if it were not): could result in impermissible Part 2 use/disclosure and associated enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: Does not apply when the proceeding is against the patient; in that scenario, Part 2 court order or separate § 2.31(d) consent is required.

source_reference.section_id: Response to state Medicaid office re: 45 CFR 164.512(e) vs §2.64
source_reference.source_text: As we understand the commenter’s example and question, the underlying proceedings are not against the subject of the records or “patient,” and therefore the covered entity would be permitted to redisclose the records in accordance the HIPAA Privacy Rule permission at 45 CFR 164.512(e).

examples: A Medicaid agency receives Part 2 records for payment; it later receives an administrative tribunal order where the patient is a witness in a fraud case against another person and responds using HIPAA 164.512(e).
A hospital receives Part 2 records under TPO consent and receives a court order in a civil case not against the patient; it evaluates under HIPAA 164.512(e).
A covered entity mistakenly treats a criminal proceeding against the patient as ‘not against’ and discloses without Part 2 order/consent, creating Part 2 compliance risk.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Part 2 enforcement exposure if misclassified and disclosed without required Part 2 order/consent

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

dependencies.rationale: BR_19_006 provides an alternative pathway for judicial/administrative disclosures when the proceeding is not against the patient. BR_001_002 remains the governing restriction framework requiring entities not to disclose unless permitted by the applicable Part 2/HIPAA-aligned rules.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If Part 2 baseline analysis is skipped, a covered entity may misapply 164.512(e) in circumstances that are actually 'against the patient,' causing an impermissible Part 2 disclosure.

dependencies.rationale: The ability to use HIPAA 164.512(e) for proceedings not against the patient presumes lawful receipt of Part 2 records under a valid TPO consent. BR_23_008 helps establish that the original disclosure was permitted as TPO consent-based.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_23_008 is not met, reliance on BR_19_006 may be inappropriate because the original disclosure may not have been lawful; compliance analysis must shift to incident/improper disclosure handling.

dependencies.rationale: Absent BR_23_009 statements, the consent may not qualify as a compliant Part 2 all-future TPO consent; that undercuts the predicate condition for applying the BR_19_006 redisclosure pathway.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_23_009 is not met, responding under HIPAA 164.512(e) may be built on a flawed premise (invalid original consent), raising Part 2 enforcement and remediation needs.

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 criminal court order must be narrowly limited to essential record portions
business_rules.description: For a court order authorizing disclosure/use of Part 2 patient records for criminal investigation or prosecution of the patient, the order must (1) limit disclosure/use to those parts of the patient’s record essential to fulfill the objective of the order; (2) limit disclosure to the law enforcement and prosecutorial officials responsible for, or conducting, the investigation/prosecution; (3) limit those officials’ use to investigating and prosecuting extremely serious crimes or suspected crimes specified in the application; and (4) include other measures necessary to limit use/disclosure to the public interest and need found by the court. Extremely serious crimes include those that directly threaten loss of life or serious bodily injury (for example, homicide, rape, kidnapping, armed robbery, assault with a deadly weapon, or child abuse and neglect).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a custodian of Part 2 patient records or a law enforcement/prosecutorial official applies for a court order under § 2.65 to criminally investigate or prosecute a patient and seeks disclosure/use of Part 2 records or testimony relaying information within the records.
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: disclosures are minimized and limited to essential parts and authorized officials for specified extremely serious crimes. If violated: overbroad or improperly directed disclosures may be impermissible under Part 2 and subject the discloser and/or recipients to Part 2 enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt; the order content must include limitations and additional measures necessary to confine use/disclosure to the court-determined public interest and need.

source_reference.section_id: §2.65(e)(1)-(3) discussion and note on extremely serious crimes
source_reference.source_text: Paragraph (e)(1) requires that such order must limit disclosure and use to those parts of the patient’s record as are essential to fulfill the objective of the order... limit the disclosure to those law enforcement and prosecutorial officials... and limit their use of the records to investigating and prosecuting extremely serious crimes... Paragraph (e)(3) requires that the order include other measures as are necessary to limit use and disclosure...

examples: A prosecutor requests the entire substance use treatment file for a suspected armed robbery; the court order must limit production to only the essential portions and specify only the investigating/prosecuting officials may use it for the specified crime.

A law enforcement request seeks Part 2 records for simple drug possession; because illegal substance use alone is not an extremely serious crime, the order should not authorize disclosure on that basis.
A court order authorizes disclosure but fails to include additional measures to limit redisclosure; the covered entity requests clarification/narrowing before producing records.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Part 2 enforcement exposure for overbroad/unauthorized use or disclosure

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_19_005 allows proceeding use/disclosure only with Part 2 court order under §2.64/§2.65 or separate consent. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_19_007 defines mandatory scope limits for §2.65 orders in criminal investigation/prosecution against the patient.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If §2.65 order requirements are not met, disclosures may be overbroad or improperly directed, creating Part 2 noncompliance and liability exposure.

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 records in DRS must follow HIPAA access
business_rules.description: When a covered entity (or records maintained for a covered entity) receives 42 CFR Part 2 records and those records are part of the covered entity’s designated record set, the records become PHI and are subject to the HIPAA Privacy Rule right-of-access requirements (45 CFR 164.524). A covered entity’s Part 2 records usually fall into designated record set categories such as medical and billing records or health plan claims and case/medical management record systems.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (1) Part 2 records are received by or for a covered entity, and (2) the records are part of the covered entity’s designated record set (records used, in whole or in part, to make decisions about individuals).
business_rules.consequences: If conditions are met: covered entity must treat the Part 2 records as PHI for purposes of HIPAA right of access and process individual access requests under HIPAA. If violated: patient may file complaints and the covered entity may face HIPAA/Part 2 enforcement and corrective action obligations based on facts and circumstances.
business_rules.exceptions: If the Part 2 records are not part of the designated record set, HIPAA right-of-access obligations tied to the designated record set may not apply to those specific records (other HIPAA/Part 2 obligations may still apply).

source_reference.section_id: Discussion of HIPAA right of access and Part 2 records in designated record set (citing 45 CFR 164.524 and 164.501)
source_reference.source_text: We clarify that when part 2 records are received by or for a covered entity and are part of a designated record set they become PHI and are subject to the HIPAA Privacy Rule access requirements. Generally, the HIPAA Privacy Rule gives individuals the right to access all of their PHI in a designated record set. A “designated record set” is a group of records maintained by or for a covered entity that are a provider’s medical and billing records, a health plan’s enrollment, payment, claims adjudication, and case or medical management record systems, and any other records used, in whole or in part, by or for the covered entity to make decisions about individuals.

examples: A hospital receives SUD treatment records from an affiliated Part 2 program and stores them in the EHR chart used for clinical decisions; the patient’s access request must be handled under HIPAA right-of-access rules.

A health plan receives Part 2 records supporting prior authorization and places them in its case management system used to make coverage decisions; the plan must treat them as PHI in the designated record set.

A covered entity receives Part 2 records but keeps them in a segregated quality-improvement repository not used to make decisions about individuals; the entity evaluates whether that repository is part of the designated record set before applying HIPAA access processing to those records.
business_rules.enforcement_action: HHS OCR investigation and corrective action plan; potential civil monetary penalty depending on facts

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

dependencies.rationale: BR_21_001 is triggered when Part 2 records are received by/for a covered entity and are part of the designated record set (DRS), making them PHI subject to HIPAA access. BR_19_010 governs when a BA acting as a QSO can properly receive Part 2 records (QSOA required; BAA alone insufficient). If the QSOA prerequisite is missing, the legality of the receipt/maintenance that would place records into a CE’s DRS is undermined.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_19_010 is not satisfied, the organization may have an impermissible Part 2 disclosure to the service provider, complicating subsequent HIPAA-right-of-access handling and increasing Part 2 enforcement risk.

business_rules.rule_name: First accounting of disclosures must be free once per 12 months
business_rules.description: Under the HIPAA Privacy Rule accounting of disclosures provision (45 CFR 164.528(c)(2)), a covered entity must provide the first accounting of disclosures to an individual in any 12-month period without charge. For each subsequent accounting request by the same individual within the same 12-month period, the covered entity may impose a reasonable, cost-based fee, but only if it informs the individual of the fee in advance and provides an opportunity to withdraw or modify the request.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an individual requests an accounting of disclosures of PHI from a covered entity under 45 CFR 164.528.
business_rules.consequences: If it is the first request in a 12-month period, the covered entity must not charge a fee. If it is a subsequent request in the same 12-month period, the covered entity may charge a reasonable, cost-based fee only after advance notice and offering the individual the ability to withdraw or modify the request. Charging a fee for the first request, or charging a fee without advance notice/withdrawal option, is noncompliant.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: HIPAA Privacy Rule—fees for accounting of disclosures (45 CFR 164.528(c)(2))
source_reference.source_text: For an accounting of disclosures, the HIPAA Privacy Rule at 45 CFR 164.528(c)(2) requires a covered entity provide the first accounting to an individual in any 12-month period without charge. The covered entity may impose a reasonable, cost-based fee for each subsequent request for an accounting by the same individual within the 12-month period, provided that the covered entity informs the individual in advance of the fee and provides the individual with an opportunity to withdraw or modify the request.

examples: A patient requests an accounting in March and again in August of the same year; the March accounting must be free, and the August accounting may be charged a reasonable, cost-based fee with advance notice and a chance to modify/withdraw.
A covered entity quotes a fee before processing a second request and the patient narrows the date range to avoid the fee; this is compliant because the patient was allowed to modify the request.
A covered entity charges $25 for the first accounting in a 12-month period; this violates 45 CFR 164.528(c)(2) as described in the excerpt.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR enforcement for HIPAA Privacy Rule noncompliance (e.g., corrective action plan and potential civil monetary penalties)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

dependencies.rationale: 11_009 requires intermediaries to maintain a 3-year list of disclosures under Part 2 §2.24, and notes alignment with a 3-year look-back period rationale. 23_005 addresses HIPAA accounting of disclosures and the fee constraints for responding. Together they form a coordinated operational capability: maintaining disclosure logs and responding to individuals’ disclosure-accounting/list requests.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If disclosure lists/logging are not maintained to support the 3-year look-back (11_009), the organization may be unable to provide accurate accountings/lists and may mishandle request responses (including fee handling) under 23_005.

dependencies.rationale: Rule 31_009 establishes a Part 2 notice-content obligation about accounting rights (3-year lookback for electronic records under Part 2). Rule 23_005 governs HIPAA accounting fee limits and conditions. When a covered entity fulfills accounting requests involving PHI (including Part 2 records treated as PHI in many contexts), these rules collectively shape what must be communicated and how the accounting service can be charged.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If not aligned, patients may receive inaccurate statements about accounting rights or be improperly charged for accounting requests, increasing complaint and enforcement risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Restriction Requests Mandatory Only When Paid in Full
business_rules.description: The requirement to accept a patient’s request for restrictions on disclosures is mandatory only for services for which the patient has paid in full. In that context, complying with a request not to disclose records/PHI to the patient’s health plan may reduce provider labor otherwise required to submit claims. The final rule also adds that covered entities must use reasonable effort to accommodate a patient’s request for restrictions (increasing the estimated handling burden).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a patient requests restrictions on disclosure and the restricted disclosure would be to a health plan; acceptance is mandatory only if the services at issue have been paid in full by the patient. Also applies to covered entities subject to the final rule’s “reasonable effort” accommodation obligation for restriction requests.
business_rules.consequences: If conditions are met: covered entity must accept the restriction for paid-in-full services and must use reasonable effort to accommodate the request. If violated: potential enforcement exposure for failing to honor mandatory restriction requests and failing to accommodate restriction requests with reasonable effort.
business_rules.exceptions: Requests not tied to paid-in-full services are not described as mandatory acceptance in this excerpt; other legal requirements may still apply.

source_reference.section_id: RIA—Requests for Privacy Protection for Records (parallel to 45 CFR 164.522)
source_reference.source_text: “costs remain low… in part because the requirement to accept a patient’s request for restrictions is mandatory only for services for which the patient has paid in full… The estimated number of responses… and the average burden doubled… to account for the final rule adding the requirement that covered entities use reasonable effort to accommodate patient’s request for restrictions.”

examples: A patient pays cash in full for SUD counseling and asks the provider not to disclose the encounter to the patient’s health plan; the provider must accept the restriction and use reasonable effort to implement it.
A patient requests a restriction but did not pay in full; acceptance is not mandatory under the rule description, though the entity should evaluate other applicable laws/policies.
A covered entity receives a restriction request and must make reasonable effort (e.g., apply EHR billing flags, adjust claim submission workflow) to accommodate it.
business_rules.enforcement_action: HHS enforcement investigation and corrective action plan

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Part 2 breach definition adopts HIPAA 164.402 and presumes breach unless low risk
business_rules.description: For Part 2 breach notification purposes, the definition of “breach” is adopted by reference to HIPAA at 45 CFR 164.402: an acquisition, access, use, or disclosure not permitted that compromises the security or privacy of the information. The event is presumed to be a breach unless a required risk assessment demonstrates a low probability that the information has been compromised, consistent with the incorporated HIPAA framework as applied to Part 2 records.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a covered entity (or other lawful holder) experiences an acquisition, access, use, or disclosure of Part 2 records in a manner not permitted by Part 2/HIPAA-aligned provisions incorporated by reference.
business_rules.consequences: If met (breach presumed and not rebutted by low-probability risk assessment): entity must follow the incorporated HIPAA breach notification framework for Part 2 records as referenced for Part 2 breach notification implementation. If low probability is demonstrated: the event may be treated as not a breach under the incorporated standard.
business_rules.exceptions: The presumption can be rebutted by a risk assessment showing low probability of compromise (per the incorporated HIPAA breach framework).

source_reference.section_id: Breach
source_reference.source_text: HIPAA defines “breach” as “the acquisition, access, use, or disclosure of protected health information in a manner not permitted under subpart E which compromises the security or privacy of the protected health information.” ... a breach is presumed to have occurred when an “acquisition, access, use, or disclosure” of PHI occurs in a manner not permitted under the HIPAA Privacy Rule unless a risk assessment shows a low probability that health information has been compromised.

source_reference.section_id: Breach Notification Overview (45 CFR 164.402 definition of breach)
source_reference.source_text: Paragraph (k)(1) of 42 U.S.C. 290dd–2 incorporated a definition of the term breach, giving it the same meaning as under the HIPAA regulations. The HIPAA Breach Notification Rule at 45 CFR 164.402 defines breach as “the acquisition, access, use, or disclosure of protected health information in a manner not permitted under subpart E of this part which compromises the security or privacy of the protected health information.”

examples: An impermissible disclosure of unsecured Part 2 information triggers a HIPAA-style breach analysis under 164.402 and related 164.402-164.414 workflows.
If the risk assessment supports low probability of compromise, the incident may be treated as not a breach under the incorporated standard.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Civil monetary penalty and corrective action plan risk under incorporated breach notification framework

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules incorporate HIPAA’s breach definition (45 CFR 164.402) into Part 2 breach evaluation. The primary adds the standard HIPAA presumption/rebuttal framing used for breach determinations.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_26_004 selected: includes breach presumption + low-probability risk assessment concept in addition to the definitional incorporation.

dependencies.rationale: BR_26_004 presumes breach when an impermissible event occurs unless a risk assessment shows low probability of compromise. Whether the event is 'impermissible' requires applying the Part 2 restrictions framework described in BR_001_002.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the entity misclassifies an impermissible disclosure as permitted (or vice versa), it may fail to trigger required breach handling or may over-notify, both creating compliance and operational risk.

dependencies.rationale: 12_003 requires safeguards and reporting of unauthorized uses/disclosures by contractors/subcontractors/representatives. Those same events are the typical triggers for applying the 26_004 breach presumption and risk assessment. The rules work together operationally: controls/reporting feed breach determination.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 12_003 contracting/safeguard/reporting requirements are not implemented, the lawful holder may fail to detect or timely assess incidents, increasing the risk of missed breach determinations and noncompliant notification decisions under the adopted breach standard.

dependencies.rationale: BR_24_001 drives when breach classification and risk assessment processes aligning with 45 CFR 164.402 (as incorporated for Part 2 by BR_26_004) must be in place.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_24_001 sequencing is not followed, breach determinations may be inconsistent or noncompliant after the compliance date, risking notification failures and enforcement.

business_rules.rule_name: Public health disclosure without consent is limited to de-identified Part 2 records
business_rules.description: Disclosures to public health authorities are permitted without consent only for Part 2 records that have been de-identified. If a patient elects to consent to the disclosure of identifiable Part 2 records to a public health authority, the consent must include a specific designation of the recipient.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when disclosing Part 2 records to a public health authority. The "without consent" permission applies only to de-identified records; identifiable disclosures require a patient consent that specifically names/designates the public health authority recipient.
business_rules.consequences: If met: de-identified public health disclosure can proceed without patient consent; identifiable disclosure proceeds only with specifically designated consent. If violated: identifiable public health disclosure without specific recipient designation (or without consent) is noncompliant and may trigger enforcement and patient harm risks.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt; de-identification is a prerequisite for no-consent public health disclosures under the described permission.

source_reference.section_id: Disclosures to public health authorities
source_reference.source_text: disclosures for TPO. Under the HIPAA Privacy Rule, disclosures to public health authorities are not considered disclosures for TPO and we apply this same interpretation to part 2. To the extent that a patient elects to consent to the disclosure of identifiable records to a public health authority, the consent must include a specific designation of

examples: A Part 2 program provides de-identified SUD surveillance data to a state health department without obtaining patient consent.
A provider wants to disclose identifiable Part 2 records to the county public health authority for contact tracing; the patient consent must specifically name/designate that authority.
A health plan discloses identifiable Part 2 records to a public health agency based on a general TPO consent that does not specifically designate the agency—this is noncompliant.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Federal confidentiality enforcement exposure for impermissible public health disclosure

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_26_005 requires specific recipient designation when patients consent to disclose identifiable Part 2 records to a public health authority. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_consent_requirement_34_003 defines how Part 2 consents must designate recipients by name/class.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the consent does not properly designate the public health authority recipient, the identifiable disclosure is impermissible.

dependencies.rationale: BR_26_005 is a specific Part 2 permission/constraint for public health authority disclosures (only de-identified without consent; identifiable requires specifically designated recipient in consent). This sits under the broader 'do not disclose unless permitted' rule in BR_001_002.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Skipping the baseline Part 2 restriction analysis can lead to incorrect reliance on HIPAA public health permissions for identifiable Part 2 records, resulting in impermissible disclosures.

dependencies.rationale: 26_005 allows identifiable public health disclosures only with a properly executed consent naming the authority. For adults lacking capacity, 010_010 dictates who can sign and limits certain consent pathways (e.g., program-director consent limited to obtaining payment when no adjudication).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the wrong party signs or the consent is used beyond allowed purposes under 010_010, the 26_005 consent basis fails and the disclosure becomes impermissible.

dependencies.rationale: BR_24_001 sets the implementation horizon for policy changes that operationalize BR_26_005’s de-identification vs consent-with-specific-designation branching logic.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_24_001-driven implementation is late, public health reporting may incorrectly include identifiable Part 2 records without proper consent, creating high-risk improper disclosure events.

dependencies.rationale: BR_22_005 requires de-identified aggregate reporting for research outputs. BR_26_005 permits disclosure to public health authorities without consent only if Part 2 records are de-identified. The same de-identification standard underpins both, so the organization’s de-identification method/control environment must support compliance with both rules.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If de-identification controls are weak/inconsistent, both research outputs and public health disclosures may inadvertently include identifiable Part 2 information, increasing Part 2 and HIPAA exposure.

business_rules.rule_name: Physician review required before CMPs or termination
business_rules.description: Before CMS imposes EMTALA civil monetary penalties (CMPs) or terminates a hospital’s provider agreement, a professional medical review (physician peer review) is required to evaluate the case using information that was available to the hospital at the time of the alleged violation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an EMTALA investigation may lead to (1) CMP imposition or (2) termination of a hospital’s Medicare provider agreement, and the hospital performed a screening examination (that is, the alleged violation involves the adequacy/appropriateness of screening, stabilization, or transfer decisions).
business_rules.consequences: If performed: physician review findings inform the Regional Office (RO) compliance determination and enforcement recommendation. If not performed when required: enforcement action (CMPs/termination) risks being procedurally unsupported and may require remand/redo of review before action proceeds.
business_rules.exceptions: Physician review is not necessary when the hospital did not screen the individual (that is, no medical screening examination was performed).

source_reference.section_id: VIII. Task 6- Professional Medical Review
source_reference.source_text: Physician review is required prior to the imposition of CMPs or the termination of a hospital’s provider agreement to determine if: • The screening examination was appropriate... When used in the context of EMTALA, “appropriate” means that the screening examination was suitable for the symptoms presented and conducted in a non-disparate fashion.

examples: A hospital is investigated for transferring an unstable ED patient; before CMPs are imposed, the RO obtains physician peer review assessing screening, stabilization, and transfer appropriateness using records available at the time.

A hospital faces potential provider agreement termination after multiple EMTALA complaints; physician review is conducted to determine whether screening and stabilization met EMTALA standards, independent of outcome.
A patient left without being screened (no MSE performed). The RO may proceed without physician review for the 'appropriateness of screening' element because no screening occurred.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty or provider agreement termination

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_30_001 requires a physician peer review before CMPs/termination in specified EMTALA cases. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_30_002 defines the standard used by physician reviewers to evaluate screening compliance (symptom-suitable, non-disparate; not necessarily correct diagnosis).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If reviewers use the wrong standard (e.g., negligence/correct diagnosis), the review may be invalid or misleading, affecting enforcement outcomes and due process.

dependencies.rationale: 30_001 requires professional medical review prior to CMP/termination, using information available at the time of the alleged EMTALA violation. The central log required by 010_006 helps identify the encounter, timing, and disposition (refused/denied/treated/admitted/stabilized/transferred/discharged) needed to frame the review.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the central log is incomplete/inaccurate, the professional review may be undermined (missing cases, wrong timelines, unclear dispositions), increasing procedural and substantive enforcement risk.

dependencies.rationale: 30_001 peer review evaluates screening/stabilization/transfer decisions using contemporaneous information. 010_005 requires retaining transfer-related records for 5 years; those records (certifications, acceptance communications, transport details) are commonly key inputs to the review.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If transfer records are not retained, the hospital may be unable to demonstrate compliance during the professional medical review, increasing likelihood of adverse findings and enforcement recommendations.

dependencies.rationale: 30_001 applies when an EMTALA investigation may lead to CMPs/termination and involves screening/stabilization/transfer decisions. For cases involving transfer of an unstabilized EMC individual, 010_004 provides the substantive criteria (patient request after informed risks, physician/QMP certification) that the review will assess against available facts.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 010_004 criteria are not met/documented, professional review may support noncompliance findings and increase risk of enforcement escalation.

business_rules.rule_name: EMTALA screening 'appropriate' means suitable and non-disparate
business_rules.description: For EMTALA professional medical review, an ‘appropriate’ medical screening examination is defined as a screening suitable for the symptoms presented and conducted in a non-disparate fashion. ‘Appropriate’ does not mean ‘correct’ diagnosis; physician negligence alone is not necessarily an EMTALA violation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when assessing whether a covered entity’s emergency department screening complied with EMTALA and when physician reviewers evaluate alleged screening violations.
business_rules.consequences: If screening was symptom-suitable and non-disparate: supports a finding of compliance for the screening requirement even if diagnosis was incorrect. If screening was disparate or not suitable for symptoms: supports a noncompliance finding and adverse enforcement recommendation.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; however, if no screening occurred, the ‘appropriateness’ evaluation is not applicable to a performed exam (see physician review exception in Task 6).

source_reference.section_id: VIII. Task 6- Professional Medical Review
source_reference.source_text: Under EMTALA, the term “appropriate” does not mean “correct”, in the sense that the treating emergency physician is not required to correctly diagnose the individual’s medical condition. The fact that a physician may have been negligent in his screening of an individual is not necessarily an EMTALA violation. When used in the context of EMTALA, “appropriate” means that the screening examination was suitable for the symptoms presented and conducted in a non-disparate fashion.

examples: Two patients present with identical chest pain symptoms; if one receives a full cardiac workup and the other is sent home without comparable evaluation due to insurance status, the screening may be disparate and not 'appropriate'.
An ED physician performs a symptom-appropriate abdominal pain workup but misses appendicitis; incorrect diagnosis alone does not establish EMTALA screening noncompliance.
A hospital uses a standardized triage and MSE protocol applied consistently to all patients with stroke symptoms; this supports non-disparate, symptom-suitable screening.
business_rules.enforcement_action: civil monetary penalty or provider agreement termination

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: One EMTALA noncompliance event is a violation
business_rules.description: A single occurrence of noncompliance with EMTALA requirements constitutes a violation and is sufficient for an adverse recommendation when assessing compliance findings.
business_rules.conditions: Applies during the assessment of compliance and completion of the deficiency report (Form CMS-2567) for EMTALA investigations, regardless of frequency beyond one event.
business_rules.consequences: If any EMTALA requirement is not met at least once: cite as a violation and support an adverse recommendation. Hospitals may face enforcement actions such as CMPs, corrective action requirements, or provider agreement termination depending on severity and RO determination.
business_rules.exceptions: Older cases where the hospital implemented corrective actions with no repeat violations may require consultation with the RO concerning appropriate recommendations.

source_reference.section_id: IX. Task 7- Assessment of Compliance and Completion of the Deficiency Report (A. Analysis)
source_reference.source_text: Analyze your findings relative to each provision of the regulations for the frequency and dates of occurrence. A single occurrence of non-compliance with the EMTALA requirements constitutes a violation and is sufficient for an adverse recommendation. Older cases where the hospital implemented corrective actions with no repeat violations may require consultation with the RO concerning appropriate recommendations.

examples: An investigation finds one instance where a patient with emergency symptoms was refused an MSE; even if isolated, it is sufficient to recommend an adverse finding for EMTALA screening compliance.
A hospital has a single documented delay of stabilizing treatment due to insurance inquiries; the single occurrence supports a violation citation.
A 4-year-old case shows one violation but the hospital implemented corrective action and has had no repeats; surveyors consult the RO on the appropriate recommendation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS enforcement referral and adverse recommendation

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_30_003 states a single occurrence of EMTALA noncompliance constitutes a violation. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_18_008 is an EMTALA-related CMS posting requirement; a single failure to conspicuously post required signs can constitute that 'single occurrence.'
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If signage is not compliant even once (or is persistently noncompliant), the organization is exposed to an EMTALA deficiency citation and adverse recommendation logic.

dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_REPORTING_30_005 imposes a receiving-hospital reporting duty for suspected EMTALA-violative transfers. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_30_003 makes a single noncompliance occurrence sufficient for a violation/adverse recommendation.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the hospital fails to report a suspected EMTALA-violative transfer, the single failure can be cited and contribute to enforcement action.

dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_30_003 is applied after assessing whether an EMTALA requirement was violated. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_30_002 defines the compliance test for screening examinations (symptom-suitable and non-disparate), which is a common alleged violation type.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If screening is incorrectly evaluated (e.g., equating negligence with EMTALA violation), the organization may be wrongly cited or wrongly cleared, affecting enforcement posture.

dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_30_006 governs investigator conduct (no premature violation statements; no draft CMS-2567 left behind) to preserve RO authority. This is procedurally sequential to the compliance assessment and deficiency reporting process in which BR_COVERED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_30_003 is applied.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If investigators improperly communicate findings or leave drafts, the investigation process may be procedurally compromised, requiring corrective administrative steps and potentially delaying enforcement resolution.

business_rules.rule_name: Receiving hospitals must report suspected EMTALA transfers
business_rules.description: A receiving hospital must report suspected incidences of individuals with an emergency medical condition transferred in violation of EMTALA transfer requirements, as reflected in the EMTALA deficiency tag for §489.20(m).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a hospital receives an individual with an emergency medical condition and has reason to suspect the transfer occurred in violation of EMTALA (§489.24(e)).
business_rules.consequences: If suspected violations are reported: supports compliance with receiving hospital responsibilities. If not reported: supports a deficiency citation and may trigger further CMS review/enforcement actions.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Outline of Data Tags Used for Citing Violations (A/C-2401)
source_reference.source_text: A/C-2401 §489.20(m) Receiving Hospitals Must Report Suspected Incidences of Individuals With An Emergency Medical Condition Transferred in Violation of §489.24(e)

examples: A tertiary hospital receives an unstable patient without transfer documentation and suspects the sending facility transferred without meeting EMTALA requirements; it reports the suspected violation per §489.20(m).

A receiving hospital identifies that the patient arrived without qualified transport or life support measures and the sending hospital appeared capable; the receiving hospital reports the suspected improper transfer.
A receiving hospital receives a patient with complete transfer documentation showing patient-requested transfer and physician certification; no suspicion exists, so no report is triggered.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS deficiency citation (EMTALA tag A/C-2401)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Surveyors must not leave draft CMS-2567 with hospital
business_rules.description: During EMTALA investigations and deficiency reporting, investigators must not tell the hospital whether a violation was identified, because the RO makes the compliance determination. Investigators must not leave a draft Form CMS-2567 with the hospital and must inform the CEO (or designee) that the RO will send the deficiency information once complete.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when surveyors/investigators are conducting an EMTALA investigation and preparing deficiency documentation for RO review and completion of Form CMS-2567.
business_rules.consequences: If followed: preserves integrity of the RO compliance determination process and prevents premature disclosure of draft deficiencies. If violated: may compromise investigation process, create procedural defects, and require corrective administrative steps (e.g., re-communication through RO channels).
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Pre-Task 6 Instructions (RO determination and CMS-2567 handling)
source_reference.source_text: must not tell the hospital whether or not a violation was identified since it is the responsibility of the RO to make that determination. Inform the CEO (or his or her designee) that the RO will make the determination of compliance... Do not leave a draft of the deficiencies of Form CMS-2567 with the hospital. Inform the hospital that the RO will send that information to the hospital once it is complete.

examples: At the exit conference, the investigator explains that the RO will determine compliance and does not disclose whether EMTALA violations were found; the hospital later receives the completed CMS-2567 through official channels.
A surveyor drafts CMS-2567 findings and mistakenly leaves the draft on-site; the RO requires retrieval and reissues communications to prevent reliance on a draft document.
A hospital CEO asks if the case is 'a violation'; the investigator responds that only the RO makes that determination and provides process information only.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS investigation procedural noncompliance (administrative corrective action)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Office space lease must be written and ≥1 year
business_rules.description: To meet the Stark Law rental of office space exception at 42 CFR 411.357(a), the lease arrangement must be set out in writing, signed by the parties, and specify the premises it covers. The lease duration must be at least 1 year. If terminated with or without cause during the first year, the parties may not enter into a new lease arrangement for the same space during the first year of the original lease arrangement. A holdover immediately after expiration can qualify only if the expired lease met all requirements at expiration, the holdover is on the same terms and conditions, and the holdover continues to satisfy the requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a covered entity (or other DHS entity) relies on the Stark compensation exception for rental of office space under 42 CFR 411.357(a).
business_rules.consequences: If all conditions are satisfied, payments under the lease are treated as not constituting a financial relationship for purposes of 42 CFR 411.353. If not satisfied (e.g., not written/signed, term < 1 year, impermissible re-lease within first year, or holdover not compliant), the arrangement may fail the exception and may be treated as a financial relationship that can trigger the Stark referral prohibition for DHS under 42 CFR 411.353.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text beyond the explicit holdover allowance in 42 CFR 411.357(a)(7).

source_reference.source_text: Payments for the use of office space made by a lessee to a lessor if the arrangement meets the following requirements: (1) The lease arrangement is set out in writing, is signed by the parties, and specifies the premises it covers. (2) The duration of the lease arrangement is at least 1 year... if the lease arrangement is terminated with or without cause, the parties may not enter into a new lease arrangement for the same space during the first year... If the lease arrangement expires after a term of at least 1 year, a holdover lease arrangement... satisfies the requirements... if... on the same terms and conditions...

examples: A hospital leases 1,000 sq ft of clinic space to a physician group under a signed written lease for 3 years; it specifies the exact suite—this satisfies the writing/signature/premises and ≥1-year requirements.
A 6-month written lease is signed for office space; because the term is less than 1 year, the arrangement fails 411.357(a)(2) and cannot rely on this exception.

A 2-year lease expires and continues month-to-month on identical terms; this can qualify as a holdover only if the original lease met all requirements at expiration and the holdover continues to meet (a)(1)-(6).
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS Stark Law noncompliance exposure (referral prohibition for DHS and related payment consequences)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Leased space must exclude lessor and related parties
business_rules.description: Under the Stark Law rental of office space exception at 42 CFR 411.357(a)(3), the space rented or leased must not exceed what is reasonable and necessary for legitimate business purposes and must be used exclusively by the lessee when being used by the lessee. “Exclusive use” means the lessee (and any other lessees of the same office space) uses the office space to the exclusion of the lessor (or any person or entity related to the lessor). The lessor (or any person or entity related to the lessor) may not be an invitee of the lessee to use the office space. Payments for common areas are permitted only if they do not exceed the lessee’s pro rata share of expenses based on the ratio of space used exclusively by the lessee to total space (other than common areas) occupied by all persons using the common areas.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered entities and other DHS entities using the 42 CFR 411.357(a) rental of office space exception for arrangements implicating Stark.
business_rules.consequences: If exclusive use (to the exclusion of the lessor/related parties) and reasonable-and-necessary space conditions are met, the arrangement can satisfy this element of the exception. If the lessor or a related party uses the space during times reserved for the lessee, or is invited to use it, the exception element is not met and the arrangement may fail 42 CFR 411.357(a).
business_rules.exceptions: Common area payments are allowed if limited to pro rata share as specified; multiple lessees may use the same space if the lessor/related parties are excluded.

source_reference.source_text: The space rented or leased does not exceed that which is reasonable and necessary... and is used exclusively by the lessee... and is not shared with or used by the lessor or any person or entity related to the lessor... exclusive use means that the lessee (and any other lessees of the same office space) uses the office space to the exclusion of the lessor (or any person or entity related to the lessor). The lessor (or any person or entity related to the lessor) may not be an invitee...

examples: Two physician groups share a leased ultrasound room in alternating blocks while the hospital-lessor never uses the room during those blocks; the exclusive use test can still be met because multiple lessees are allowed if the lessor is excluded.

A hospital leases clinic rooms to a physician group but continues using one of the rooms for hospital outpatient visits during the physician’s scheduled time; exclusive use is violated because the lessor uses the space.
A lessee pays for common waiting room space at a rate calculated as its pro rata share based on its exclusively leased square footage; this can be permissible common area compensation under (a)(3).
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS Stark Law noncompliance exposure (referral prohibition for DHS and related payment consequences)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_BILLING_COMPLIANCE_33_001 sets core Stark lease exception prerequisites. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_BILLING_COMPLIANCE_33_002 is an additional element (exclusive use; reasonable/necessary space) that is only relevant if a qualifying lease exists.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the lease is not written/signed or term is invalid, the arrangement fails the exception regardless of exclusive use, increasing Stark risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Office rent must be FMV set in advance and non-referral based
business_rules.description: For the Stark Law rental of office space exception at 42 CFR 411.357(a)(4)-(6), rental charges over the term must be set in advance and consistent with fair market value (FMV). Charges must not be determined in any manner that takes into account the volume or value of referrals or other business generated between the parties, and must not use a formula based on a percentage of revenue (raised, earned, billed, collected, or otherwise attributable) from services performed or business generated in the office space. Per-unit of service rental charges are also prohibited to the extent such charges reflect services provided to patients referred by the lessor to the lessee. The lease arrangement must be commercially reasonable even if no referrals were made between the lessee and lessor.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a covered entity uses the 42 CFR 411.357(a) exception to protect a lease arrangement involving a physician (or other referral source) relationship that implicates Stark.
business_rules.consequences: If rent is FMV, set in advance, not referral-based, and commercially reasonable absent referrals, the arrangement can meet these exception elements. If rent varies with referrals or uses prohibited percentage/per-unit constructs tied to referred patients, the exception elements are not met and the arrangement may fail 42 CFR 411.357(a).
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.source_text: The rental charges over the term of the lease arrangement are set in advance and are consistent with fair market value. The rental charges... are not determined... in any manner that takes into account the volume or value of referrals... or using a formula based on—(A) A percentage of the revenue... attributable to the services performed or business generated in the office space; or (B) Per-unit of service rental charges, to the extent that such charges reflect services provided to patients referred by the lessor to the lessee. The lease arrangement would be commercially reasonable even if no referrals...

examples: A clinic pays $28/sq ft/year fixed for 2 years based on an FMV appraisal, regardless of patient volume; this supports set-in-advance and FMV.
Rent is set at 6% of professional fees collected from services performed in the leased suite; this is a prohibited percentage-of-revenue formula under (a)(5)(ii)(A).
A lease is priced as $50 per MRI performed, and the lessor refers MRI patients to the lessee; to the extent the per-unit formula reflects referred patients, it violates (a)(5)(ii)(B).
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS Stark Law noncompliance exposure (referral prohibition for DHS and related payment consequences)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_BILLING_COMPLIANCE_33_003 contains pricing/referral-related requirements for the same Stark lease exception. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_BILLING_COMPLIANCE_33_001 defines baseline structural requirements that must be satisfied first for the exception to be available.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Absent a compliant written/term lease, even perfectly FMV rent does not save the arrangement under the office space rental exception.

dependencies.rationale: Exclusive use/reasonable space (33_002) and FMV/set-in-advance/non-referral-based rent (33_003) are separate required prongs; failure of either causes exception failure.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Even with FMV rent, non-exclusive use can fail the exception; even with exclusive use, referral-based rent can fail the exception.

business_rules.rule_name: Imaging referrals must list suppliers within 25 miles
business_rules.description: For the physician self-referral disclosure requirement described in the cited text, the referring physician’s notice should be reasonably understood by all patients and must include, for each supplier on the list, at minimum the supplier’s name, address, and telephone number. If fewer than 5 other suppliers are located within a 25-mile radius of the referring physician’s office location at the time of referral, the physician must list all other suppliers present within that 25-mile radius. Providing the written list is not required if no other suppliers provide the services for which the individual is being referred within the 25-mile radius.
business_rules.conditions: Applies at the time of referral for the relevant imaging service when the physician is required to provide a written list of alternate suppliers in relation to the referral and geographic availability.
business_rules.consequences: If the written list is provided with required content (or properly omitted when no other suppliers exist), the physician satisfies the disclosure element described. Failure to provide the required list when other suppliers exist within 25 miles may create noncompliance risk for the applicable referral-related requirements described in the regulation text.
business_rules.exceptions: No written list is required if no other suppliers provide the services within a 25-mile radius.

source_reference.section_id: Supplier list requirement (25-mile radius; fewer than 5 suppliers)
source_reference.source_text: The notice should be written in a manner sufficient to be reasonably understood by all patients and should include for each supplier on the list, at a minimum, the supplier’s name, address, and telephone number. (ii) If there are fewer than 5 other suppliers located within a 25-mile radius... the physician must list all of the other suppliers... within a 25-mile radius... Provision of the written list of alternate suppliers will not be required if no other suppliers provide the services...

examples: A cardiologist refers a patient for an MRI and provides a handout listing 5 imaging centers within 25 miles, each with name, address, and phone number.
A rural clinic has only 3 imaging suppliers within 25 miles; the physician must list all 3 suppliers (not just a subset).
No imaging suppliers exist within 25 miles for the service; the physician is not required to provide a written alternate supplier list.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS program integrity risk (potential Stark-related noncompliance for applicable arrangements)

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Academic medical center faculty time safe harbor is 20% or 8 hours
business_rules.description: For the academic medical center exception conditions described, a physician will be deemed to meet the academic/clinical teaching requirement if the physician spends at least 20 percent of professional time or 8 hours per week providing academic services or clinical teaching services (or a combination). A physician who does not meet at least 20 percent or 8 hours per week is not precluded from qualifying under the paragraph, meaning other reasonable and consistent calculation methods may still support qualification.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a covered entity is evaluating whether services provided by an academic medical center meet the exception conditions described, including the referring physician’s faculty/teaching service requirements.
business_rules.consequences: If the physician meets at least 20% professional time or 8 hours/week, the physician is deemed to satisfy this element. If the physician does not meet the threshold, the physician is not automatically disqualified but must be evaluated under the reasonable and consistent method referenced.
business_rules.exceptions: A physician below 20%/8 hours/week may still qualify; the rule text explicitly states they are not precluded from qualifying.

source_reference.section_id: 42 CFR 411.355(e)(1)(i)(D) (excerpted in chunk)
source_reference.source_text: Parties should use a reasonable and consistent method for calculating a physician’s academic services and clinical teaching services. A physician will be deemed to meet this requirement if he or she spends at least 20 percent of his or her professional time or 8 hours per week providing academic services or clinical teaching services... A physician who does not spend at least 20 percent... or 8 hours per week... is not precluded from qualifying...

examples: A faculty cardiologist spends 1 day/week (8 hours) supervising residents; this meets the deemed threshold.
A faculty surgeon documents 25% of professional time in clinical teaching; this meets the deemed threshold.

A physician spends 6 hours/week teaching but the medical center documents additional academic service contributions using a reasonable, consistent method; the physician may still qualify even below 8 hours/week.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CMS Stark Law noncompliance exposure if exception conditions not met

examples: Medicare Advantage health plan (MA organization)
Hospital-based physician group billing electronically
Health care clearinghouse performing claims translation services

business_rules.rule_name: Medical staff incidental benefits are capped at $36 per instance
business_rules.description: Under the Stark Law medical staff incidental benefits exception at 42 CFR 411.357(m) as described, a hospital may provide noncash items or services to members of its medical staff when used on the hospital’s campus and certain conditions are met.
