source_reference.source_text: Between compliance risk assessments, the compliance officer should continue to scan for unidentified or new risks, by, for example, monitoring for legal and regulatory changes, enforcement actions and OIG work plan developments, and new entity acquisitions, strategies, or initiatives, and evaluating audits and investigation results. When the compliance officer or the Compliance Committee identifies a new risk, the risk should be assessed with the same methods used in the compliance risk assessment.

examples: After a new state Medicaid policy change, the compliance officer performs a targeted risk assessment using the same scoring method used in the annual risk assessment and adds a focused audit to the work plan.
Following a merger, the compliance committee assesses newly introduced referral and billing risks using the standard risk assessment methodology rather than waiting for the next annual cycle.
An internal investigation reveals a new documentation pattern; the compliance officer assesses it as a new risk and proposes monitoring controls based on the same risk framework.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Compliance program deficiency cited in enforcement resolutions; increased audit findings due to unmanaged emerging risks

examples: A health plan performs a risk analysis after integrating a new vendor API that transmits ePHI.
A business associate performs a risk analysis following expansion to new cloud regions.

dependencies.rationale: BR_24_007 requires annual compliance risk assessments for small entities. BR_27_010 requires ongoing scanning between those formal assessments using the same methods when new risks are identified.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the entity does not conduct formal risk assessments, it lacks the baseline methodology and cadence needed to execute structured interim risk scanning and assessment.

dependencies.rationale: BR_27_010 requires scanning for new/unidentified risks and assessing them. BR_31_001 ensures compliance concerns are recorded with findings and corrective actions, enabling structured identification of new risks and evidence-based reassessment.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the log is incomplete, new risks may be missed or assessed without adequate internal evidence, delaying response and increasing noncompliance duration.

business_rules.rule_name: Risk assessments must include medical necessity and safety issues
business_rules.description: When conducting risk assessments, compliance governance (e.g., compliance committees) should ensure that medical necessity, patient safety, and other quality compliance issues are included in the risk universe. This expands the risk analysis scope beyond purely financial or operational concerns to include clinical and quality compliance risks that can drive fraud/abuse exposure and patient harm.
business_rules.conditions: Applies whenever the regulated entity performs an enterprise compliance risk assessment or develops/updates the compliance risk universe used to plan audits/monitoring activities, particularly for Medicare/Medicaid or other regulated reimbursement lines of business.
business_rules.consequences: If included: audit plans and monitoring are more likely to detect early compliance failures tied to patient safety and medical necessity. If not included: the entity may under-detect clinical/quality compliance risks and increase exposure to fraud and abuse enforcement and patient safety events.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in source; organizations may tailor scope based on risk-based rationale, but the cited guidance recommends inclusion of these domains.

source_reference.section_id: V. Other Compliance Considerations > risk assessments and risk universe
source_reference.source_text: When conducting risk assessments, Compliance Committees should ensure that medical necessity, patient safety, and other quality compliance issues are included in the risk universe.

examples: A compliance committee updates its annual risk universe to include medical necessity for inpatient admissions and patient safety events tied to billing (e.g., pressure injury documentation) and builds audits accordingly.
A new telehealth entrant focuses only on cybersecurity risks; after adopting OIG guidance, it adds medical necessity and quality-of-care risks to its compliance risk assessment.
A health plan adds “stinting on care” and “discrimination against costly patients” to its risk universe when expanding into capitation-based payment models.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Increased risk of fraud and abuse enforcement exposure if key risk domains are omitted from compliance risk assessments

examples: A health plan performs a risk analysis after integrating a new vendor API that transmits ePHI.
A business associate performs a risk analysis following expansion to new cloud regions.

dependencies.rationale: BR_39_001 applies 'when conducting risk assessments' and defines required scope content (medical necessity, patient safety, quality). BR_24_007 establishes that a small entity should conduct at least annual compliance risk assessments.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If risk assessments are not performed, the entity will not build a risk universe that includes patient safety/medical necessity risks, increasing fraud/abuse and harm exposure.

dependencies.rationale: BR_27_010 requires assessing new risks between formal assessments. BR_39_001 requires that risk assessments include quality/medical necessity issues; applying that scope to interim assessments strengthens coverage and reduces missed clinical risks.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If interim risk assessments ignore clinical/quality factors, emerging patient-safety/medical-necessity risks may go unaddressed until the next annual cycle.

business_rules.rule_name: FDA legacy devices may defer MFA only if patched
business_rules.description: The proposed MFA requirement includes an exception for certain FDA-authorized devices in use. For a device within the meaning of FDCA section 201(h) that has been authorized for marketing by FDA pursuant to a submission received before March 29, 2023, the exception applies only if the regulated entity has deployed any updates or patches required or recommended by the device manufacturer.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a regulated entity seeks to rely on the proposed MFA exception for an FDA-authorized device (FDCA 201(h)) where the FDA marketing authorization submission was received before 2023-03-29, and the device is in use and relevant to ePHI system operations.
business_rules.consequences: If manufacturer-required or manufacturer-recommended updates/patches are not deployed, the regulated entity cannot rely on the exception and must implement MFA (or otherwise meet the MFA requirement without relying on this exception).
business_rules.exceptions: Exception limited to FDA-authorized devices meeting the submission date condition and only where updates/patches required or recommended by the manufacturer have been deployed.

source_reference.section_id: Proposed 45 CFR 164.312(f)(2)(iii)(C) device exceptions; submission before March 29, 2023
source_reference.source_text: within the meaning of section 201(h) of the Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act that has been authorized for marketing by the FDA. The first would be for a device authorized by the FDA for marketing pursuant to a submission received before March 29, 2023, while

examples: A legacy FDA-authorized infusion pump (submission before 2023-03-29) cannot support MFA; the hospital may rely on the exception only if it has deployed manufacturer-recommended security patches.
A clinical device meets the pre-2023-03-29 submission criterion but the hospital has not applied a critical manufacturer patch; the exception is unavailable.
A device is not FDA-authorized under FDCA 201(h); this exception does not apply even if it is “legacy.”
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR enforcement action and potential civil monetary penalty for failure to implement required technical safeguards

examples: A regulated entity implements MFA for remote access into its network containing ePHI systems.
A regulated entity implements MFA for cloud console access managing ePHI workloads.

business_rules.rule_name: Unsupported FDA devices may defer MFA only if patched
business_rules.description: The proposed MFA requirement includes an exception for an FDA-authorized device (FDCA 201(h)) authorized pursuant to a submission received on or after March 29, 2023 that is no longer supported by its manufacturer. The exception applies only where the regulated entity has deployed any updates or patches required or recommended by the manufacturer.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a regulated entity seeks to rely on the proposed MFA exception for an FDA-authorized device with FDA marketing authorization submission received on/after 2023-03-29, and the device is no longer supported by its manufacturer, and the device is in use within systems relevant to ePHI.
business_rules.consequences: If updates/patches required or recommended by the manufacturer are not deployed, the regulated entity cannot rely on the exception and must implement MFA (or otherwise meet the MFA requirement).
business_rules.exceptions: Exception limited to FDA-authorized devices meeting the submission date condition, no-longer-supported condition, and patch/update deployment condition.

source_reference.section_id: Proposed 45 CFR 164.312(f)(2)(iii)(C) device exceptions; on/after March 29, 2023 and unsupported
source_reference.source_text: The second would be for a device authorized... pursuant to a submission received on or after March 29, 2023, that is no longer supported by its manufacturer. In both cases, the exception would only apply where... the regulated entity has deployed any updates or patches required or recommended by the manufacturer of the device.

examples: A post-2023-03-29 FDA-authorized imaging device is no longer supported and cannot support MFA; the facility may rely on the exception only if all manufacturer-recommended patches have been deployed.
A device is unsupported but the facility has not applied the last recommended firmware update; the exception cannot be used.
A supported device with submission on/after 2023-03-29 is still supported by the manufacturer; it does not qualify for this specific exception path.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR enforcement action and potential civil monetary penalty for failure to implement required technical safeguards

examples: A regulated entity implements MFA for remote access into its network containing ePHI systems.
A regulated entity implements MFA for cloud console access managing ePHI workloads.

business_rules.rule_name: Notify covered entities when contingency plan is activated
business_rules.description: A proposed HIPAA Security Rule program change includes a requirement burden estimate for each regulated entity to notify covered entities or business associates, as applicable, upon activation of a contingency plan (estimated at 0.5 hours per regulated entity). For business associates, this means providing timely notice to affected covered entities when the business associate activates its contingency plan in a manner that could impact ePHI confidentiality, integrity, availability, or service delivery.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a business associate activates its contingency plan for systems/services involving ePHI and the activation could affect a covered entity’s operations or ePHI handling; applies once the proposed notification requirement is finalized and effective.
business_rules.consequences: If the business associate fails to notify, the covered entity may be unable to initiate coordinated response actions (e.g., emergency access procedures, alternate workflows), increasing risk to ePHI availability and continuity of care. Non-notification may constitute a contractual breach and increase enforcement exposure during OCR investigation of the incident/occurrence.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited burden summary; final rule may define timing, content, and triggering criteria.

source_reference.section_id: New burdens—notification upon contingency plan activation (0.5 hours)
source_reference.source_text: (10) A burden of .5 hours for each regulated entity to notify covered entities or business associates, as applicable, upon activation of a contingency plan.

examples: A business associate hosting ePHI triggers disaster recovery to a secondary region and notifies each covered entity customer within the vendor’s incident comms SLA, including expected impact and recovery steps.

A BA activates contingency operations after ransomware encrypts a production environment but does not inform the covered entity; the covered entity continues sending files into a disrupted pipeline, worsening availability issues.

A BA notifies the covered entity that MFA is temporarily infeasible due to an incident and describes compensating controls implemented under its contingency plan so the covered entity can align emergency access procedures.
business_rules.enforcement_action: corrective action plan

examples: A cloud-hosting business associate notifies a covered entity that disaster recovery failover was initiated for an ePHI system.
A billing vendor notifies a health plan that its contingency plan was activated due to a system disruption impacting claims processing involving ePHI.

dependencies.rationale: BR_34_008 requires compensating controls per contingency plan/emergency access procedures during an emergency where MFA is infeasible. BR_35_001 proposes that business associates notify covered entities upon contingency plan activation that could impact ePHI CIA or service delivery.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If notification is not provided, covered entities may not coordinate alternate workflows or emergency access steps, increasing care disruption and ePHI risk; may increase enforcement/contractual exposure.

dependencies.rationale: BR_33_010 describes emergency infeasibility and compensating controls via contingency planning. BR_35_001 requires notifying covered entities upon activation of a contingency plan that could impact ePHI CIA or service delivery (once finalized).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the BA fails to notify, the covered entity may be unable to mitigate operational/ePHI impacts during the emergency, increasing incident severity and potential OCR scrutiny.

business_rules.rule_name: Safeguards must evolve to address cloud and connected device risks
business_rules.description: Regulated entities must maintain reasonable and appropriate safeguards to protect against reasonably anticipated threats/hazards and unauthorized uses/disclosures of ePHI, and these safeguards must evolve as the healthcare environment changes. The rule expectation includes adopting safeguards to address new risks such as maintaining ePHI in the cloud, connecting medical devices and other technology to networks, and connecting ePHI systems to networks shared with systems that do not create/receive/maintain/transmit ePHI.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the regulated entity’s operating environment changes in a way that introduces new reasonably anticipated threats/hazards to ePHI (e.g., cloud adoption, network convergence, connected medical devices).
business_rules.consequences: If complied with: safeguards remain reasonable/appropriate for the current threat environment. If violated: outdated safeguards may be deemed insufficient, increasing breach likelihood and OCR enforcement risk for failing to address reasonably anticipated threats.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the provided excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: Need for safeguards to evolve with environment (reasonably anticipated threats; cloud; connected devices)
source_reference.source_text: By requiring that regulated entities maintain reasonable and appropriate safeguards to protect against reasonably anticipated threats or hazards or unauthorized uses or disclosures of ePHI, Congress clearly anticipated that the administrative, physical, and technical safeguards implemented to protect the security of ePHI would need to change ... For example, regulated entities must be expected to adopt safeguards that address new risks ... such as those posed by maintaining ePHI in the cloud; the connection of medical devices ... to networks; and the connection of information systems ... to the same networks as those do not perform such activities.

examples: A provider migrates EHR storage to a cloud provider and updates logging, encryption key management, and access controls to address cloud-specific risks.
A hospital connects infusion pumps to the enterprise network and implements segmentation and device monitoring to reduce ePHI exposure from connected devices.
A health plan merges networks so claims and HR systems share a flat network; it implements segmentation to protect ePHI systems from non-ePHI system compromises.
business_rules.enforcement_action: OCR enforcement action and potential civil monetary penalty for Security Rule noncompliance

examples: The HIPAA Security Rule applies to a hospital system operating an EHR with ePHI.
The HIPAA Security Rule applies to a cloud service vendor acting as a business associate processing ePHI.

dependencies.rationale: BR_24_007 requires periodic risk assessment for small entities. BR_40_010 requires safeguards to address reasonably anticipated threats as environment changes; risk assessment output informs what safeguards must evolve.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If risk assessments are not performed, safeguard evolution may lag behind new threats, increasing breach likelihood and OCR enforcement risk for failing to address anticipated hazards.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_007_001 selected: more complete HIPAA Security Rule articulation (explicitly includes availability along with confidentiality and integrity).
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules impose the HIPAA Security Rule requirement to implement administrative, physical, and technical safeguards for ePHI to protect core security properties. Differences are minor (one mentions considering costs/capabilities; one explicitly includes availability). Same underlying obligation and regulated population (HIPAA covered entities and business associates).
duplicate_groups.merged_description: HIPAA covered entities and business associates must implement administrative, physical, and technical safeguards to protect ePHI and ensure the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of all ePHI the entity creates, receives, maintains, or transmits; in designing safeguards, entities may consider organizational size/capabilities and costs consistent with the HIPAA Security Rule’s flexibility-of-approach.
merged_examples: Implement access controls, audit controls, and transmission security for systems handling ePHI.
Adopt facility security and workstation/device controls plus administrative policies (risk management, workforce training, sanctions).

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_14_001 selected: most operationally specific (lists the diligence actions and the 60-day timing requirement).
duplicate_groups.rationale: All rules describe the same Part 2 investigative-agency safe harbor prerequisite: perform ALL specified reasonable-diligence actions within ≤60 days before requesting records or placing an undercover agent/informant. One duplicate is higher-level/summary but still the same requirement.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: To qualify for the Part 2 investigative-agency safe harbor (limitation on civil/criminal liability when Part 2 records are unknowingly received), the investigative agency must complete ALL required reasonable-diligence steps to determine whether Part 2 applies, and must perform the steps no more than 60 days before requesting records or placing an undercover agent/informant. Required steps include checking SAMHSA’s treatment locator, relevant state databases, and publicly available provider notice/website/location indicators, and other specified diligence actions.
merged_examples: Before issuing a subpoena to a clinic, investigators run SAMHSA and state-locator checks and document results within the prior 60 days.
Before deploying an informant, the agency verifies Part 2 applicability indicators and retains screenshots/records of the checks.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: 42 CFR 2.3(b); 42 U.S.C. 290dd-2 (as amended by CARES Act)

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_33_008 selected: cleaner, more complete statement of the intermediary disclosure-list duty (writing requirement, 3-year lookback, 30-day response, required elements).
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules are the same Part 2 intermediary obligation under §2.24: upon written patient request, provide a disclosure list for general-designation disclosures limited to the past 3 years, within 30 days, including required disclosure details.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Under 42 CFR 2.24, when a patient consented to disclosures using a general designation and submits a written request, the intermediary must provide a list of persons/entities to which records were disclosed under that general designation. The request must be in writing, limited to disclosures made within the prior 3 years, and the intermediary must respond within 30 days or fewer, providing required elements for each disclosure (e.g., recipient name(s), disclosure date, and brief description of the information disclosed).
merged_examples: A patient emails a signed written request to the intermediary asking for the general-designation disclosure list for the last 3 years; the intermediary responds within 30 days with recipient names and dates.
An intermediary rejects an oral request and instructs the patient how to submit a written request, then fulfills it within the 30-day window.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_005_001 selected: most complete (explicitly aligns with HIPAA complaint process concepts and notes ability to complain to the Secretary).
duplicate_groups.rationale: All rules require a Part 2 program to maintain an internal complaint intake process for Part 2 noncompliance allegations. Duplicates vary only in emphasis (internal process vs. ‘no requirement that patient complain to program first’).
duplicate_groups.merged_description: A Part 2 program must establish and maintain an internal process to receive complaints alleging noncompliance with 42 CFR Part 2. Patients are permitted to file complaints with the Secretary using HIPAA-aligned complaint mechanisms and are not required to complain to the program first; however, the program must still maintain the internal complaint intake process.
merged_examples: The program posts a complaint submission method (paper/electronic) and logs complaints for investigation and resolution.
A patient files directly with HHS instead of the program; the program still must have its own complaint process available.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_004_002 selected: more operational guidance (halt ongoing/automatic mechanisms; no ‘pull back’ expectation).
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules express the same core Part 2 revocation handling concept: written revocation stops future disclosures, but entities are not expected to retrieve already-disclosed records. The primary includes additional operational steps (stop automatic disclosures).
duplicate_groups.merged_description: After receiving a written revocation of Part 2 consent, the disclosing program/holder is not expected to retrieve or ‘pull back’ records already disclosed under a valid consent. At minimum, the revocation must be implemented prospectively by halting further uses/disclosures within the scope of revocation and stopping ongoing or automatic disclosure mechanisms when feasible/appropriate upon receipt of revocation notice.
merged_examples: A program receives written revocation and disables an automatic HIE push-feed for that patient going forward, without attempting to recall prior disclosures.
A recipient retains records already received but honors revocation by ceasing new redisclosures once informed.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: 42 CFR Part 2 (consent/revocation provisions); 45 CFR 164.508(b)(5) (analogous HIPAA authorization revocation concept)

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_032_004 selected: clearer articulation and broader clarification language (while retaining same §2.65 requirement).
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules require criminal-proceeding Part 2 court-order applications under §2.65 to use a fictitious patient name and prohibit inclusion of patient identifying information unless the proceeding record is sealed. Same procedural requirement and confidentiality objective.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: An application for a court order under 42 CFR 2.65 to authorize use/disclosure of Part 2 records (or testimony relaying contents) to criminally investigate or prosecute a patient must use a fictitious name (e.g., ‘John Doe’) to refer to the patient and must not contain or otherwise use/disclose patient identifying information unless the court has ordered the record of the proceeding sealed from public scrutiny.
merged_examples: A prosecutor files a §2.65 application naming ‘John Doe’ and seeks a sealing order before submitting identifying exhibits.
A filing that includes the patient’s name without a sealing order is rejected/subject to corrective action to prevent disclosure.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_032_006 selected: most complete sequencing (secure, cease use/disclosure, apply within ≤120 days, disposition if no order sought).
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules impose the same investigative-agency obligations when Part 2 records are discovered in good faith during investigation/prosecution of a Part 2 program/holder: secure per §2.16, immediately cease use/disclosure, and apply for a court order within a reasonable time not to exceed 120 days; otherwise return/destroy within the same outer limit.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Upon discovering in good faith that it received Part 2 records while investigating/prosecuting a Part 2 program or record holder, an investigative agency must secure the records per 42 CFR 2.16, immediately cease using or disclosing the records until it obtains a court order authorizing use/disclosure, and apply for the court order within a reasonable time not to exceed 120 days after discovery. If no court order is sought, the agency must return or destroy the records within the same 120-day outer limit.
merged_examples: An agency quarantines the records in a restricted evidence vault and stops analyst access pending court authorization.
If authorization is not pursued, the agency documents destruction/return within 120 days.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_025_004 selected: more explicit on who may determine emergency and the treatment-purpose constraint.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules describe the same Part 2 medical emergency exception allowing disclosure without prior written consent when there is a bona fide emergency and it is infeasible to obtain consent; disclosure must be limited to medical personnel for emergency treatment.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: In a bona fide medical emergency—generally urgent clinical care for an immediately life-threatening condition where it is infeasible to obtain the individual’s consent—patient identifying information from Part 2 records may be disclosed without consent to medical personnel to support emergency treatment, limited to information relevant to treating the emergency. Any health care provider treating the patient for the emergency may determine that a medical emergency exists for this purpose.
merged_examples: During an overdose, the ED requests SUD medication history; the Part 2 program discloses relevant information for immediate treatment.
A non-emergent request for records ‘just in case’ does not qualify and requires consent or another Part 2 permission.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_24_004 selected: includes the staff-safety nuance while preserving the requirement to document the discloser identity.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules require emergency disclosures under Part 2 to document the name of the person making the disclosure. The primary adds interpretive nuance about documenting entity name in limited settings for safety while not prohibiting identifying the individual.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: When a Part 2 program makes a disclosure during a medical emergency under 42 CFR 2.51, the disclosure documentation must include the name of the person making the disclosure; the program is not prevented from identifying the individual discloser, and in limited settings may document only the entity name to protect staff safety, consistent with the stated interpretive approach.
merged_examples: Emergency disclosure log includes ‘Disclosed by: Jane Smith, RN’ (or the entity name where permitted/needed for safety).
A record that omits the discloser entirely is incomplete for Part 2 emergency documentation.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_009_006 selected: most comprehensive (requestor access, first-service timing, emergency timing, and posting/website duties).
duplicate_groups.rationale: All rules impose the same Part 2 §2.22 Patient Notice distribution obligations: provide upon request; provide to patient no later than first service after compliance date (including electronic first service); provide after emergency as soon as reasonably practicable; and ensure posting where physical/online presence exists.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: A Part 2 program must provide the §2.22 Patient Notice to anyone who requests it, and must provide it to a patient no later than the date of first service delivery (including electronic first service delivery) after the Patient Notice compliance date. In emergency treatment situations, the notice must be provided as soon as reasonably practicable after the emergency. If the program has a physical service delivery site, it must post the notice prominently; if it maintains a website describing customer services/benefits, it must make the notice electronically available as required by Part 2.
merged_examples: Telehealth-first encounter: the patient receives the Patient Notice electronically no later than the first telehealth service date.
Emergency admission: notice is provided after stabilization as soon as reasonably practicable.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_037_007 selected: most complete operational statement (copy or scope explanation; may be combined with Notice; includes feasibility note re conveying revocation).
duplicate_groups.rationale: All rules implement the same finalized §2.32(b) accompaniment requirement for consent-based Part 2 disclosures: each disclosure must include either a copy of the consent or a clear explanation of consent scope. Wording differences are non-substantive; some versions note combination with the Notice to Accompany Disclosure.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: For each disclosure of Part 2 records made with the patient’s written consent, the disclosing entity must accompany the disclosure with either (a) a copy of the written consent or (b) a clear explanation of the scope of the consent provided; the consent copy may be combined with the required Notice to Accompany Disclosure. Where feasible, disclosers should also convey to recipients when a consent has been revoked to reduce post-revocation redisclosure risk.
merged_examples: EHR exchange: attach an electronic PDF of the signed consent (or include an electronic scope statement) with the disclosure payload.
Paper fax: include the consent copy behind the cover sheet along with the Part 2 Notice to Accompany Disclosure.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_039_003 selected: includes the key guardrail that records retain their Part 2 character even when not segregated.
duplicate_groups.rationale: All rules express the same non-segmentation allowance for records received under a single all-future TPO consent by a Part 2 program/covered entity/business associate: Part 2 does not require segregation/segmentation upon receipt under this consent construct. The primary also preserves the essential caveat to continue applying Part 2 protections (especially proceedings restrictions).
duplicate_groups.merged_description: When a Part 2 program, covered entity, or business associate receives Part 2 records pursuant to a single consent for all future TPO uses/disclosures, the recipient is not required by Part 2 to segregate or segment those records from other records solely because they are Part 2 records. However, the records must retain their Part 2 status/characteristic so continuing Part 2 protections (including proceedings restrictions absent separate consent or court order) remain enforced.
merged_examples: A health system integrates Part 2 data into the enterprise EHR without segmentation, but flags the data to prevent use in proceedings against the patient absent Part 2 authorization.
An HIE receiving records under a broad TPO consent does not create a separate database, but maintains policy controls to prevent prohibited downstream uses.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_003_003 selected: more fully scoped conditions (adult; temporary incapacity; no bona fide emergency; director authority limited to payment-only).
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules state the same Part 2 §2.15(a)(2) limitation: when an adult lacks capacity (and no emergency provision is being used), the program director may exercise consent authority only for the sole purpose of obtaining payment from a third-party payer/plan.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Absent a bona fide medical emergency, when an adult patient temporarily lacks capacity to take knowing/effective action on their own behalf, the Part 2 program director’s authority under 42 CFR 2.15(a)(2) is limited to consenting to disclosures only for the sole purpose of obtaining payment for services from a third-party payer/health plan, and does not extend to other uses/disclosures beyond payment.
merged_examples: Patient is unconscious and billing submission requires limited disclosure to the insurer; director-authorized disclosure is limited to payment information.
Director cannot authorize disclosure to another provider for treatment coordination under this pathway; another Part 2 permission is needed.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_037_010 selected: more complete (includes the threat/capacity exception pathway and director determinations).
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules cover the same minor-consent structure under Part 2 in the described scenario: where state law allows a minor to obtain SUD treatment, written consent for Subpart C disclosures must be provided by both minor and parent/guardian/authorized person, with a narrowly described exception pathway tied to incapacity and substantial threat determinations.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Where state law allows a minor to obtain SUD treatment, any written consent for a use or disclosure under Part 2 Subpart C must be given by both the minor and the minor’s parent/guardian/other person authorized under state law. If the minor lacks capacity to make a rational choice due to extreme youth or mental/physical condition and there is a substantial threat to life or physical well-being that may be reduced by communicating relevant facts, the program director may make the limited disclosure of relevant facts consistent with the stated exception pathway.
merged_examples: A minor and guardian both sign the Part 2 consent before records are sent to a payer/other recipient under Subpart C.
Director documents incapacity and substantial threat and discloses only relevant facts to reduce the threat.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: 42 CFR Part 2 (minor consent provisions; Subpart C)

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_032_003 selected: clearer statement of the prohibition on conditioning access on written consent.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules state the same Part 2 patient-access operational requirement: the Part 2 program must not require the patient’s written consent/authorization as a precondition for the patient to access their own Part 2 records under §2.23.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: A Part 2 program must not require a patient’s written consent or other authorization as a precondition to providing the patient access to the patient’s own records under 42 CFR 2.23; patient access may be provided without obtaining written consent solely for that access.
merged_examples: The program provides inspection/copying access after identity verification, without requiring the patient to sign a Part 2 release form.
A policy that forces the patient to execute a consent to receive their own record is noncompliant with §2.23 as described.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_21_004 selected: more explicit that the prohibition applies to information obtained via patient access to the Part 2 record.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules express the same restriction: information obtained by a patient accessing their Part 2 record cannot be used or disclosed to initiate/substantiate criminal charges or to conduct a criminal investigation of the patient, consistent with Part 2’s criminal-use restrictions.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Information obtained through a patient’s access to their Part 2 record remains subject to restrictions prohibiting use or disclosure to initiate or substantiate criminal charges against the patient or to conduct a criminal investigation of the patient; entities holding such information must not use/disclose it for those criminal purposes absent a permitted Part 2 basis.
merged_examples: A prosecutor cannot rely on patient-accessed Part 2 record content to initiate criminal charges against the patient absent Part 2 authorization.
A law enforcement request for patient-accessed content for investigation triggers the same Part 2 restriction.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_CREATES_RECEIVES_MAINTAINS_OR_TRANSMITS_HIPAA_PRIVACY_013_008 selected: most precise conditions (public health authority + HIPAA de-identification standard + ‘no reasonable basis’ language).
duplicate_groups.rationale: All rules state the same Part 2 public health limitation: disclosure without consent to a public health authority is permitted only if the Part 2 information is de-identified in accordance with HIPAA’s 45 CFR 164.514(b) standard.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: A Part 2 program may disclose Part 2 records to a public health authority without patient consent only if the information disclosed is de-identified in accordance with 45 CFR 164.514(b) such that there is no reasonable basis to believe it can be used to identify a patient/individual.
merged_examples: A program provides de-identified overdose trend data to a state public health authority without consent.
Providing identifiable names/addresses to public health without consent is impermissible under this public health pathway.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_26_004 selected: includes breach presumption + low-probability risk assessment concept in addition to the definitional incorporation.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules incorporate HIPAA’s breach definition (45 CFR 164.402) into Part 2 breach evaluation. The primary adds the standard HIPAA presumption/rebuttal framing used for breach determinations.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: For Part 2 breach notification purposes, the definition of “breach” is adopted by reference to HIPAA at 45 CFR 164.402: an acquisition, access, use, or disclosure not permitted that compromises the security or privacy of the information. The event is presumed to be a breach unless a required risk assessment demonstrates a low probability that the information has been compromised, consistent with the incorporated HIPAA framework as applied to Part 2 records.
merged_examples: An impermissible disclosure of unsecured Part 2 information triggers a HIPAA-style breach analysis under 164.402 and related 164.402-164.414 workflows.
If the risk assessment supports low probability of compromise, the incident may be treated as not a breach under the incorporated standard.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_19_001 selected: most complete (explicitly includes insurance verification and tags; same EMTALA duty).
duplicate_groups.rationale: All rules prohibit delaying EMTALA-required screening and/or stabilizing treatment to inquire about payment/insurance status. Same EMTALA requirement and enforcement consequence profile.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: A participating hospital must not delay providing the required medical screening examination (MSE) or further examination/treatment needed to stabilize an emergency medical condition in order to inquire about method of payment, insurance status, or to verify insurance information; financial inquiries must not delay EMTALA clinical obligations.
merged_examples: Registration can collect insurance only after MSE and initiation of stabilizing treatment without delaying care.
Telling a patient to wait for insurance verification before triage/MSE is an EMTALA delay violation risk.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: 42 CFR 489.24; CMS EMTALA guidance (e.g., Tag A-2408/C-2408)

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_12_004 selected: most specific trigger logic (request made on behalf, prudent layperson standard, ancillary services routinely available).
duplicate_groups.rationale: All rules impose EMTALA’s screening duty: provide an appropriate MSE to individuals who come to the ED seeking emergency evaluation, regardless of Medicare status or ability to pay. Differences are only additional clarifying triggers in the primary.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Hospitals with a dedicated emergency department must provide an appropriate medical screening examination (MSE), within the capability of the emergency department (including routinely available ancillary services), to any individual who comes to the ED and requests examination/treatment (or a request is made on the individual’s behalf), or when a prudent layperson would conclude the individual needs examination or treatment for a medical condition; EMTALA applies regardless of Medicare eligibility or ability to pay.
merged_examples: A bystander requests help for a collapsed person at the ED entrance; the hospital must provide an MSE.
A patient arrives with chest pain; the hospital must screen even if uninsured.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_010_005 selected: clear retention statement with timeframe and EMTALA transfer context; duplicates add tag citation only.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules require retention of EMTALA transfer records for 5 years from date of transfer. Identical timeframe, context, and record category.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Hospitals with dedicated emergency departments must maintain medical and other records related to individuals transferred to and from the hospital under EMTALA transfer processes for at least five (5) years from the date of transfer.
merged_examples: Maintain transfer certification, receiving facility acceptance, and transfer packet records for ≥5 years.
Produce retained transfer records promptly during an EMTALA complaint investigation.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_010_004 selected: slightly more complete enumeration of certification pathways including QMP signature path.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules state the same EMTALA unstabilized-transfer prerequisites: transfer permitted only if patient requests after informed of risks/obligations OR physician certification benefits>risks (with QMP pathway if physician not physically present) plus associated appropriate transfer requirements.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: A hospital may transfer an individual with an unstabilized emergency medical condition only if the transfer is an appropriate transfer and one of the EMTALA prerequisite bases is satisfied: (1) the individual (or representative) requests transfer in writing after being informed of risks and hospital obligations; or (2) a physician signs a certification that the expected medical benefits outweigh the risks; or (3) if the responsible physician is not physically present in the ED, a qualified medical person may sign consistent with bylaws/rules with subsequent physician involvement/countersign as required.
merged_examples: Patient requests transfer in writing after risks/benefits explained; transfer proceeds with appropriate transfer elements met.
Physician signs benefits>risks certification with summary; transfer proceeds to accepting facility with capacity.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_013_004 selected: most complete legal-effect explanation (authorizes vs compels; holder may refuse without compulsory process).
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules state the same Part 2 legal-effect principle: a Part 2 court order authorizes disclosure but does not compel; compulsion requires subpoena/other compulsory process in addition to an authorizing order.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: A Part 2 court order entered under Subpart E authorizes a use or disclosure that would otherwise be prohibited, but does not itself compel disclosure. To compel disclosure, a subpoena or similar compulsory legal process is required in addition to an authorizing court order; without a valid subpoena/compulsory process (or if it has expired/been quashed), the record holder may refuse disclosure even if an authorizing order exists.
merged_examples: Requester obtains a Part 2 authorizing order but no subpoena; the holder may decline production absent compulsion.
A subpoena alone without an authorizing Part 2 order cannot compel disclosure of Part 2 records.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_MULTI_FACTOR_AUTHENTICATION_regulatory_35_001 selected: includes explicit compliance expectation for timely progress (no-year-long stagnation example) and the three-part requirement (plan, implement, migrate).
duplicate_groups.rationale: All rules describe the same proposed MFA exception condition: if an asset doesn’t support MFA, the entity must have and execute a written migration plan and actually migrate ePHI within a reasonable/appropriate period; a plan with no progress for a year is not reasonable.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: If a regulated entity uses a technology asset that does not support multi-factor authentication (MFA) and relies on the proposed exception for that reason, it must establish a written plan to migrate ePHI to MFA-capable technology assets, implement that plan, and actually migrate ePHI within a reasonable and appropriate timeframe. A plan with no migration progress for an entire year is not considered reasonable and appropriate.
merged_examples: Legacy application lacks MFA; entity documents migration plan, executes milestones quarterly, and completes migration within the planned period.
Entity writes a plan but takes no steps for 12 months; the exception conditions are not met.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_MULTI_FACTOR_AUTHENTICATION_regulatory_35_002 selected: most operational (ties compensating controls to contingency plan and emergency access procedures; explicitly time-limited).
duplicate_groups.rationale: All rules state the same proposed emergency MFA exception: if MFA is infeasible due to an emergency/occurrence impacting systems or ePHI CIA, the entity may temporarily deviate only for the limited infeasibility period and must apply compensating controls consistent with contingency planning/emergency access procedures.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: When MFA is infeasible during an emergency or other occurrence adversely affecting relevant electronic information systems or the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of ePHI, the regulated entity may rely on the proposed emergency exception only for the limited period MFA is infeasible and only if it implements reasonable and appropriate compensating controls in accordance with its contingency plan and emergency access procedures.
merged_examples: During an outage, entity uses emergency access accounts with enhanced monitoring and time-limited credentials until MFA services are restored.
Entity disables MFA indefinitely without compensating controls; the exception would not apply.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_CREATES_RECEIVES_MAINTAINS_OR_TRANSMITS_HIPAAPRIVACY_016_001 selected: most complete (explicitly links 164.514(b) and “no reasonable basis” language; notes recognized methods).
duplicate_groups.rationale: All rules require the same de-identification standard alignment: when de-identifying Part 2 records, use HIPAA’s 45 CFR 164.514(b) standard and ensure there is no reasonable basis to believe the data can identify a patient.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: When a regulated entity de-identifies Part 2 records for any purpose (e.g., disclosure, research, public health), it must de-identify in accordance with the HIPAA de-identification requirements at 45 CFR 164.514(b), such that there is no reasonable basis to believe the information can be used to identify a particular patient/individual, consistent with the Part 2 final rule’s alignment approach.
merged_examples: Remove HIPAA Safe Harbor identifiers or apply expert determination per 164.514(b) prior to disclosure.
If de-identification is insufficient and a patient can be re-identified, the data remains Part 2 patient identifying information.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REPORTING_010_007 selected: directly states incorporation of HIPAA breach notification provisions to Part 2 program breaches of unsecured records.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules state the same incorporation: HIPAA Breach Notification Rule provisions (45 CFR 164.400-414, referenced via 45 CFR parts 160/164 subpart D) apply to breaches of unsecured Part 2 records by Part 2 programs in the same manner as to breaches of unsecured PHI.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: For Part 2 programs, HIPAA breach notification provisions (45 CFR part 160 and 45 CFR part 164 subpart D, including 45 CFR 164.400–164.414) apply to breaches of unsecured Part 2 records in the same manner they apply to a covered entity’s breaches of unsecured PHI; Part 2 programs must follow HIPAA-aligned breach notification workflows when a Part 2 breach occurs.
merged_examples: Unsecured Part 2 laptop theft triggers HIPAA-style notification timing/content requirements for individuals and the Secretary (and media when applicable).
Failure to complete required notifications is a Part 2 and incorporated HIPAA breach-notification compliance risk.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_11_001 selected: slightly more operational detail (within capacity/capability; continue until emergency ceases or appropriate transfer).
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules state the same EMTALA stabilization duty framing: hospitals must treat/stabilize within capability and continue care until the EMC is no longer an emergency or the patient is properly transferred; inappropriate discharge/transfer violates EMTALA.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: When an individual has an emergency medical condition, the hospital must provide examination and treatment to stabilize the individual within its capacity and capability and continue providing care until the condition ceases to be an emergency or the individual is properly transferred under EMTALA; inappropriate discharge or transfer of an EMC patient is an EMTALA violation.
merged_examples: Hospital provides stabilizing treatment and admits or appropriately transfers if stabilization is beyond capability.
Hospital discharges an EMC patient without stabilization or an appropriate transfer, creating EMTALA violation risk.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REPORTING_032_002 selected: most specific about the ‘no wrong door’ concept and no dual-filing requirement.
duplicate_groups.rationale: All rules state the same Part 2 complaint right: individuals may file complaints to the Secretary (HIPAA-style process) alleging Part 2 violations by covered Part 2 actors, and policies must not require filing with both the program and the Secretary.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: For alleged violations of 42 CFR Part 2, an individual may file a complaint with the Secretary in the same manner as under 45 CFR 160.306 against a Part 2 program, covered entity, business associate, QSO, or other lawful holder. The complainant is not required to file with both the Part 2 program and the Secretary; direct filing with HHS is permitted (a ‘no wrong door’ approach).
merged_examples: A patient complains directly to HHS/OCR without first using the program’s internal complaint process.
A policy requiring ‘complain to us first and also to HHS’ is inconsistent with this complaint-right framing.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_040_001 selected: clearer normative requirement statement (must implement a process) without relying on burden-estimate framing.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules describe the same proposed Security Rule administrative requirement: implement a process to notify other regulated entities when a workforce member’s access to ePHI is terminated or changed, to enable timely downstream access control updates.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Under proposed 45 CFR 164.308(a)(9)(ii), regulated entities must implement an operational process to notify other regulated entities when a workforce member’s access to ePHI is terminated or changed, where cross-entity/shared access relationships exist, so recipients can promptly revoke/adjust access and reduce unauthorized access risk.
merged_examples: Upon employee termination, the health system notifies an affiliated entity using federated identity so accounts are disabled in both environments.
Role change reduces privileges; downstream entities receiving notice remove elevated access in shared systems.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_040_003 selected: most direct requirement statement (request and obtain BA verification reports; operational tracking/retention).
duplicate_groups.rationale: All three rules address the same proposed 45 CFR 164.308(b) obligation for covered entities to request/obtain business associate technical safeguard verification reports; two are framed as burden estimates but the business logic is identical.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Under proposed 45 CFR 164.308(b), covered entities must request and obtain verification reports/artifacts from their business associates regarding deployment/compliance with required technical safeguards under the Security Rule and must implement a process to request, receive, track, and retain these reports as vendor oversight evidence.
merged_examples: Annual BA attestation plus supporting evidence (e.g., control mappings, audit summaries) is collected and retained in vendor governance tooling.
Failure to collect verification leaves the covered entity unable to evidence required oversight during OCR review.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_BUSINESS_ASSOCIATE_REGULATORY_040_002 selected: clearest requirement language (BA must analyze/produce verification report sufficiently detailed to evidence compliance).
duplicate_groups.rationale: All rules impose the same proposed requirement on business associates: analyze cybersecurity measures against technical safeguard requirements and produce a verification report for covered entities upon request; duplicates differ only in burden-estimate framing.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Under proposed 45 CFR 164.308(b), business associates handling ePHI must analyze how their cybersecurity measures comply with applicable Security Rule technical safeguard requirements and produce a verification report (attestation/evidence package) for covered entities; the report should be sufficiently detailed to evidence compliance with the safeguards the BA is responsible for implementing and should be retained for oversight purposes.
merged_examples: BA provides a control-attestation mapped to proposed technical safeguards plus evidence of implementation/testing.
BA cannot produce verification; covered entity treats vendor as higher risk and may require remediation or termination.

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_BUSINESS_ASSOCIATE_REGULATORY_040_005 selected: most direct requirement statement (BA must implement a process to obtain subcontractor verification).
duplicate_groups.rationale: All rules describe the same proposed subcontractor-layer requirement: business associates must request and obtain verification that subcontractors handling ePHI comply with applicable technical safeguards and retain the evidence.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Under proposed 45 CFR 164.308(b), business associates using subcontractors that create, receive, maintain, or transmit ePHI must implement a process to request and obtain subcontractor verification of compliance with applicable Security Rule technical safeguards, and retain the verification as part of downstream vendor assurance.
merged_examples: BA collects subcontractor attestations and periodically refreshes them based on risk and system changes.
BA fails to obtain subcontractor verification and cannot demonstrate downstream assurance during an OCR inquiry.

deduplication_details.analysis_notes: Deduplication was conservative: rules were merged only when timeframes/thresholds/actors and required actions aligned. Many near-duplicates were intentionally not merged when one rule added distinct obligations (e.g., notice + consent-copy requirements vs consent-copy/scope-only; public health de-identification plus separate consent naming requirement; retaliation vs waiver vs complaint-process components). Several duplicates existed due to (a) repeated summaries vs operational rules, and (b) burden-estimate versions of the same proposed HIPAA Security Rule requirements; these were merged where the underlying obligation was identical.

dependencies.strength_justification: Part 2’s general prohibition/control framework is the legal prerequisite for using consent as a permitted pathway for disclosure.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_001_002 establishes that Part 2 restrictions apply regardless of subpoena/law-enforcement assertions; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_002_007 is one of the specific mechanisms that can make a disclosure permissible under Part 2.
dependencies.impact: If the entity ignores Part 2 applicability (source), it may improperly treat consent as sufficient in situations where Part 2 otherwise prohibits/limits disclosure, leading to unlawful disclosure risk.
examples: Record holder receives a subpoena and assumes disclosure is allowed if patient once signed a consent—Part 2 still controls and consent must satisfy Part 2 rules.
Law enforcement requests records and asserts it already has the info; Part 2 still applies and consent must be valid under Part 2 to disclose.

dependencies.strength_justification: Consent-validity gating depends on the content/format requirements that define what ‘conforming’ means.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_002_009 disqualifies consents that substantially fail to conform; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_002_007 supplies the required elements against which conformity is assessed.
dependencies.impact: If the content standard is not applied, the entity may treat a deficient consent as valid and disclose unlawfully.
examples: Consent lacks a meaningful description of information—entity discloses anyway.
Consent uses an invalid recipient class description—entity fails to detect deficiency.

dependencies.strength_justification: Proceedings-related consents cannot be combined, limiting the operational ‘single TPO consent’ concept in that context.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_005_003 supports single-consent workflow for TPO; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_002_010 prohibits combining proceedings consents with any other purpose, effectively overriding any attempt to fold proceedings authorization into a broader single consent.
dependencies.impact: If combined improperly, the consent may be treated as deficient for proceedings disclosures, risking unauthorized disclosure and enforcement.
examples: A single consent form authorizes ‘TPO + testimony in criminal proceeding’—not permitted.
A consent includes administrative proceeding disclosures plus routine payment disclosures—impermissible combination.

dependencies.strength_justification: Operational single-consent implementation relies on the detailed content requirements that make such a consent valid.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_005_003 describes the single-consent approach for TPO; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_002_007 provides the consent elements and rules needed to operationalize it compliantly.
dependencies.impact: If consent content rules are not met, the intended administrative simplification (single consent) fails and disclosures may become unauthorized.
examples: Program rolls out ‘single TPO consent’ but omits required intermediary/recipient details.
Program uses a generic ‘share my records’ consent that is not meaningfully specific.

dependencies.strength_justification: Written revocation is the trigger that creates the duty to stop future disclosures and halt automatic mechanisms.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_004_001 defines the required form (written) for revoking TPO consent; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_004_002 prescribes operational consequences after receipt of a written revocation.
dependencies.impact: If written revocation is not properly captured/recognized, ongoing disclosures may continue improperly, creating repeated Part 2 violations.
examples: Patient emails a signed revocation; program must stop auto-feeds to an HIE per revocation scope.
Patient orally revokes; program must assist with written revocation rather than treat oral revocation as effective.

dependencies.strength_justification: A valid written revocation creates ‘known revoked’ status, which disqualifies disclosure based on that consent.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_002_009 prohibits disclosure if consent is known revoked; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_004_001 defines the required written form that makes a revocation effective for TPO consents.
dependencies.impact: If revocations are mishandled, staff may incorrectly believe consent remains active and disclose unlawfully.
examples: Signed written revocation is scanned into the chart but not propagated to disclosure workflows.
Front desk receives written revocation but treats it as ‘informational only’ and does not stop disclosures.

dependencies.strength_justification: Who may give consent is a foundational legal validity condition; for certain minors, only the minor can consent.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_002_007 sets general consent content/structure, but BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_001_004 overrides the signer/authority requirement when minors have legal capacity under state law.
dependencies.impact: If a parent/guardian signs instead of the legally authorized minor, the consent is invalid and any resulting disclosure is unauthorized.
examples: State law allows a 16-year-old to consent to SUD treatment; program accepts parent consent to disclose for payment—invalid.
Program discloses to a guardian based on guardian-signed consent where minor had sole consent authority.

dependencies.strength_justification: This is a narrow legal exception to ordinary patient consent authority and scope (payment-only) during temporary incapacity absent emergency.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_002_007 governs standard written consent; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_003_003 allows the program director to exercise consent only for payment purposes when an adult lacks capacity and no medical emergency provision is used.
dependencies.impact: If misapplied, disclosures for treatment/operations during incapacity may occur without valid authority, constituting Part 2 violations.
examples: Adult is incapacitated post-surgery; director authorizes disclosure to insurer for payment only (allowed).
Director authorizes disclosure to another provider for treatment while patient lacks capacity (not allowed under this rule).

dependencies.strength_justification: When counseling notes disclosure relies on consent, it must still satisfy the baseline Part 2 consent elements.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_002_008 imposes additional constraints for SUD counseling notes; any written consent used for such disclosures must still meet the general Part 2 consent requirements in BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_002_007.
dependencies.impact: Without baseline compliant consent, the stricter counseling-notes pathway cannot be satisfied; disclosure becomes unauthorized.
examples: Program obtains counseling-notes consent but omits recipient class and purpose—invalid basis for disclosure.
Program uses a broad TPO consent to disclose counseling notes without meeting counseling-notes constraints.

dependencies.strength_justification: Threat-protection and workforce-compliance requirements are core elements that operationalize the general safeguard mandate.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_007_001 states the general CIA safeguard duty; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_007_002 specifies protecting against anticipated threats/impermissible uses and ensuring workforce compliance—key components of implementing 007_001.
dependencies.impact: If threat modeling/workforce compliance is missing, safeguards may be nominal and fail OCR scrutiny.
examples: Entity has technical controls but no workforce sanctions/training enforcement; fails 007_002.
Entity does not address foreseeable ransomware threats; safeguards are not designed against anticipated hazards.

dependencies.strength_justification: Distribution/posting rules depend on the notice being compliant in content first.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_009_006 requires providing/posting the §2.22 notice; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_001_005 specifies mandatory content elements that must be present in that notice.
dependencies.impact: Posting/distributing a defective notice creates systemic noncompliance across all patients/requestors.
examples: Website posts a notice missing the subpoena/court-order accompaniment statement.
Paper notice handed to patients lacks the statement about proceedings restrictions.

dependencies.strength_justification: Admission-time notice obligations presume a compliant notice artifact with required content.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_009_004 requires informing patients at admission about federal confidentiality protections; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_001_005 supplies required statements that must be included in the program’s patient notice.
dependencies.impact: Patients may receive incomplete/incorrect information about protections and court-order limits, raising complaint/enforcement risk.
examples: At admission, staff provides an outdated notice lacking court-order/proceedings details.
Capacity-delayed notice is eventually provided, but content is missing required statements.

dependencies.strength_justification: Both rules specify overlapping/related required statements regarding court-order use/disclosure and patient opportunity to be heard.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_001_005 broadly enumerates required notice statements; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_004_003 reinforces a key court-order statement element for inclusion in the Patient Notice.
dependencies.impact: If the complementary statement is omitted, the notice may fail Part 2 requirements and mislead patients about court-ordered disclosures.
examples: Notice includes proceedings limitation but omits opportunity-to-be-heard language.
Notice includes opportunity-to-be-heard but omits other required proceedings-related context.

dependencies.strength_justification: After a material change, revising the notice must be followed by prompt distribution; order matters to avoid implementing changes before the notice effective date.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_009_007 governs revision/effective-date controls for material changes; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_009_006 governs distribution/posting to patients/requestors. Revised notices must be distributed per 009_006 after 009_007 actions.
dependencies.impact: If revision controls are skipped, the program may implement changes not reflected in patient-facing notice or distribute incorrect versions.
examples: Program changes complaint contact information and implements immediately without distributing revised notice.
Program revises notice but fails to post the revised notice at physical site/website.

dependencies.strength_justification: Electronic/website notice mechanics apply as the method of satisfying general notice distribution/posting obligations when electronic channels are used.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_009_006 sets the general duty to provide/post notice (including website posting); BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_007_003 specifies how to meet those duties when a website/email/electronic first service delivery is involved.
dependencies.impact: If electronic-specific requirements are not met, the program may be noncompliant even if it believes it ‘posted a notice’ generally.
examples: Program emails notice without patient agreement for electronic notice.
Program learns an email bounced but fails to provide a paper copy.

dependencies.strength_justification: Compliance timing determines when the notice distribution obligation must be fully implemented and auditable.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_008_009 provides the effective/compliance dates for the final rule changes; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_009_006 obligations must be operationalized by the compliance date to avoid enforceable noncompliance.
dependencies.impact: If implementation is not completed by the compliance date, the entity may face enforceable notice-distribution deficiencies.
examples: Program delays website posting capability past the compliance date.
Program does not update intake workflows to deliver notice by first service date after the compliance date.

dependencies.strength_justification: Operational build for written-revocation handling must align to the compliance timeline for the final rule requirements.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_008_009 sets the compliance date; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_004_001 requires written revocation handling for TPO consents as part of the final rule operational changes.
dependencies.impact: If workflows are not updated by the compliance date, the organization may mishandle revocations and continue disclosures improperly.
examples: EHR lacks a structured revocation capture field by the compliance date.
Staff continue treating oral revocations as effective, causing inconsistent processing.

dependencies.strength_justification: Single-consent operational changes must be planned and implemented by the compliance date to be reliably used for TPO disclosures.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_008_009 provides the implementation horizon; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_005_003 describes the single-consent approach that organizations operationalize under the final rule.
dependencies.impact: If not implemented by the compliance date, the entity may be unable to rely on the intended streamlined consent framework and may introduce inconsistent consent handling.
examples: Program fails to update consent packets by the compliance date; disclosures rely on ad hoc legacy forms.
Program updates forms but not downstream disclosure systems, causing mismatches and missed revocation handling.

dependencies.strength_justification: Non-retaliation protections are reinforced and expanded across both rules and should be implemented as a unified control set.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_003_005 prohibits retaliation for exercising Part 2 rights, including filing complaints; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_005_002 reiterates non-adverse-action protections in complaint contexts and links them to intake/registration/billing workflows.
dependencies.impact: If not aligned, the program may have policy gaps (e.g., registration forms or staff behavior) that create retaliation risk.
examples: Patient who filed a complaint is denied scheduling priority or receives threats—retaliation.
Billing staff flags complainants for extra scrutiny as a punitive measure.

dependencies.strength_justification: Waiver-of-complaint-right prohibitions must be embedded into the same workflow controls that prevent adverse action and improper conditional forms.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_003_006 prohibits requiring waiver of complaint rights as a condition of treatment/payment/enrollment; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_005_002 consolidates the ‘no adverse action’ and ‘no waiver as condition’ expectations for operational workflows.
dependencies.impact: If waiver language persists, patient rights are chilled and the program is exposed to enforcement/corrective action.
examples: Intake paperwork states ‘I waive my right to file complaints’ as a condition of treatment.
Enrollment agreement requires complaint-right waiver to receive services.

dependencies.strength_justification: A waiver’s time limits/termination rules only apply if an EMTALA sanctions waiver has been properly issued under §1135 prerequisites.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_002_002 defines the conditions for issuance of an EMTALA sanctions waiver; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_002_003 governs the duration and termination of an issued waiver for a facility.
dependencies.impact: If issuance prerequisites are not met, the facility cannot claim waiver timing protections and remains fully subject to standard EMTALA sanctions.
examples: Hospital activates disaster protocol but no PHE exists—cannot invoke waiver duration rules.
President declared emergency but Secretary did not exercise §1135 authority—no waiver issuance.

dependencies.strength_justification: Sanctions protection for redirection/inappropriate transfers is only available if a valid §1135 EMTALA waiver has been issued.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_002_004 applies only when operating under a valid EMTALA waiver; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_002_002 governs whether such a waiver can be issued at all.
dependencies.impact: Absent a valid waiver, redirection or inappropriate transfers may trigger standard EMTALA sanctions.
examples: ED redirects patients to an alternate site without an issued waiver—sanctions risk.
Hospital performs emergency-necessitated transfer without meeting waiver prerequisites.

dependencies.strength_justification: Non-discrimination constraint is evaluated in the context of waiver operations; the waiver must exist to assess the ‘not exempt even under waiver’ rule in practice.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_002_005 applies ‘even if operating under an EMTALA waiver’; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_002_002 determines whether the waiver can exist based on federal declarations and §1135 activation.
dependencies.impact: If waiver is not valid, the hospital is subject to standard EMTALA rules and sanctions regardless; if waiver is valid, discrimination still triggers sanctions.
examples: Hospital claims waiver but prerequisites not met; actions reviewed under standard EMTALA.
Hospital has a valid waiver but redirects uninsured patients differently—sanctions still apply.

dependencies.strength_justification: Extensions are only relevant if CMS has provided time-limited, area-wide waiver coverage under a valid pandemic PHE waiver framework.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_002_006 requires a pandemic infectious disease PHE and time-limited area-wide waivers; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_002_002 establishes the core §1135 issuance prerequisites (including PHE declaration and §1135 authority exercise).
dependencies.impact: If prerequisites are not met, extension requests are unavailable and waiver protections expire/never attach.
examples: Hospital requests extension during a non-pandemic emergency—extensions not available.
Hospital requests extension but no §1135 waiver authority was exercised—no extension basis.

dependencies.strength_justification: Redirection/transfer sanctions protection only applies during the waiver’s active time window and while conditions remain satisfied.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_002_003 defines when a waiver starts/ends and termination triggers (72 hours or PHE termination; deactivation of plans/protocol); BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_002_004 provides the non-sanctioned conduct during active waiver operations.
dependencies.impact: If the waiver period has ended, the hospital can be sanctioned for conduct previously protected under the waiver.
examples: Hospital redirects patients on day 5 after disaster protocol activation in a non-pandemic event—waiver expired.
State preparedness plan is deactivated; hospital continues waiver-based redirection—no longer protected.

dependencies.strength_justification: The ‘still sanctionable if discriminatory’ limitation is applied during active waiver operations defined by the waiver duration/termination rules.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_002_005 is triggered during waiver-based redirection/transfers; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_002_003 defines whether the facility is still within waiver conditions/time window.
dependencies.impact: If a hospital mistakenly assumes waiver remains active, it may continue practices (including discriminatory ones) believing sanctions won’t apply—raising enforcement risk.
examples: Hospital uses payer-based criteria after waiver termination—sanctions exposure is high.
