Hospital applies discriminatory redirection during waiver window—sanctions apply notwithstanding waiver.

dependencies.strength_justification: Notification is tied to disaster protocol activation, which is also the trigger for the waiver period start.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_002_003 ties waiver operation to disaster protocol activation (and start of the 72-hour clock in non-pandemic settings); BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REPORTING_002_001 requires notifying the State Survey Agency at the time the disaster protocol is activated when relying on the waiver.
dependencies.impact: If notification is missed at activation, the facility may have difficulty substantiating waiver reliance later during investigations/surveys.
examples: Hospital activates protocol at 08:00 but notifies SA two days later—documentation gap on waiver reliance.
Hospital activates protocol but fails to notify SA entirely; later claims waiver protections for redirection.

dependencies.strength_justification: The reporting duty is relevant only when the facility is covered by and relying on an EMTALA area-wide waiver that is issuable only under §1135 prerequisites.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REPORTING_002_001 applies when taking advantage of an EMTALA area-wide waiver; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_002_002 defines when an EMTALA sanctions waiver may be issued.
dependencies.impact: If there is no valid waiver issuable/issued, the facility cannot correctly frame actions as waiver-reliant and the ‘waiver notification’ rationale fails.
examples: Facility activates disaster protocol during a local incident without federal declarations—no §1135 waiver context.
Facility claims area-wide waiver coverage but prerequisites were not met; notification does not cure lack of waiver.

dependencies.strength_justification: Proper on-call response requires an accurate on-call list that identifies who is available for stabilizing treatment.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_004_006 requires maintaining an on-call list identifying available physicians/specialists; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_PATIENT_SAFETY_001_010 addresses appropriate on-call physician response location (hospital vs office) for EMCs. The on-call list is a necessary control to operationalize appropriate response.
dependencies.impact: If the on-call list is incomplete/inaccurate, EMC patients may face delayed specialist evaluation, inappropriate referrals to offices, and EMTALA/patient safety exposure.
examples: On-call specialist is not clearly listed; ED sends patient to the specialist’s private office rather than obtaining hospital evaluation.
ED cannot reach the appropriate on-call specialist due to outdated list, delaying stabilizing treatment.

dependencies.strength_justification: Participation in a community call plan changes the required contents of the on-call list, requiring inclusion of other hospitals’ on-call physicians per the plan.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_004_006 specifies that if participating in a community call plan, the on-call list must include physicians at other hospitals pursuant to the plan; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_004_007 defines what constitutes an acceptable formal community call plan.
dependencies.impact: If the CCP is not properly defined, the on-call list may be noncompliant/ambiguous, increasing EMTALA survey risk and care delays.
examples: Hospital uses an informal CCP without signatures; on-call list includes other-hospital physicians without a compliant plan basis.
On-call list omits other hospitals’ coverage assignments because the CCP lacks clear delineation.

dependencies.strength_justification: Annual reassessment is an explicit CCP requirement and must occur to keep the plan acceptable/defensible for CMS survey purposes.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_004_007 states CCP must include required elements (including the annual assessment); BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_003_009 provides the detailed annual reassessment expectation and QA/PI approach.
dependencies.impact: If annual reassessment is not performed, the CCP may be deemed inadequate, increasing EMTALA compliance exposure for on-call coverage failures.
examples: CCP exists but no annual reassessment of specialty needs is performed.
Reassessment occurs but does not analyze specialty on-call needs for the communities served.

dependencies.strength_justification: De-identification policies are a specialized subset of the broader Part 2 security/policy framework for protecting patient identifying information.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_009_001 requires formal policies/procedures to protect Part 2 patient identifying information (paper and electronic); BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_009_002 requires formal policies/procedures for de-identification methods achieving low re-identification risk.
dependencies.impact: If the broader policy framework is missing, de-identification controls may be ad hoc, increasing risk that ‘de-identified’ data is treated as an unauthorized disclosure.
examples: Program shares ‘de-identified’ datasets without a formally approved method or governance.
Program lacks secure destruction/transfer policies and de-identification outputs are re-identifiable due to poor controls.

dependencies.strength_justification: Acknowledging patient presence in an SUD-only identified facility requires written consent ‘in accordance with Subpart C,’ which requires Part 2-compliant consent elements.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_001_003 requires written consent per Subpart C (or court order) to acknowledge presence; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_002_007 defines Subpart C consent content requirements that make such consent valid for the intended disclosure.
dependencies.impact: If consent lacks required elements, acknowledgment may become an impermissible disclosure of Part 2 patient identifying information.
examples: Family calls an SUD-only facility; staff confirms presence based on an incomplete consent missing recipient designation.
Facility is not SUD-only but acknowledgment risks revealing SUD status; staff relies on a deficient consent.

dependencies.strength_justification: Even where consent is required for acknowledgment, the consent must not be expired, revoked, materially false, or substantially nonconforming.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_001_003 allows acknowledgment only with written consent (or court order); BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_002_009 bars disclosures based on invalid/deficient consents, which would include acknowledgment disclosures.
dependencies.impact: If acknowledgment is made using an expired or revoked consent, it may be treated as an impermissible Part 2 disclosure.
examples: Staff confirms presence based on a consent that expired last month.
Staff confirms presence after receiving a written revocation but does not update the front desk script/workflow.

dependencies.strength_justification: Failure to perform the BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_14_001 reasonable diligence is the explicit trigger condition for the preclusion consequence in BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_11_008.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_11_008 applies when an agency later discovers it possesses Part 2 records and did not use reasonable diligence under §2.3; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_14_001 defines the safe-harbor diligence actions and the 60-day timing requirement that must be satisfied to avoid preclusion.
dependencies.impact: If diligence is not performed correctly/timely, the agency is precluded from seeking a court order to use/disclose those Part 2 records later discovered in its possession.
examples: Agency requests records without diligence, later learns they were Part 2, and is blocked from seeking a court order to use them.
Undercover placement occurred without the required locator/NPP checks; later-discovered Part 2 records cannot be legitimized via court order.

dependencies.strength_justification: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_15_005 refines how BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_14_001 diligence must be initiated and when it can stop; it is a strong interpretive dependency for implementing the safe harbor correctly.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_15_005 states that liability limitation applies only if all specified diligence actions are initiated, and that once any initiated action yields knowledge of Part 2 applicability, no further confirmation steps are required. This governs execution of BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_14_001.
dependencies.impact: If entities misunderstand initiation/stop rules, they may either (a) fail safe-harbor prerequisites (missing initiated steps) or (b) waste effort and still fail to evidence correct compliance logic.
examples: After finding the provider listed in SAMHSA locator, the agency stops further checks; that is acceptable under 15_005.
Agency performs only some steps (e.g., website check) and claims safe harbor; 15_005 indicates this fails because all actions must be initiated.

dependencies.strength_justification: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_14_002 sets a strong limiting condition on safe-harbor reliance established under BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_14_001.
dependencies.rationale: Even if the agency attempts to satisfy BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_14_001, BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_14_002 clarifies that safe harbor does not protect knowing improper acquisition or acquisition under false pretenses—constraining when the 14_001 pathway is usable.
dependencies.impact: If actors knowingly obtain records improperly, they cannot rely on safe harbor; compliance logic must branch away from safe-harbor protection even if diligence steps were attempted.
examples: An investigator lies to obtain SUD records; safe harbor is unavailable even if some diligence steps were done.
Records are knowingly requested using an incorrect legal process; safe harbor does not shield liability.

dependencies.strength_justification: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_14_006 sets the compliance deadline for implementing Patient Notice updates, including the specific header/contact-person requirement in BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_14_004.
dependencies.rationale: The program must implement the finalized §2.22 notice requirements within 24 months; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_14_004 is one of the concrete Patient Notice elements that must be updated by that deadline.
dependencies.impact: If the deadline passes without updating the Patient Notice header/contact process, the entity is out of compliance with finalized Patient Notice requirements.
examples: Program revises its Patient Notice header to include the discussion right and named contact person before the 24-month date.
Program misses the implementation date; surveys/audits find the notice header lacks the mandated discussion right.

dependencies.strength_justification: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_14_006 requires completion of notice-update work by a fixed date; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_14_005 governs how that updated content may be packaged (separate vs combined) within the compliance window.
dependencies.rationale: Entities deciding to issue a combined notice must ensure all required elements for each regime are present, and must complete those updates by the 24-month compliance deadline.
dependencies.impact: A combined notice missing required Part 2 elements after the deadline can create immediate noncompliance, even if HIPAA elements are present.
examples: Entity issues a joint HIPAA NPP + Part 2 Patient Notice that includes all Part 2 header elements before the deadline.
Entity combines notices but omits Part 2-required content; after the deadline, the combined notice is not acceptable.

dependencies.strength_justification: If notices are combined under BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_14_005, the combined notice must include all Part 2 Patient Notice elements, including the header/contact-person requirement in BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_14_004.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_14_005 allows a combined notice only if it contains every required element for each regime; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_14_004 is one of those required Part 2 elements.
dependencies.impact: If the combined notice omits the discussion right/contact-person header element, the combined notice fails Part 2 notice requirements.
examples: Combined notice includes: ‘You have the right to discuss this notice; contact: Privacy Officer…’.
Combined notice lacks the header statement; it is noncompliant even if other sections are complete.

dependencies.strength_justification: A court’s ability to issue a compliant Part 2 authorizing order (content constraints) generally follows a properly filed application that protects identifiers; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_013_005 governs application content, enabling the BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_013_006 order-content stage.
dependencies.rationale: In noncriminal-purpose applications, the applicant must use a fictitious name and avoid identifiers unless a permitted basis exists. This protects confidentiality during the process that leads to an authorizing order that must contain §2.64 limitations.
dependencies.impact: If the application improperly includes identifiers, confidentiality may be breached during the court process and the order pathway can be procedurally defective.
examples: A civil litigant files a ‘John Doe’ application without patient identifiers; court then issues a narrowly tailored order.
Applicant includes the patient’s name without consent/sealing; improper disclosure occurs during filing.

dependencies.strength_justification: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_013_004 depends on having a valid authorizing Part 2 court order; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_013_006 specifies the required content limitations that make an order valid/compliant under Part 2.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_013_004 distinguishes between an authorizing order vs compulsion; the authorizing order must meet Part 2 requirements (essential parts only, limited persons, protective measures). BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_013_006 provides those requirements.
dependencies.impact: If an order lacks required limitations, reliance on it as an authorizing Part 2 order is risky and may lead to unauthorized disclosures or evidentiary challenges.
examples: Order limits disclosure to essential lab results and seals the record—supports compliant response to subpoena.
Order is broad and unspecific; holder treats it as sufficient and over-discloses.

dependencies.strength_justification: To lawfully use/disclose Part 2 records in civil/administrative/legislative proceedings against a patient absent consent, entities must rely on a valid Part 2 court order process; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_013_004 defines when an order authorizes vs compels disclosure and when a subpoena is required.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_14_007 prohibits proceeding use/disclosure/testimony absent consent or court order. BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_013_004 governs the mechanics of using the court-order pathway (authorizes only; subpoena compels; without both, holder may refuse).
dependencies.impact: If entities attempt disclosure in proceedings without a valid authorizing order (and compulsory process when compulsion is asserted), they risk Part 2 violations and/or improper refusal/production decisions.
examples: Civil proceeding: holder refuses disclosure because only a subpoena exists (no Part 2 authorizing order).
Civil proceeding: authorizing order exists but subpoena expired; holder may refuse under 013_004.

dependencies.strength_justification: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_14_008 restricts criminal/admin/legislative proceeding use/testimony absent consent or court order; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_013_004 governs the required legal process for an authorizing order and when compulsion requires a subpoena/mandate.
dependencies.rationale: For proceedings, the order pathway must be correctly implemented: a subpoena alone is insufficient; an authorizing order alone does not compel; both may be needed depending on posture. This is foundational to complying with the restriction rule.
dependencies.impact: Mismanaging the order/subpoena interaction leads to unauthorized disclosures (Part 2 violations) or failure to comply with valid compulsory process when permitted.
examples: Criminal proceeding: prosecutor serves subpoena without Part 2 order; holder cannot disclose under Part 2.
Criminal proceeding: court issues authorizing order and mandate accompanies it; holder discloses within limits.

dependencies.strength_justification: If the entity relies on consent (rather than a court order) to use/disclose Part 2 records in proceedings against the patient, the consent must be standalone as required by BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_20_002; otherwise BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_14_007’s exception (consent) is not satisfied.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_14_007 allows proceeding use/disclosure only with patient consent or court order. BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_20_002 dictates the consent form-factor: it cannot be combined with any other purpose consent.
dependencies.impact: If consent is bundled (e.g., with TPO), the disclosure remains restricted and the entity risks unauthorized disclosure in a proceeding.
examples: Program uses a separate ‘Proceeding Consent’ form before producing records for an administrative hearing against the patient.
Program relies on a general TPO consent to disclose in a civil proceeding; the disclosure is noncompliant.

dependencies.strength_justification: Proceeding disclosures/testimony are restricted absent consent or court order; when relying on consent, BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_20_002 is a critical prerequisite to treat the consent as valid for BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_14_008.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_14_008 restricts use/disclosure/testimony in criminal/admin/leg proceedings against the patient. A consent exception is only satisfied if the consent is standalone and specific to the proceeding purpose.
dependencies.impact: Using a combined consent invalidates the consent pathway and can cause a Part 2 violation if records are used/disclosed in proceedings.
examples: Criminal proceeding: patient signs standalone proceeding consent; records can be disclosed within scope.
Program uses a bundled intake consent to authorize disclosure to prosecutors; consent is invalid for that purpose.

dependencies.strength_justification: Both rules enforce the same core control—separating proceeding-related consent from other purposes; they mutually reinforce compliance design for consent management.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_17_001 establishes separation for investigation/proceeding consents vs other purposes (e.g., TPO); BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_20_002 further specifies that proceeding consent cannot be combined with any other purpose consent. Together they define the guardrail against “bundled” consents for legal uses.
dependencies.impact: If entities implement only general consent workflows, they may inadvertently use invalid consents for proceeding disclosures, triggering Part 2 violations.
examples: EHR consent module forces ‘Proceeding Consent’ to be a separate document type from ‘TPO Consent’.
Compliance audit finds a single consent used for both treatment and criminal proceeding disclosures.

dependencies.strength_justification: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_013_002 provides the broad prohibition principle against using Part 2 records to initiate/substantiate criminal charges/investigations; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_14_008 operationalizes and expands proceeding contexts (including administrative/legislative).
dependencies.rationale: When evaluating whether a proposed use/disclosure in a criminal/proceeding context is permitted, entities should apply the foundational restriction in 013_002 and the expanded proceeding framing in 14_008 to ensure consistent enforcement of the limitation.
dependencies.impact: If the 013_002 restriction is not recognized, entities may unlawfully use Part 2 records to support proceedings against the patient, even if obtained downstream.
examples: A downstream recipient attempts to use Part 2 records to support criminal prosecution; both 013_002 and 14_008 restrict this absent consent/order.
An agency uses Part 2 records in a legislative hearing against the patient; 14_008 clarifies inclusion of legislative proceedings.

dependencies.strength_justification: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_21_004 is a specific instantiation of the broader prohibited-purpose restriction in BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_013_002 for information obtained via patient access.
dependencies.rationale: 013_002 applies to any person who obtains Part 2 records from covered pathways; 21_004 clarifies that even when information is obtained through patient access, it still may not be used/disclosed for criminal charge/investigation purposes against the patient—strengthening enforcement against an end-run around Part 2.
dependencies.impact: Without this linkage, parties might attempt to treat patient-accessed copies as usable for prosecution; 21_004 closes that route consistent with 013_002’s policy.
examples: Prosecutor tries to introduce a patient’s accessed copy of Part 2 record as evidence; prohibited by 21_004 consistent with 013_002.
Hospital staff consider sharing patient-accessed record content with law enforcement for investigation; barred by 21_004/013_002.

dependencies.strength_justification: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_17_003 creates a specific no-consent exception for internal supervised training that supersedes the general requirement in BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_20_001 to obtain separate consent for SUD counseling notes.
dependencies.rationale: 20_001 requires a separate consent for uses/disclosures of SUD counseling notes; 17_003 permits use/disclosure without consent for the program’s own supervised training programs (and clarifies that A/V recordings are excluded). This is an exception pathway that overrides the default consent requirement when conditions are met.
dependencies.impact: If entities do not recognize the override, they may unnecessarily require consents for internal training; if they overapply the exception, they may disclose notes without consent outside permitted training and violate Part 2.
examples: Program uses SUD counseling notes in supervised trainee case review without patient consent—permitted by 17_003.
Program shares SUD counseling notes with an external training vendor without consent—17_003 does not apply; 20_001 consent is required.

dependencies.strength_justification: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_12_002 provides the facility-wide acuity/resource assessment basis that supports staffing design and ongoing compliance with minimum staffing standards such as BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_12_001.
dependencies.rationale: While 12_001 is a minimum RN coverage requirement (with exceptions), 12_002 requires facilities to assess and adjust staffing to meet acuity needs and document the rationale; that assessment is a key compliance artifact used to justify staffing levels and detect gaps, including RN coverage planning.
dependencies.impact: Without the facility-wide assessment, the facility may be unable to evidence a compliant basis for staffing decisions and may be at higher risk of survey deficiencies if coverage gaps occur.
examples: Annual assessment identifies higher acuity wing; facility adjusts schedules to preserve 24/7 RN coverage.
Facility lacks updated assessment during a survey after RN coverage lapses; deficiency risk increases.

dependencies.strength_justification: Both rules enforce the same EMTALA anti-delay principle; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_19_002 is a more specific prohibition that complements the broader no-delay mandate in BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_19_001.
dependencies.rationale: 19_001 prohibits delays for payment/insurance inquiry; 19_002 specifically prohibits seeking payer authorization before MSE and initiation of stabilizing treatment. Implementing 19_001 typically requires also implementing the specific 19_002 control to prevent prior-authorization delays.
dependencies.impact: If the broader no-delay principle is not implemented into specific PA workflows, MSE/stabilization may be delayed and EMTALA noncompliance may result.
examples: Registration staff are trained not to request insurance authorization until after MSE and stabilizing care begins.
ED calls insurer for pre-auth before MSE—violates 19_002 and undermines 19_001’s no-delay requirement.

dependencies.strength_justification: EMTALA appropriate transfer of an unstabilized individual presupposes that the hospital has already provided the required MSE and initiated stabilizing treatment; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_19_002 enforces that these steps occur before payer-authorization activity that could delay care and improperly drive transfer timing.
dependencies.rationale: 010_004 governs when/how an unstabilized transfer may occur (request/certification by physician/QMP). 19_002 prevents the hospital from seeking prior authorization before MSE and initiation of stabilizing care—reducing the risk that transfer decisions/certifications are contaminated by financial clearance processes.
dependencies.impact: If 19_002 is violated, a transfer may be delayed or inappropriately pursued for financial reasons, increasing EMTALA violation risk even if paperwork is otherwise completed.
examples: Hospital completes MSE and begins stabilization, then certifies benefits outweigh risks and arranges transfer—aligned with 19_002 and 010_004.
Hospital seeks insurer authorization before MSE and delays care, then transfers—high EMTALA risk notwithstanding certification.

dependencies.strength_justification: The receiving hospital’s duty to accept an appropriate unstabilized transfer (when specialized capabilities and capacity exist) is activated by the sending hospital initiating an EMTALA “appropriate transfer” process described in BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_010_004.
dependencies.rationale: 010_004 defines conditions under which an unstabilized transfer can proceed (patient request after risk disclosure or physician/QMP certification). 20_005 defines the recipient-side obligation not to refuse an appropriate transfer when conditions are met. The workflow is sequential across institutions: appropriate transfer request/certification precedes acceptance.
dependencies.impact: If the sending hospital does not meet appropriate transfer conditions, the receiving hospital may dispute the transfer as not “appropriate,” and the overall transfer may fail or create enforcement exposure on either side depending on facts.
examples: Referring ED obtains physician certification and contacts specialized center; center must accept if it has capacity.
Referring hospital lacks required certification; receiving hospital requests correction before accepting to ensure EMTALA appropriateness.

dependencies.strength_justification: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_19_002 explicitly flags that receiving hospitals cannot delay acceptance of unstabilized transfers pending financial information; this is a strong operational complement to the acceptance duty in BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_20_005.
dependencies.rationale: 20_005 establishes the duty to accept appropriate transfers when specialized capability and capacity exist. 19_002 prohibits PA/financial authorization delays and notes that receiving-hospital delay pending financial info may itself be a violation—tightening the acceptance workflow controls.
dependencies.impact: If financial clearance is inserted before acceptance, the receiving hospital may violate EMTALA even when it has capacity and specialized capability.
examples: Receiving hospital accepts transfer immediately based on capacity/capability and defers insurance authorization.
Receiving hospital requests deposit/insurance authorization before acceptance—EMTALA risk under 19_002/20_005.

dependencies.strength_justification: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_010_005 record-retention duties are triggered by the occurrence of EMTALA transfers governed by BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_010_004.
dependencies.rationale: 010_005 applies to individuals transferred to/from the hospital under EMTALA transfer processes; 010_004 defines when an unstabilized EMTALA transfer can occur and the required documentation/certification conditions that become part of the retained record set.
dependencies.impact: If transfer-related records are not retained for 5 years, the hospital may be unable to defend EMTALA transfer appropriateness and is at risk for recordkeeping deficiencies supporting sanctions.
examples: Hospital retains certification, risk discussion, and transfer paperwork for 5 years after transfer.
Complaint investigation occurs 4 years after transfer; missing records create EMTALA recordkeeping deficiency.

dependencies.strength_justification: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_010_006 central-log entries must reflect dispositions including transfers; therefore transfer execution under BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_010_004 is a moderate-order dependency for accurate log completion.
dependencies.rationale: 010_006 requires that the ED central log indicate whether the individual was treated/admitted/stabilized/transferred/discharged. When a transfer occurs (including an appropriate unstabilized transfer under 010_004), the log must record that disposition.
dependencies.impact: If transfers are not correctly reflected on the log, the hospital may be cited for EMTALA central-log deficiencies even if clinical actions were appropriate.
examples: ED log records ‘Transferred’ and destination facility for an unstabilized EMTALA transfer.
Transfer occurs but the log shows ‘Discharged’; investigators cite inaccurate central log.

dependencies.strength_justification: The refusal-documentation workflow in BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_19_003 is typically triggered after the hospital has offered examination/stabilizing treatment that must not be delayed by payer authorization under BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_19_002.
dependencies.rationale: 19_003 applies when the individual refuses further exam/stabilizing treatment after it is offered and risks/benefits are explained. 19_002 ensures that the hospital reaches the point of offering/initiating stabilizing care without PA delays; that creates the conditions where refusal documentation is necessary if refusal occurs.
dependencies.impact: If PA delays prevent timely offering of care, refusal documentation may be incomplete or misleading, increasing EMTALA investigation risk and weakening the record of informed refusal.
examples: After stabilizing care is initiated, patient refuses additional testing; record documents refusal and attempts to obtain written refusal.
Hospital delays treatment while seeking PA; patient leaves—documentation is deficient and EMTALA risk increases.

dependencies.strength_justification: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_20_006’s retaliation protections are activated in the context of transfer authorization decisions for unstabilized EMCs, which are governed by BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_010_004.
dependencies.rationale: 20_006 prohibits penalizing physicians/QMPs for refusing to authorize transfer of an unstabilized EMC and employees for reporting EMTALA violations. Those protected activities occur within the transfer-authorization/certification context described in 010_004.
dependencies.impact: If the organization does not treat transfer-authorization decisions as protected activity, it may retaliate and incur EMTALA noncompliance findings even if transfer paperwork is otherwise compliant.
examples: Physician refuses to sign transfer certification because risks outweigh benefits; hospital cannot penalize physician.
Nurse reports suspected EMTALA transfer violation; hospital cannot take adverse employment action.

dependencies.strength_justification: Both rules implement a 3-year look-back disclosure reporting obligation (accounting vs intermediary list); BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_20_003 provides the patient-accounting baseline that BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_11_009 aligns with for intermediaries.
dependencies.rationale: 11_009 extends intermediary list-of-disclosures look-back to 3 years and explains alignment with §2.25 accounting of disclosures (20_003). Implementing one generally requires shared logging/data-retention controls to generate disclosure histories over a 3-year period.
dependencies.impact: If disclosure logging cannot support 3-year output, the entity/intermediary may fail patient disclosure transparency obligations and create enforcement/complaint exposure.
examples: Intermediary maintains disclosure logs for 3 years to respond to list requests, matching accounting principles.
Entity can only produce 2 years of disclosures; patient request cannot be fulfilled and noncompliance risk increases.

dependencies.strength_justification: An intermediary’s disclosure-list duties in BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_11_009 are triggered by the intermediary receiving records under a general designation consent; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_21_005 defines the required consent elements that make that intermediary receipt valid under Part 2.
dependencies.rationale: 11_009 applies when an organization qualifies as an intermediary and receives records under a general designation in a written patient consent. 21_005 requires that the consent name the intermediary and specify member participants or a properly limited class. Without a valid intermediary consent, the intermediary relationship (and downstream disclosure governance) is defective.
dependencies.impact: If the consent lacks required intermediary/member participant naming/limitations, disclosures to/through the intermediary may be impermissible and the intermediary may be unable to lawfully operate the disclosure-list workflow.
examples: Consent names the intermediary and identifies permitted member participants; intermediary can track/produce 3-year disclosure list.
Consent uses a vague general designation without naming the intermediary; intermediary receipt is not authorized.

dependencies.strength_justification: Both rules require/assume the HIPAA de-identification standard (45 CFR 164.514) as the operative test for when Part 2 protections no longer apply to data; they strongly reinforce a single de-identification compliance gate across public health and research contexts.
dependencies.rationale: 013_001 states that properly de-identified data under 164.514(b) is outside Part 2; 20_004 aligns the research de-identification standard with 164.514. Implementations should use the same de-identification controls, documentation, and expert/statistical methods for both.
dependencies.impact: If entities use inconsistent or weaker de-identification approaches, they may erroneously treat identifiable Part 2 data as de-identified and disclose impermissibly in either research or public health contexts.
examples: Entity applies 164.514 safe harbor/removal of identifiers for a public health dataset and for a research dataset using the same standard.
Research team uses a non-164.514 method and discloses; Part 2 protections still apply because de-identification is insufficient.

dependencies.strength_justification: The documentation duty is triggered only when the underlying disclosure is made under the Part 2 medical emergency exception.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATEDENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_24_004 applies only when a Part 2 program discloses Part 2 information due to a medical emergency under 42 CFR Part 2 (i.e., the scenario permitted by BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_025_004).
dependencies.impact: If the emergency criteria are not met, the disclosure may be impermissible; if the emergency disclosure occurs but the discloser name is not documented, the program is noncompliant with Part 2 emergency-disclosure documentation requirements.
examples: ED clinician determines overdose is life-threatening and consent is infeasible; Part 2 info is disclosed to treating team → program must document who made the disclosure.
Stroke patient arrives unconscious; Part 2 SUD medication history is disclosed to medical personnel → documentation must include the discloser name.

dependencies.strength_justification: Contractual breach-notice requirements (23_004) are a process control that exists because the Part 2 program has breach notification obligations (23_003).
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAAPRIVACY_23_004 is designed to ensure timely awareness of breaches by QSOs/BAs so the Part 2 program can fulfill the breach notification duties that apply to Part 2 programs under BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAAPRIVACY_23_003.
dependencies.impact: Without the underlying notification obligation framework, the contractual timing requirement loses purpose; without the contract, the program may miss deadlines for notifying affected individuals.
examples: Vendor hosting Part 2 records suffers breach → contract must require timely notice so the program can notify patients.
QSO payroll/HR system is compromised and includes Part 2 identifiers → program needs vendor notice to meet patient notification obligations.

dependencies.strength_justification: A functional compliance officer role is a practical and governance prerequisite to executing the escalation/inquiry duties described in 027_001.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_027_001 assumes a compliance officer exists and can promptly coordinate with leadership/counsel. BR_REGULATEDENTITY_REGULATORY_029_001 establishes that role with sufficient authority/resources.
dependencies.impact: If the compliance officer role is not established/empowered, suspected noncompliance may not be escalated or assessed promptly, increasing enforcement and patient-safety risk.
examples: Hotline allegation received but no empowered compliance officer exists → escalation and materiality assessment breaks down.
Billing staff reports suspected upcoding → without a resourced compliance lead, inquiry may be delayed or biased.

dependencies.strength_justification: 027_002’s 60-day reporting clock depends on completing the reasonable inquiry and forming the belief standard described in 027_001/027_002 conditions.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REPORTING_027_002 requires a reasonable inquiry and a reason-to-believe standard before reporting; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_027_001 governs prompt escalation and coordination with counsel to assess material violation indicators.
dependencies.impact: If escalation/inquiry is not performed promptly, the entity may miss the 60-day reporting expectation or report without an adequate factual basis, undermining credibility/cooperation posture.
examples: Potential Stark overpayment identified → compliance escalates and coordinates inquiry before determining credible evidence and reporting.
Anonymous tip alleging kickbacks → inquiry and leadership notification precede decision to self-report.

dependencies.strength_justification: For severe scenarios, immediate notification supersedes the more general 'no more than 60 days after determination' approach.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REPORTING_027_003 specifies a higher-urgency reporting pathway (immediate notification) for serious violations (criminal conduct, major patient safety impact, systemic failure/CIA breach), overriding the timing posture implied by BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REPORTING_027_002 for those cases.
dependencies.impact: If an entity treats a 027_003 scenario as merely a 027_002 scenario, delayed reporting can worsen enforcement outcomes and patient-safety exposure.
examples: Clear criminal diversion scheme identified → immediate government notification instead of waiting to complete a full internal investigation.
Systemic CIA breach affecting quality of care → notify government prior to or simultaneous with internal investigation.

dependencies.strength_justification: Removing subjects from work activity is a downstream investigation-integrity control that generally follows initiation of an investigation/escalation decision.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_027_004 is invoked during internal investigations when counsel/compliance believes integrity is at risk; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_027_001 governs the prompt escalation and initiation/coordination of the investigative posture.
dependencies.impact: If escalation/investigation coordination does not occur, the entity may fail to implement interim controls (like removing subjects), increasing tampering/retaliation/spoliation risk.
examples: Employee under investigation for false documentation remains in role → risk of record alteration; removal decision should follow investigation initiation.
Billing manager under FCA inquiry continues supervising coders → risk of intimidation; interim action supports integrity.

dependencies.strength_justification: Quarterly board meetings with the compliance officer require that the compliance officer role exists and has stature/access.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_027_005 presumes an empowered compliance officer can report and meet in executive session; BR_REGULATEDENTITY_REGULATORY_029_001 establishes the role with authority and senior-leader stature.
dependencies.impact: If no empowered compliance officer exists, board oversight becomes structurally deficient and risk reporting is impaired.
examples: Board schedules quarterly compliance executive session → requires a designated compliance officer with authority.
Board requests compliance risk dashboard updates → depends on empowered compliance leadership.

dependencies.strength_justification: Board assurance of independence/resources (027_006) necessarily depends on having a defined compliance officer position to empower.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_027_006 is a governance obligation to ensure the compliance officer has power/independence/resources and access; BR_REGULATEDENTITY_REGULATORY_029_001 creates the role and baseline authority/resources.
dependencies.impact: Without a formal compliance officer role, board cannot realistically fulfill empowerment/access expectations, weakening the compliance program.
examples: Board grants compliance officer direct access and budget → requires established compliance officer role.
Board restructures reporting lines to ensure independence → depends on having a designated compliance officer.

dependencies.strength_justification: Independence/conflict-of-interest controls are a substantive component of the 'power, independence, and resources' expectation.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATEDENTITY_REGULATORY_029_002 operationalizes independence by restricting conflicting duties/reporting lines, directly supporting the governance expectation in BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_027_006.
dependencies.impact: If independence safeguards are absent, the compliance officer’s ability to inform the board without repercussions may be compromised, increasing suppression/bias risk.
examples: Compliance officer reports to CFO and oversees billing → conflicts with independence safeguards and undermines board empowerment intent.
Compliance officer provides legal advice as counsel → blurs roles and can impair independent escalation to the board.

dependencies.strength_justification: Effective quarterly board sessions require the compliance officer to have uninhibited access and protection from repercussions.
dependencies.rationale: The executive session and candid discussion contemplated by BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_027_005 are practically dependent on the access/independence guarantees described in BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_027_006.
dependencies.impact: If uninhibited access is not assured, board meetings may occur but be ineffective, with material risks not surfaced.
examples: Compliance officer fears retaliation for reporting CEO concerns → board sessions fail to surface key risks without 027_006 protections.
Board cannot obtain candid compliance status due to constrained access → governance oversight weakens.

dependencies.strength_justification: An annual audit program is typically owned/managed by a resourced compliance function; without empowered leadership, auditing is unlikely to be effective.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATEDENTITY_REGULATORY_24_006 expects annual audits informed by risk assessment and remediation. An empowered compliance officer per BR_REGULATEDENTITY_REGULATORY_029_001 is a practical prerequisite to design/execute/track audits and remediation.
dependencies.impact: Without empowered compliance leadership, audits may be inconsistent, poorly scoped, or not remediated, increasing likelihood of undetected systemic noncompliance.
examples: Small entity launches annual claims audit → requires compliance officer authority to access data and enforce remediation.
Audit identifies repayment need → compliance officer coordinates repayment and process changes.

dependencies.strength_justification: Audit results commonly create the 'reasonable indications' that trigger escalation and materiality assessment duties.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_027_001 is triggered by reports or reasonable indications of suspected noncompliance. BR_REGULATEDENTITY_REGULATORY_24_006 generates those indications through auditing/monitoring and identifies systemic issues requiring escalation.
dependencies.impact: If annual audits are not performed, the organization may lack reliable triggers for prompt escalation, allowing issues to persist until discovered externally.
examples: Annual audit detects pattern of medically unnecessary services → triggers escalation and counsel coordination.
Audit flags potential systemic coding issue → initiates materiality assessment and investigation plan.

dependencies.strength_justification: Related-party cost reporting compliance is commonly tested through annual audit/monitoring activities.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATEDENTITY_REGULATORY_24_006 establishes periodic audits and remediation. BR_REGULATEDENTITY_BILLINGCOMPLIANCE_029_003 requires market/actual cost constraints for related-party costs in Medicare cost reports, which is a typical audit focus area.
dependencies.impact: If audits do not test related-party reporting, inflated costs may persist, increasing overpayment/FCA/CMP exposure when payers or MACs audit later.
examples: Audit reviews intercompany management fees in cost report → validates lower-of-cost-or-market requirement.
Audit finds lack of support for market comparables → triggers remediation and cost report adjustment.

dependencies.strength_justification: Annual audits are a primary control to detect impermissible resident overcharges beyond authorized cost-sharing.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_BILLING_COMPLIANCE_30_009 prohibits collecting beyond allowed Medicare/Medicaid cost-sharing. BR_REGULATEDENTITY_REGULATORY_24_006’s annual audit and remediation framework is a direct mechanism to test and correct billing/collection compliance.
dependencies.impact: Without audits/remediation, improper collections may continue, increasing refund obligations and enforcement risk.
examples: Audit samples resident statements and refunds excess collections → validates anti-supplementation compliance.
Audit detects routine charging for covered supplies → triggers education and process change.

dependencies.strength_justification: Auditing is a standard control to detect noncompliant financial relationships/referrals and billing tied to Stark risk.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_BILLING_COMPLIANCE_30_010 prohibits billing for DHS referred under prohibited financial relationships absent an exception. BR_REGULATEDENTITY_REGULATORY_24_006 supports ongoing monitoring to identify arrangements and claims at risk and implement remediation/repayment.
dependencies.impact: If auditing does not cover Stark risk areas, prohibited referrals/billing may persist, increasing repayment/CMP/FCA exposure.
examples: Audit crosswalks physician contracts to DHS claims → identifies potential Stark exception failures.
Audit detects missing written agreement for compensation arrangement → triggers corrective action.

dependencies.strength_justification: If the program relies on a single all-future TPO consent to satisfy 26_003, the consent must include the allowed recipient class/purpose/expiration structure described in 23_008.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_26_003 requires obtaining consent before using/disclosing for TPO. BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENTREQUIREMENT_23_008 defines key sufficiency elements for the commonly used single, all-future TPO consent form; without these elements, the consent may be invalid and cannot satisfy 26_003 for the intended TPO disclosures.
dependencies.impact: Invalid/insufficient consent undermines the legal basis for TPO uses/disclosures, making downstream use/disclosure noncompliant.
examples: Consent lists recipients as 'my treating providers and health plans' and purpose as 'TPO' with expiration 'none' → supports satisfying 26_003.
Consent fails to state a permitted expiration structure for all-future TPO → cannot safely rely on it for 26_003.

dependencies.strength_justification: Required redisclosure and refusal-consequence statements are material validity elements for all-future TPO consent used to satisfy 26_003.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_26_003 depends on having valid consent. BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENTREQUIREMENT_23_009 specifies mandatory statements for an all-future TPO consent; missing statements can render the consent incomplete/invalid, undermining 26_003 compliance.
dependencies.impact: If the consent omits required statements, disclosures made under that consent may be impermissible under Part 2.
examples: Consent includes HIPAA redisclosure caveat and refusal consequences → valid basis for TPO disclosure workflow.
Consent omits 'information may no longer be protected by Part 2' statement → consent may be invalid for TPO disclosures.

dependencies.strength_justification: Expiration language is a required consent element; a blank expiration field can invalidate the consent relied upon for TPO disclosures.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_26_003 requires obtaining consent; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_26_007 clarifies how to satisfy the expiration element for all-future TPO consents (e.g., 'none' or 'until revoked').
dependencies.impact: If expiration is blank, the consent may fail Part 2 form requirements, jeopardizing permissibility of TPO uses/disclosures.
examples: Consent uses expiration 'none' for all-future TPO → supports valid consent under 26_003.
E-consent leaves expiration field empty → consent may be insufficient, making TPO disclosures noncompliant.

dependencies.strength_justification: Fundraising is carved out of Part 2 'health care operations' for purposes of relying on general TPO consent; therefore the general TPO consent rule is insufficient in that context.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_26_003 permits TPO uses/disclosures with consent. BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_26_006 narrows what can be treated as 'operations' for Part 2 by stating TPO consent alone does not authorize fundraising, requiring an opt-out guardrail approach.
dependencies.impact: If the program relies only on general TPO consent to justify fundraising use/disclosure, fundraising-related activity may be noncompliant under Part 2.
examples: Program uses Part 2 data to solicit donations based solely on TPO consent → noncompliant absent the opt-out guardrail.
Program adds fundraising opt-out language to TPO consent workflow → aligns fundraising uses with 26_006.

dependencies.strength_justification: The copy-of-consent attachment requirement applies to disclosures made with written consent; obtaining consent is a process prerequisite to making such disclosures.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_025_002 is triggered when a disclosure is made with written patient consent. BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_26_003 establishes that consent must be obtained before TPO use/disclosure; once the entity discloses on that basis, it must attach the applicable consent form with each disclosure.
dependencies.impact: If consent is not properly obtained/managed, disclosures may be impermissible; if disclosure occurs but the consent form is not attached, the disclosure package is noncompliant.
examples: Part 2 program sends records to health plan under TPO consent → must include the consent form with the disclosure.
Program discloses to treating provider with consent but fails to attach consent → documentation noncompliance risk.

dependencies.strength_justification: The notice-to-accompany-disclosure requirement is tied to consent-based disclosures, which presuppose a valid consent workflow.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_025_003 requires an updated notice to accompany each disclosure made with written consent. BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_26_003 sets the consent-based legal basis for TPO disclosures; once disclosing on consent, the disclosure must include the required notice language.
dependencies.impact: If consent-based disclosures occur without the required notice, recipients may mishandle/redisclose and the program faces Part 2 notice noncompliance.
examples: Electronic disclosure to payer under TPO consent → must include updated Part 2 notice text.
Paper disclosure packet includes consent but outdated notice → notice requirement failure.

dependencies.strength_justification: Both requirements are typically executed together as parts of the same consent-based disclosure package and jointly support compliant recipient handling.
dependencies.rationale: When making disclosures with written consent, Part 2 programs must both (a) attach the applicable consent form and (b) include the required notice. These controls jointly define the permissions and redisclosure limitations communicated to the recipient.
dependencies.impact: If either element is missing, the disclosure package is incomplete and enforcement risk increases (invalid evidence of authorization and/or missing redisclosure limitation notice).
examples: Program transmits records with both consent form and updated notice in one electronic bundle.
Program includes notice but omits the consent copy → recipient lacks proof of authorization scope.

dependencies.strength_justification: Electronic satisfaction of the copy-of-consent requirement depends on 26_008’s recognition that electronic form/signatures can meet 2.31/2.32.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_26_008 clarifies that consent and accompanying copy/notice obligations can be met electronically (with valid e-signatures). This enables compliant execution of BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_025_002 in electronic disclosure workflows.
dependencies.impact: If electronic signatures are not valid or electronic attachment workflows are missing, the program may fail to meet the attachment requirement in routine electronic exchanges.
examples: Portal e-consent captured with legally valid e-signature; consent PDF is attached electronically to each disclosure.
EHR transmits records but fails to include the consent artifact electronically → noncompliant disclosure package.

dependencies.strength_justification: Electronic delivery of the required Part 2 notice is supported by 26_008’s allowance for electronic form to satisfy 2.32 requirements.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_025_003 requires a notice to accompany each consent-based disclosure. BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_26_008 clarifies the notice/copy-of-consent requirements can be met electronically, enabling compliant electronic exchange packaging.
dependencies.impact: If electronic notice delivery is not implemented, disclosures in electronic workflows may systematically miss the required notice content.
examples: HL7/FHIR exchange includes attached notice text in an electronic bundle.
Vendor interface strips the notice attachment during export → notice fails to accompany disclosure.

dependencies.strength_justification: Tolling delays when the accounting-of-disclosures obligations become effective; the substantive timeframe requirement is not enforceable until the tolled date arrives.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_23_007 tolls the effective/compliance dates for Part 2 accounting-of-disclosures requirements until the HIPAA/HITECH accounting rule is finalized. BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_23_006 specifies the 3-year lookback timeframe content for those accountings; it only operationally applies when the obligation is in force.
dependencies.impact: If an entity treats 23_006 as immediately required despite tolling, it may implement misaligned processes; if it ignores 23_006 after tolling ends, it will produce noncompliant accountings.
examples: Patient requests accounting before HIPAA/HITECH accounting rule finalization → new Part 2 accounting obligations are tolled.
After tolled compliance date is reached → accounting must include consent-based disclosures from prior 3 years.

dependencies.strength_justification: 26_002 is a direct tolling rule for 42 CFR 2.25 accounting; it controls when the 3-year lookback requirement becomes operational.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_26_002 specifies that §2.25 effective/compliance dates are tolled until HIPAA 45 CFR 164.528 is revised. BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_23_006 defines what the accounting must include (3-year consent-based lookback) once the accounting requirement is active.
dependencies.impact: Misinterpreting tolling can cause either premature implementation burden or late compliance once the tolling ends.
examples: Compliance team schedules §2.25 build before HIPAA 164.528 finalization → may be premature per tolling.
After revised 164.528 is finalized and compliance date set → entity must implement §2.25 content rules including 3-year lookback.

dependencies.strength_justification: Whether and when to apply modified requirements to pre-existing records is governed by the effective/compliance date framework.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATEDENTITY_REGULATORY_24_002 applies in the context of implementing finalized changes relative to the compliance date. BR_REGULATEDENTITY_REGULATORY_24_001 defines the effective and compliance dates and the implementation runway that drives the timing for applying new requirements to all Part 2 records.
dependencies.impact: If the organization mismanages the compliance date, it may either delay applying new requirements to legacy records (post-compliance noncompliance) or implement inconsistently by record creation date.
examples: Entity prepares system changes for the compliance date and applies to all Part 2 records at go-live.
Entity tries to treat pre-compliance records under old rules after the compliance date → inconsistent handling risk.

dependencies.strength_justification: Both rules define the effective/compliance timeline; aligning them is important for a single implementation plan.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_26_001 and BR_REGULATEDENTITY_REGULATORY_24_001 describe the final rule’s implementation time horizon (publication + 60 days effective; ~24 months to comply). They reinforce a unified project plan for Part 2 compliance changes.
dependencies.impact: If interpreted inconsistently, the organization may set the wrong deadline and mis-sequence implementation activities, increasing risk of late compliance.
examples: PMO uses publication date to calculate compliance deadline → validates that 24 months after publication matches 22 months after effective date framing.
Entity uses the earlier/later date incorrectly → leads to underprepared policies/training by the actual compliance date.

dependencies.strength_justification: Applying the modified Part 2 requirements to all records (regardless of creation date) is necessary for consistent consent-based TPO workflows across the record set.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_26_003 governs TPO uses/disclosures based on consent and patient rights notice. BR_REGULATEDENTITY_REGULATORY_24_002 instructs entities not to segment handling by record creation date, which supports uniform application of the new consent workflow to legacy Part 2 records after the compliance date.
dependencies.impact: If the entity segregates records by creation date, it may apply outdated consent rules to older records post-compliance date, creating inconsistent and potentially noncompliant disclosures.
examples: EHR applies the same TPO consent logic to legacy and new Part 2 records after compliance date.
Entity attempts dual workflows (old vs new) by record date → increased error and improper disclosure risk.

dependencies.strength_justification: The EHR donation exception is an explicit legal exception pathway that, when satisfied, permits what would otherwise be prohibited under Stark.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_BILLING_COMPLIANCE_30_010 prohibits DHS referrals/billing where a financial relationship exists unless an exception applies. BR_REGULATEDENTITY_REGULATORY_029_006 defines conditions of the Stark EHR items/services exception that can legalize an otherwise prohibited financial relationship.
dependencies.impact: If the exception conditions (e.g., 15% contribution, no donor financing, required written terms) are not met, the arrangement may violate Stark, requiring repayment and creating CMP/FCA risk.
examples: Hospital donates cybersecurity/EHR tools to physicians; physicians pay 15% before donation → may fit exception and avoid Stark prohibition.
Donor finances physician’s 15% contribution → exception fails; Stark risk triggers billing prohibition/repayment.

dependencies.strength_justification: Group practice profit share/productivity bonus rules are a major determinant of whether compensation arrangements meet Stark requirements and avoid the volume/value prohibition.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_BILLING_COMPLIANCE_30_010 sets the Stark baseline. BR_REGULATEDENTITY_REGULATORY_029_007 provides special rules that, when satisfied, support compliance for group practice profit shares/productivity bonuses by defining permissible ways to allocate 'overall profits' and avoid impermissible linkage to referral volume/value.
dependencies.impact: If group compensation fails the special rules, referrals can become prohibited and resulting DHS billing is at high Stark enforcement/repayment risk.
examples: Group defines component of 5+ physicians and splits overall profits per capita → deemed not directly related to volume/value.
Group uses DHS-specific productivity bonus tied to physician referrals → compensation may violate Stark requirements.

dependencies.strength_justification: Entrance conference confidentiality requirements are executed at the start of an EMTALA complaint investigation, which is initiated per 31_001.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_31_001 describes the unannounced initiation of EMTALA investigations. BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_31_003 governs the entrance conference and mandates confidentiality of complainant/patient identity unless written consent is obtained.
dependencies.impact: If confidentiality is breached during investigation initiation, the process risks privacy violations, reputational harm, and additional oversight even apart from EMTALA substantive findings.
examples: Surveyors begin unannounced EMTALA investigation → leadership conference occurs and complainant identity is withheld.
Hospital reveals complainant/patient identity during entrance conference without consent → confidentiality violation risk.

dependencies.strength_justification: Sampling methodology is part of how an EMTALA investigation/survey is conducted once initiated.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_31_001 triggers an EMTALA complaint investigation; BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_26_009 specifies record sampling expectations (e.g., 20 ED records, facility staff cannot select sample) used to perform the investigation consistently.
dependencies.impact: If sampling is not performed appropriately, the investigation may miss patterns/violations and can lead to inadequate corrective action validation on revisit surveys.
examples: Survey team selects 20 ED cases including transfers and LWBS → aligns with sampling methodology.
Facility staff pre-selects 'best' charts → violates sampling rules and undermines investigation integrity.

dependencies.strength_justification: Investigation documentation timelines and CMS-2567 completion depend on the investigation record review process that uses sampling.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REPORTING_31_002 governs post-onsite investigation documentation and submission timelines; the findings that must be documented derive from the record review work performed using the sampling approach in BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_26_009.
dependencies.impact: If sampling/record review is incomplete or biased, CMS-2567 documentation may be incomplete or inaccurate, delaying CMS determinations and potentially requiring follow-up surveys.
examples: Surveyors complete sampled chart reviews and substantiate potential violations → CMS-2567 submitted within 10 business days.
Inadequate sampling misses repeat-transfer pattern → later discovered, prompting additional survey and documentation burden.

dependencies.strength_justification: Refusal-to-sign documentation is key evidentiary support that surveyors rely on when documenting EMTALA findings on CMS-2567.
dependencies.rationale: BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_DOCUMENTATION_31_004 requires documenting refusal to sign and best efforts. BR_REGULATED_ENTITY_REPORTING_31_002 requires complete documentation of substantiated potential violations on CMS-2567; contemporaneous refusal documentation helps validate compliance with screening/consent best-efforts expectations.
dependencies.impact: If refusal/best-efforts documentation is absent, survey documentation may interpret gaps as process failures, increasing likelihood of adverse findings.
examples: Patient refuses to sign AMA/refusal form; staff documents refusal and best efforts → supports defense during EMTALA survey documentation.
Chart lacks any note of refusal/best efforts → surveyors document deficiency more readily on CMS-2567.

dependencies.strength_justification: Part 2 criminal-charge/investigation prohibition in 040_008 only applies if the information meets the Part 2 applicability triggers defined in 040_007.
dependencies.rationale: Rule 040_007 defines when SUD/AUD information is within Part 2 scope. Rule 040_008 incorporates that trigger as a condition for the criminal investigation/prosecution restriction, so applicability determination is a gating step.
dependencies.impact: If 040_007 is not correctly applied, the organization may misclassify data as non-Part 2 and improperly use/disclose it for criminal investigation/prosecution purposes, creating high enforcement risk.
examples: A program treats post-1972 SUD data as non-Part 2 and shares it with law enforcement to support a criminal case against the patient.
Legacy records maintained as part of an ongoing treatment episode are ignored, and investigators use them for patient prosecution without Part 2 controls.

dependencies.strength_justification: 040_008 establishes a core prohibition on using/disclosing Part 2 information for criminal charges/investigation of the patient; 032_006 operationalizes required steps when an investigative agency discovers it has Part 2 records in that context.
dependencies.rationale: When the use/disclosure purpose is criminal investigation/prosecution of a patient, the agency must halt use/disclosure and obtain court authorization (032_006) to avoid violating the restriction described in 040_008.
dependencies.impact: If 032_006 steps are not followed, the agency’s continued use/disclosure is likely prohibited under 040_008, increasing risk of Part 2 enforcement, evidentiary challenges, and sanctions.
examples: Investigators discover Part 2 records in their files and continue reviewing them for patient prosecution without pausing and seeking an order.
An agency receives Part 2 records in good faith and shares them internally for patient-criminal case development before obtaining a court order.

dependencies.strength_justification: 032_006 requires obtaining a court order within a defined window; 032_004 defines mandatory application safeguards (fictitious name/sealing) needed to seek that order compliantly.
dependencies.rationale: To meet 032_006’s requirement to apply for court authorization, the application process must comply with 032_004’s de-identification-by-fictitious-name and sealing constraints to avoid impermissible disclosure in the application itself.
dependencies.impact: If 032_004 is not satisfied, the act of seeking the order may itself disclose patient identifiers impermissibly, compounding noncompliance and increasing litigation/sanctions risk.
examples: Prosecutor files an order application naming the patient without a sealing order, while trying to comply with 032_006’s court-order requirement.
An investigative agency drafts a court application that includes patient identifiers in publicly accessible pleadings.

dependencies.strength_justification: A compliant court order process (032_005) in this context depends on a compliant application process (032_004) that protects patient identifiers unless sealed.
dependencies.rationale: If the application is procedurally defective (contains identifiers without sealing), it undermines the lawful basis for the order process and creates Part 2 disclosure risk at the front end of the court authorization pathway.
dependencies.impact: Failure to comply with 032_004 can lead to impermissible disclosure, court pushback/sealing issues, and heightened Part 2 enforcement exposure even if an order is ultimately issued.
examples: Court denies or limits the order after finding the application improperly identified the patient publicly.
Application uses patient’s real name and becomes part of an unsealed public docket.

dependencies.strength_justification: 032_006 prohibits continued use/disclosure until a court order authorizes it; 032_005 defines the required constraints that the authorizing order must contain.
dependencies.rationale: The only pathway in 032_006 to resume/continue use/disclosure is obtaining a 2.65 order. That order must meet 032_005’s minimization and recipient/purpose limitations to be valid for compliant use/disclosure.
dependencies.impact: If an order is missing required limitations (or production exceeds the order), continued use/disclosure remains noncompliant, with high enforcement and sanctions risk.
examples: Agency obtains a broad order and discloses entire record to multiple units beyond the responsible prosecutors.
Order authorizes disclosure but does not limit use to investigation/prosecution of the specified extremely serious crime.

dependencies.strength_justification: 032_003’s patient-access-without-consent rule applies to access requests for Part 2 records; 040_007 provides the key applicability trigger for whether the requested information is Part 2-covered.
dependencies.rationale: Correctly classifying the requested data as Part 2-covered is necessary to apply the correct access standard and avoid imposing improper preconditions.
dependencies.impact: Misclassification can cause improper denial/delay of access or improper policy requirements (e.g., demanding written consent), driving Part 2 complaint and enforcement exposure.
examples: Program treats SUD data as non-Part 2 and imposes HIPAA-authorization-like steps before releasing to the patient.
Program fails to recognize pre-1972 records are covered due to ongoing episode maintenance and mishandles access rights.

dependencies.strength_justification: 17_006 creates a specific exception/limitation for SUD counseling notes: they are not required to be disclosed to the patient even though Part 2 patient access rights apply broadly.
dependencies.rationale: When the requested content is properly classified as SUD counseling notes maintained separately, 17_006 governs the disclosure obligation and modifies how 32_003 should be operationalized for that subset of records.
dependencies.impact: If this override is not applied, the program may mistakenly treat counseling notes as always-disclosable (increasing sensitive disclosure risk) or may incorrectly require written consent for patient access.
examples: Patient requests counseling notes; staff releases them automatically under a general access workflow without clinician discretion.
Program denies access to the entire record unless the patient signs a consent, rather than providing access to the Part 2 record while handling counseling notes per 17_006.

dependencies.strength_justification: Complaint pathways (032_002) become materially more relevant and likely to be used when patient access rights (032_003) are violated; together they form an enforceable rights-and-remedies structure.
dependencies.rationale: Although 032_002 is not a formal prerequisite, it functions as the accountability mechanism when 032_003 is not followed (patients can complain directly to the Secretary).
dependencies.impact: If 032_003 fails, complaint volume and enforcement exposure increase because 032_002 enables direct reporting without internal gating.
examples: Program requires written consent before giving a patient their own record; patient files a complaint directly with HHS.
Patient is told they must complain to the program first; complaint policy violates 032_002 and exacerbates the access issue.

dependencies.strength_justification: Notice-timing failures (032_001) are a common trigger for Part 2 complaints; 032_002 defines the permitted complaint channeling and prevents improper gatekeeping.
dependencies.rationale: These rules combine to create a compliant patient-facing transparency and recourse framework: provide updated notice timely, and allow complaints to be filed directly with the Secretary.
dependencies.impact: If updated notices are late or missing, 032_002 facilitates direct complaints and increases the probability of investigation and corrective action demands.
examples: Program materially changes treatment-related requirements but does not provide updated notice by the first day of care; patient complains to the Secretary.
During an emergency, notice is not provided as soon as reasonably practicable; patient bypasses internal process per 032_002.

dependencies.strength_justification: 33_008’s disclosure-list obligation is triggered by disclosures made pursuant to a patient consent with a general designation; 35_009 ensures the consent is Part 2-valid in the first place.
dependencies.rationale: If the consent instrument is not Part 2-compliant, disclosures under it are unauthorized, and the intermediary’s disclosure-tracking obligations become part of a noncompliant chain.
dependencies.impact: Invalid consent can turn an intended general-designation workflow into an impermissible disclosure pattern, increasing complaint risk and forcing remediation (re-consent + re-disclosure corrections).
examples: Intermediary uses a generic consent form with ‘any provider’ language and later cannot properly satisfy 33_008 because the disclosures were unauthorized.
Patient requests a 3-year disclosure list; intermediary discovers the underlying consents lacked Part 2 elements.

dependencies.strength_justification: For minors where state law allows minor SUD treatment, 037_010 imposes a more specific dual-consent requirement that supersedes general consent-form sufficiency assumptions.
dependencies.rationale: Even if a consent has the standard 2.31 elements (35_009 context), it may still be invalid for minors unless signed by both minor and parent/guardian (unless the threat/capacity pathway applies).
dependencies.impact: Failure to apply the override can lead to unauthorized disclosures involving minors and high-risk enforcement and patient harm (privacy breach) scenarios.
examples: Minor signs a Part 2 consent alone; program discloses records to a school counselor without obtaining parent/guardian consent where required.
Program discloses to prevent a substantial threat but fails to document director capacity determination and limits to relevant facts.

dependencies.strength_justification: Maintaining ‘SUD counseling notes’ separately (17_001) depends on correctly defining what qualifies as SUD counseling notes and what must be excluded (16_001).
dependencies.rationale: If excluded elements are incorrectly placed into the counseling-notes bucket, the segregation boundary becomes clinically and operationally invalid, undermining the separate-maintenance control.
dependencies.impact: Miscalssification can cause inappropriate withholding of clinically necessary information from the main medical record or inappropriate disclosure of sensitive notes due to mixing content types.
examples: Medication monitoring and treatment plan summaries are placed into counseling notes, making routine care coordination difficult.
EHR module labels progress summaries as counseling notes, resulting in overly restrictive access for treating clinicians.

dependencies.strength_justification: 17_006’s access/disclosure treatment for counseling notes assumes those notes are maintained separately, which is required/expected by 17_001.
dependencies.rationale: The discretionary disclosure concept for counseling notes is operationally feasible only if the notes are segregated; otherwise, access workflows may inadvertently include them in standard record releases.
dependencies.impact: If separation is not implemented, the program may accidentally disclose counseling notes or be unable to honor discretionary withholding without blocking access to the rest of the record.
examples: Patient portal exports the full record including counseling notes because notes are stored in the same document set.
ROI staff cannot separate counseling notes during fulfillment and either over-disclose or over-withhold.

dependencies.strength_justification: 006_008 applies only to information that qualifies as ‘SUD counseling notes’; 16_001 governs the correct content boundaries for that category.
dependencies.rationale: Consent-exception pathways for counseling notes (originator treatment, training, legal defense) require that the material truly meets the counseling-notes definition; otherwise, the entity may inappropriately apply the exception to general medical record content.
dependencies.impact: Misclassification could result in disclosures without consent of information that is not eligible for the exception, creating Part 2 compliance risk.
examples: Program treats lab results as counseling notes and discloses them without consent under a training exception.
