Entity uses the legal-defense exception for records that are actually part of the general Part 2 record, not counseling notes.

dependencies.strength_justification: An effective CMS-required compliance and ethics program for nursing facilities (032_007) strongly depends on having effective reporting channels (31_008), including anonymous, independent paths.
dependencies.rationale: While 31_008 is not the sole component of a compliance program, it is a core detection and escalation mechanism that supports prevention/detection goals required by 032_007.
dependencies.impact: Without anonymous/independent reporting, compliance issues are more likely to go undetected or be suppressed, increasing survey/enforcement exposure and undermining compliance program effectiveness.
examples: A nursing facility has a compliance plan on paper but no anonymous hotline; staff fear retaliation and do not report billing concerns.
Reporting options are not posted or included in training, causing underreporting of resident care concerns.

dependencies.strength_justification: A Security Rule compliance audit cycle (35_004) is a practical validation mechanism for verifying implementation of the access-change notification process required by 040_001 (if finalized/effective).
dependencies.rationale: Audit procedures can test whether the entity actually notifies other regulated entities when workforce access changes and whether evidence is retained (tickets, logs, notices).
dependencies.impact: If audits are not performed/documented, the entity may be unable to demonstrate operational compliance with 040_001 and may miss control failures leading to unauthorized access.
examples: Audit discovers terminated employee accounts remain active at an affiliated entity because notifications were not sent.
OCR investigation requests evidence of access-change notifications; entity cannot produce audit records or workflow artifacts.

dependencies.strength_justification: Monthly pharmacist medication regimen review (036_007) is a primary monitoring/validation mechanism for detecting unnecessary drugs and supporting psychotropic oversight requirements in 036_008.
dependencies.rationale: 036_008’s requirements (diagnosed condition necessity, gradual dose reductions, behavioral interventions unless contraindicated) depend on active monitoring; pharmacist reviews systematically identify noncompliant psychotropic use patterns.
dependencies.impact: Without monthly reviews, inappropriate psychotropic initiation/continuation may persist, increasing CMS deficiency risk and resident harm.
examples: Consultant pharmacist flags an antipsychotic without documented diagnosis or without GDR attempts.
Pharmacist review identifies missed behavioral interventions prior to continuing a psychotropic drug.

dependencies.strength_justification: Compliance with psychotropic necessity/GDR requirements (036_008) is a key prerequisite control to preventing psychotropics from functioning as impermissible chemical restraints under 036_009.
dependencies.rationale: Psychotropics are a common chemical-restraint vector; ensuring they are clinically necessary and actively reduced where appropriate helps demonstrate the medication is for medical symptoms rather than convenience/discipline.
dependencies.impact: If 036_008 is not met, psychotropic use is more likely to be deemed a chemical restraint, increasing enforcement risk and patient harm exposure.
examples: Resident receives sedating antipsychotic for staff convenience without a diagnosed condition—chemical restraint risk.
Facility continues psychotropic therapy indefinitely without GDR attempts or documented contraindication.

dependencies.strength_justification: 38_008’s allowance for modifying compensation while still meeting ‘set in advance’ depends on the baseline definition/requirements in 38_007.
dependencies.rationale: You can only determine whether a modification still qualifies as ‘set in advance’ if you first satisfy and apply the definitional criteria of what ‘set in advance’ means and how it must be documented.
dependencies.impact: If 38_007 is not met (no pre-service writing/objectively verifiable formula), modifications cannot preserve exception compliance, increasing Stark Law noncompliance and repayment risk.
examples: Parties change compensation retroactively after services were furnished; fails ‘set in advance’ under 38_007/38_008.
Compensation formula is too vague to verify; later modification does not cure the lack of objective detail.

dependencies.strength_justification: To rely on the in-office ancillary services exception, entities must satisfy both a location option (such as 38_001) and the billing requirement (38_004); they operate together as co-required elements.
dependencies.rationale: Meeting the 35/30-hour location test does not by itself satisfy the exception unless DHS billing is also performed by a permitted biller per 38_004.
dependencies.impact: Even if location conditions are met, noncompliant billing arrangements can break the exception and trigger Stark repayment/penalty exposure.
examples: Group meets 35/30-hour test, but DHS is billed by an unrelated entity not permitted under 38_004.
Location qualifies, but agent-biller arrangement fails 42 CFR 424.80(b)(5) requirements.

dependencies.strength_justification: The ‘usual-care with 8/6-hour’ location option (38_002) must be paired with the in-office ancillary billing requirement (38_004) to complete exception compliance.
dependencies.rationale: Exception reliance is multi-element; satisfying one location pathway still requires correct billing-by-permitted-entity to avoid prohibited referral billing risk.
dependencies.impact: Incorrect biller selection can negate exception protection even where the patient-usual-care and office-hour thresholds are met.
examples: Usual-care test is met, but DHS is billed under a non-group third-party billing number.
Office is open 8 hours/week, but services are billed by a non-wholly-owned entity without proper structure.

dependencies.strength_justification: The ‘physician present’ location option (38_003) must still be combined with 38_004’s billing-of-service requirements to satisfy the overall exception.
dependencies.rationale: Presence/premises criteria address where and under what occupancy conditions DHS is furnished, while 38_004 addresses who may bill; both are required for exception protection.
dependencies.impact: Entities that meet presence criteria but bill through a disallowed party still face Stark billing/payment prohibitions.
examples: Referring physician is present and orders DHS, but DHS is billed by a separate management company not permitted under 38_004.
Physician present criteria met; agent billing fails Medicare reassignment rules.

dependencies.strength_justification: 38_005 (temporary noncompliance) is only relevant when an exception element (including billing under 38_004) becomes noncompliant and the entity seeks a limited cure period to continue billing.
dependencies.rationale: Temporary noncompliance is a fallback mechanism triggered by a break in compliance; billing-of-service failures are a common break scenario that can prompt reliance on the cure provisions if conditions are met.
dependencies.impact: If the entity cannot satisfy 38_005’s prerequisites (prior 180-day compliance, beyond control, cure within 90 days, etc.), claims during the noncompliance period may be prohibited and repayable.
examples: Group inadvertently bills DHS under the wrong NPI for 30 days and attempts to rely on 38_005 while correcting processes.
Billing agent contract changes cause short-term noncompliance; entity documents prompt corrective steps to fit within 38_005.

dependencies.strength_justification: 38_006’s reconciliation protection requires the arrangement to otherwise comply with an applicable exception; ‘set in advance’ (38_007) is a common required element for many compensation exceptions.
dependencies.rationale: If compensation was not set in advance in a compliant way, the arrangement may fail its exception, making reconciliation timing insufficient to restore protection.
dependencies.impact: Late or incomplete compliance with core exception elements can prevent use of reconciliation protection, increasing repayment/penalty exposure.
examples: Arrangement terminates and parties reconcile within 90 days, but compensation was never documented in advance—protection may fail.
Formula existed but was not objectively verifiable; reconciliation does not cure the underlying exception defect.

dependencies.strength_justification: When simultaneous on-call is allowed, written backup policies (003_010) are strongly needed to ensure the hospital can still meet EMTALA on-call response expectations in 001_009.
dependencies.rationale: A defined contingency plan is essential to operationally achieve timely in-person specialty response when the scheduled on-call physician is unavailable due to competing facility obligations.
dependencies.impact: Without backup procedures, the hospital is more likely to experience delayed specialist response and potential EMTALA violations for failure to provide appropriate stabilizing treatment.
examples: On-call surgeon is covering two hospitals and is at the other facility; no backup plan exists and ED patient waits hours.
Hospitals do not communicate simultaneous call coverage details; treating physician cannot secure required in-person consultation.

dependencies.strength_justification: Both rules address improper remuneration intended to influence healthcare decision-making; applying beneficiary inducements concepts (31_006) complements reserved-bed anti-kickback risk controls (032_009).
dependencies.rationale: Although they target different actors/beneficiaries (patients vs referral sources), they share core fraud-and-abuse logic: remuneration should not be intended to influence selection/referrals for reimbursable services.
dependencies.impact: If inducement/AKS risk concepts are not jointly managed, organizations may design arrangements (e.g., reserved beds) with hidden remuneration intent, increasing program integrity and false claims exposure.
examples: Hospital pays a nursing facility excessive reserved-bed fees to secure Federal program referrals (AKS risk) while also offering patient perks to steer selection (CMP risk).
Arrangement includes double-dipping for occupied beds and is defended as ‘operational convenience’ without evaluating inducement intent.

dependencies.strength_justification: Creates an explicit regulatory exception that removes the formal policy/procedure obligation for a defined class of lawful holders (informal caregivers).
dependencies.rationale: Before applying the Part 2 formal security P&P requirement to a lawful holder, the entity must be classified; if the holder is an informal caregiver, the P&P requirement is inapplicable.
dependencies.impact: If the exception is not checked, an informal caregiver may be incorrectly required to implement formal policies/procedures, creating false noncompliance findings and unnecessary controls.
examples: A family member stores a loved one’s Part 2 discharge summary at home; the program incorrectly treats them as required to maintain written security policies.
A friend holding printed Part 2 instructions is audited under the organization’s Part 2 security P&P standard, despite the exception.

dependencies.strength_justification: Exception directly negates the minimum-required formal policy/procedure obligation for the excluded holder category.
dependencies.rationale: The obligation in 42 CFR 2.16(a) to maintain formal policies/procedures applies only if the lawful holder is not an informal caregiver excluded by the exception.
dependencies.impact: Misapplication causes over-enforcement of formal security documentation requirements on excluded holders, distorting compliance posture and training expectations.
examples: An informal caregiver is told they must implement document destruction/sanitization procedures like a provider organization.
A program’s compliance policy incorrectly extends formal P&P requirements to all lawful holders without exception screening.

dependencies.strength_justification: Both rules articulate the same Part 2 security P&P requirement (42 CFR 2.16) and together represent the complete topic coverage needed for implementation.
dependencies.rationale: Organizations typically operationalize the requirement by combining the minimum-elements view (010_008) with the detailed topic coverage (037_001) to ensure no required areas are missed.
dependencies.impact: If only one is implemented, required paper/electronic handling elements may be incomplete, leading to partial compliance and increased breach/unauthorized disclosure risk.
examples: Policies address electronic transmission but omit paper transfer/removal and hard-copy media sanitization.
Destruction procedures exist for paper but not for EHR exports and associated electronic media.

dependencies.strength_justification: HIPAA safeguard expectations and Part 2 security policy requirements are typically implemented as a unified privacy/security control set; each strengthens the other operationally.
dependencies.rationale: Entities that are both HIPAA covered entities and Part 2 programs generally rely on HIPAA safeguard frameworks to implement Part 2-required security policies and procedures comprehensively.
dependencies.impact: If HIPAA safeguards are weak, Part 2 security P&P are less likely to be effective, increasing unauthorized use/disclosure and audit failure likelihood.
examples: Workforce access controls are not enforced, undermining Part 2 confidentiality even if a written Part 2 policy exists.
Improper disposal practices violate both HIPAA safeguard expectations and Part 2 security handling requirements.

dependencies.strength_justification: Security P&P and breach notification P&P are interlocking parts of a coherent incident lifecycle program (prevent → detect → respond/notify).
dependencies.rationale: Breach notification procedures are most reliably executed when security policies define how records are protected, handled, and evaluated as “unsecured” vs protected, and how incidents are managed.
dependencies.impact: Without aligned security P&P, breach response may be inconsistent, poorly documented, or delayed, increasing enforcement exposure after an incident.
examples: No defined process exists to determine whether compromised Part 2 data were secured/encrypted, delaying notification decisions.
Incident response teams cannot determine required mitigation steps because handling/destruction standards were never defined.

dependencies.strength_justification: The four-factor risk assessment is only triggered after determining an impermissible use/disclosure of unsecured PHI that does not fall within an exception—this is a legal decision gate.
dependencies.rationale: Rule 007_006 establishes breach presumption and exceptions; only if the incident is not excluded does 007_007 apply to rebut the presumption via required risk assessment factors.
dependencies.impact: If 007_006 is not applied first, entities may incorrectly skip exceptions or apply risk assessments to non-breaches, causing under- or over-notification and enforcement risk.
examples: A workforce member’s good-faith, within-scope access is treated as a breach and assessed unnecessarily.
An impermissible disclosure is incorrectly treated as non-breach without performing the required four-factor assessment.

dependencies.strength_justification: Breach notification policies must be triggered by a correct breach determination; 007_006 defines what constitutes a breach (or not) at the threshold level.
dependencies.rationale: Part 2 breach notification procedures are aligned to HIPAA breach constructs; the organization must first classify the incident using the breach definition/exceptions before proceeding to notification workflows.
dependencies.impact: If breach definition logic is not applied, the entity may notify when not required or fail to notify when required, increasing OCR/Part 2 enforcement risk.
examples: An inadvertent internal disclosure between authorized staff is incorrectly reported to patients as a breach.
A true impermissible disclosure is mislabeled as ‘not a breach’ and never enters the breach notification workflow.

dependencies.strength_justification: Notification and documentation steps depend on the risk assessment outcome (low probability rebuttal vs reportable breach).
dependencies.rationale: If the four-factor assessment cannot establish low probability of compromise, the incident is treated as a breach and Part 2 breach notification procedures must be executed.
dependencies.impact: If the assessment is missing/deficient, the entity cannot defensibly avoid notification, increasing enforcement exposure and audit failure risk.
examples: No documentation of whether PHI was actually viewed; the entity cannot support a low-probability finding.
Mitigation steps are not evaluated, weakening the risk assessment conclusion and forcing late notifications.

dependencies.strength_justification: Breach response programs require contemporaneous investigation documentation to evidence actions taken, mitigation, and notification decisions.
dependencies.rationale: 24_003 calls for documenting actions taken in response to breaches; 027_007 specifies what an internal investigation record should contain to make the response defensible.
dependencies.impact: Without investigation records, the entity may be unable to prove timely response, mitigation, and rationale for notification decisions, increasing penalties and corrective action likelihood.
examples: An incident is handled verbally with no witness/document log; later the entity cannot reconstruct decisions for OCR.
Disciplinary/corrective action is taken but not documented, undermining claims of mitigation and prevention measures.

dependencies.strength_justification: Both are required content components of the Part 2 patient-facing notice; together they form a complete compliant notice structure.
dependencies.rationale: 009_005 addresses plain-language notice structure and core sections (uses/disclosures, rights, complaints, contact), while 31_009 adds mandatory rights statements (3-year accounting) and effective date rules.
dependencies.impact: If either component set is missing, the notice may be deficient under Part 2, triggering mandated revisions and re-distribution and increasing enforcement exposure.
examples: Notice includes uses/disclosures but omits the mandated 3-year accounting right statement.
Notice has required rights but lacks the required header/contact/complaint process information.

dependencies.strength_justification: The notice statement in 31_009 must accurately reflect an underlying enforceable patient right; 007_008 defines the accounting right and scope.
dependencies.rationale: To draft a compliant notice statement about the 3-year accounting of disclosures, the program must align notice language with the actual accounting obligations (including EHR/TPO limitations).
dependencies.impact: If the underlying accounting rule is misunderstood, the notice may misstate rights (over/under inclusive), leading to patient complaints and Part 2 noncompliance findings.
examples: Notice promises accounting of all TPO disclosures, but the program only accounts for EHR-based TPO disclosures, creating a mismatch.
Notice omits accounting rights entirely because the program did not implement the accounting process.

dependencies.strength_justification: Both address patient-facing communications about rights/complaints; accessibility/meaningful access requirements strengthen the notice/complaint process implementation.
dependencies.rationale: 009_005 requires notice to describe complaint processes; 025_009 provides civil-rights-informed guidance to ensure those materials are understandable and accessible for LEP and disability accommodations.
dependencies.impact: If accessibility is not addressed, complaint materials may be unusable for portions of the population, increasing civil rights and Part 2 complaint-handling risk.
examples: Complaint instructions exist only in English for a predominantly Spanish-speaking population.
No auxiliary aids are provided for patients with visual/hearing impairments to understand complaint submission steps.

dependencies.strength_justification: The abbreviated notice is an operational option that supports broader notice obligations when EHR character limits constrain notice delivery.
dependencies.rationale: When disclosures require a notice-to-accompany-disclosure and systems limit message length, 19_008 provides a compliant mechanism consistent with the broader notice communication expectations reflected in 009_005.
dependencies.impact: If the abbreviated notice option is not used where needed, disclosures may occur without required accompanying notice, increasing Part 2 compliance risk.
examples: HIE exchange truncates the notice text; the organization fails to use the approved 80-character abbreviated notice.
EHR outbound interface sends Part 2 data with no accompanying notice due to character-limit constraints.

dependencies.strength_justification: Single-consent-for-future-TPO relies on §2.31-compliant consent content; 016_001 defines required consequence statements when treatment is conditioned on signing.
dependencies.rationale: To operationalize a single future TPO consent, the consent form must satisfy §2.31 content requirements, including transparency about refusal consequences when conditioning treatment.
dependencies.impact: If consequence statements are omitted in conditioning contexts, the consent may be noncompliant, undermining reliance on the single-consent mechanism for ongoing TPO uses/disclosures.
examples: Program requires signature to receive services but the consent form does not state what happens if the patient refuses to sign.
A single-consent workflow is implemented, but forms fail §2.31 content requirements, invalidating routine TPO disclosures.

dependencies.strength_justification: These are parallel patient restriction rights under HIPAA and Part 2 program obligations; together they define the restriction ruleset for Part 2 programs that are also covered entities.
dependencies.rationale: HIPAA establishes restriction request rights (including self-pay-to-plan restriction); Part 2 program rule 007_009 mirrors and adds Part 2-specific conditions and enforcement expectations for agreed restrictions.
dependencies.impact: If only one standard is operationalized, the program may mishandle restriction requests (especially paid-in-full health plan restriction), leading to impermissible disclosures.
examples: Patient self-pays in full and requests no disclosure to health plan; program fails to implement the mandatory restriction.
Program agrees to a restriction but later violates it due to incomplete workflow mapping.

dependencies.strength_justification: Restriction-request rights may be exercised by a personal representative; determining PR status is a decision gate for who can invoke the right.
dependencies.rationale: Before honoring a restriction request made by someone other than the patient, the entity must determine if that person qualifies as the patient’s personal representative for relevant PHI.
dependencies.impact: If PR status is not assessed, the entity may improperly deny a valid representative or improperly accept restrictions from an unauthorized person.
examples: A court-appointed guardian requests a restriction; the hospital denies because it fails to recognize PR authority.
A non-authorized family member requests a restriction and is incorrectly treated as PR, leading to improper disclosures/record flags.

dependencies.strength_justification: Accounting requests may be made by a personal representative; PR identification determines who can request and receive the accounting.
dependencies.rationale: Entities must treat a valid PR as the individual for relevant PHI, including fulfilling accounting-of-disclosures rights where applicable.
dependencies.impact: Failure to verify PR status can cause unauthorized disclosure of accounting data or improper denial, triggering complaints and enforcement risk.
examples: Executor/PR requests accounting for deceased patient’s records where permitted; request is mishandled due to missing PR verification workflow.
An unauthorized person receives an accounting report containing sensitive Part 2 disclosure information.

dependencies.strength_justification: Restriction requests under Part 2 can be made by a PR where the PR stands in the patient’s place for relevant PHI; PR status is a gating condition.
dependencies.rationale: A program must confirm legal authority before accepting and implementing restrictions requested by someone acting on the patient’s behalf.
dependencies.impact: If PR workflows are absent, restrictions may be improperly accepted/denied, leading to violations (e.g., disclosures to plans contrary to a valid restriction).
examples: A PR requests the paid-in-full restriction; the program refuses because it does not recognize PR authority.
A non-PR requests restriction and is incorrectly granted control over Part 2 disclosures.

dependencies.strength_justification: 15_002 explicitly states that where consent is required for deceased-patient Part 2 disclosures, the personal representative may provide that consent—requiring PR determination first.
dependencies.rationale: To obtain valid consent for disclosures identifying a deceased patient as having SUD (outside vital statistics/cause-of-death reporting), the entity must confirm the requester is the legally authorized personal representative.
dependencies.impact: If PR status is not established, disclosures may proceed without valid consent or be improperly blocked, creating compliance and patient/family harm.
examples: A deceased patient’s PR authorizes disclosure to an attorney; entity denies due to not recognizing PR authority.
Entity accepts consent from a family member without PR authority and discloses Part 2 information improperly.

dependencies.strength_justification: For redisclosures permitted by HIPAA without authorization (notably TPO), 005_004 removes the need to use the authorization pathway described in 17_004.
dependencies.rationale: 17_004 is used when an authorization is needed and must be Part 2-specific; 005_004 clarifies that many HIPAA redisclosures (e.g., TPO) do not require additional authorization, reducing unnecessary authorization collection.
dependencies.impact: If the override is missed, organizations may impose unnecessary authorizations, delaying care coordination and operations; if misapplied the other way, disclosures may occur without required authorization.
examples: Hospital requires Part 2-specific authorization for routine treatment coordination that HIPAA permits without authorization.
Staff assumes no authorization is ever needed for redisclosure of received Part 2 records and discloses for non-TPO purposes improperly.

dependencies.strength_justification: Authorization-based disclosures must still comply with HIPAA’s general limits/safeguards; 006_003 provides the foundational HIPAA privacy rule constraints.
dependencies.rationale: Even when using a HIPAA authorization to disclose Part 2 records (17_004), the covered entity must apply appropriate safeguards and comply with HIPAA use/disclosure conditions.
dependencies.impact: If 006_003 controls are absent, authorized disclosures may still be executed insecurely or beyond permissible scope, increasing OCR risk.
examples: Authorization is valid, but records are emailed without appropriate safeguards, leading to impermissible exposure.
Disclosure exceeds the minimum necessary or agreed scope due to weak privacy controls.

dependencies.strength_justification: Transfer obligations are evaluated after stabilization efforts; if stabilization/admission is not possible or patient requests transfer, transfer processes apply.
dependencies.rationale: 18_003 is triggered when the patient requests transfer or the hospital lacks capability/capacity to stabilize/admit; that determination arises after the stabilization duty context is reached.
dependencies.impact: If stabilization workflows are not executed/assessed first, the hospital may transfer prematurely or fail to arrange a necessary transfer, increasing EMTALA enforcement risk.
examples: Hospital transfers a trauma patient without attempting available stabilizing interventions within capacity.
Hospital delays transfer despite lacking specialty capability, worsening patient outcome and EMTALA exposure.

dependencies.strength_justification: Defines when EMTALA obligations are triggered outside the ED; if triggered, the hospital must provide an appropriate MSE within capability.
dependencies.rationale: When an individual presents on hospital property (not ED) with request/signs meeting prudent layperson standard, EMTALA screening obligations apply, leading to MSE requirements.
dependencies.impact: If the trigger is not recognized, the hospital may fail to screen/stabilize individuals outside the ED, creating EMTALA violations.
examples: Patient collapses in the parking lot; staff directs them off-campus without initiating EMTALA screening.
Family requests help for a symptomatic person in a hospital-owned building within the campus radius; hospital fails to respond with an MSE process.

dependencies.strength_justification: Stabilization duty and definition apply after an EMC determination made via MSE; stabilization standards guide subsequent treatment and transfer decisions.
dependencies.rationale: The MSE determines whether an EMC exists; once identified, 013_007 defines stabilization and requires stabilizing treatment within capability.
dependencies.impact: If MSE-to-stabilization sequencing is broken, the hospital may fail to treat EMCs appropriately or misclassify stability, increasing risk of improper transfer/discharge.
examples: ED identifies EMC but fails to provide further exam/treatment to stabilize.
Hospital labels a patient 'stable' without applying the stabilization definition tied to transfer risk of deterioration.

dependencies.strength_justification: QMP certification pathway requires physician consultation/agreement; 11_004 establishes the consult requirement when physician not physically present.
dependencies.rationale: 08_004 allows QMP signature with later physician countersign and consultation; 11_004 underpins the required consult process and governance via bylaws/board-approved documents.
dependencies.impact: If consult is not performed/documented, QMP certification pathway may fail, invalidating transfer justification and increasing EMTALA liability.
examples: QMP signs certification without consulting physician; countersignature later cannot cure missing consult evidence.
Hospital bylaws do not define QMP authority, undermining validity of the QMP pathway.

dependencies.strength_justification: Sending required EMC-related records is a required component of an appropriate transfer; without it, the appropriate transfer standard is not fully met.
dependencies.rationale: 08_006 specifies the medical records that must accompany an appropriate transfer; it operationalizes 08_005’s requirement to ensure appropriate treatment continuity and risk minimization.
dependencies.impact: If records are not sent, transfer may be deemed inappropriate and patient safety is compromised due to missing clinical information.
examples: Diagnostic test results are not sent; receiving facility delays care awaiting records.
Certification/consent copy is missing from the transfer packet, undermining EMTALA defensibility.

dependencies.strength_justification: Qualified personnel/transport and life support measures are required elements of effecting an appropriate transfer.
dependencies.rationale: An 'appropriate transfer' includes safe execution; 08_007 defines transport staffing/equipment and life support measures needed to meet the standard implied by 08_005.
dependencies.impact: If transport is unsafe or lacks required life support, the transfer may be EMTALA-noncompliant and creates significant patient harm risk.
examples: Critical patient is transported without appropriate monitoring or oxygen support.
No qualified personnel accompany a high-risk transfer requiring advanced life support.

dependencies.strength_justification: 18_006 sets the overarching requirement to obtain/attempt written informed refusal; 08_008 provides specific documentation content for transfer refusal scenarios.
dependencies.rationale: When refusal involves transfer, 08_008 operationalizes 18_006 with required elements (risks/benefits, reasons for refusal, description of proposed transfer, and alternative documentation if signature refused).
dependencies.impact: If refusal documentation is incomplete, the hospital may be unable to demonstrate EMTALA compliance and may receive survey deficiencies and enforcement actions.
examples: Patient refuses transfer; chart lacks reasons for refusal and evidence that risks/benefits were explained.
Patient refuses to sign; hospital fails to document the refusal-to-sign and steps taken.

dependencies.strength_justification: Receiving-hospital acceptance obligations apply to appropriate transfers where the receiving facility has specialized capability and capacity; appropriateness criteria frame the transfer context.
dependencies.rationale: 18_004 is evaluated in the context of a transfer that should be appropriate; 08_005 defines acceptance/capacity elements that align with the receiving hospital’s duty to accept when specialized capability exists.
dependencies.impact: If the transfer context is not evaluated as appropriate/capacity-based, receiving hospitals may improperly refuse or accept transfers, increasing EMTALA noncompliance risk.
examples: Specialty center with capacity refuses a transfer despite specialized capability.
Receiving facility claims no acceptance duty but does not assess capacity and capability consistently.

dependencies.strength_justification: The receiving hospital’s duty to report suspected EMTALA-violative transfers relies on evaluating the transfer against appropriate transfer criteria.
dependencies.rationale: To 'suspect' an unstable improper transfer, the receiving hospital must compare the circumstances against appropriate transfer requirements (e.g., acceptance agreement, stabilization efforts, documentation/transport adequacy).
dependencies.impact: If the receiving hospital cannot validate against the appropriate transfer standard, it may fail to report within 72 hours, risking termination of its provider agreement.
examples: Receiving hospital notes no prior acceptance call and minimal documentation, suggesting inappropriate transfer and triggering reporting duty.
Patient arrives without required records and unstable; receiving hospital delays reporting due to lack of EMTALA criteria assessment.

dependencies.strength_justification: The on-campus movement rule applies after an individual is initially screened (or presents to ED); the screening context is required to evaluate movement conditions.
dependencies.rationale: 12_006 requires the patient be initially screened (or present to ED) and then moved on-campus for further screening/stabilization; thus 12_004’s MSE context is antecedent.
dependencies.impact: If the initial screening context is missing, on-campus movement may be treated as an EMTALA transfer or discriminatory movement based on ability to pay, increasing enforcement risk.
examples: Patient is moved to another department without initial ED screening; movement is later treated as an improper transfer.
Patients with same condition are not moved uniformly, suggesting payment-based disparate treatment.

dependencies.strength_justification: Stability/appropriateness disputes in investigations rely heavily on contemporaneous clinician documentation, including physician certifications when used.
dependencies.rationale: When the hospital asserts the patient was stable, surveyors assess reasonableness; transfer certification and its risk/benefit summary are key evidence for stability/appropriateness determinations.
dependencies.impact: If certification documentation is weak/missing, the hospital’s ability to meet its burden of proof is reduced, increasing likelihood of an EMTALA violation finding.
examples: Investigation requests proof of stability; hospital lacks the certification summary of risks/benefits used to justify transfer.
QIO review finds documentation insufficient to support claimed stability at transfer time.

dependencies.strength_justification: Medical record completeness directly affects ability to defend stability and appropriateness during an EMTALA investigation where the burden of proof rests on the hospital.
dependencies.rationale: Surveyors may request records supporting clinical status and transfer appropriateness; missing transfer packets and EMC-related records weaken the hospital’s evidentiary showing.
dependencies.impact: If documentation is incomplete, the hospital may be unable to demonstrate that it was reasonable to believe no material deterioration would occur during transfer.
examples: Key diagnostic results were not available/sent; hospital cannot show what information supported the stability decision.
On-call refusal documentation is missing, complicating surveyor review of transfer appropriateness.

dependencies.strength_justification: Broad Part 2 TPO use/disclosure is only lawful if the underlying written consent properly designates recipients (name/class and intermediary rules where applicable).
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_CONSENTREQUIREMENT_38_009 relies on the existence of a valid single consent for all future TPO uses/disclosures. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_consent_requirement_34_003 defines mandatory recipient designation elements for that consent; without them, the consent cannot be relied upon.
dependencies.impact: If recipient designations are missing/defective, the organization cannot lawfully rely on the single TPO consent; downstream TPO uses/disclosures become impermissible Part 2 disclosures.
examples: A Part 2 program uses a generic consent that does not designate an HIE/intermediary correctly; subsequent TPO sharing through the HIE cannot rely on the consent.
A consent lists no recipient class; the covered entity’s payment disclosure to a payer is not supported by a valid Part 2 consent.

dependencies.strength_justification: The single TPO consent construct for disclosures to HIPAA CEs/BAs requires a specific redisclosure statement; missing it makes the consent deficient for that pathway.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_consent_requirement_34_004 mandates inclusion of the HIPAA-redisclosure statement (with the proceedings-against-patient carve-out) when disclosing Part 2 records for TPO to a HIPAA covered entity or business associate—core to the broad TPO workflow described in BR_COVERED_ENTITY_CONSENTREQUIREMENT_38_009.
dependencies.impact: Without the required statement, the consent should not be used to support disclosure; proceeding anyway creates Part 2 impermissible disclosure risk.
examples: A Part 2 program discloses to a hospital for treatment using a consent that lacks the redisclosure statement; the disclosure is not properly supported.
A BA receives Part 2 records under a deficient consent and then shares internally for operations; the chain is tainted by deficient consent content.

dependencies.strength_justification: The permission to continue TPO uses/disclosures under a single consent is expressly conditioned on non-revocation and on having valid expiration/revocation terms.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_CONSENTREQUIREMENT_38_009 permits ongoing HIPAA-permitted TPO use/disclosure until written revocation. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_consent_requirement_34_005 defines required revocation language and expiration/expiration event; it also defines the trigger (revocation in writing) that shuts off future reliance.
dependencies.impact: If revocation/expiration terms are missing, consent is deficient and cannot support ongoing uses; if a revocation occurs, continued TPO uses/disclosures based on the consent become impermissible.
examples: A patient revokes consent in writing; the CE continues disclosing to a health plan for payment—noncompliant after revocation.
A consent form omits an expiration event/date; program treats it as indefinite and continues disclosures—consent is deficient.

dependencies.strength_justification: Cease-use/cease-disclosure obligations only trigger after knowledge of a written revocation; this is a hard stop control.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_039_004 operationalizes the consequences of a revocation, which is defined/required by BR_COVERED_ENTITY_consent_requirement_34_005 (right to revoke, writing, reliance exceptions).
dependencies.impact: If revocation handling is not implemented, the entity will continue uses/disclosures after revocation, creating high-risk Part 2 violations.
examples: Revocation is received by HIM; downstream analytics team continues using Part 2 data for operations—impermissible after revocation.
Revocation is filed but not propagated to an HIE feed; ongoing redisclosures continue.

dependencies.strength_justification: Revocation-effect rules supersede the general permission to use/disclose under broad TPO consent.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_CONSENTREQUIREMENT_38_009 grants ongoing TPO permissions until revocation; BR_COVERED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_039_004 requires cessation upon revocation (with narrow reliance/insurance exceptions). Operationally, it overrides the default 'continue TPO' state.
dependencies.impact: If the override is not applied, the organization will keep relying on an invalidated consent and disclose unlawfully.
examples: A payer requests records after revocation; the CE discloses under old consent—violates the cease-disclosure override.
A BA continues sending Part 2 data to participants despite being informed of revocation.

dependencies.strength_justification: An HIE/HIN stop-redisclosure obligation is triggered by written revocation; failure creates immediate impermissible redisclosure risk.
dependencies.rationale: BR_BUSINESS_ASSOCIATE_HIPAA_PRIVACY_006_005 requires HIE/HIN lawful holders to cease redisclosure upon learning of written revocation. The concept and trigger of revocation derives from BR_COVERED_ENTITY_consent_requirement_34_005.
dependencies.impact: If revocations are not recognized/processed by the HIE/HIN, it may continue redisclosing Part 2 records unlawfully across multiple participants.
examples: Patient revokes consent; HIE continues routing CCDs containing Part 2 SUD data to multiple providers.
HIE receives revocation but does not stop a standing subscription feed.

dependencies.strength_justification: The §2.32 attachment requirement presupposes a valid consent; recipient designation is a core validity element.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_039_002 operationalizes attaching consent/scope explanation per disclosure. That workflow only makes sense and is compliant if the consent itself meets §2.31 recipient designation requirements in BR_COVERED_ENTITY_consent_requirement_34_003.
dependencies.impact: Disclosure workflow can be procedurally complete yet substantively noncompliant due to invalid consent content.
examples: Batch interface sends 'scope explanation' referencing a consent that lacks correct recipient class—still noncompliant.
HIE receives a consent attachment that doesn’t list intermediary/participants correctly.

dependencies.strength_justification: Valid consent content (revocation/expiration) is needed before implementing per-disclosure attachment processes as compliant.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_039_002 applies to disclosures under consent; BR_COVERED_ENTITY_consent_requirement_34_005 is a core validity gate for whether a consent may be relied upon at all.
dependencies.impact: If consents lack required terms, attachment processes will distribute deficient consents, increasing systemic Part 2 disclosure violations.
examples: Electronic consent template missing expiration event; system dutifully attaches it to every disclosure.
Revocation language omitted; recipient receives a consent copy that is not compliant.

dependencies.strength_justification: Recipient redisclosure permissions under HIPAA for Part 2 records in the TPO pathway depend on the consent including the required redisclosure statement.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_040_006 describes further disclosure permissions for recipients of Part 2 records disclosed for TPO. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_consent_requirement_34_004 is the consent element that sets expectations and is required when the recipient is a HIPAA CE/BA in that TPO disclosure context.
dependencies.impact: If the statement is missing, the initial disclosure is not properly supported; downstream redisclosures compound risk.
examples: A Part 2 program discloses to a hospital for treatment without the required statement; the hospital then relies on HIPAA permissions—chain is noncompliant.
A BA receives Part 2 data and shares for operations; initial consent lacked the statement.

dependencies.strength_justification: The 'broad TPO consent' operational model enables the HIPAA-permitted redisclosure model; without the broad consent construct, the downstream redisclosure framework may not apply.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_040_006 is triggered when Part 2 records are disclosed for TPO to a CE/BA. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_CONSENTREQUIREMENT_38_009 provides the broad consent mechanism under which such disclosures are operationally made and relied upon over time.
dependencies.impact: If the broad consent is absent/invalid/revoked, the downstream redisclosure logic cannot be safely applied; disclosures must stop or find an alternate lawful basis.
examples: No single TPO consent exists; a BA assumes it can share Part 2 data under HIPAA anyway—impermissible.
Consent was revoked but recipient continues redisclosing under HIPAA—impermissible.

dependencies.strength_justification: Proceedings-against-patient uses/disclosures are explicitly carved out of the general HIPAA-permitted redisclosure model and require Part 2 procedures.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_040_006 permits further disclosures under HIPAA except for proceedings against the patient. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_19_005 specifies the required Part 2 procedures (court order under §2.64/§2.65 or separate §2.31(d) consent) for that carve-out scenario.
dependencies.impact: If the carve-out is ignored, recipients may disclose Part 2 records into proceedings against the patient without required court order/consent—high enforcement risk.
examples: Hospital discloses Part 2 records to prosecutor in a case against the patient without Part 2 court order.
BA provides testimony derived from Part 2 records in an administrative proceeding against the patient without separate consent.

dependencies.strength_justification: If the proceeding is not against the patient, the entity may proceed under HIPAA 164.512(e) rather than Part 2 court-order procedures—this changes the required compliance path.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_19_005 requires Part 2 procedures for proceedings against the patient. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_19_006 specifies that when the proceeding is not against the patient, HIPAA 164.512(e) governs instead, overriding the need to obtain Part 2 court orders/consent for that scenario.
dependencies.impact: Misclassification (treating an 'against the patient' proceeding as 'not against') can cause impermissible disclosure; the reverse can cause unnecessary burden/delay but is less likely to be unlawful.
examples: Patient is a witness, not the defendant; entity responds under HIPAA 164.512(e) without Part 2 court order.
Entity mistakenly treats a criminal prosecution of the patient as 'not against' and discloses under HIPAA 164.512(e)—impermissible.

dependencies.strength_justification: If using a §2.65 court order path for criminal investigation/prosecution, the order must meet the limiting criteria; otherwise the authorization is defective/overbroad.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_19_005 allows proceeding use/disclosure only with Part 2 court order under §2.64/§2.65 or separate consent. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_19_007 defines mandatory scope limits for §2.65 orders in criminal investigation/prosecution against the patient.
dependencies.impact: If §2.65 order requirements are not met, disclosures may be overbroad or improperly directed, creating Part 2 noncompliance and liability exposure.
examples: Court order requests entire SUD chart; custodian discloses without limiting to essential parts—noncompliant with §2.65 constraints.
Order allows broad agency distribution beyond responsible officials; disclosure violates the limitation criteria.

dependencies.strength_justification: Integration without segregation increases reliance on 'retain Part 2 status' controls to prevent prohibited proceedings-related uses/disclosures.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_039_003 allows non-segregation but requires Part 2 characteristic retention. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_039_005 emphasizes retention specifically to enforce the continuing prohibition on proceedings-against-patient uses/disclosures absent consent/court order.
dependencies.impact: If retention controls are weak in integrated records, organizations can inadvertently disclose Part 2 data into investigations/proceedings, violating Part 2.
examples: Part 2 data is merged into the EHR; legal team pulls records for a prosecution against the patient without recognizing Part 2 restrictions.
Integrated record exports to eDiscovery without Part 2 tagging and constraints.

dependencies.strength_justification: The proceedings-against-patient restriction depends on the recipient continuing to treat the records as Part 2 records after integration.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_19_005 applies to Part 2 records received under TPO consent and later requested for proceedings against the patient. If the recipient fails to retain Part 2 status after integration (contrary to BR_COVERED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_039_003), the recipient may not apply required Part 2 court order/consent safeguards.
dependencies.impact: Failure to retain Part 2 status leads to misrouting under ordinary HIPAA subpoenas and impermissible disclosures.
examples: Recipient treats Part 2 notes as ordinary PHI and discloses under a subpoena in a case against the patient.
Recipient cannot identify Part 2-derived elements when responding to law enforcement requests.

dependencies.strength_justification: Treating Part 2 records as PHI in the designated record set intersects with integration and status-retention controls; both must align for compliant handling and disclosure.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_21_001 states Part 2 records in a CE’s designated record set become PHI subject to HIPAA access rights. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_CONSENT_REQUIREMENT_039_003 allows integration without segregation but requires retention of Part 2 status—necessary to avoid stripping Part 2 protections while meeting HIPAA DRS obligations.
dependencies.impact: If the organization conflates 'PHI in DRS' with 'no Part 2 restrictions', it may disclose Part 2 data improperly (e.g., in proceedings).
examples: CE processes access requests under HIPAA but fails to maintain Part 2 status for other downstream uses/disclosures.
CE integrates Part 2 records into billing systems (DRS) and later discloses for legal proceedings without Part 2 safeguards.

dependencies.strength_justification: When an HIE is a HIPAA BA, it is excluded from the Part 2 'intermediary' definition; this changes whether intermediary-specific consent designation requirements apply.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_consent_requirement_34_003 includes additional requirements when the recipient is an intermediary. BR_BUSINESS_ASSOCIATE_HIPAAPRIVACY_15_007 clarifies that BAs are excluded from 'intermediary,' overriding application of §2.31(a)(4) intermediary consent requirements for that BA-HIE pathway.
dependencies.impact: If misclassified, the CE may apply the wrong consent workflow: either imposing unnecessary intermediary participant listing (burden/delay) or failing to meet intermediary requirements for non-BA intermediaries (noncompliance).
examples: A CE routes Part 2 data through its BA HIE and incorrectly requires intermediary participant naming; causes operational burden.
A non-BA intermediary is mistakenly treated as a BA; consent lacks required intermediary participant designation—noncompliance.

dependencies.strength_justification: The BA HIE’s ability to receive and redisclose Part 2 records under HIPAA is premised on receipt under a broad TPO consent construct.
dependencies.rationale: BR_BUSINESS_ASSOCIATE_HIPAA_PRIVACY_17_005 describes BA HIE receipt and redisclosure of Part 2 records under a broad TPO consent/general designation. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_CONSENTREQUIREMENT_38_009 provides the single-consent-for-future-TPO mechanism enabling that model.
dependencies.impact: If the broad TPO consent is absent/invalid/revoked, the HIE’s HIPAA-based redisclosure posture is not safely available; disclosures must stop or use another lawful basis.
examples: HIE receives Part 2 data assuming broad consent exists; consent was revoked—continued sharing is impermissible.
Broad consent never obtained; HIE rediscloses to participants under HIPAA anyway—noncompliance risk.

dependencies.strength_justification: Breach notification obligations are triggered by the existence of a 'breach' under the incorporated definition; this is the gating legal determination.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_26_004 adopts HIPAA’s breach definition for Part 2 records and establishes presumption/low-probability risk assessment logic. BR_BUSINESS_ASSOCIATE_HIPAAPRIVACY_016_001 requires BA notice to the covered entity upon breach of unsecured PHI (including Part 2 by CARES Act incorporation).
dependencies.impact: If breach determination is not performed correctly, a BA may fail to notify the CE (or notify unnecessarily), causing downstream notification failures and enforcement exposure.
examples: BA discovers unauthorized access to Part 2 records but skips risk assessment and fails to notify the CE—noncompliance.
BA performs risk assessment and documents low probability; event treated as not a breach—no notification required.

dependencies.strength_justification: QSO breach notification applicability depends on whether an incident meets the incorporated breach definition for Part 2 records.
dependencies.rationale: BR_BUSINESS_ASSOCIATE_REPORTING_005_005 clarifies QSOs are subject to breach notification obligations; those obligations are triggered when an incident is a 'breach' as defined/implemented in BR_COVERED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_26_004.
dependencies.impact: If the breach definition is misapplied, QSOs may fail to execute required notifications, creating regulatory and contractual exposure.
examples: QSO experiences exfiltration of Part 2 records; failure to classify as a breach leads to missed notifications.
QSO documents low-probability risk assessment and avoids unnecessary notification.

dependencies.strength_justification: QSO clarification reinforces that BA-like entities must follow the BA-to-CE notification model when applicable.
dependencies.rationale: BR_BUSINESS_ASSOCIATE_REPORTING_005_005 clarifies QSOs are included in breach notification costing/coverage; BR_BUSINESS_ASSOCIATE_HIPAAPRIVACY_016_001 provides the operational core requirement (BA notifies CE). Implemented together, they define who must notify and when.
dependencies.impact: If complementary alignment is missed, QSOs may assume they are exempt and fail to notify covered entities, delaying or preventing required notifications.
examples: A QSO vendor believes Part 2 breach rules do not apply; fails to notify CE after breach discovery.
A QSO implements BA notification workflows consistent with 17932(b) and meets Part 2-aligned obligations.

dependencies.strength_justification: A specific mandatory acceptance requirement supersedes the more general 'may approve or deny' framing for the specified scenario.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_hipaa_privacy_34_001 states restriction requests may be approved or denied consistent with HIPAA concepts (subject to more restrictive law). BR_COVERED_ENTITY_hipaa_privacy_34_002 creates a mandatory 'must agree' duty for restricting disclosure to a health plan for payment/operations when not otherwise required by law.
dependencies.impact: If the override is ignored, the Part 2 program may unlawfully deny a restriction it must accept, creating enforceable noncompliance.
examples: Patient requests restriction to health plan for payment; program denies despite not being legally required to disclose—noncompliant.
Program accepts the restriction and blocks payment/operations disclosures to the health plan.

dependencies.strength_justification: Both rules govern restriction-to-health-plan handling; one in HIPAA PHI context and one in Part 2 context, creating an integrated operational restriction pathway.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_025_007 defines when acceptance of restriction requests is mandatory for health plan disclosures (paid-in-full services) and requires reasonable effort to accommodate restrictions. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_hipaa_privacy_34_002 creates a Part 2-specific mandatory restriction acceptance duty for health plan disclosures for payment/operations (when not required by law).
dependencies.impact: If workflows are not harmonized, the organization may inconsistently honor restrictions across Part 2 and non-Part 2 information, leading to inappropriate plan disclosures.
examples: Patient paid cash in full and requests no plan disclosure; HIPAA workflow blocks but Part 2 workflow fails—noncompliant under Part 2 rule.
Organization implements a unified restriction flag that applies to both PHI and Part 2 records when conditions are met.

dependencies.strength_justification: Identifiable Part 2 disclosures to a public health authority require patient consent that specifically designates the recipient; recipient designation is a consent validity element.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_HIPAA_PRIVACY_26_005 requires specific recipient designation when patients consent to disclose identifiable Part 2 records to a public health authority. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_consent_requirement_34_003 defines how Part 2 consents must designate recipients by name/class.
dependencies.impact: If the consent does not properly designate the public health authority recipient, the identifiable disclosure is impermissible.
examples: Patient signs consent naming 'public health' generically; disclosure to a specific county health department occurs—noncompliant.
Consent specifically names 'State Department of Health'; disclosure proceeds compliantly.

dependencies.strength_justification: If the lease fails threshold requirements (writing/signed/term), there is no viable path to satisfy downstream exception elements like exclusive use.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_BILLING_COMPLIANCE_33_001 sets core Stark lease exception prerequisites. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_BILLING_COMPLIANCE_33_002 is an additional element (exclusive use; reasonable/necessary space) that is only relevant if a qualifying lease exists.
dependencies.impact: If the lease is not written/signed or term is invalid, the arrangement fails the exception regardless of exclusive use, increasing Stark risk.
examples: Handshake lease without signatures; even if space is used exclusively, exception fails.
Lease term is 6 months; exclusive use is irrelevant because threshold element fails.

dependencies.strength_justification: FMV/set-in-advance analysis is downstream of having a qualifying written lease with minimum term requirements.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_BILLING_COMPLIANCE_33_003 contains pricing/referral-related requirements for the same Stark lease exception. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_BILLING_COMPLIANCE_33_001 defines baseline structural requirements that must be satisfied first for the exception to be available.
dependencies.impact: Absent a compliant written/term lease, even perfectly FMV rent does not save the arrangement under the office space rental exception.
examples: Lease is not signed; rent is FMV—exception still fails.
Parties re-lease the same space during the first year after early termination—structural failure makes pricing compliance moot.

dependencies.strength_justification: Both are required elements of the same Stark lease exception and must be satisfied together to protect the arrangement.
dependencies.rationale: Exclusive use/reasonable space (33_002) and FMV/set-in-advance/non-referral-based rent (33_003) are separate required prongs; failure of either causes exception failure.
dependencies.impact: Even with FMV rent, non-exclusive use can fail the exception; even with exclusive use, referral-based rent can fail the exception.
examples: Lessor uses the space during lessee hours; despite FMV rent, exception fails.
Rent is a percentage of collections; despite exclusive use, exception fails.

dependencies.strength_justification: Timing rules for payment only apply once the required contribution amount is established (15% of donor cost) and must be paid.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_BILLING_COMPLIANCE_036_002 defines the required 15% cost contribution (including who may pay). BR_COVERED_ENTITY_BILLING_COMPLIANCE_036_001 defines when that contribution must be paid (before initial/replacement; reasonable intervals for updates).
dependencies.impact: If contribution is not properly defined/ensured as actually paid, the timing workflow fails and the EHR donation exception may not be met.
examples: Physician never pays the 15% contribution; donor still provides EHR—exception risk.
Physician’s organization pays the 15% on physician’s behalf before initial delivery—compliant.

dependencies.strength_justification: The ability to waive the 15% contribution for cybersecurity technology hinges on fully satisfying the separate cybersecurity exception requirements.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_BILLING_COMPLIANCE_036_003 allows avoiding the EHR-exception cost contribution for cybersecurity donations only if the cybersecurity donation satisfies all requirements of 42 CFR 411.357(bb). BR_COVERED_ENTITY_BILLING_COMPLIANCE_036_004 sets those required 411.357(bb) conditions.
dependencies.impact: If cybersecurity exception conditions are not met, treating the cybersecurity donation as exempt from the 15% contribution can cause Stark exception failure and payment/penalty exposure.
examples: Donation eligibility is tied to referral volume; cybersecurity exception fails and contribution waiver is invalid.
Written documentation is missing; parties cannot rely on 411.357(bb) and must reassess contribution requirements.

dependencies.strength_justification: When cybersecurity donation qualifies under 411.357(bb), it displaces (waives) the 15% contribution otherwise required under the EHR donation exception for that cybersecurity component.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_BILLING_COMPLIANCE_036_002 sets the general 15% contribution rule for EHR donations. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_BILLING_COMPLIANCE_036_003 creates a conditional pathway where contribution is not required for cybersecurity technology/services if the cybersecurity exception is met.
dependencies.impact: If override conditions are misunderstood, parties may either over-collect (unnecessary burden) or under-collect (exception failure) contributions.
examples: Donor treats cybersecurity donation as exempt without meeting 411.357(bb); fails to collect 15%—risk.
Donor meets 411.357(bb) and does not require physician to pay 15% for cybersecurity tools—compliant.

dependencies.strength_justification: Distribution deeming tests apply to 'overall profits' which must be correctly defined first (including component size rules).
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_BILLING_COMPLIANCE_036_005 defines 'overall profits' and component requirements (≥5 physicians) and verifiability. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_BILLING_COMPLIANCE_036_006 provides deeming pathways for distributions not directly relating to volume/value of referrals; those pathways presuppose that 'overall profits' are correctly constructed under 036_005.
dependencies.impact: If overall profits are incorrectly scoped (e.g., component too small), even a seemingly compliant distribution method may fail Stark group practice tests.
examples: Group uses a 3-physician component for overall profits; then uses per-capita distribution—fails due to incorrect 'overall profits' definition.
Group uses ≥5 physician component and applies per-capita distribution—fits deeming pathway.

dependencies.strength_justification: The professional medical review’s conclusions depend on the operative definition of an 'appropriate' medical screening exam.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_30_001 requires a physician peer review before CMPs/termination in specified EMTALA cases. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_30_002 defines the standard used by physician reviewers to evaluate screening compliance (symptom-suitable, non-disparate; not necessarily correct diagnosis).
dependencies.impact: If reviewers use the wrong standard (e.g., negligence/correct diagnosis), the review may be invalid or misleading, affecting enforcement outcomes and due process.
examples: Peer review flags EMTALA violation solely because diagnosis was wrong; 30_002 indicates that is not the standard.
Peer review compares screening consistency across similarly situated patients to assess disparate treatment.

dependencies.strength_justification: Single-event violation logic means a signage deficiency can independently trigger a violation finding and adverse recommendation.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_30_003 states a single occurrence of EMTALA noncompliance constitutes a violation. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_18_008 is an EMTALA-related CMS posting requirement; a single failure to conspicuously post required signs can constitute that 'single occurrence.'
dependencies.impact: If signage is not compliant even once (or is persistently noncompliant), the organization is exposed to an EMTALA deficiency citation and adverse recommendation logic.
examples: ED entrance lacks EMTALA rights signage; survey documents one observation—sufficient to cite violation.
Non-traditional waiting area lacks Medicaid participation sign; single-instance observation triggers deficiency under 30_003 logic.

dependencies.strength_justification: Failure to report suspected improper transfers—even once—can independently support an EMTALA deficiency under single-occurrence logic.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_REPORTING_30_005 imposes a receiving-hospital reporting duty for suspected EMTALA-violative transfers. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_30_003 makes a single noncompliance occurrence sufficient for a violation/adverse recommendation.
dependencies.impact: If the hospital fails to report a suspected EMTALA-violative transfer, the single failure can be cited and contribute to enforcement action.
examples: Receiving hospital suspects sending facility transferred unstable patient but does not report—single failure supports citation.
ED physician flags suspected violation in chart; compliance team does not report to CMS—noncompliance.

dependencies.strength_justification: Determining whether screening noncompliance occurred (and thus whether a single occurrence exists) depends on the definition of 'appropriate' screening.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_30_003 is applied after assessing whether an EMTALA requirement was violated. BR_COVERED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_30_002 defines the compliance test for screening examinations (symptom-suitable and non-disparate), which is a common alleged violation type.
dependencies.impact: If screening is incorrectly evaluated (e.g., equating negligence with EMTALA violation), the organization may be wrongly cited or wrongly cleared, affecting enforcement posture.
examples: Single disparate screening instance (insured vs uninsured) supports a violation finding under 30_003.
Single case with incorrect diagnosis but non-disparate screening should not be deemed a violation solely on diagnosis error.

dependencies.strength_justification: Proper investigation communications and handling of draft deficiencies supports the integrity of the deficiency reporting process leading to RO determinations.
dependencies.rationale: BR_COVERED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_30_006 governs investigator conduct (no premature violation statements; no draft CMS-2567 left behind) to preserve RO authority. This is procedurally sequential to the compliance assessment and deficiency reporting process in which BR_COVERED_ENTITY_REGULATORY_30_003 is applied.
dependencies.impact: If investigators improperly communicate findings or leave drafts, the investigation process may be procedurally compromised, requiring corrective administrative steps and potentially delaying enforcement resolution.
examples: Surveyor tells hospital 'you’re in violation' during exit conference—process defect requiring RO clarification.
Surveyor leaves draft CMS-2567; hospital disputes based on draft language before RO completion.

dependencies.strength_justification: Part 2 establishes a default prohibition; consent is the primary mechanism that lifts the prohibition for most first-instance disclosures.
dependencies.rationale: BR_003_002 frames that Part 2 use/disclosure is prohibited unless a permitted circumstance exists. BR_11_010 operationalizes the most common permitted circumstance—prior written consent (or a specific exception).
dependencies.impact: If the entity does not apply the Part 2 prohibition logic first, it may disclose without valid consent/exception, creating an impermissible disclosure event.
examples: A Part 2 program receives a TPO request and discloses without verifying an applicable exception/consent exists.
A lawful holder assumes HIPAA TPO rules apply and omits Part 2 consent analysis for SUD records.

dependencies.strength_justification: Single all-future TPO consent is a specialized form of Part 2 written consent; without the underlying consent requirement, the single-consent model cannot be relied upon.
dependencies.rationale: BR_20_001 depends on the existence of a valid written consent paradigm under Part 2. BR_11_010 provides that baseline requirement and the scope (first-instance disclosure and treatment redisclosure consent).
dependencies.impact: If prior written consent requirements are not met, the organization cannot validly rely on the single-consent-for-all-future-TPO approach.
examples: A program uses a single TPO consent workflow but the consent is missing required elements, so it is not valid under Part 2.
A recipient treats an old HIPAA authorization as the Part 2 TPO consent without confirming it meets Part 2 consent requirements.

dependencies.strength_justification: Redisclosure permissions for health care operations in the cited framework require a valid consent that includes health care operations; the single TPO consent is the enabling instrument in this ruleset.
dependencies.rationale: BR_13_010 is triggered by disclosures made under a consent that includes health care operations. BR_20_001 defines the single consent for all future TPO uses/disclosures (including HCO).
dependencies.impact: If no valid TPO/HCO-inclusive consent exists, the disclosure and subsequent HIPAA-based redisclosure logic described in BR_13_010 cannot be used.
examples: A Part 2 program discloses for audit/quality review without a consent that includes HCO and then claims HIPAA redisclosure permission applies.
A BA recipient rediscloses for operations believing HCO consent existed, but the consent on file covered only treatment.

dependencies.strength_justification: The recipient’s HIPAA-permitted redisclosure pathway described is contingent on a TPO disclosure made under the applicable consent framework.
dependencies.rationale: BR_20_002 applies when Part 2 records are disclosed for TPO to a covered entity/BA. BR_20_001 supplies the enduring consent authority enabling those TPO disclosures until revoked.
dependencies.impact: Without valid (and unrevoked) TPO consent, the recipient may not be permitted to use/redisclose under HIPAA as described, increasing Part 2 redisclosure violations.
examples: A hospital receives SUD records and rediscloses to a billing vendor assuming HIPAA TPO applies, but the Part 2 TPO consent was revoked.
A CE receives Part 2 data for treatment without valid consent and then uses it for operations under HIPAA.

dependencies.strength_justification: Redisclosure rules are downstream of the initial lawful disclosure; initial consent/exception analysis must occur before applying recipient onward-use rules.
dependencies.rationale: BR_12_002 governs further use/disclosure by recipients. Those pathways presuppose the record was received under a Part 2-permitted disclosure basis (often via BR_11_010 consent).
dependencies.impact: If initial disclosure was not permitted, subsequent reliance on recipient redisclosure pathways is invalid and compounds compliance exposure across the disclosure chain.
examples: A recipient argues it is a CE so it can use/redisclose, but the original disclosure lacked Part 2 consent.
A lawful holder rediscloses to a treatment provider without the patient’s redisclosure consent for treatment.

dependencies.strength_justification: BR_12_002 provides the general recipient onward-use gate; BR_20_002 provides a specific TPO-to-CE/BA redisclosure rule with an additional proceedings-against-patient limitation.
dependencies.rationale: BR_20_002 is best interpreted as a specialized implementation of BR_12_002 for the TPO-to-CE/BA scenario, clarifying how HIPAA permissions apply and what remains prohibited.
dependencies.impact: If the general redisclosure gate (BR_12_002) is not enforced, organizations may over-read BR_20_002 as blanket redisclosure permission and miss non-TPO constraints.
examples: A BA rediscloses Part 2 data for a non-TPO purpose claiming HIPAA allows it, without checking the Part 2 redisclosure pathway criteria.
A CE uses Part 2 records in an administrative proceeding against the patient contrary to BR_20_002’s limitation.

dependencies.strength_justification: Revocation handling obligations become operationally relevant only when uses/disclosures are being made based on an authorization/consent that can be revoked.
dependencies.rationale: BR_20_001 states TPO consent remains valid until revoked; BR_004_008 defines the post-revocation obligation to cease further uses/disclosures with narrow exceptions.
dependencies.impact: If revocation logic is not linked to the TPO consent model, entities may continue TPO disclosures after revocation, causing systemic impermissible disclosures.
examples: A patient revokes the TPO consent; the CE continues to share via HIE because the consent registry was not updated.
A BA continues data feeds for payment operations after being informed of revocation.

dependencies.strength_justification: HIE-specific cessation is an operational downstream step after the recipient becomes aware of revocation and must cease further disclosures.
dependencies.rationale: BR_004_009 is the HIE implementation of BR_004_008 for exchange networks: once informed, stop onward disclosures to other member participants.
dependencies.impact: If the general revocation cessation rule is not executed, the HIE will likely continue member participant disclosures, multiplying downstream redisclosure violations.
examples: An HIE receives written revocation but continues routing results to member providers for weeks.
A consent revocation is processed at the CE, but not propagated to the HIE, so onward disclosures continue.

dependencies.strength_justification: The notice-to-accompany requirement is triggered by disclosures made with the patient’s written consent; consent is therefore the trigger condition.
dependencies.rationale: BR_19_009 applies to each consent-based disclosure. BR_11_010 establishes when a consent-based disclosure is required/used in the first instance.
dependencies.impact: If consent governance is not in place, notice packaging will be inconsistently applied or omitted, increasing recipient confusion and redisclosure violations.
examples: A program sends Part 2 data with written consent but forgets the Part 2 notice because the workflow didn’t flag it as Part 2 consent-based.
An HIE transmits records under consent but uses a generic HIPAA disclosure footer rather than the Part 2 notice.

dependencies.strength_justification: Identifier-removal requirements operationalize and validate whether the broader de-identification standard has been met.
dependencies.rationale: BR_16_001 requires HIPAA de-identification per 45 CFR 164.514(b); BR_18_010 specifies removal of identifiers and secure handling of re-identification codes consistent with meeting that standard.
dependencies.impact: If identifier removal and code controls are not implemented, the entity’s claim of de-identification is unreliable and disclosures premised on de-identification become impermissible.
examples: A dataset omits names but leaves record numbers that function as unique identifiers, defeating de-identification.
A re-identification key is shared with a downstream partner, creating a reasonable basis to identify individuals.

dependencies.strength_justification: Public health disclosures without consent are permitted only if the records are de-identified to the HIPAA standard; failure defeats the permission.
dependencies.rationale: BR_13_008 is a permission conditioned on de-identification under 45 CFR 164.514(b). BR_16_001 defines the de-identification standard to be met whenever Part 2 data is de-identified.
dependencies.impact: If de-identification does not meet the HIPAA standard, the disclosure without consent is not permitted and becomes a Part 2 violation.
examples: A Part 2 program sends line-level SUD encounter data with dates/ZIP that enable identification to a public health authority without consent.
A program discloses a small-cell dataset that creates a reasonable basis for re-identification.

dependencies.strength_justification: Research reports may include Part 2 data only in de-identified aggregate form; BR_16_001 defines the de-identification standard that must be met.
dependencies.rationale: BR_22_005 requires de-identification per 45 CFR 164.514(b) so there is no reasonable basis to identify a patient. BR_16_001 is the cross-cutting de-identification rule for Part 2 alignment with HIPAA.
dependencies.impact: If de-identification does not meet HIPAA standards, publication/reporting becomes an impermissible disclosure/redisclosure under Part 2 (and potentially HIPAA).
examples: A researcher publishes a case-series table with small counts enabling re-identification of a known community member.
A report includes dates of service and location in a way that makes individuals identifiable.
